Links should have descriptive text (not "click here") and should be navigable via keyboard. --- ### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **`href` = Hypertext Reference** ŌĆö the destination URL of an anchor link. - It is a **mandatory attribute** for the `` element to function as a hyperlink. - **URL types:** Absolute, Relative, Root-Relative, Fragment, Protocol-based, Data URI. - **Protocol schemes:** `https://`, `mailto:`, `tel:`, `sms:`, `ftp://`, `javascript:`. - **`target="_blank"`** opens a link in a new tab ŌĆö always pair with `rel="noopener noreferrer"` for security. - The **`download` attribute** forces file download instead of navigation. - The **`` element** overrides the base URL for all relative `href` resolutions in a document. - **Fragment links** (`#id`) enable in-page navigation by targeting element `id` attributes. - **Accessibility best practice:** Use *descriptive link text* and ensure keyboard navigability. - **SEO best practice:** Use *meaningful `href` values* and avoid JavaScript-only navigation patterns. - **Empty `href=""`** reloads the current page ŌĆö considered a bad practice. - **`hreflang`** attribute can be added to specify the language of the linked resource. - **Common exam trap:** Differentiating between `href` (links to a resource) and `src` (embeds a resource). - **KTU 2024 focus:** Expect questions on *real-world web navigation scenarios*, *security attributes*, and *accessibility considerations*.

topics.title: Linking to Pages on the Web
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEITT833 - WEB DESIGNING > - **Module:** Module Module 1 > - **Topic:** Linking to Pages on the Web # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Hyperlink (Hypertext Link):** A Hyperlink is a clickable element (text, image, or other HTML content) that connects one web resource to another, enabling navigation across the World Wide Web. In HTML, hyperlinks are created using the **Anchor Element ``** along with the **`href`** (Hypertext Reference) attribute to specify the destination URL. In the context of KTU 2024 Scheme Web Designing, "Linking to Pages on the Web" refers to the structural mechanism of establishing **navigational connectivity** between HTML documents, resources, and external services using standardized URL (Uniform Resource Locator) addressing and the anchor element. ### The Anatomy of a Web Link A complete, well-formed web link has the following components: $$\text{Link} = \underbrace{\text{Protocol}}_{\text{scheme}} + \underbrace{\text{Domain/IP}}_{\text{host}} + \underbrace{\text{Path}}_{\text{location}} + \underbrace{\text{Fragment}}_{\text{section}}$$ Formally expressed in the standard URL syntax: $$\text{URL} = \underbrace{\texttt{scheme://}}_{\text{protocol}} \underbrace{\texttt{host[:port]}}_{\text{server}} \underbrace{\texttt{/path}}_{\text{resource}} \underbrace{\texttt{?query}}_{\text{parameters}} \underbrace{\texttt{\#fragment}}_{\text{anchor}}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Key Terminology (KTU Board-Standard Vocabulary):** > - **URL** ŌĆō Uniform Resource Locator; the complete address of a web resource. > - **URN** ŌĆō Uniform Resource Name; a persistent, location-independent name. > - **URI** ŌĆō Uniform Resource Identifier; the superset of both URL and URN. > - **Hypertext** ŌĆō Text displayed on a computer display or other electronic devices with references (hyperlinks) to other text that the reader can immediately access. > - **Navigation** ŌĆō The act of moving between web pages via hyperlinks. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **­¤ōī The "Postal Mail" Analogy** > Imagine the World Wide Web as a massive global postal system: > - **Web Page** = A letter or document you want to send/view. > - **Browser** = Your personal postman who delivers the mail. > - **URL (Uniform Resource Locator)** = The complete postal address written on the envelope (country ŌåÆ state ŌåÆ city ŌåÆ street ŌåÆ house number). > - **Hyperlink** = The pre-written address on a letter. When you click it, the browser instantly "travels" to that address. > - **Anchor Text** = The visible words on the envelope that tell you *what* the letter contains (e.g., "Click here for Admission Form"). > - **Relative Path** = Directions from your *current location* to a nearby shop (e.g., "go 2 streets ahead, turn left"). > - **Absolute Path** = A complete GPS coordinate that works *from anywhere* on Earth (e.g., "Kochi, Kerala, India, MG Road, House #42"). Just as postal addresses allow letters to find recipients, **URLs allow hyperlinks to find web pages** ŌĆö anywhere on the planet. ## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Reserved Characters > [!IMPORTANT] > **Bolded Standards for Board Examinations:** > - The default maximum practical URL length in browsers is **2,083 characters** (Internet Explorer) and **65,535 characters** (modern browsers). > - The **HTTP standard port** is **80**; **HTTPS** uses **443**. > - HTML anchor elements must be properly closed (either with `` in HTML5 or self-closed in XHTML). > - The **`href`** attribute is the **mandatory** attribute for an anchor to be a functional link. > - URLs are **case-insensitive** for the domain name portion but **case-sensitive** for the path. ### Reserved Characters That Must Be Percent-Encoded in URLs | Character | Name | Encoded As | Purpose | |-----------|------------|------------|-----------------| | (space) | Space | %20 | Separator | | # | Hash | %23 | Fragment marker | | ? | Question | %3F | Query separator | | & | Ampersand | %26 | Query parameter | | / | Slash | %2F | Path separator | --- # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The HTML Anchor Element ŌĆö Theoretical Breakdown The anchor element `` is an **inline element** that creates a hyperlink to: 1. Another web page (external or internal). 2. A specific location within the same page (using fragments). 3. An email address (using `mailto:`). 4. A telephone number (using `tel:`). 5. A downloadable file (using the `download` attribute). ### Logical Step-by-Step Operation **Step 1: User Encounters an Anchor Element** - The browser parses the HTML and identifies ``. - The text or image inside `...` is rendered with default link styling (typically **blue underlined**). **Step 2: User Initiates Interaction** - The `cursor` changes to a pointer (hand icon) on hover. - A click event is triggered, intercepted by the anchor's `href`. **Step 3: Browser Resolves the URL** - If the URL is **relative**, the browser converts it to absolute using the current page's URL as the base. - If the URL is **absolute**, it is used directly. **Step 4: Browser Fetches the Resource** - An **HTTP/HTTPS GET request** is sent to the target server. - The server responds with the requested document. **Step 5: Browser Renders the Destination** - The new page replaces the current page (default), or opens in a new tab/window (depending on the `target` attribute). ## 2.2 The Four States of a Hyperlink (CSS Pseudo-Classes) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Focus Area:** A hyperlink has **4 visual states** that must be styled using CSS pseudo-classes. | State | Pseudo-Class | Trigger Condition | Default Browser Style | |-------|--------------|-------------------|----------------------| | Unvisited | `:link` | Link has never been clicked | **Blue** + underline | | Visited | `:visited` | Link has been clicked before | **Purple** + underline | | Hover | `:hover` | Mouse pointer is over the link | (no default) | | Active | `:active` | Link is being clicked | **Red** + underline | ### LVHA Ordering Rule (LoVe-HAte) The pseudo-classes **must** be declared in this specific order to work correctly: $$\texttt{:link} \rightarrow \texttt{:visited} \rightarrow \texttt{:hover} \rightarrow \texttt{:active}$$ Any deviation causes **specificity conflicts** and incorrect visual feedback. ## 2.3 The `target` Attribute ŌĆö Window/Frame Behavior The `target` attribute specifies *where* the linked document will open: | Value | Behavior | KTU Exam Note | |-------|----------|----------------| | `_self` | Opens in the **same** window/tab (default) | Most common | | `_blank` | Opens in a **new** window or tab | Use for external links | | `_parent` | Opens in the **parent** frame | Frameworks | | `_top` | Opens in the **full body** of the window | Breaks out of frames | | `framename` | Opens in a **named** iframe | Targeted content | ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Concept | Syntax / Formula | Example | Notes | |---------|------------------|---------|-------| | Absolute URL | `protocol://domain/path` | `https://www.ktu.edu.in/syllabus` | Full address; works from anywhere | | Relative URL | `./page.html` or `../folder/page.html` | `index.html`, `../about/team.html` | Resolved relative to current page | | Root-Relative URL | `/path/to/page` | `/courses/web-design` | Resolved from server root | | In-Page Anchor | `#section-id` | `#module1` | Scrolls to element with matching `id` | | Email Link | `mailto:user@domain` | `mailto:admissions@ktu.edu.in` | Opens default mail client | | Phone Link | `tel:+country-number` | `tel:+914842500000` | Mobile-friendly | | Download Link | `` | `` | Prompts download | | New Tab Target | `target="_blank"` | `` | External links | | Security (New Tab) | `rel="noopener noreferrer"` | `` | Prevents tabnabbing | ## 2.5 URL Resolution Logic ŌĆö The "Why" and "How" When a user clicks a link with a **relative URL**, the browser applies a deterministic algorithm: > [!NOTE] > **Resolution Algorithm:** > 1. Identify the **base URL** of the current document. > 2. If the relative URL begins with `/`, it is resolved against the **root** of the base URL's origin. > 3. If the relative URL begins with `./` or just a filename, it is resolved against the **directory** of the current document. > 4. If the relative URL begins with `../`, the browser **moves up one directory level** and then resolves. > 5. The `#fragment` is appended *after* the resource is fetched. ### Example Walkthrough Suppose the current page is at: `https://ktu.edu.in/dept/cse/syllabus.html` | Link in HTML | Resolved Absolute URL | |--------------|----------------------| | `index.html` | `https://ktu.edu.in/dept/cse/index.html` | | `./notes.pdf` | `https://ktu.edu.in/dept/cse/notes.pdf` | | `../me/syllabus.html` | `https://ktu.edu.in/dept/me/syllabus.html` | | `/index.html` | `https://ktu.edu.in/index.html` | | `https://google.com` | `https://google.com` (already absolute) | ## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why This Topic Matters in Practice:** > - **SEO (Search Engine Optimization):** Search engines like Google use hyperlinks (both internal and external) as a primary ranking signal. > - **Web Accessibility (WCAG):** Screen readers depend on meaningful anchor text; proper link design is a legal accessibility requirement. > - **Cybersecurity:** Misconfigured `target="_blank"` links without `rel="noopener"` expose users to **reverse tabnabbing** attacks. > - **Information Architecture:** Web Designers use hyperlinks to build the **navigation backbone** of every web application. > - **Web Crawlers & Bots:** Search engine crawlers follow hyperlinks to index the web ŌĆö the entire WWW depends on hyperlinks. --- # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Resolving a Relative URL Step-by-Step Let us derive the absolute URL when the document base is `https://ktu.edu.in/courses/web/index.html` and the link is `../notes/syllabus.pdf`. ### Step 1: Identify the Base Directory We strip the filename from the base URL to obtain the **base directory**: $$\text{Base URL} = \texttt{https://ktu.edu.in/courses/web/index.html}$$ Removing the filename `index.html`: $$\text{Base Directory} = \texttt{https://ktu.edu.in/courses/web/}$$ ### Step 2: Tokenize the Relative Path The relative path `../notes/syllabus.pdf` is tokenized into: - `..` ŌåÆ move up one directory - `notes` ŌåÆ enter the "notes" subdirectory - `syllabus.pdf` ŌåÆ target file ### Step 3: Apply the `..` Operation (Move Up) Starting from `https://ktu.edu.in/courses/web/`, moving up one directory: $$\texttt{https://ktu.edu.in/courses/web/} \xrightarrow{\texttt{..}} \texttt{https://ktu.edu.in/courses/}$$ ### Step 4: Append the Subdirectory and Filename $$\texttt{https://ktu.edu.in/courses/} + \texttt{notes/syllabus.pdf}$$ $$= \texttt{https://ktu.edu.in/courses/notes/syllabus.pdf}$$ ### Final Resolved Absolute URL $$\boxed{\texttt{https://ktu.edu.in/courses/notes/syllabus.pdf}}$$ ## 3.2 Derivation: Multi-Level Relative Path Resolution Given base URL: `https://example.com/a/b/c/page.html` and link: `../../d/e.html` ### Step 1: Strip Filename ŌåÆ Base Directory $$\text{Base Directory} = \texttt{https://example.com/a/b/c/}$$ ### Step 2: First `..` (Up One Level) $$\texttt{https://example.com/a/b/c/} \xrightarrow{\texttt{..}} \texttt{https://example.com/a/b/}$$ ### Step 3: Second `..` (Up Another Level) $$\texttt{https://example.com/a/b/} \xrightarrow{\texttt{..}} \texttt{https://example.com/a/}$$ ### Step 4: Append `d/e.html` $$\texttt{https://example.com/a/} + \texttt{d/e.html} = \texttt{https://example.com/a/d/e.html}$$ ### Final Result $$\boxed{\texttt{https://example.com/a/d/e.html}}$$ ## 3.3 Derivation: Fragment Resolution (In-Page Anchors) Given a current page `https://ktu.edu.in/syllabus.html` and a link `#module-3`. ### Step 1: Identify Fragment Identifier The `#` symbol indicates a **fragment identifier**, not a new resource. ### Step 2: Browser Searches the DOM The browser looks for an element with `id="module-3"` in the **already-loaded document**. ### Step 3: Scroll Behavior The browser scrolls the page so that the matched element is at the top of the viewport. ### Mathematical Representation $$\text{Final URL} = \text{Base URL} + \texttt{\#} + \text{fragment-id}$$ $$\text{Final URL} = \texttt{https://ktu.edu.in/syllabus.html} + \texttt{\#} + \texttt{module-3}$$ $$\boxed{\texttt{https://ktu.edu.in/syllabus.html\#module-3}}$$ > **Conversion Logic:** No new HTTP request is made; the browser simply scrolls within the existing DOM. ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Link Type Decision Tree Use the following decision matrix to select the correct link type for any scenario: | Scenario | Recommended `href` Value | `target` | Additional Attributes | |----------|-------------------------|----------|----------------------| | Link to another page in same folder | `page2.html` | `_self` | (none) | | Link to page in parent folder | `../page2.html` | `_self` | (none) | | Link to external website | `https://example.com` | `_blank` | `rel="noopener noreferrer"` | | Link to section in same page | `#section-id` | `_self` | (none) | | Link to section in another page | `page.html#section-id` | `_self` | (none) | | Open default email client | `mailto:user@domain.com` | `_self` | `subject=` via query string | | Initiate phone call (mobile) | `tel:+914841234567` | `_self` | (none) | | Download a file | `file.pdf` | `_self` | `download` | | Link an image (clickable) | `` wrap | varies | Alt text required | ## 3.5 Length Calculation: Why URL Length Matters Some forms and servers have URL length limits. Given a query string with parameters, the total URL length is: $$L_{\text{total}} = L_{\text{protocol}} + L_{\text{domain}} + L_{\text{path}} + L_{\text{query}} + L_{\text{fragment}} + N_{\text{reserved}}$$ Where: - $L_{\text{protocol}}$ = length of `https://` = **8** - $L_{\text{domain}}$ = length of domain name - $L_{\text{path}}$ = length of resource path - $L_{\text{query}}$ = length of `?key=value&...` - $L_{\text{fragment}}$ = length of `#section` - $N_{\text{reserved}}$ = number of reserved characters (each `&`, `=`, `?`, `#` adds **1 char**) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Practice:** If $L_{\text{total}} > 2048$ characters, prefer **POST requests** over query strings, as older browsers/proxies may truncate long URLs. --- # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart: The Hyperlink Click Lifecycle ```mermaid flowchart TD A[User Views Web Page] --> B[Browser Renders anchor a element] B --> C{User Hovers over Link} C --> D[CSS hover state activates] D --> E{User Clicks Link} E --> F[Browser Parses href attribute] F --> G{Is URL Absolute or Relative} G --> H[Relative] G --> I[Absolute] H --> J[Resolve Relative Path against Base URL] I --> K[Use URL as is] J --> L{Is target attribute set} K --> L L --> M[target blank] L --> N[target self default] M --> O[Open New Tab or Window] N --> P[Replace Current Page] O --> Q[Send HTTP GET Request to Server] P --> Q Q --> R[Server Responds with Resource] R --> S[Browser Renders New Page] S --> T[Page Ready for User Interaction] ``` ## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: URL Structure Anatomy ```mermaid flowchart LR A[https://] --> B[www.ktu.edu.in] B --> C[:443] C --> D[/courses/] D --> E[web-design/] E --> F[index.html] F --> G[?semester=5] G --> H[#module1] ``` > **Caption:** The anatomy of a complete URL showing protocol, subdomain, domain, TLD, port, path, query, and fragment components. ## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram: Relative URL Resolution Engine ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Input: Base URL] --> B[Strip Filename to Get Base Directory] B --> C[Input: Relative URL] C --> D{Starts with slash /} D -->|Yes| E[Resolve from Server Root] D -->|No| F{Starts with dot dot slash ../} F -->|Yes| G[Count Number of .. segments] F -->|No| H[Append to Base Directory] G --> I[Move Up N Directory Levels] I --> J[Append Remaining Path] H --> K[Append Filename to Base Directory] E --> L[Output: Absolute URL] J --> L K --> L ``` ## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram: Link States (LVHA Order) ```mermaid stateDiagram-v2 [*] --> Unvisited Unvisited --> Visited: User clicks link Visited --> Hover: Mouse pointer enters link area Hover --> Active: Mouse button pressed Active --> Visited: Mouse button released Hover --> Unvisited: Mouse pointer leaves Visited --> Unvisited: Browser cache cleared Unvisited --> [*] Visited --> [*] ``` ## 4.5 Mermaid Tree: Types of Hyperlinks ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Hyperlinks] --> B[External Links] A --> C[Internal Links] A --> D[Special Purpose Links] B --> B1[Absolute URL to another website] B --> B2[Opens in new tab with target blank] C --> C1[Relative path to same site] C --> C2[Root relative path] C --> C3[In-page anchor fragment] C --> C4[Cross-page anchor] D --> D1[mailto: email link] D --> D2[tel: phone link] D --> D3[download attribute file link] D --> D4[sms: text message link] ``` --- # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## PART A: 3-Mark Questions ### **Q1. [KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2024]** **Define a hyperlink. List the four states of a hyperlink as represented in CSS.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** A **hyperlink** is a clickable element in a web page (usually text or an image) that, when activated, navigates the user to another web resource, document, or location. It is created using the HTML anchor element `` with the `href` attribute specifying the destination. The four states of a hyperlink in CSS are: 1. **`:link`** ŌĆō the default, unvisited state. 2. **`:visited`** ŌĆō the state after the user has clicked the link. 3. **`:hover`** ŌĆō the state when the mouse pointer is over the link. 4. **`:active`** ŌĆō the state at the moment the link is being clicked. *(1 mark for definition + 1 mark for naming 4 states + 1 mark for brief description of each state ŌĆö total 3 marks)* --- ### **Q2. [KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023]** **Differentiate between an absolute URL and a relative URL. Give one example for each.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** | Feature | Absolute URL | Relative URL | |---------|--------------|--------------| | Definition | Contains the **complete address** including protocol and domain | Contains the **partial path** resolved from the current page | | Portability | Works from **any location** on the web | Works **only within the same site** | | Length | Longer | Shorter | | Example | `https://www.ktu.edu.in/syllabus.html` | `../syllabus.html` or `index.html` | **Examples:** - **Absolute URL:** `Google` - **Relative URL:** `Contact Us` (assumes contact.html is in the same folder as the current page) *(1 mark for each differentiation point + 1 mark for examples ŌĆö total 3 marks)* --- ## PART B: 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice) ### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam ŌĆō July 2024]** **(a)** Explain the HTML `` (anchor) element in detail. Discuss its essential and optional attributes with suitable examples. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Describe the different **types of hyperlinks** used in web design. Write the syntax and a real-world use case for each. **(7 Marks)** --- #### **Solution to Question A:** **Part (a) ŌĆö The HTML Anchor Element (7 Marks)** The HTML anchor element `` is the **fundamental element** for creating hyperlinks. It is an inline element that wraps the clickable content (text, image, or block-level element). **Essential Attributes:** **1. `href` (Hypertext Reference):** The **mandatory** attribute that specifies the destination URL. Without it, the anchor is not a clickable link. ```html Visit KTU Official Website ``` **2. `target`:** Specifies *where* the linked document opens. | Value | Purpose | |-------|---------| | `_self` | Opens in the same window (default) | | `_blank` | Opens in a new tab/window | | `_parent` | Opens in the parent frame | | `_top` | Opens in the full window body | | `framename` | Opens in a named iframe | ```html Open Google in New Tab ``` **3. `rel`:** Defines the relationship between the current document and the linked document. - `rel="noopener"` ŌĆö prevents the new page from accessing `window.opener`. - `rel="noreferrer"` ŌĆö prevents passing the Referer header. - `rel="external"` ŌĆö semantic hint that the link is external. - `rel="nofollow"` ŌĆö SEO hint not to follow the link for ranking. **4. `download`:** Prompts the browser to download the linked resource instead of navigating to it. ```html Download Syllabus PDF ``` **5. `title`:** Provides advisory text shown on hover (also improves accessibility). ```html Contact ``` **6. `hreflang`:** Specifies the language of the linked document. ```html Version Fran├¦aise ``` --- **Part (b) ŌĆö Types of Hyperlinks (7 Marks)** **1. External Hyperlinks** ŌĆö Link to a page on a *different* website using an absolute URL. *Use case:* Linking from a KTU webpage to the AICTE portal. ```html AICTE Official Portal ``` **2. Internal Hyperlinks (Relative)** ŌĆö Link to a page within the *same* website. *Use case:* Navigating between pages of a college website. ```html About Us CSE Department ``` **3. In-Page Anchor Links (Fragment Identifiers)** ŌĆö Link to a *specific section* within the same page. *Use case:* Table of contents on a long syllabus page. ```html Go to Module 3 ...

Module 3: CSS Styling

``` **4. Cross-Page Anchor Links** ŌĆö Link to a *specific section* in another page. *Use case:* Linking to a particular module on the syllabus page from the home page. ```html View Module 1 Syllabus ``` **5. Email Links (`mailto:`)** ŌĆö Opens the user's default email client. *Use case:* "Contact Admissions" link on a college website. ```html Email Admissions ``` **6. Telephone Links (`tel:`)** ŌĆö Initiates a phone call on mobile devices. *Use case:* "Call Us" button on a contact page. ```html Call KTU Helpline ``` **7. Download Links** ŌĆö Triggers a file download using the `download` attribute. *Use case:* Providing PDF brochures, application forms, or course notes. ```html Download Application Form ``` --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023 ŌĆō Alternative Choice]** **(a)** Explain the **structure of a URL** with a neat diagram. Differentiate between **absolute, relative, and root-relative** URLs with examples. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Discuss the **four CSS link states** (`:link`, `:visited`, `:hover`, `:active`). Why must they be styled in the **LVHA order**? Write the CSS code to style a navigation menu. **(7 Marks)** --- #### **Solution to Question B:** **Part (a) ŌĆö URL Structure (7 Marks)** A **URL (Uniform Resource Locator)** is the address used to access resources on the web. Its general structure is: $$\texttt{scheme://host[:port]/path[?query][\#fragment]}$$ **Components Explained:** | Component | Purpose | Example | |-----------|---------|---------| | **Scheme (Protocol)** | Defines how the resource is transferred | `http`, `https`, `ftp`, `mailto` | | **Host** | Domain name or IP of the server | `www.ktu.edu.in` | | **Port** | Optional; defaults to 80 (http) or 443 (https) | `:8080` | | **Path** | Location of the resource on the server | `/courses/web/index.html` | | **Query** | Parameters sent to the server | `?id=101&sem=5` | | **Fragment** | In-page section identifier | `#module1` | **Example Fully Decomposed URL:** `https://www.ktu.edu.in:443/courses/web/index.html?sem=5#module1` | Part | Value | |------|-------| | Scheme | `https` | | Host | `www.ktu.edu.in` | | Port | `443` | | Path | `/courses/web/index.html` | | Query | `sem=5` | | Fragment | `module1` | **Three Types of URLs ŌĆö Comparison:** | Type | Definition | Example | When to Use | |------|------------|---------|-------------| | **Absolute** | Complete address from scratch | `https://ktu.edu.in/about.html` | External sites | | **Relative** | Path relative to **current page** | `about.html` or `../dept/cse.html` | Same-site navigation | | **Root-Relative** | Path relative to **server root** | `/about.html` | Large multi-folder sites | **Resolution Examples:** If current page = `https://ktu.edu.in/dept/cse/index.html`: - `about.html` ŌåÆ `https://ktu.edu.in/dept/cse/about.html` - `../me/index.html` ŌåÆ `https://ktu.edu.in/dept/me/index.html` - `/index.html` ŌåÆ `https://ktu.edu.in/index.html` --- **Part (b) ŌĆö Four CSS Link States & LVHA Order (7 Marks)** A hyperlink has **four pseudo-classes** that represent its interaction states. They **must** be declared in the **LVHA (LoVe-HAte)** order: `:link`, `:visited`, `:hover`, `:active`. **Why LVHA Order Matters:** The CSS specificity of all four pseudo-classes is **identical (0,0,1,0)**. When two rules have equal specificity, the **last-declared rule wins**. Therefore, the order in the stylesheet directly determines which rule applies. If declared in wrong order (e.g., `:hover` before `:link`), the `:link` rule would override the hover effect, and the user would never see hover feedback. **CSS Code Example ŌĆö Styling a Navigation Menu:** ```css /* Step 1: Unvisited link (default state) */ a.nav-link:link { color: #FFFFFF; background-color: #003366; text-decoration: none; padding: 10px 15px; font-weight: bold; } /* Step 2: Visited link (after click) */ a.nav-link:visited { color: #E0E0E0; background-color: #003366; } /* Step 3: Hover state (mouse over) */ a.nav-link:hover { color: #FFFF00; background-color: #0055AA; text-decoration: underline; } /* Step 4: Active state (being clicked) */ a.nav-link:active { color: #FF0000; background-color: #000000; } ``` **Explanation of Each Rule:** 1. **`:link`** ŌĆö Sets the base appearance (white text on dark blue background, no underline). 2. **`:visited`** ŌĆö Slightly dims the text to indicate the user has already visited the page. 3. **`:hover`** ŌĆö Brightens the link to yellow and underlines it as visual feedback. 4. **`:active`** ŌĆö Flashes red momentarily to confirm the click was registered. **Bonus ŌĆö Removing the Default Underline from All Link States:** ```css a { text-decoration: none; } ``` **Accessibility Best Practice:** Always provide a `:focus` state for keyboard users: ```css a.nav-link:focus { outline: 2px solid #FFAA00; } ``` --- > [!WARNING] > **ŌÜĀ’ĖÅ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning ŌĆö Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **LVHA Order Mistake:** Declaring `:hover` *before* `:link` will cause the default link color to override the hover effect. Examiners deduct **2 marks** for wrong ordering. > 2. **Forgetting `rel="noopener"`** with `target="_blank"` is a security flaw. Examiners expect mention of this in cybersecurity-oriented questions. > 3. **Confusing `mailto:` with `email`:** `mailto:` is the correct protocol; writing `email:` in `` will not work. **ŌłÆ1 mark** for this error. > 4. **Missing `id` attribute** when using fragment identifiers (`#section`) results in non-functional in-page links. Always mention the target element must have a matching `id`. > 5. **Case-sensitivity error:** URLs are case-sensitive in the *path* portion. `/Index.html` and `/index.html` are different files on Linux servers. > 6. **Forgetting to close the `` tag** in HTML5 (no self-closing allowed for anchors) is a common syntax error that costs marks. > 7. **Omitting the `href` attribute** makes the `` element a "dead link" ŌĆö it is technically valid HTML5 (acts as a placeholder) but not a hyperlink. --- ## ­¤ōī Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Summary ŌĆö Memorize Before Exam:** > - **`` is the syntax** for creating a hyperlink; `href` is mandatory. > - **Four link states:** `:link`, `:visited`, `:hover`, `:active` ŌĆö must follow **LVHA (LoVe-HAte)** order. > - **Three URL types:** Absolute (full address), Relative (path from current page), Root-Relative (`/path` from server root). > - **`.` = current directory**, **`..` = parent directory** in relative URLs. > - **`target="_blank"`** opens in a new tab; **always pair with `rel="noopener noreferrer"`** for security. > - **`mailto:user@domain`** opens default email client; can include `?subject=...&body=...`. > - **`tel:+countrycode-number`** initiates a phone call on mobile devices. > - **`download` attribute** forces a file download instead of navigation. > - **Fragment identifier (`#id`)** scrolls to an element with matching `id` ŌĆö no new HTTP request is made. > - **Port 80 = HTTP**, **Port 443 = HTTPS** ŌĆö these are default and usually omitted. > - **URL encoding:** spaces become `%20`, `#` becomes `%23` when used in path/query values. > - **External links** should use **absolute URLs** + `target="_blank"`. > - **Internal links** should use **relative URLs** for portability and shorter code. > - **Anchor text** must be **descriptive** (avoid generic "Click Here") for SEO and accessibility. > - **`hreflang`** attribute specifies the language of the linked resource. > - **In-page anchors** require the target element to have a **unique `id`** matching the `#fragment`. > - **Reserved URL characters** (`#`, `?`, `&`, `=`, space) must be **percent-encoded** in values. > - **Maximum practical URL length** is ~2,083 chars (legacy IE limit) ŌĆö use POST for longer payloads. > - **`` is an inline element** but can wrap block-level elements in HTML5. ---

topics.title: Linking Within Your Site
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEITT833 - WEB DESIGNING > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Linking Within Your Site # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Linking Within Your Site** (also known as **Internal Linking** or **Intrasite Hyperlinking**) is the process of creating hyperlinks in HTML that connect one resource (typically a webpage, document, or media file) to another resource located **within the same website domain or root directory**. In the context of the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for *OEITT833 ŌĆō Web Designing*, this concept falls under the foundational HTML anchor (``) element, where the `href` (Hypertext Reference) attribute is used to define a destination URI (Uniform Resource Identifier) that is **resolvable relative to the current document's location**. Technically, an internal link is constructed using the **HTML anchor element** with a path value that does not require a full protocol and domain specification (e.g., `http://www.example.com/...`). Instead, it uses **relative paths, root-relative paths, or document-relative paths** to navigate within the same website's folder hierarchy. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The anchor tag `` with its `href`, `title`, and `target` attributes is a **mandatory Module 1 topic**. Examiners frequently test the difference between *absolute*, *document-relative*, and *root-relative* paths. --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine a **large shopping mall** (your website) with multiple floors, each containing several shops (your web pages). To help visitors move from one shop to another: - **A signboard that says "Go to 2nd Floor, Shop 14"** is a **relative link** ŌĆö it tells you where to go *from your current position*. - **A signboard that says "Go to MG Road Mall, 2nd Floor, Shop 14"** is an **absolute link** ŌĆö it gives the full address, even if you are already inside the mall. - **A signboard that says "From the Main Entrance (root), take the elevator to 2nd Floor, Shop 14"** is a **root-relative link** ŌĆö it always starts from the top of the mall regardless of where you are currently standing. > [!NOTE] > **Plain English Summary:** Internal linking is the practice of connecting pages of the *same* website using short, efficient paths ŌĆö rather than typing out the full website address every time. It's how navigation menus, "Back to Top" buttons, table-of-contents links, and page footers work. --- ## 1.3 Key Terminology (Bolded for Retention) | Term | Meaning | |---|---| | **Anchor Tag (``)** | The HTML element that defines a hyperlink. | | **HREF Attribute** | The attribute that specifies the **destination URL** of the link. | | **Relative Path** | A path expressed **relative to the current document's location**. | | **Absolute Path** | A full URL including protocol, domain, and folder path. | | **Root-Relative Path** | A path that **always begins from the website's root directory** (denoted by `/`). | | **Target Attribute** | Specifies **where** the linked document opens (`_self`, `_blank`, `_parent`, `_top`). | | **Fragment Identifier** | A `#id` suffix that scrolls to a specific section of *the same page*. | > [!TIP] > Always use **relative links** for intrasite navigation. They survive domain changes, work on `localhost` during development, and make site migration effortless. Examiners **love** this point. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet ## 2.1 Anatomy of the HTML Anchor Tag The basic syntax of an internal hyperlink is: ```html Visible Link Text ``` **Component Breakdown:** 1. **``** ŌĆö The *anchor* element (opening tag). 2. **`href="..."`** ŌĆö The **Hypertext Reference**; mandatory attribute that holds the destination URL/path. 3. **`title="..."`** ŌĆö Optional. Provides a tooltip on mouse hover (also an **accessibility cue** for screen readers). 4. **`target="..."`** ŌĆö Optional. Controls *where* the link opens: - `_self` (default): Opens in the **same browser tab/window**. - `_blank`: Opens in a **new tab/window**. - `_parent`: Opens in the **parent frame**. - `_top`: Opens in the **full body of the window**, breaking out of all frames. 5. **Visible Link Text** ŌĆö The clickable content placed between the opening and closing `` tags. It can be plain text, an image, or another HTML element. > [!NOTE] > **Best Practice (KTU 2024 Aligned):** Never use generic text like *"Click here"*. Use **descriptive link text** such as *"Download the syllabus PDF"* or *"Read the HTML tutorial"*. This is a **Web Content Accessibility Guidelines (WCAG)** requirement. --- ## 2.2 The Three Types of Internal Paths This is the **highest-weightage concept** in any Module 1 exam. You must master all three. ### **Type 1: Document-Relative Path (Most Common)** The destination is described **in relation to the location of the *current* HTML file**. - Same folder: `href="contact.html"` - Subfolder: `href="pages/about.html"` - Parent folder: `href="../index.html"` - Two levels up: `href="../../index.html"` ### **Type 2: Root-Relative Path (Server-Absolute)** The destination is described **from the website's root directory** (always begins with `/`). - From root: `href="/index.html"` - From root, into a subfolder: `href="/products/shoes.html"` > [!WARNING] > Root-relative paths **do not work** when you simply double-click the HTML file on your local machine. They require a **web server** (e.g., XAMPP, WAMP, or the VS Code Live Server extension). This is a classic KTU viva question! ### **Type 3: Absolute Path (Full URL)** The full web address is given. For *internal* linking, this is **discouraged** because: - It breaks when the domain changes. - It is unnecessarily verbose. - It does not load faster even though some students think so. **Example:** `href="https://www.ktu.edu.in/syllabus.html"` --- ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet | Link Type | Syntax Pattern | Resolves From | Best Used When | Survives Domain Change? | |---|---|---|---|---| | **Document-Relative** | `about.html`, `../about.html` | Current page's folder | Static sites, local development | Ō£ģ Yes | | **Root-Relative** | `/about.html`, `/css/style.css` | Website root (`/`) | CMS, large multi-folder sites | Ō£ģ Yes | | **Absolute (Internal)** | `https://site.com/about.html` | Domain root | External sharing, canonical links | ŌØī No (breaks) | | **Fragment (Same Page)** | `#section2` | Current page | Table of contents, "Back to Top" | Ō£ģ Yes | | **Email Link** | `mailto:user@ktu.in` | OS default mail client | Contact pages | Ō£ģ Yes | --- ## 2.4 Special Internal Link: Fragment Identifiers (In-Page Anchors) A fragment identifier lets you link to a **specific section of the same page** (or another page). You need two parts: 1. **The Target:** Give an `id` to the destination element. ```html

Chapter 3: CSS

``` 2. **The Link:** Use `#` followed by the `id`. ```html Jump to Chapter 3 ``` To link to a fragment **on a different page** in your site: ```html Go to Module 2 of Syllabus ``` > [!TIP] > **Engineering Utility:** In modern single-page applications (SPAs) and long-form documentation (like KTU's own e-learning portal), fragment links are the **primary navigation mechanism**. They reduce HTTP requests and improve perceived performance. --- ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Applications - **Navigation Bars:** Every website's main menu uses a list of internal links. - **Breadcrumbs:** "Home ŌĆ║ Products ŌĆ║ Shoes" are internal links showing the user's location. - **Sitemaps:** The `sitemap.xml` file and HTML sitemap pages rely on internal links for **SEO (Search Engine Optimization)**. - **Pagination:** "Next Page ŌĆ║" and "ŌĆ╣ Previous" are internal links. - **In-page TOC:** Long articles use fragment links for quick jumps. - **Web Crawlers (Googlebot, Bingbot):** They discover new pages *by following* internal links. A site with no internal links is **invisible** to search engines. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Examples, Code Walkthroughs & Path Resolution ## 3.1 The Fundamental Example: A Two-Page Website Let's build the **simplest possible multi-page website** to understand internal linking. ### **Step 1: Folder Structure** ``` myWebsite/ Ōöé Ōö£ŌöĆŌöĆ index.html ŌåÉ Home page ŌööŌöĆŌöĆ contact.html ŌåÉ Contact page ``` ### **Step 2: Inside `index.html` ŌĆö Linking to Contact** ```html Home - My Website

Welcome to My Website

To reach out, please visit our Contact Page.

``` **Logic of `href="contact.html"`:** - The browser looks for a file named `contact.html` **in the same folder** as the current page (`index.html`). - Since both files are siblings, no `../` is needed. ### **Step 3: Inside `contact.html` ŌĆö Linking Back to Home** ```html Contact - My Website

Contact Us

Return to the Home Page.

``` > [!NOTE] > This is the **minimum viable multi-page website**. Every larger website is built on this same principle. --- ## 3.2 Example 2: Nested Folder Structure (The Most Tested Scenario) Consider a more realistic structure: ``` companySite/ Ōöé Ōö£ŌöĆŌöĆ index.html Ōö£ŌöĆŌöĆ about/ Ōöé Ōö£ŌöĆŌöĆ team.html Ōöé ŌööŌöĆŌöĆ history.html Ōö£ŌöĆŌöĆ products/ Ōöé Ōö£ŌöĆŌöĆ index.html Ōöé ŌööŌöĆŌöĆ software.html ŌööŌöĆŌöĆ assets/ ŌööŌöĆŌöĆ brochure.pdf ``` **Rules of Path Resolution (from any source file):** | Destination | From `index.html` | From `about/team.html` | From `products/index.html` | |---|---|---|---| | `about/team.html` | `about/team.html` | `team.html` | `../about/team.html` | | `index.html` (root) | `index.html` | `../index.html` | `../index.html` | | `assets/brochure.pdf` | `assets/brochure.pdf` | `../assets/brochure.pdf` | `../assets/brochure.pdf` | | `products/index.html` | `products/index.html` | `../products/index.html` | `index.html` | > [!IMPORTANT] > **`../` means "go up one folder level"**. Every `../` cancels one folder. To go up two levels, use `../../`. --- ## 3.3 Worked-Out Example: Linking with `target` Attribute ```html Target Attribute Demo

Internal Linking Demo

About Us (same tab)

Our History (new tab)

Email us at: support@ktu.in

``` **Logic of the `mailto:` link:** - `mailto:` triggers the **default email client** (Outlook, Gmail, etc.). - `?subject=...` pre-fills the subject line. - `&body=...` pre-fills the email body. - This is technically *not* an internal link (it opens an external app), but is grouped with `` element behavior in the KTU syllabus. --- ## 3.4 Example: In-Page Navigation with Fragment Identifiers ```html Long Article - In-Page Links

Complete Web Design Guide

Table of Contents

  • 1. HTML Basics
  • 2. CSS Styling
  • 3. JavaScript Intro

1. HTML Basics

HTML is the backbone...

2. CSS Styling

CSS adds visual appeal...

3. JavaScript Intro

JavaScript adds interactivity...

Ōåæ Back to Top

``` **Logic Explained:** - Each `
` has a unique `id`. - The TOC links use `href="#idname"` to jump to that section. - The "Back to Top" link uses `href="#top"` ŌĆö note that we did NOT set `id="top"` anywhere! Some browsers infer the top of the page when no matching ID is found, but the **WCAG-recommended practice** is to add `` or `

` at the very beginning. --- ## 3.5 The Three "Don'ts" of Internal Linking (Common Mistakes) 1. **Don't** use absolute URLs for internal links. - ŌØī `` - Ō£ģ `` 2. **Don't** use spaces or special characters in file names. - ŌØī `my page.html` - Ō£ģ `my-page.html` or `myPage.html` 3. **Don't** forget the closing `` tag ŌĆö it will make all subsequent content part of the link. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Site Link Architecture (Mermaid Block Diagram) The following diagram maps the **internal link flow** of a typical KTU-style educational website. It shows the parent-child relationship of pages and the hyperlinks that connect them. ```mermaid flowchart TD homepage[HOME PAGE index html]:::main about[ABOUT US about html]:::main team[OUR TEAM about team html]:::nested history[HISTORY about history html]:::nested courses[COURSES courses index html]:::main btech[B TECH btech html]:::nested mtech[M TECH mtech html]:::nested contact[CONTACT contact html]:::main topbtn[BACK TO TOP fragment top]:::fragment homepage --> about homepage --> courses homepage --> contact about --> team about --> history about --> homepage courses --> btech courses --> mtech team --> homepage history --> homepage btech --> homepage mtech --> homepage contact --> homepage homepage -.->|in page jump| topbtn about -.->|in page jump| topbtn contact -.->|in page jump| topbtn classDef main fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef nested fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef fragment fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 ``` **Reading the Diagram:** - **Solid arrows** (`-->`) represent **document-relative links** between different HTML files. - **Dotted arrows** (`-.->`) represent **fragment links** (in-page jumps). - **Yellow nodes** are top-level pages; **blue nodes** are nested pages; **green nodes** are virtual fragment targets. --- ## 4.2 Path Resolution Flowchart This diagram explains the **decision logic** a browser (or a student writing a test) should follow when constructing an internal link. ```mermaid flowchart TD start[START: I need to link to another page]:::start same[Is the target in the SAME folder?]:::question sub[Is the target in a SUB folder?]:::question parent[Is the target in a PARENT folder?]:::question diff[Use DOCUMENT RELATIVE path]:::action dotdot[Use ../ to go up one level]:::action forward[Use foldername slash to go down]:::action root[Use ROOT RELATIVE path starting with /]:::action endnode[FINAL: Write href attribute]:::endnode start --> same same -->|YES| diff same -->|NO| sub sub -->|YES| forward sub -->|NO| parent parent -->|YES| dotdot parent -->|NO| root diff --> endnode forward --> endnode dotdot --> endnode root --> endnode classDef start fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px classDef question fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px classDef action fill:#ADD8E6,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px classDef endnode fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px ``` --- ## 4.3 Component Anatomy of an Anchor Tag ```mermaid flowchart LR A[LESS THAN a]:::tag B[HREF equals quote]:::attr C[DESTINATION URL or PATH]:::value D[QUOTE]:::attr E[GREATER THAN]:::tag F[VISIBLE LINK TEXT]:::content G[LESS THAN SLASH a GREATER THAN]:::tag A --> B --> C --> D --> E --> F --> G classDef tag fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px classDef attr fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px classDef value fill:#F0E68C,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px classDef content fill:#98FB98,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px ``` > [!NOTE] > This visual decomposition helps in viva voce. A student should be able to point to each part and explain its role. # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Question 1** [KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2024] **Explain the difference between absolute and relative URLs with suitable examples.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** An **absolute URL** is a *complete* web address that includes the protocol (e.g., `http://` or `https://`), the domain name, and the full path to the resource. **Example:** `https://www.ktu.edu.in/syllabus/oeitt833.html` A **relative URL** is a *partial* path that is resolved **relative to the location of the current document**. It omits the protocol and domain. **Examples:** - `sylabbus.html` (same folder) - `../index.html` (parent folder) - `pages/about.html` (subfolder) **Key Difference:** Absolute URLs are self-contained and work from any context, while relative URLs are shorter, portable across domains, and preferred for *internal* linking. **(3 marks ŌĆō 1 for definition of each + 1 for distinguishing feature)** --- ### **Question 2** [KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023] **What is the purpose of the `target` attribute in an anchor tag? List any two of its values.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** The **`target` attribute** specifies **where the linked document will open** when the user clicks the link. It is an optional attribute of the `` element. **Two values:** 1. **`_self`** ŌĆö Opens the linked document in the **same browser window/tab** where the link was clicked. This is the **default** value. 2. **`_blank`** ŌĆö Opens the linked document in a **new browser window or tab**, leaving the original page intact. (Other acceptable values: `_parent`, `_top`) **(3 marks ŌĆō 1 for purpose + 1 for each of two values)** --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each ŌĆö Internal Choice) > KTU ESE Module 1 pattern: You are given a choice between **Question A** and **Question B**, each worth 14 marks with sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks. --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam ŌĆō Model Paper Pattern] **(a)** Explain the different types of internal links that can be created within a website using the HTML `` element. Discuss **document-relative**, **root-relative**, and **absolute** paths with syntax examples for each. *(7 marks)* **(b)** Design the HTML code for a simple educational website with the following structure: - A home page (`index.html`) with navigation links. - A page for "About Us" located in a subfolder named `about/`. - A "Contact" page with an email link and a "Back to Top" fragment link. - A "Courses" page that has an in-page Table of Contents jumping to three sections. Show all HTML code with proper internal linking. *(7 marks)* --- #### **Model Solution to Question A (a):** **Internal links** are hyperlinks that connect resources **within the same website**. They are created using the HTML `` (anchor) element with the `href` attribute. There are three main types: **1. Document-Relative Path** *(2 marks)* These paths are resolved **from the current page's folder location**. They are the most common in static websites. - Same folder: `Contact` - Subfolder: `Team` - Parent folder: `Home` **2. Root-Relative Path** *(2 marks)* These paths **always begin with a forward slash `/`**, which represents the website's root directory. The browser resolves them from the domain root, regardless of the current page's location. - From root: `Home` - Subfolder from root: `Team` These require a **web server** to function correctly. **3. Absolute Path** *(2 marks)* The **full URL** is specified, including the protocol and domain. Although technically valid for internal linking, it is **discouraged** because: - It is verbose. - It breaks when the domain name changes. - It is harder to maintain. Example: `About KTU` **Conclusion** *(1 mark)*: For internal linking, **document-relative paths are the most portable and maintainable** choice for static websites, while **root-relative paths** are preferred for content management systems. --- #### **Model Solution to Question A (b):** **File 1: `index.html` (Home Page)** ```html ABC Educational Institute - Home

Welcome to ABC Educational Institute

  • Home
  • About Us
  • Courses
  • Contact

This is the home page of our institute. Click the links above to navigate.

Ōåæ Back to Top

``` **File 2: `about/about.html` (About Us Page)** ```html About Us - ABC Institute

About Us

  • Home
  • About Us
  • Courses
  • Contact

We are a leading educational institute...

``` **File 3: `courses.html` (Courses Page with In-Page TOC)** ```html Courses - ABC Institute

Our Courses

  • Home
  • About Us
  • Courses
  • Contact

Table of Contents

  • B.Tech Programs
  • M.Tech Programs
  • Diploma Programs

B.Tech Programs

Four-year undergraduate programs...

M.Tech Programs

Two-year postgraduate programs...

Diploma Programs

Three-year diploma programs...

Ōåæ Back to Top

``` **File 4: `contact.html` (Contact Page with Email Link)** ```html Contact Us - ABC Institute

Contact Us

  • Home
  • About Us
  • Courses
  • Contact

Email us at: info@abcInstitute.in

Ōåæ Back to Top

``` **Folder Structure Used:** ``` abcInstitute/ Ōö£ŌöĆŌöĆ index.html Ōö£ŌöĆŌöĆ contact.html Ōö£ŌöĆŌöĆ courses.html ŌööŌöĆŌöĆ about/ ŌööŌöĆŌöĆ about.html ``` **Key Points to Note in the Solution:** - `index.html` ŌåÆ `about/about.html` (forward into subfolder) - `about/about.html` ŌåÆ `index.html` (using `../` to come up one level) - `#top` used as fragment link for "Back to Top" - `mailto:` link for email - `
` paired with `` for in-page navigation --- ### **Question B (14 Marks)** [Alternative Choice] **(a)** What is the HTML anchor tag? Explain its syntax. Discuss the roles of the `href`, `title`, and `target` attributes with examples. *(7 marks)* **(b)** Write an HTML program to demonstrate **fragment identifiers** (in-page anchors) by creating a single webpage with at least three sections. Include a Table of Contents at the top that links to each section, and a "Back to Top" link in every section. *(7 marks)* --- #### **Model Solution to Question B (a):** The **HTML anchor tag (``)** is the element used to create **hyperlinks** ŌĆö clickable elements that navigate the user to another resource (web page, file, email, or location on the same page). **Basic Syntax:** ```html Link Text ``` **Attributes Explained:** **1. `href` (Hypertext Reference)** *(2.5 marks)* - **Role:** Specifies the *destination* of the link. It is a **mandatory** attribute. - **Example:** `KTU Website` - For internal links, `href` can be a relative path, root-relative path, or fragment identifier. **2. `title` (Tooltip Text)** *(2 marks)* - **Role:** Provides *additional information* about the link, shown as a tooltip when the user hovers the mouse over the link. It is also used by **screen readers** for accessibility. - **Example:** `Syllabus` **3. `target` (Link Opening Behavior)** *(2 marks)* - **Role:** Specifies *where* the linked document should open. - **Values:** - `_self` (default): Same window/tab. - `_blank`: New window/tab. - `_parent`: Parent frame. - `_top`: Full window (breaks out of all frames). - **Example:** `Google (opens in new tab)` **Conclusion** *(0.5 mark)*: The `` tag is the most fundamental interactive element in HTML. Mastering its attributes is essential for proper navigation design. --- #### **Model Solution to Question B (b):** ```html Fragment Identifier Demo

HTML Fragment Identifiers - Demonstration

Table of Contents

  • Section 1: What is HTML?
  • Section 2: What is CSS?
  • Section 3: What is JavaScript?

Section 1: What is HTML?

HTML (HyperText Markup Language) is the standard markup language for creating web pages. It uses tags to describe the structure of a document.

Example: <h1>Hello World</h1>

Ōåæ Back to Top

Section 2: What is CSS?

CSS (Cascading Style Sheets) is used to style and layout web pages. It controls colors, fonts, spacing, and even animations.

Example: h1 { color: blue; }

Ōåæ Back to Top

Section 3: What is JavaScript?

JavaScript is the programming language of the web. It enables interactive behavior, dynamic content, and asynchronous communication with servers.

Example: alert("Hello World!");

Ōåæ Back to Top

``` **Explanation of the Code:** 1. **`

`** at the very top defines the fragment target for the "Back to Top" links. 2. **`
` block** contains a Table of Contents with three links, each pointing to a fragment (`#section1`, `#section2`, `#section3`). 3. **Each `
`** has a unique `id` matching the fragment in the link. 4. **Every section** ends with a "Back to Top" link using `href="#top"`. 5. Clicking any TOC link scrolls the page *smoothly* to the corresponding section (modern browsers support this natively; for older browsers, CSS `scroll-behavior: smooth` can be added). --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks:** > > 1. **Forgetting `../`** ŌĆö When linking from a subfolder to the parent folder, students often write `href="index.html"` instead of `href="../index.html"`. This is the **#1 reason for broken links** and a guaranteed deduction of 1-2 marks. > > 2. **Wrong slash direction** ŌĆö Use `/` (forward slash) for paths, **never** `\` (backslash). Backslashes work only on Windows file systems, not in URLs. > > 3. **Missing `id` attribute** ŌĆö Fragment links fail silently if the target element has no `id`. Examiners check this! > > 4. **Forgetting the closing `` tag** ŌĆö Causes the rest of the page to become part of the link, which examiners notice immediately. > > 5. **Using `target="_blank"` without `rel="noopener"`** ŌĆö In modern web design, opening a new tab without `rel="noopener noreferrer"` is a **security vulnerability**. While KTU may not deduct for this in basic modules, mentioning it in the answer shows **advanced understanding** and earns bonus marks. > > 6. **Confusing root-relative with absolute** ŌĆö A root-relative path **starts with `/` but does NOT include the domain**. Writing `href="/about.html"` is root-relative; writing `href="https://site.com/about.html"` is absolute. Mixing these up is a classic 3-mark deduction. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist ŌĆö Print This Before the Exam!** - Ō£ģ The HTML anchor element is `` and its **mandatory** attribute is `href`. - Ō£ģ **Three internal path types:** Document-relative, Root-relative, Absolute. - Ō£ģ `../` = go up **one** folder level. `../../` = go up **two** levels. - Ō£ģ `/` at the **start** of a path = root of the website (requires a web server). - Ō£ģ `mailto:user@domain.com` opens the default email client. - Ō£ģ Fragment links use `#idname` and require a matching `id` on the target element. - Ō£ģ The `target` attribute has four values: `_self`, `_blank`, `_parent`, `_top`. - Ō£ģ Always use **descriptive link text** (accessibility/WCAG). - Ō£ģ File names should have **no spaces** ŌĆö use hyphens (`-`) or camelCase. - Ō£ģ Internal links should **never** use the full absolute URL of the same site. - Ō£ģ The `title` attribute provides tooltips and helps screen readers. - Ō£ģ For multi-page navigation, **copy the same `
` block** to every page, with `href` paths adjusted based on each page's folder location. - Ō£ģ Closing `` tag is **mandatory** ŌĆö omitting it causes the entire subsequent content to become clickable. - Ō£ģ An `` tag can wrap any HTML element ŌĆö text, images, headings, or even divs. - Ō£ģ Search engines (Googlebot) discover new pages **only by following internal links** ŌĆö a site with poor internal linking has poor SEO.

topics.title: Targeting a New Browser Window
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEITT833 - WEB DESIGNING > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Targeting a New Browser Window # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition In **HTML (HyperText Markup Language)** and **JavaScript (ECMAScript)**, *Targeting a New Browser Window* refers to the controlled process of directing a hyperlink (`` element), a form submission, or a programmatic action so that the resulting document is loaded into a freshly instantiated browser **window**, **tab**, or **inline frame (iframe)** rather than replacing the current document. The mechanism is governed by the HTML `target` attribute (formally standardized in the **W3C HTML 4.01 Specification** and retained in the **HTML Living Standard / HTML5**), and supplemented in client-side scripting by the DOM (Document Object Model) `Window.open()` API. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight (OEITT833 ŌĆō Module 1):** > The KTU 2024 Scheme explicitly lists *"Targeting a new browser window"* under the HTML fundamentals unit, requiring students to demonstrate proficiency in the `target` attribute keywords (`_self`, `_blank`, `_parent`, `_top`, custom frame names) and the JavaScript `window.open()` method with all its parameters (URL, name, specs, replace). The exact semantic definition per the W3C is: > *"If the `target` attribute of an `a` or `area` element is present, it specifies the browsing context in which the linked resource should be displayed."* ŌĆö W3C HTML Living Standard, Section: Links ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Plain-English Intuition Imagine you are reading a **physical book** in a quiet reading room (your **current browser tab**). Every time the book references another resource (a footnote, a glossary entry, a picture in the appendix), the librarian (the **web browser**) has to decide where to put it: - **Option A:** Replace the page you are currently reading with the new content (this is `_self`). - **Option B:** Hand you a brand-new booklet on a separate table so you can glance at it without losing your place (this is `_blank`). - **Option C:** Place the new content in a designated "appendix frame" built into the book (this is a *named target / iframe*). > [!NOTE] > **Plain English Summary:** *Targeting a new browser window* is the act of telling the browser **"do not overwrite my current view ŌĆö instead, spawn a new viewing surface (tab/window) and load the destination there."* The instruction is given either declaratively in HTML (`target="_blank"`) or imperatively in JavaScript (`window.open(url, "_blank")`). ## 1.3 Standard Reserved Target Keywords (KTU High-Yield) The HTML specification defines **four reserved keywords** plus any number of **user-defined frame names**. Memorizing these is mandatory for the KTU exam. | Keyword | Behavior | Common Use Case | |---|---|---| | `_self` | Loads in the **same** browsing context (default if `target` is omitted). | Same-tab navigation. | | `_blank` | Loads in a **new, unnamed** browsing context. | Opening external links in a new tab. | | `_parent` | Loads in the immediate **parent** browsing context. | Breaking out of an ` Return to Outer Page ``` **Step-by-step explanation:** - **Step 1:** The user is viewing `inner.html` inside an `
View Products Contact Us ``` **Step-by-step explanation:** - **Step 1:** An `

Visit KTU Official Website (opens in new tab)

``` **Line-by-line explanation (distributed across 7 marks):** - **`` and ``** (0.5 mark): Standard HTML5 boilerplate declaration. - **`

topics.title: Mail Links, Image Formats
topics.content:
# Ō£ē’ĖÅ MAIL LINKS & ­¤¢╝’ĖÅ IMAGE FORMATS ŌĆö KTU 2024 SCHEME > **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEITT833 - WEB DESIGNING > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Mail Links, Image Formats --- ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Mail Links (Mailto Hyperlinks) A **Mail Link**, commonly known as a **mailto link**, is a special type of HTML hyperlink (anchored within the `` tag) whose `href` attribute begins with the URI scheme `mailto:`. When a user clicks this link, instead of opening a web page in the browser, it instructs the operating system to launch the user's default desktop email client (e.g., Outlook, Thunderbird, Apple Mail) and pre-populate a new message draft with predefined parameters such as the recipient address, subject line, CC/BCC fields, and the message body. #### ­¤ōī Formal KTU 2024 Definition > A **Mailto link** is an HTML anchor element that uses the `mailto:` URI scheme to invoke the user's default mail user agent (MUA), allowing web designers to provide a single-click mechanism for initiating email composition directly from a web page. #### ­¤ÆĪ Conceptual Analogy ŌĆö "The Pre-Filled Letter" Imagine walking into a Post Office. Instead of starting from scratch ŌĆö finding a blank envelope, writing the receiver's address, writing the subject ŌĆö you pick up a **pre-printed template envelope** where the receiver's address, the subject heading, and even a few lines of body content are already filled in by a helpful assistant. You only need to add your personal touch (the rest of the body) and "post" it. A `mailto` link is exactly this digital pre-printed envelope embedded in a webpage. #### ­¤öæ Key Characteristics - Uses the **``** anchor tag with **`href="mailto:..."`** scheme. - Activates the **default mail client** (user-agent dependent). - Supports **pre-population** of email fields using query parameters (`?`, `&`). - Works only if a mail client is **configured** on the user's device; otherwise shows a warning or no action. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Mailto links fall under the broader topic of **"Creating Hyperlinks"** in Module 1 of Web Designing. You must know the syntax, the parameters (`subject`, `cc`, `bcc`, `body`), and the limitation that mailto **cannot guarantee** delivery ŌĆö it merely launches the email composer. --- ### 1.2 Image Formats in Web Design An **Image Format** (or graphics file format) is a standardized method of encoding and storing digital images in a file. For web design, image formats determine how visual content is **compressed**, **rendered**, **transmitted** over the network, and **displayed** by browsers. The choice of format directly impacts **page load speed**, **visual quality**, and **accessibility**. #### ­¤ōī Formal KTU 2024 Definition > A **Web Image Format** is a digital file standard used to store graphical data for display within web browsers, characterized by its compression algorithm (lossy/lossless), color depth support, transparency capability, and suitability for specific content types (photographs, illustrations, icons, animations). #### ­¤ÆĪ Conceptual Analogy ŌĆö "The Suitcase Packing Problem" Think of an image as a large pile of colorful clothes you need to pack into a suitcase for a trip. **JPEG** is like vacuum-compressing thick woollens ŌĆö you save a lot of space (small file), but some fluffiness/detail is lost forever. **PNG** is like carefully folding clothes ŌĆö it takes more suitcase space (larger file), but every shirt looks exactly as it was at home (no quality loss). **GIF** is a small suitcase that fits only a few colors ŌĆö great for logos but bad for photos. **SVG** is a paper blueprint of the clothes (instructions, not pixels) ŌĆö you can scale it to any size perfectly. #### ­¤Śé’ĖÅ The "Big Four" Web Image Formats | Format | Full Name | Best For | |---|---|---| | **GIF** | Graphics Interchange Format | Simple animations, icons, logos with limited colors | | **JPEG** | Joint Photographic Experts Group | Photographs and complex realistic images | | **PNG** | Portable Network Graphics | Logos, transparent images, screenshots | | **SVG** | Scalable Vector Graphics | Icons, logos, illustrations that scale infinitely | > [!NOTE] > **Key Term ŌĆö Compression:** The process of reducing file size by encoding data more efficiently. There are two types: > - **Lossy Compression:** Discards some original data permanently (JPEG). Smaller files, lower quality. > - **Lossless Compression:** Retains all original data (PNG, GIF). Larger files, identical quality. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** In Module 1, you are expected to identify each major image format, explain its **compression type**, **color depth**, **transparency support**, **animation support**, and the **ideal use case** in web pages. --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Anatomy of a Mailto Link A `mailto` URI is constructed by combining a **recipient address** with optional **header fields** separated by a question mark `?`, and multiple fields are chained with ampersands `&`. #### ­¤ö® Structural Breakdown 1. **Base Scheme:** `mailto:` ŌĆö the protocol identifier (mandatory). 2. **Recipient(s):** One or more email addresses separated by commas. 3. **Query String Delimiter:** `?` separates the address from the parameters. 4. **Header Parameters:** Key-value pairs, URL-encoded, joined by `&`. #### ­¤ōŗ Supported Mailto Parameters (RFC 6068 Standard) | Parameter | Purpose | URL-Encoded Name | |---|---|---| | `subject` | Pre-fills the email subject line | `%20` for spaces | | `cc` | Carbon copy recipient(s) | Comma-separated | | `bcc` | Blind carbon copy recipient(s) | Comma-separated | | `body` | Pre-fills the message body | Newlines as `%0D%0A` | #### ­¤¦Ā Why and How It Works - The browser detects the `mailto:` scheme and **delegates** the URI to the OS. - The OS looks up the **default mail handler** (registered application for the `mailto` protocol). - That mail client parses the URI, splits the parameters, and **populates** the compose window. - The actual sending of the email is performed by the mail client ŌĆö **NOT** by the browser or webpage. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Critical Limitation:** If the user has **no default mail client** configured (common on locked-down corporate PCs, Chromebooks in kiosk mode, or mobile browsers without mail apps), clicking a mailto link does nothing, opens Gmail in a new tab (on Android), or shows an error. This is a known UX pitfall. --- ### 2.2 Image Formats ŌĆö Deep Theoretical Comparison #### 2.2.1 GIF (Graphics Interchange Format) - **Invented by:** CompuServe in **1987**. - **Compression:** Lossless (LZW algorithm). - **Color Palette:** Limited to **8-bit** ŌåÆ maximum **256 colors**. - **Transparency:** Supports **1-bit transparency** (fully transparent OR fully opaque ŌĆö no semi-transparency/alpha). - **Animation:** Ō£ģ Yes ŌĆö supports frame-based animation, the de facto format for short looping animations before video became lightweight. - **Best For:** Tiny icons, simple logos with flat colors, animated banners. #### 2.2.2 JPEG / JPG (Joint Photographic Experts Group) - **Invented by:** JPEG Committee in **1992**. - **Compression:** **Lossy** (uses Discrete Cosine Transform ŌĆö DCT). - **Color Palette:** **24-bit** ŌåÆ up to **16.7 million colors** (true color). - **Transparency:** ŌØī **No native transparency** support. - **Animation:** ŌØī No animation. - **Best For:** Real-world photographs, hero images, complex gradients. - **Quality Slider:** Adjustable compression level from **0** (lowest quality, smallest file) to **100** (highest quality, largest file). #### 2.2.3 PNG (Portable Network Graphics) - **Invented by:** W3C + PNG Group, released in **1996** (as a free, open alternative to GIF). - **Compression:** **Lossless** (uses DEFLATE algorithm ŌĆö same as ZIP). - **Color Palette:** **24-bit** truecolor, plus optional **8-bit alpha channel** for transparency. - **Transparency:** Ō£ģ **Full alpha-channel transparency** (256 levels of opacity from 0 to 255). - **Animation:** ŌØī No native animation (use APNG or rely on WebP for that). - **Variants:** PNG-8 (256 colors, like GIF) and PNG-24 (true color, no alpha) and PNG-32 (true color + alpha). - **Best For:** Logos, UI screenshots, images requiring transparent backgrounds, diagrams. #### 2.2.4 SVG (Scalable Vector Graphics) - **Invented by:** W3C, released in **1999**. - **Nature:** **Vector** format (not pixel/raster) ŌĆö defined by mathematical shapes, paths, and text. - **Compression:** Text-based XML; highly compressible with GZIP. - **Scalability:** Ō£ģ **Infinitely scalable** without any quality loss. - **Transparency:** Ō£ģ Full transparency. - **Animation:** Ō£ģ Supports SMIL animations and CSS/JS-based animations. - **Best For:** Icons, logos, illustrations, charts, infographics, responsive design. - **File Extension:** `.svg` (XML-based, human-readable). #### 2.2.5 Other Formats Worth Knowing | Format | Type | Use Case | |---|---|---| | **WebP** | Raster (lossy + lossless) | Modern replacement for JPEG/PNG, ~30% smaller files. Supported by all modern browsers. | | **BMP** | Raster (uncompressed) | Legacy Windows bitmap. Very large files, rarely used on the web. | | **TIFF** | Raster (lossless) | Professional photography, print media. NOT used on web (huge files). | | **ICO** | Raster | Favicons, Windows shortcuts. | --- #### ­¤¦« KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet ŌĆö Image Format Decision Table | Feature | GIF | JPEG | PNG | SVG | |---|---|---|---|---| | **Compression Type** | Lossless | Lossy | Lossless | N/A (Vector) | | **Max Colors** | 256 (8-bit) | 16.7M (24-bit) | 16.7M + alpha (32-bit) | Unlimited | | **Transparency** | 1-bit (binary) | ŌØī None | Ō£ģ Full alpha | Ō£ģ Full | | **Animation** | Ō£ģ Yes | ŌØī No | ŌØī No (use APNG) | Ō£ģ Yes | | **Scalability** | Pixelated on zoom | Pixelated on zoom | Pixelated on zoom | Ō£ģ Infinite | | **Best Use** | Icons, simple animations | Photographs | Logos, screenshots, transparency | Vector graphics, icons | | **File Size** | Small | Very small (tunable) | Medium-large | Very small | | **Browser Support** | Universal | Universal | Universal | Modern (IE excluded) | > [!NOTE] > **Practical Engineering Rule of Thumb:** > - Photograph on the web ŌåÆ **JPEG** (or **WebP** for modern sites). > - Logo with transparent background ŌåÆ **PNG** (or **SVG** if it's a simple vector logo). > - Tiny animated icon ŌåÆ **GIF**. > - Icon set, scalable logo, illustration ŌåÆ **SVG**. --- ### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Mailto Links** are ubiquitous in **contact pages**, **support links**, **"Email the author"** footers, and **newsletter subscription CTAs**. They serve as the simplest zero-backend mechanism for letting users contact a website owner without exposing the email address to spam bots. - **Image Format Selection** is a critical performance decision in **front-end engineering**. Choosing WebP over JPEG can reduce page weight by **25ŌĆō35%**, directly improving **Core Web Vitals** (LCP ŌĆö Largest Contentful Paint) and **SEO rankings**. SVG icons reduce HTTP requests when inlined, eliminating separate image downloads entirely. --- ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Step-by-Step Construction of a Mailto Link Let us derive a fully-featured mailto link that pre-populates a **recipient**, **CC**, **BCC**, **subject**, and **body** containing two lines of text. #### Step 1 ŌĆö Start with the base scheme and recipient The base scheme is `mailto:` followed by the primary recipient's address. $$ \texttt{href} = \texttt{"mailto:admissions@ktu.edu"} $$ #### Step 2 ŌĆö Add a `?` to introduce parameters The question mark separates the address from the parameter list. $$ \texttt{href} = \texttt{"mailto:admissions@ktu.edu?"} $$ #### Step 3 ŌĆö Add the `subject` parameter Subjects with spaces must be URL-encoded. A space is encoded as `%20`. $$ \texttt{href} = \texttt{"mailto:admissions@ktu.edu?subject=Admission\%20Query"} $$ #### Step 4 ŌĆö Add the `cc` parameter using `&` The ampersand chains multiple parameters. $$ \texttt{href} = \texttt{"mailto:admissions@ktu.edu?subject=Admission\%20Query&cc=registrar@ktu.edu"} $$ #### Step 5 ŌĆö Add the `bcc` parameter $$ \texttt{href} = \texttt{"mailto:admissions@ktu.edu?subject=Admission\%20Query&cc=registrar@ktu.edu&bcc=audit@ktu.edu"} $$ #### Step 6 ŌĆö Add the `body` parameter with a multi-line message Newlines in the body are encoded as `%0D%0A` (carriage return + line feed). $$ \texttt{href} = \texttt{"mailto:admissions@ktu.edu?subject=Admission\%20Query&cc=registrar@ktu.edu&bcc=audit@ktu.edu&body=Dear\%20Sir\%2C\%0D\%0AI\%20wish\%20to\%20apply\%20for\%20B.Tech."} $$ #### Step 7 ŌĆö Wrap it inside a complete HTML anchor element ```html Email the Admissions Office ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Coding Pitfall:** In HTML source, the ampersand `&` separating parameters **must** be written as `&` to be valid XML/HTML. The browser decodes it back to `&` when interpreting the URL. Forgetting this is a common source of validation errors. --- ### 3.2 Step-by-Step Analysis ŌĆö Choosing the Right Image Format Let us work through a structured decision process for a typical KTU student portfolio website. #### Scenario 1: Hero banner photograph of the campus 1. Is it a photograph with millions of colors and gradients? ŌåÆ **Yes**. 2. Is transparency needed? ŌåÆ **No**. 3. Is animation needed? ŌåÆ **No**. 4. ŌåÆ **Choose JPEG** (or **WebP** for a modern upgrade). Quality: **75ŌĆō85%** for a good balance. #### Scenario 2: University logo on the navigation bar 1. Is it a photograph? ŌåÆ **No** (it has flat colors, geometric shapes). 2. Is transparency needed? ŌåÆ **Yes** (background of nav-bar may be any color). 3. Is the logo used at multiple sizes? ŌåÆ **Yes** (mobile vs desktop). 4. ŌåÆ **Choose SVG** (preferred) or **PNG-24** with alpha. #### Scenario 3: A spinning "Loading..." indicator 1. Is animation needed? ŌåÆ **Yes**. 2. Is full color depth needed? ŌåÆ **No** (usually 2ŌĆō3 colors). 3. ŌåÆ **Choose GIF** (or **APNG/WebP animation** for higher quality). #### Scenario 4: A printable PDF of the syllabus - Not an image; out of scope of this topic. --- ### 3.3 Analytical Matrix ŌĆö Comparative Table for Exam Writing | Property | GIF | JPEG | PNG | SVG | |---|---|---|---|---| | **Compression** | Lossless (LZW) | Lossy (DCT) | Lossless (DEFLATE) | Vector (XML) | | **Color Depth** | 8-bit | 24-bit | 24/32-bit | Unlimited | | **Max Colors** | 256 | 16.7M | 16.7M + alpha | N/A | | **Transparency** | 1-bit (binary) | None | 8-bit alpha (256 levels) | Full | | **Animation** | Yes | No | No (APNG is separate) | Yes (SMIL/CSS) | | **Scalability** | Poor | Poor | Poor | Infinite | | **Typical Extension** | `.gif` | `.jpg` / `.jpeg` | `.png` | `.svg` | | **Year Introduced** | 1987 | 1992 | 1996 | 1999 | | **MIME Type** | `image/gif` | `image/jpeg` | `image/png` | `image/svg+xml` | | **Ideal Use** | Animated icons, simple graphics | Photographs | Transparent images, screenshots | Logos, icons, illustrations | --- ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Flow Diagram ŌĆö What Happens When a User Clicks a Mailto Link ```mermaid flowchart TD A[User clicks mailto link in browser] --> B{Browser detects mailto: scheme} B -- Yes --> C[Browser extracts address and parameters] B -- No --> D[Browser navigates to URL normally] C --> E[Browser hands URI to Operating System] E --> F{OS looks up default mail handler} F -- Mail client configured --> G[Mail client opens compose window] G --> H[Compose window pre-filled with To, CC, BCC, Subject, Body] H --> I[User reviews and clicks Send] I --> J[Email transmitted via SMTP by mail client] F -- No mail client configured --> K[Browser shows error or no action] K --> L[User experience FAILED] ``` --- ### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture ŌĆö Web Image Format Selection ```mermaid flowchart TD Start[Need to display an image on a webpage] --> Q1{Is the source vector based?} Q1 -- Yes --> SVGPath[Use SVG: logos icons illustrations] Q1 -- No --> Q2{Is animation required?} Q2 -- Yes --> Q3{How many colors in animation?} Q3 -- Few colors andlt 256 --> GIFPath[Use GIF] Q3 -- Many colors --> APNGPath[Use APNG or WebP animation] Q2 -- No --> Q4{Does image need transparency?} Q4 -- No --> Q5{Is it a photograph with many colors?} Q5 -- Yes --> JPEGPath[Use JPEG or WebP lossy] Q5 -- No --> PNG8Path[Use PNG 8 bit] Q4 -- Yes --> PNGPath[Use PNG 24 or PNG 32 with alpha channel] SVGPath --> End[Render in browser via img tag or inline] GIFPath --> End APNGPath --> End JPEGPath --> End PNG8Path --> End PNGPath --> End ``` --- ### 4.3 Conceptual Diagram ŌĆö Compression Type Comparison ```mermaid flowchart LR Original[Original Image Data] --> A{Compression Type} A -- Lossy JPEG --> CompressedLossy[Smaller file: parts of data discarded permanently] CompressedLossy --> Q1[Quality slightly reduced: cannot be reversed] A -- Lossless PNG/GIF --> CompressedLoss[Smaller file: data reorganized mathematically] CompressedLoss --> Q2[Quality identical to original: fully reversible on decompression] ``` --- ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam ŌĆö June 2024]** **Define a mailto link. Write the basic HTML syntax to create a mailto link that opens an email composer for `support@ktu.edu` with the subject "Help Required".** **Model Answer:** A **mailto link** is an HTML hyperlink that uses the `mailto:` URI scheme to launch the user's default email client and pre-populate a new message window. The browser delegates the URI to the OS, which hands it to the registered mail user agent (MUA). **HTML Syntax:** ```html Contact Support ``` The `%20` is the URL-encoded representation of a space in the subject line. When clicked, this link opens the user's mail client (e.g., Outlook) with the **To** field set to `support@ktu.edu` and the **Subject** field pre-filled with "Help Required". --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam ŌĆö Dec 2023]** **Differentiate between lossy and lossless image compression.
