Name one image format that uses each.** **Model Answer:** | Feature | Lossy Compression | Lossless Compression | |---|---|---| | **Definition** | Permanently discards some original image data to reduce file size | Reorganizes data mathematically so the file can be restored to its exact original quality | | **File Size Reduction** | Large reduction (often 10├Ś or more) | Modest reduction (typically 2├ŚŌĆō3├Ś) | | **Quality** | Reduced ŌĆö artifacts may appear at high compression | Identical to original ŌĆö no quality loss | | **Reversibility** | ŌØī Not reversible (destructive) | Ō£ģ Fully reversible on decompression | | **Example Format** | **JPEG** (uses DCT algorithm) | **PNG** (uses DEFLATE), **GIF** (uses LZW) | | **Best Use** | Photographs on the web | Logos, screenshots, images requiring transparency | --- ### 5.2 Part B ŌĆö Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) --- #### **Question A (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam ŌĆö Model Paper 2024, Module 1]** **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the structure of a mailto URI as per **RFC 6068**. List all standard parameters and write a complete HTML code segment demonstrating a mailto link that pre-fills **To**, **CC**, **BCC**, **Subject**, and a **multi-line Body**. **(b) [7 Marks]** Compare **GIF, JPEG, PNG, and SVG** image formats across the following dimensions: compression type, color depth, transparency support, animation support, scalability, and ideal web use case. Present your answer in a structured table. **Model Solution:** **(a) Mailto URI Structure (RFC 6068)** The mailto URI follows the syntax: $$ \texttt{mailto:} \;\; \textit{addr} \;\; \texttt{[ ?} \;\; \textit{header} \;\; \texttt{]^{*}} $$ Where: - `addr` is one or more email addresses (comma-separated). - `header` is a sequence of `field=value` pairs joined by `&`. - Whitespace must be URL-encoded as `%20`. - Newlines in the `body` are encoded as `%0D%0A`. **Standard Parameters:** | Parameter | Purpose | |---|---| | `subject` | Pre-fills the Subject line | | `cc` | Pre-fills the CC (Carbon Copy) field | | `bcc` | Pre-fills the BCC (Blind Carbon Copy) field | | `body` | Pre-fills the body of the message | **Complete HTML Code:** ```html Contact Us

Get in Touch

Click here to email us

``` **Explanation of Encoding:** - `%20` ŌåÆ space - `%2C` ŌåÆ comma - `%0D%0A` ŌåÆ carriage return + line feed (newline) - `&` ŌåÆ HTML-encoded ampersand (decoded to `&` by the browser) When the user clicks, the mail client opens with: - **To:** info@ktu.edu - **Cc:** webmaster@ktu.edu - **Bcc:** audit@ktu.edu - **Subject:** Website Query - **Body:** Dear Team, [newline] I have a question regarding the syllabus. [newline] [newline] Regards, [newline] Student --- **(b) Comparative Table of Image Formats** | Property | GIF | JPEG | PNG | SVG | |---|---|---|---|---| | **Compression Type** | Lossless (LZW) | Lossy (DCT) | Lossless (DEFLATE) | Vector (XML) | | **Color Depth** | 8-bit | 24-bit | 24-bit / 32-bit (with alpha) | Unlimited (mathematical) | | **Max Colors** | 256 | 16.7 million | 16.7M + 256 alpha levels | Unlimited | | **Transparency** | 1-bit binary (on/off) | ŌØī Not supported | Ō£ģ 8-bit alpha (256 levels) | Ō£ģ Full alpha | | **Animation** | Ō£ģ Yes (frame-based) | ŌØī No | ŌØī No (use APNG) | Ō£ģ Yes (SMIL/CSS/JS) | | **Scalability** | Pixelated when enlarged | Pixelated when enlarged | Pixelated when enlarged | Ō£ģ Infinite, lossless | | **File Extension** | `.gif` | `.jpg` / `.jpeg` | `.png` | `.svg` | | **Ideal Web Use** | Animated icons, simple flat-color graphics | Photographs, hero images, complex realistic images | Logos with transparent backgrounds, UI screenshots, diagrams | Icons, scalable logos, illustrations, responsive design | | **File Size** | Small for limited colors | Very small (tunable quality) | Medium to large | Very small (text-based) | | **Year Released** | 1987 | 1992 | 1996 | 1999 | **Key Takeaway:** - Use **JPEG** for photographs, **PNG** for images needing transparency, **GIF** for simple animations, and **SVG** for vector graphics that need to scale. --- #### **Question B (14 Marks) ŌĆö Alternative Choice** **[KTU University Exam ŌĆö Model Paper 2024, Module 1]** **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain with examples how **mailto links** can include multiple recipients, a CC, a BCC, and a subject line. Discuss the **limitations** of mailto links in modern web design. **(b) [7 Marks]** With a suitable flowchart/diagram, describe the **decision process** for choosing the correct image format (GIF, JPEG, PNG, SVG) for a given web scenario. Illustrate with **three real-world examples**. **Model Solution:** **(a) Mailto Link with Multiple Parameters** **Single recipient with subject:** ```html Email John ``` **Multiple recipients (comma-separated):** ```html Email Team ``` **Recipient + CC + BCC + Subject + Body:** ```html Send Meeting Notes ``` **Limitations of Mailto Links:** 1. **No Mail Client = No Action:** If the user's device has no default mail client (common on corporate PCs, kiosks), clicking the link does nothing. The webpage cannot detect or recover from this gracefully without JavaScript. 2. **Spam Bot Exposure:** Email addresses embedded in HTML source are harvested by automated crawlers, leading to spam. Mitigation: encode addresses using JavaScript obfuscation or use contact forms. 3. **No Tracking:** The web server has no visibility into whether the user actually sent the email ŌĆö it only knows the link was rendered. 4. **Length Restrictions:** Some mail clients truncate very long mailto URIs (especially mobile clients), silently dropping parameters. 5. **Inconsistent Parameter Support:** Not all mail clients support `cc`, `bcc`, and `body` parameters equally. For example, older versions of Outlook ignore `bcc`. 6. **Security Risk:** A malicious mailto link with a pre-filled body could phish users (e.g., "Click to claim your prize ŌĆö your account is compromised"). Modern browsers sometimes warn or sanitize. 7. **No Server-Side Processing:** Mailto is a pure client-side mechanism; it cannot integrate with backend systems like CRMs, ticketing systems, or transactional email services without replacing it with a contact form. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Students often forget to URL-encode spaces (using `%20`) and newlines (using `%0D%0A`) in the body parameter. Writing a raw multi-line body without encoding **will not work** in real browsers. Always show encoding in your exam answer. --- **(b) Image Format Decision Process** **Decision Logic:** 1. Is the image **vector-based** (mathematical shapes, paths, text)? ŌåÆ Use **SVG**. 2. Else, is **animation** required? - Yes ŌåÆ Use **GIF** (simple) or **WebP/APNG** (modern). - No ŌåÆ Continue. 3. Is **transparency** required? - Yes ŌåÆ Use **PNG-24 or PNG-32** (with alpha channel). - No ŌåÆ Continue. 4. Is it a **photograph** with millions of colors and gradients? - Yes ŌåÆ Use **JPEG** (tuned to 75ŌĆō85% quality) or **WebP**. - No ŌåÆ Use **PNG-8** (lossless, 256 colors). **Three Real-World Examples:** **Example 1 ŌĆö College Logo on the Navbar:** - The logo is a flat-color design with text and geometric shapes. - Needs transparency to blend with any navbar background. - Will be displayed at different sizes (mobile, tablet, desktop). - **Decision:** Vector + transparency + responsive ŌåÆ **SVG** (best) or **PNG-32** (fallback). **Example 2 ŌĆö Hero Photograph of Campus Building:** - A high-resolution photograph with complex colors, shadows, and gradients. - No transparency or animation needed. - **Decision:** Photograph + no transparency ŌåÆ **JPEG** at 80% quality (best balance of size and clarity). **Example 3 ŌĆö A "New!" Pulsing Badge Icon:** - A small badge with a pulsing animation drawing attention. - Few colors (red, white, yellow). - **Decision:** Animation + few colors ŌåÆ **GIF** with a 12-frame loop, or **SVG** with a CSS pulse animation. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning ŌĆö Common Mistakes:** > 1. **Conflating GIF transparency with PNG transparency:** GIF supports only **1-bit** (binary) transparency ŌĆö pixels are either fully transparent OR fully opaque. PNG supports **8-bit alpha** with 256 levels of partial transparency. Students who write "GIF supports transparency" without qualification lose marks. > 2. **Forgetting `&` in HTML mailto:** Writing a raw `&` in HTML fails W3C validation. Use `&` and let the browser decode it. > 3. **Saying "SVG is for photographs":** SVG is for **vector** graphics. Photographs are raster by nature and must use JPEG/PNG/WebP. > 4. **Confusing lossy with low quality:** Lossy compression is a **trade-off** technique. At moderate levels (75ŌĆō85%), JPEG is visually indistinguishable from the original for most users. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember ­¤ōī - Ō£ģ **Mailto links** use the `mailto:` URI scheme inside an `` tag's `href`. - Ō£ģ Supported parameters: **`subject`**, **`cc`**, **`bcc`**, **`body`** ŌĆö joined by `&`, separated from address by `?`. - Ō£ģ Whitespace ŌåÆ `%20`; Newline ŌåÆ `%0D%0A`; Comma ŌåÆ `%2C`. - Ō£ģ In HTML source, **`&` must be written as `&`**. - Ō£ģ Mailto links depend on the **OS-level default mail client**; if none exists, the link is broken. - Ō£ģ Mailto links are **client-side only** ŌĆö no server-side tracking, no spam protection out of the box. - Ō£ģ **GIF** ŌåÆ Lossless, 256 colors, 1-bit transparency, **animation**, small flat graphics. - Ō£ģ **JPEG** ŌåÆ **Lossy**, 16.7M colors, no transparency, no animation, **photographs**. - Ō£ģ **PNG** ŌåÆ Lossless, 16.7M colors + alpha, full transparency, no animation, **logos/screenshots**. - Ō£ģ **SVG** ŌåÆ **Vector**, infinite scalability, full transparency, supports animation, **icons and logos**. - Ō£ģ Compression trade-off: **Lossy = small file + lower quality**; **Lossless = larger file + exact quality**. - Ō£ģ **WebP** is a modern format offering both lossy and lossless modes with ~30% smaller files than JPEG/PNG; supported by all modern browsers. - Ō£ģ For any web image decision: ask **"Vector? Animation? Transparency? Photograph?"** in that order.

topics.title: Img Element
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEITT833 - WEB DESIGNING > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Img Element # ­¤ōś SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition The **`` element** in HTML (HyperText Markup Language) is a **void (replaced) inline element** used to embed an external image resource into a web document. It is a *self-closing* tag that pulls image data from a specified source and renders it inline with the surrounding content flow. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 1 ŌĆō Web Fundamentals)** > As per the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for *OEITT833 ŌĆō Web Designing*, the `` element is a foundational building block of HTML, classified under "Embedding Media and Graphics." Mastery of its attributes is essential for Part A (3 marks) and Part B (14 marks) university exam questions. The W3C/MDN standard syntax is: ```html ``` > [!NOTE] > **Core Definition Box** > - **Element Type:** Void / Replaced / Inline > - **Closing Tag:** NOT required (self-closing) > - **W3C Specification:** HTML Living Standard ŌĆö Section 4.8.3 > - **Primary Purpose:** Visual content embedding (photographs, icons, illustrations, diagrams) ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Think of the `` element as a **picture frame mounted on a wall**: - The **frame itself** is the `` tag ŌĆö it has a fixed size, position, and purpose. - The **photograph inside the frame** comes from the `src` attribute ŌĆö it points to a real photo stored somewhere (your hard drive, a server, or the cloud). - The **label written under the frame** is the `alt` attribute ŌĆö describing what the picture shows, especially useful when someone is visually impaired (using a screen reader) or when the picture fails to load. > ­¤öæ **Intuitive Takeaway:** A picture frame isn't a photograph; it's a *placeholder* that references a photograph. Similarly, `` doesn't store image data ŌĆö it *references* it. ## 1.3 Element Classification Standards | Property | Value | KTU Significance | |----------|-------|------------------| | **Tag Type** | Void (Empty) | No closing tag required ŌĆö important for MCQ/short-answer questions | | **Display Role** | Replaced Element | Browser replaces the tag with the actual image content | | **Content Model** | None | Cannot contain child elements or text | | **Default Display** | `inline` | Sits within text flow by default | | **Global Attributes** | All allowed | `id`, `class`, `style`, `title`, `tabindex`, etc. | ## 1.4 Why the `` Element Matters in Web Design > [!IMPORTANT] > The **average human brain processes images in 13 milliseconds** ŌĆö 60,000├Ś faster than text. The `` element is therefore the most performance-critical and accessibility-sensitive tag in HTML. - **Engagement:** Web pages with optimized images retain **2.3├Ś more user attention** than text-only pages. - **SEO (Search Engine Optimization):** Properly tagged images improve search ranking. - **Accessibility:** Screen readers depend on `alt` text to describe visual content. - **Performance:** Image size directly impacts page load speed (a key metric in **Core Web Vitals**). # ­¤ōś SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Attribute Sheet ## 2.1 Mandatory Attributes (The Core Four) The `` element has two **mandatory attributes** and many optional ones. Every KTU question on this topic tests at least the first two. ### ­¤ö╣ A. `src` (Source) ŌĆö Mandatory - **Purpose:** Specifies the URL or file path of the image to be displayed. - **Values:** Absolute URL, relative URL, or data URI. - **Behavior:** If the URL is invalid, the browser falls back to the `alt` text (or a broken-image icon). **Example:** ```html ``` ### ­¤ö╣ B. `alt` (Alternative Text) ŌĆö Mandatory for Accessibility - **Purpose:** Provides textual fallback when the image cannot be loaded or read by assistive technologies. - **Best Practice:** Describe the *function* or *content* of the image, not just its appearance. **Examples:** ```html ``` > [!NOTE] > **KTU Examiner Tip:** Decorative images that add no information should use `alt=""` (empty alt) to inform screen readers to skip them. This is a frequent 3-mark question. ### ­¤ö╣ C. `width` and `height` ŌĆö Strongly Recommended - **Purpose:** Defines the display dimensions in **CSS pixels (px)**. - **Why it matters:** Specifying dimensions *reserves layout space* and prevents **Cumulative Layout Shift (CLS)** ŌĆö a Core Web Vital metric. **Example:** ```html ``` > [!WARNING] > Setting `width` and `height` as HTML attributes does *not* distort the image by default unless CSS overrides are applied. The browser preserves the aspect ratio of the source. ## 2.2 Optional High-Yield Attributes (KTU Frequently Tested) | Attribute | Purpose | Typical Value | KTU Priority | |-----------|---------|---------------|--------------| | `title` | Tooltip on hover | `"Hover text"` | ŌŁÉŌŁÉ | | `loading` | Lazy/eager loading | `"lazy"` / `"eager"` | ŌŁÉŌŁÉŌŁÉ | | `decoding` | Image decode hint | `"async"` / `"sync"` / `"auto"` | ŌŁÉŌŁÉ | | `srcset` | Responsive image sources | `"small.jpg 480w, large.jpg 1024w"` | ŌŁÉŌŁÉŌŁÉŌŁÉ | | `sizes` | Layout breakpoints | `"(max-width: 600px) 100vw, 50vw"` | ŌŁÉŌŁÉŌŁÉŌŁÉ | | `usemap` | Image map reference | `"#mapname"` | ŌŁÉŌŁÉŌŁÉ | | `ismap` | Server-side map | Boolean attribute | ŌŁÉŌŁÉ | | `referrerpolicy` | Referrer header control | `"no-referrer"` | ŌŁÉ | | `crossorigin` | CORS request mode | `"anonymous"` / `"use-credentials"` | ŌŁÉ | | `fetchpriority` | Hint to browser | `"high"` / `"low"` / `"auto"` | ŌŁÉŌŁÉ | ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Table) > [!IMPORTANT] > The following table consolidates all key `` element syntax, attributes, and image format selection rules for quick exam revision. | **Concept** | **Specification / Rule** | **Unit / Standard** | |-------------|--------------------------|---------------------| | Element Type | Void / Replaced / Inline | HTML Living Standard ┬¦4.8.3 | | Closing Tag | NOT required | Self-closing | | `src` (mandatory) | URL or relative path of image | URI / URN | | `alt` (mandatory) | Textual fallback for accessibility | Plain text | | `width` / `height` | Display dimensions | CSS Pixels (px) | | `loading="lazy"` | Defers offscreen image loading | Native lazy load | | `srcset` syntax | `url1 size1w, url2 size2w` | w-descriptor | | `sizes` syntax | `media-query width, fallback` | vw / px / em | | `usemap` | Hash reference to `` | Fragment ID | | Aspect Ratio Rule | $\text{AspectRatio} = \dfrac{\text{width}}{\text{height}}$ | Ratio (e.g., 16:9) | | Image Format Selection | Photograph ŌåÆ JPEG; Logo ŌåÆ PNG/SVG; Animation ŌåÆ GIF/WebP | ŌĆö | | Accessibility Rule | Informative image ŌåÆ descriptive `alt`; Decorative ŌåÆ `alt=""` | WCAG 2.1 | ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility The `` element is not just a student textbook concept ŌĆö it powers real systems: - **­¤ō░ News Portals (e.g., The Hindu, NDTV):** Embed thousands of `` tags with `srcset` for multi-device support. - **­¤øÆ E-Commerce (Amazon, Flipkart):** Product images use `loading="lazy"` and `srcset` for performance. - **­¤Ś║’ĖÅ Interactive Maps (Google Maps):** Combine `` with `` and `` for clickable image regions. - **­¤ōĖ Social Media (Instagram, Twitter):** Use responsive image techniques powered by `` and `` fallback. # ­¤ōś SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Syntax Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Deriving the Basic `` Tag Structure ### Step 1: Identify the Need for an Image You want to display a photograph called `sunset.jpg` located in the same folder as your HTML file. ### Step 2: Identify the Mandatory Attributes - You need `src` to point to the file. - You need `alt` for accessibility. ### Step 3: Add Optional Layout Attributes - You want to set a specific display size of 600 ├Ś 400 pixels. ### Step 4: Assemble the Tag ```html ``` ### Step 5: Browser Interpretation Logic $$ \text{Browser Action} = \begin{cases} \text{Render image from src} & \text{if } \text{URL is valid and decodable} \\ \text{Display alt text or broken icon} & \text{if } \text{URL is invalid} \\ \text{Notify assistive tech (alt text)} & \text{always, regardless of load} \end{cases} $$ ## 3.2 Derivative: Responsive Image Using `srcset` and `sizes` ### Step 1: Define Multiple Image Sources You have three resolutions of the same banner: - `banner-small.jpg` ŌĆö 480 px wide (for mobile) - `banner-medium.jpg` ŌĆö 800 px wide (for tablet) - `banner-large.jpg` ŌĆö 1200 px wide (for desktop) ### Step 2: Construct the `srcset` Attribute The `w-descriptor` describes the *intrinsic width* of each image file: ```html srcset="banner-small.jpg 480w, banner-medium.jpg 800w, banner-large.jpg 1200w" ``` ### Step 3: Construct the `sizes` Attribute The `sizes` attribute tells the browser *how much viewport space* the image will occupy at different breakpoints: ```html sizes="(max-width: 600px) 100vw, (max-width: 1024px) 80vw, 1200px" ``` ### Step 4: Combine into the Final Tag ```html ``` ### Step 5: Browser Decision Logic (Exhaustive) $$ \text{Selected Image} = \underset{img \in \text{srcset}}{\operatorname{arg\,max}} \left\{ w \;\big|\; w \leq \text{EffectiveViewportWidth}(sizes) \right\} $$ Where: $$ \text{EffectiveViewportWidth} = \text{Matching Media Query Width in sizes} $$ **Textual explanation of the decision process:** 1. The browser first reads the `sizes` attribute to determine the *intended display width* of the image based on the current viewport. 2. It then compares the device's **Device Pixel Ratio (DPR)** against the `srcset` candidate widths. 3. The browser *selects the smallest image* from `srcset` whose intrinsic width is greater than or equal to the calculated effective width ├Ś DPR. 4. If `srcset` is unsupported (legacy browsers), the browser falls back to the `src` attribute. ## 3.3 Derivative: Image Map with `` and `` ### Step 1: Define the Image ```html ``` ### Step 2: Define the Map (after the image) ```html ``` ### Step 3: Coordinate Geometry Reference For a **rectangular area**: $$ \text{Area} = (x_2 - x_1) \times (y_2 - y_1) $$ For a **circular area**: $$ \text{Area} = \pi r^2 $$ **Example Calculation:** For ``: - Center: (400, 200) - Radius: 80 pixels - Area: $3.14159 \times 80^2 = 3.14159 \times 6400 = 20106.18 \text{ px}^2$ ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Image Format Selection | Format | MIME Type | Best For | Compression | Transparency | Animation | Browser Support | |--------|-----------|----------|-------------|--------------|-----------|-----------------| | **JPEG** | `image/jpeg` | Photographs | Lossy | ŌØī No | ŌØī No | 100% | | **PNG** | `image/png` | Logos, screenshots | Lossless | Ō£ģ Yes (alpha) | ŌØī No | 100% | | **GIF** | `image/gif` | Simple animations | Lossless | Ō£ģ Yes (1-bit) | Ō£ģ Yes | 100% | | **WebP** | `image/webp` | Modern replacement | Both | Ō£ģ Yes | Ō£ģ Yes | 97%+ | | **SVG** | `image/svg+xml` | Vector graphics | Lossless | Ō£ģ Yes | Ō£ģ Yes (CSS) | 100% | | **AVIF** | `image/avif` | Next-gen photos | Lossy | Ō£ģ Yes | ŌØī No | 92%+ | ## 3.5 Lazy Loading Performance Equation When `loading="lazy"` is used, the browser delays image loading until the image is near the viewport: $$ \text{Load Trigger Distance} \approx 1.5 \times \text{Viewport Height (vh)} $$ This means an image is fetched when it is within **1.5 viewport heights** of the visible area, optimizing bandwidth and **Time to Interactive (TTI)**. # ­¤ōś SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: Image Rendering Pipeline ```mermaid flowchart TD A[HTML Document Loaded] --> B[Parser Encounters img Tag] B --> C{Is src Valid?} C -- Yes --> D[Fetch Image Resource] C -- No --> E[Display alt Text and Broken Icon] D --> F{Image Decoded Successfully?} F -- Yes --> G[Render Image at Specified width and height] F -- No --> E G --> H{loading Attribute Set?} H -- lazy --> I[Defer Load Until Near Viewport] H -- eager --> J[Load Immediately] I --> K[Image Visible in Viewport] J --> K E --> L[Screen Reader Reads alt Text] K --> M[User Interacts with Image] M --> N{Is Image Clickable?} N -- Yes --> O[Navigate to href via usemap or anchor] N -- No --> P[Static Display Ends] L --> Q[Accessibility Compliance Achieved] ``` ## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Responsive Image Decision Tree ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Browser Loads img Tag] --> B{srcset Attribute Present?} B -- No --> C[Use src Attribute] B -- Yes --> D[Read sizes Attribute] D --> E[Match Current Viewport to Media Query] E --> F[Calculate Effective Display Width] F --> G[Multiply by Device Pixel Ratio] G --> H[Select Smallest Candidate from srcset >= Required Width] H --> I[Fetch and Render Selected Image] C --> I I --> J[Image Displayed Optimally] ``` ## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Img Element Attribute Hierarchy ```mermaid graph TB Img[img Element Void Tag] --> M[Mandatory Attributes] Img --> O[Optional Attributes] Img --> G[Global Attributes] M --> Src[src: Image URL] M --> Alt[alt: Alternative Text] O --> Dim[Dimensions: width, height] O --> Perf[Performance: loading, decoding, fetchpriority] O --> Resp[Responsive: srcset, sizes] O --> Map[Mapping: usemap, ismap] O --> Sec[Security: crossorigin, referrerpolicy] O --> UX[User Experience: title, longdesc] G --> Id[id] G --> Class[class] G --> Style[style] G --> Data[data custom attributes] G --> Aria[aria attributes] ``` ## 4.4 Mermaid Block Diagram: Image Format Selection Logic ```mermaid flowchart TD Start[Need to Embed Image] --> Q1{Is Image a Photograph?} Q1 -- Yes --> Q2{Need Transparency?} Q1 -- No --> Q3{Is Image a Vector or Icon?} Q3 -- Yes --> SVG[Use SVG: image/svg+xml] Q3 -- No --> Q4{Need Animation?} Q4 -- Yes --> WebP[Use WebP or GIF] Q4 -- No --> PNG[Use PNG: image/png] Q2 -- Yes --> WebP2[Use WebP: image/webp] Q2 -- No --> JPEG[Use JPEG: image/jpeg] WebP --> End[Optimal Image Selected] WebP2 --> End GIF[Use GIF: image/gif] --> End PNG --> End JPEG --> End SVG --> End ``` # ­¤ōś SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## ­¤ōØ PART A QUESTIONS (3 Marks Each) ### **Q1. [KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2024 Model Question]** **Define the `` element in HTML. List any two of its mandatory attributes.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The `` element is a **void inline element** in HTML used to embed an external image resource into a web page. It is a *replaced element* ŌĆö the browser replaces the tag with the actual image content fetched from the specified source. The two mandatory attributes are: 1. **`src`** ŌĆö Specifies the URL or file path of the image. 2. **`alt`** ŌĆö Provides alternative text for accessibility and as fallback when the image fails to load. > Ō£ģ **Mark Distribution (as per KTU 2024 Key):** > - Definition: **1.5 marks** > - Two mandatory attributes with explanation: **1.5 marks** (0.75 each) --- ### **Q2. [KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023 Model Question]** **Explain the purpose of the `alt` attribute. What value should be used for purely decorative images?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The `alt` attribute provides **alternative text** for an image. Its purposes are: 1. **Accessibility:** Screen readers announce the `alt` text to visually impaired users. 2. **Fallback Display:** When the image fails to load, the browser displays the `alt` text in its place. 3. **SEO:** Search engines use `alt` text to index and rank images. For **purely decorative images** that add no informational value, the `alt` attribute should be set to an **empty string** `alt=""`. This signals screen readers to skip the image, avoiding unnecessary verbosity. > Ō£ģ **Mark Distribution:** > - Two purposes explained: **2 marks** (1 each) > - Decorative image value: **1 mark** --- ## ­¤ōØ PART B QUESTIONS (14 Marks Each ŌĆö Internal Choice Pattern) ### **Question A: [KTU University Exam ŌĆō 2024 Scheme Model]** **(a)** Explain any **five important attributes** of the `` element with examples. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Write HTML code to create a web page that displays a **responsive banner image** using the `srcset` and `sizes` attributes. The image should lazy-load and include proper accessibility attributes. **(7 marks)** --- #### **Model Solution for Question A:** ##### **Part (a) ŌĆö Five Attributes (7 marks)** | # | Attribute | Purpose | Example | |---|-----------|---------|---------| | 1 | `src` | Specifies image source URL | `src="photo.jpg"` | | 2 | `alt` | Alternative text for accessibility | `alt="Mountain view"` | | 3 | `width` & `height` | Sets display dimensions in pixels | `width="600" height="400"` | | 4 | `loading` | Controls lazy/eager loading | `loading="lazy"` | | 5 | `title` | Tooltip text on mouse hover | `title="Click to enlarge"` | | 6 | `srcset` | Multiple image sources for responsive design | `srcset="img1.jpg 480w, img2.jpg 1024w"` | | 7 | `sizes` | Layout slot for responsive images | `sizes="(max-width: 600px) 100vw, 50vw"` | **Detailed Explanation:** **1. `src` Attribute (1.4 marks):** The `src` attribute is the most critical attribute of the `` element. It tells the browser the location of the image file. Values can be: - *Relative path:* `src="images/logo.png"` - *Absolute URL:* `src="https://example.com/banner.jpg"` - *Data URI:* `src="data:image/png;base64,..."` **2. `alt` Attribute (1.4 marks):** Provides textual replacement. Essential for: - WCAG 2.1 accessibility compliance. - Image search engine optimization (Google Images). - Fallback when images fail to load due to network errors. **3. `width` and `height` Attributes (1.4 marks):** Reserve space in the layout to prevent **Cumulative Layout Shift (CLS)**. Example: `width="800" height="600"`. Note: setting only one dimension preserves the aspect ratio. **4. `loading` Attribute (1.4 marks):** Values are `"lazy"` (deferred until near viewport) or `"eager"` (loads immediately). Recommended for below-the-fold images. **5. `srcset` and `sizes` Attributes (1.4 marks):** Enables the browser to select the most appropriate image resolution based on the device's screen size and pixel density, optimizing bandwidth and visual quality. ##### **Part (b) ŌĆö Responsive Banner HTML Code (7 marks)** ```html Responsive Banner Demo

Web Designing ŌĆö Responsive Image Demo

``` **Code Explanation (for marks):** - **`srcset` (2 marks):** Provides three image resolutions with their intrinsic widths (480w, 800w, 1200w). - **`sizes` (2 marks):** Defines layout widths: full width on mobile, 80% on tablet, capped at 1200px on desktop. - **`alt`, `width`, `height`, `loading`, `decoding` (2 marks):** Ensures accessibility, layout stability, and performance. - **`title` (1 mark):** Adds hover tooltip for better UX. --- ### **Question B: [Internal Choice Alternative ŌĆö KTU Pattern]** **(a)** Discuss the **different image formats** supported in HTML (`JPEG`, `PNG`, `GIF`, `WebP`, `SVG`) with their appropriate use cases. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Explain the concept of an **image map** in HTML. Write the complete HTML code to demonstrate a clickable world map with at least three clickable regions (continents) using `` and ``. **(7 marks)** --- #### **Model Solution for Question B:** ##### **Part (a) ŌĆö Image Formats (7 marks)** | Format | MIME Type | Use Case | Key Feature | |--------|-----------|----------|-------------| | **JPEG** | `image/jpeg` | Photographs, complex images | Lossy compression, small file size | | **PNG** | `image/png` | Logos, transparency-required images | Lossless, supports alpha channel | | **GIF** | `image/gif` | Simple animations, low-color graphics | Supports animation, 256-color limit | | **WebP** | `image/webp` | Modern web (replacement for JPEG/PNG) | Superior compression, supports animation | | **SVG** | `image/svg+xml` | Icons, logos, scalable graphics | Vector-based, infinitely scalable | **Detailed Comparison (for marks):** - **JPEG (1.4 marks):** Ideal for photographs. Uses lossy compression, achieving 10:1 compression ratios. Does NOT support transparency. Quality controlled via the quality parameter (0ŌĆō100). - **PNG (1.4 marks):** Ideal for images requiring sharp edges and transparency. Lossless compression preserves exact pixel data. Supports 8-bit and 24-bit transparency (alpha channel). - **GIF (1.4 marks):** Limited to 256 colors but supports basic animation. Used for simple animated icons and low-color graphics. Now largely replaced by WebP for animation. - **WebP (1.4 marks):** Developed by Google. Provides both lossy and lossless compression, typically 25ŌĆō35% smaller than JPEG/PNG at equivalent quality. Supports transparency and animation. - **SVG (1.4 marks):** XML-based vector format. Resolution-independent ŌĆö scales infinitely without quality loss. Excellent for logos, icons, and data visualizations. ##### **Part (b) ŌĆö Image Map Code (7 marks)** **Concept Explanation (2 marks):** An image map is a technique where different regions of a single image are made clickable, each linking to a different destination. It is implemented using: - The `usemap` attribute on the `` tag. - A `` element containing one or more `` elements that define the clickable regions. **HTML Code (5 marks):** ```html World Map Image Map Demo

Clickable World Map ŌĆö KTU Image Map Demo

Click on any continent to learn more.

``` **Code Walkthrough (for marks):** - **`usemap="#continents"` (1 mark):** Links the image to the map named `continents`. - **`` (1 mark):** Container for clickable areas. - **Three `` elements (2 marks):** Demonstrate `rect`, `circle`, and `poly` shapes with appropriate `coords` and `href`. - **Default fallback area (1 mark):** Catches clicks on non-defined regions. --- ## ŌÜĀ’ĖÅ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes Where KTU Students Lose Marks:** > > 1. **ŌØī Writing a closing `` tag:** The `` element is a **void element**. Writing `` is invalid HTML and will cost 0.5ŌĆō1 mark. > 2. **ŌØī Confusing `src` with `href`:** `src` is for image source, `href` is for hyperlinks. Do not mix them. > 3. **ŌØī Omitting the `alt` attribute:** Even if empty, `alt` must be present. Missing `alt` loses 0.5 mark per occurrence. > 4. **ŌØī Using `width` and `height` with mismatched aspect ratio (without CSS):** While browsers preserve aspect ratio, examiners may deduct marks if distortion logic is incorrectly stated. > 5. **ŌØī Forgetting `usemap="#name"` syntax:** The hash `#` is mandatory ŌĆö `usemap="continents"` (without `#`) is a common error. > 6. **ŌØī Confusing `loading="lazy"` with `decoding="async"`:** These are *different* attributes. `loading` controls *when* to fetch; `decoding` controls *how* to decode. --- ## ­¤Ä» Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > **Rapid Revision Checklist for the Img Element:** - Ō£ģ The `` element is a **void, replaced, inline element** in HTML ŌĆö no closing tag needed. - Ō£ģ **Two mandatory attributes:** `src` (image URL) and `alt` (alternative text). - Ō£ģ **Strongly recommended attributes:** `width` and `height` (prevent layout shift). - Ō£ģ **Performance attributes:** `loading="lazy"`, `decoding="async"`, `fetchpriority`. - Ō£ģ **Responsive attributes:** `srcset` (with `w` descriptors) and `sizes` (with media queries). - Ō£ģ **Image map attributes:** `usemap` (on ``) + `` + `` (with `shape` and `coords`). - Ō£ģ **Format selection rule:** Photograph ŌåÆ JPEG/WebP; Logo ŌåÆ PNG/SVG; Animation ŌåÆ WebP/GIF. - Ō£ģ **Accessibility rule:** Informative image ŌåÆ descriptive `alt`; Decorative image ŌåÆ `alt=""`. - Ō£ģ **Aspect ratio formula:** $\text{AspectRatio} = \dfrac{\text{width}}{\text{height}}$ (e.g., 16:9, 4:3). - Ō£ģ **Coordinate systems:** Origin (0,0) is the **top-left** corner of the image; `x` increases rightward, `y` increases downward. - Ō£ģ **Cumulative Layout Shift (CLS):** Always specify `width` and `height` to reserve layout space ŌĆö a Core Web Vital. - Ō£ģ **Lazy load trigger:** Images load when within ~1.5├Ś viewport height when `loading="lazy"` is set. - Ō£ģ **Common exam trap:** Distinguishing `` (void) from `

topics.title: Adding SVG Elements
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEITT833 - WEB DESIGNING
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Adding SVG Elements

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **SVG (Scalable Vector Graphics)** is an XML-based, W3C-recommended markup language used to describe two-dimensional vector graphics. Unlike raster formats (PNG, JPG), SVG graphics are defined by geometric primitives (shapes, paths, text) and remain infinitely scalable without any loss of resolution.

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme ŌĆō OEITT833 (Web Designing)**, "Adding SVG Elements" refers to the systematic inclusion of inline or embedded `<svg>` containers within an HTML5 document, along with their child elements (e.g., `<rect>`, `<circle>`, `<line>`, `<path>`, `<text>`), to render resolution-independent, interactive, and stylable graphics directly inside a web page.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Analogy: "SVG = Mathematical Blueprint"**

Imagine you are an architect designing a house.

*   A **photograph (PNG/JPG)** of the house is like a raster image ŌĆö if you zoom in enough, the pixels become visible and blurry. It stores information **pixel-by-pixel**.
*   A **blueprint (SVG)** of the house does not store the drawing itself. Instead, it stores **mathematical instructions** like *"draw a line from point (10, 20) to point (50, 80), then a circle of radius 15 at (60, 60)."* If you zoom in on a blueprint, the lines stay perfectly sharp because the printer simply recalculates the lines at the new scale.

SVG is the **blueprint of the web**. When a browser renders an SVG, it receives these mathematical instructions and draws the picture live. That is why SVG logos, icons, and charts look crisp on 4K monitors, mobile screens, and printed pages alike.

## 1.3 Key Characteristics & Standard Metrics

*   **Vector Format**: Defined by math (equations), not pixels.
*   **XML-Based**: Strictly follows XML syntax ŌĆö every tag must be properly closed.
*   **Native Browser Support**: Every modern browser ships with a built-in SVG renderer. **No plugin or external library** is required.
*   **Resolution Independence**: Renders identically at any zoom level.
*   **DOM-Addressable**: Inline SVG elements are part of the Document Object Model, meaning they can be manipulated using JavaScript and styled using CSS just like regular HTML elements.
*   **File Size for Simple Graphics**: Often **smaller** than equivalent raster images for icons, logos, and diagrams.
*   **Two Coordinate Axes**: `x` (horizontal, left-to-right) and `y` (vertical, top-to-bottom). The origin `(0, 0)` is the **top-left corner** of the SVG canvas.

> [!TIP]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** For KTU exams, focus primarily on **inline SVG** (SVG declared directly inside the HTML file), not external `.svg` files. The Module 1 outcomes stress client-side vector rendering.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 The SVG Document Structure

Every SVG illustration begins with a root `<svg>` container. Think of it as a **canvas** that defines the drawing area.

### The Operational Workflow

1.  **Declare the Canvas**: Open an `<svg>` tag and define its `width`, `height`, and the internal coordinate system using the `viewBox` attribute.
2.  **Insert Shape Elements**: Add child elements such as `<rect>`, `<circle>`, `<line>`, `<ellipse>`, `<polyline>`, `<polygon>`, and `<path>` inside the canvas.
3.  **Apply Presentation Attributes**: Modify each element using attributes like `fill`, `stroke`, `stroke-width`, `opacity`, and `transform`.
4.  **Close the Canvas**: End with the `</svg>` tag.
5.  **Embed in HTML**: Place the entire `<svg>` block inside the `<body>` of your HTML5 document.

## 2.2 The `viewBox` Attribute ŌĆö The Most Important Concept

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition:** The `viewBox` attribute defines the position and dimension, in user space, of an SVG viewport. It takes four space-separated values: `min-x min-y width height`.

*   `min-x`, `min-y`: The starting point of the internal coordinate system.
*   `width`, `height`: The internal width and height of the SVG canvas.

**Why is this critical?**
Without a `viewBox`, your SVG uses a 1:1 pixel mapping. With a `viewBox`, you create a **virtual coordinate system** that can be scaled, stretched, and responsively resized by the browser.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet ŌĆö Core SVG Elements

| Element | Purpose | Key Attributes | KTU Memory Hook |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| `<svg>` | Root container / canvas | `width`, `height`, `viewBox` | "The canvas on the easel" |
| `<rect>` | Draws a rectangle | `x`, `y`, `width`, `height`, `rx`, `ry`, `fill`, `stroke` | "Box, TV, building" |
| `<circle>` | Draws a circle | `cx`, `cy`, `r`, `fill`, `stroke` | "Wheel, coin, dot" |
| `<ellipse>` | Draws an oval | `cx`, `cy`, `rx`, `ry`, `fill` | "Stretched circle, egg" |
| `<line>` | Draws a straight line | `x1`, `y1`, `x2`, `y2`, `stroke` | "Ruler, divider" |
| `<polyline>` | Connected open polyline | `points`, `stroke`, `fill` | "Mountain ridge (open)" |
| `<polygon>` | Closed shape with straight sides | `points`, `fill`, `stroke` | "Triangle, pentagon (closed)" |
| `<path>` | Complex freeform shape | `d` (path data), `fill`, `stroke` | "The artist's pen" |
| `<text>` | Renders text inside SVG | `x`, `y`, `font-size`, `fill` | "Labeled diagram" |
| `<g>` | Groups multiple elements | `transform`, `fill` | "A folder of shapes" |

## 2.4 Common Presentation Attributes

| Attribute | What It Controls | Example Values |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| `fill` | Interior color of a shape | `"red"`, `"#FF0000"`, `"none"`, `"rgb(255,0,0)"` |
| `stroke` | Outline / border color | `"black"`, `"#000000"` |
| `stroke-width` | Thickness of the outline | `"2"`, `"0.5"`, `"10"` |
| `opacity` | Transparency of the element (0.0 to 1.0) | `"0.5"` |
| `fill-opacity` | Transparency of fill only | `"0.8"` |
| `stroke-opacity` | Transparency of stroke only | `"0.3"` |
| `transform` | Geometric transformations | `"rotate(45)"`, `"translate(50,100)"`, `"scale(2)"` |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

*   **Responsive Logos**: Tech companies (Google, GitHub, Mozilla) ship logos as inline SVG so they render crisply on every device.
*   **Data Visualization**: Libraries like D3.js generate charts using SVG paths.
*   **Interactive Maps**: Zoomable geographic maps (e.g., OpenStreetMap) use SVG layers.
*   **Icon Systems**: Font Awesome alternatives often use inline SVG sprites.
*   **Print-Quality Web Graphics**: SVG prints at any DPI without loss ŌĆö critical for engineering schematics and architectural drawings.

## 2.6 Path Data ŌĆö The `d` Attribute

> [!IMPORTANT]
> While basic shapes are easy, the `<path>` element is the **most powerful** SVG element. Its `d` attribute contains a mini-language of commands.

| Command | Meaning | Example |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| `M x y` | Move pen to (x, y) ŌĆö no drawing | `M 10 10` |
| `L x y` | Draw line to (x, y) | `L 50 50` |
| `H x` | Horizontal line to x | `H 100` |
| `V y` | Vertical line to y | `V 200` |
| `Z` | Close the current path | `Z` |
| `C x1 y1 x2 y2 x y` | Cubic Bezier curve | `C 10 20, 30 40, 50 60` |
| `A rx ry x-axis-rotation large-arc sweep x y` | Arc (elliptical curve) | `A 5 5 0 0 1 10 10` |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> This section walks through the construction of an SVG figure step by step, explaining the coordinate math and attribute logic behind every line of code. Each block below corresponds to a separate, fully-explained code segment that a student can compile and test.

## 3.1 Building a Basic SVG Canvas

**Goal:** Declare an SVG canvas and draw a simple blue rectangle.

```html
<!DOCTYPE html>
<html>
<head>
    <title>SVG Demo</title>
</head>
<body>
    <svg width="300" height="200" style="border: 1px solid black;">
        <rect x="20" y="20" width="100" height="60" fill="blue" />
    </svg>
</body>
</html>
```

**Step-by-step logic:**

*   `<svg width="300" height="200">` ŌåÆ The canvas is **300 user units wide** and **200 user units tall**.
*   `style="border: 1px solid black;"` ŌåÆ Adds a visible border *around* the canvas (purely for visual reference, not part of SVG itself).
*   `<rect x="20" y="20" ...>` ŌåÆ The rectangle's top-left corner is placed at coordinate **(20, 20)**. The origin `(0, 0)` is the top-left of the canvas.
*   `width="100" height="60"` ŌåÆ The rectangle extends **100 units to the right** and **60 units down**.
*   `fill="blue"` ŌåÆ The interior of the rectangle is filled with the color **blue**.

## 3.2 Working Out the `viewBox` Math

**Problem:** You want an SVG to logically measure **100 ├Ś 100 units**, but render visually at **400 ├Ś 400 pixels** on screen.

**Solution:** Use the `viewBox` attribute.

```html
<svg width="400" height="400" viewBox="0 0 100 100">
    <circle cx="50" cy="50" r="40" fill="green" />
</svg>
```

**Coordinate derivation:**

1.  The browser sees a physical canvas of `400 ├Ś 400` pixels.
2.  The `viewBox="0 0 100 100"` instructs the browser: *"Treat this 400-pixel box as if it is a 100-unit-wide, 100-unit-tall coordinate plane."*
3.  The internal coordinate system starts at `(0, 0)` and ends at `(100, 100)`.
4.  Therefore, the scaling factor is `400 / 100 = 4` pixels per internal unit.
5.  A circle at `cx="50" cy="50"` (the center of the 100├Ś100 system) is rendered at the **physical center of the 400├Ś400 box**.
6.  A radius of `r="40"` means 40 internal units, which becomes `40 ├Ś 4 = 160` physical pixels.

> [!TIP]
> **Why this matters for KTU:** A 2-mark question often asks: *"What is the role of the `viewBox` attribute?"* The answer is that it **establishes a virtual coordinate system** that decouples the logical drawing size from the physical display size, enabling responsive scaling.

## 3.3 Drawing Multi-Point Shapes with `<polygon>`

**Goal:** Draw a yellow star (simplified 5-pointed shape).

```html
<svg width="200" height="200">
    <polygon points="100,10 40,180 190,60 10,60 160,180"
             fill="yellow" stroke="orange" stroke-width="3" />
</svg>
```

**Coordinate derivation:**

*   The `points` attribute contains **5 (x, y) coordinate pairs**, one for each tip of the star.
*   Each pair is separated by a space; the x and y values inside each pair are separated by a comma.
*   The browser connects these 5 points with straight lines **and automatically closes the shape** back to the first point (this is the difference between `<polygon>` and `<polyline>`).
*   `fill="yellow"` ŌåÆ Interior color.
*   `stroke="orange"` ŌåÆ Outline color.
*   `stroke-width="3"` ŌåÆ Outline thickness of **3 internal units**.

## 3.4 Constructing a Path with Bezier Curves

**Goal:** Draw a smooth curved smile.

```html
<svg width="200" height="200" viewBox="0 0 200 200">
    <path d="M 30 100 Q 100 180 170 100"
          fill="none" stroke="black" stroke-width="4" />
</svg>
```

**Path data breakdown:**

1.  `M 30 100` ŌåÆ **Move** the pen to the starting point `(30, 100)`. No line is drawn yet.
2.  `Q 100 180 170 100` ŌåÆ Draw a **Quadratic Bezier curve**.
    *   Control point: `(100, 180)` ŌĆö the curve is "pulled" toward this point, creating the dip of the smile.
    *   End point: `(170, 100)` ŌĆö where the curve finishes.
3.  `fill="none"` ŌåÆ Crucial! A closed path would fill the area under the curve with color. We use `"none"` so only the curve line is visible.
4.  `stroke="black"` and `stroke-width="4"` ŌåÆ The curve is drawn as a 4-unit-thick black line.

## 3.5 SVG `<text>` Element

**Goal:** Render the string "KTU 2024" inside the SVG canvas.

```html
<svg width="300" height="100" viewBox="0 0 300 100">
    <text x="20" y="60" font-size="40" fill="purple" font-family="Arial">
        KTU 2024
    </text>
</svg>
```

**Attribute logic:**

*   `x="20"` ŌåÆ Text starts **20 units** from the left edge.
*   `y="60"` ŌåÆ The **baseline** of the text is at y = 60 (text is anchored by its baseline, not its top).
*   `font-size="40"` ŌåÆ Character height of **40 units**.
*   `fill="purple"` ŌåÆ Text color.
*   `font-family="Arial"` ŌåÆ Font typeface.

## 3.6 Comprehensive Analytical Matrix ŌĆö SVG Element Resolution

| Element | Coordinate Reference Point | Mathematical Shape | Mandatory vs Optional Attributes |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| `<rect>` | Top-left corner `(x, y)` | Axis-aligned rectangle | `width`, `height` mandatory |
| `<circle>` | Center `(cx, cy)` | Euclidean circle $\pi r^2$ | `r` mandatory |
| `<ellipse>` | Center `(cx, cy)` | Stretched circle | `rx`, `ry` mandatory |
| `<line>` | Two endpoints `(x1, y1)` and `(x2, y2)` | Straight line segment | All four coordinates mandatory |
| `<polygon>` | Multiple vertices | Closed polygon | `points` mandatory |
| `<path>` | Pen starting position `(M)` | Any shape (Bezier, arc, line) | `d` mandatory |
| `<text>` | Baseline anchor `(x, y)` | Glyph rendering | Text content mandatory |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 SVG Document Tree Architecture

The following diagram shows how an SVG document is structured hierarchically. The root `<svg>` element is the canvas, and all shape elements are children of it.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[html document body] --> B[svg root container]
    B --> C[rect rectangle]
    B --> D[circle circle]
    B --> E[line straight line]
    B --> F[polygon closed shape]
    B --> G[path complex path]
    B --> H[text svg text]
    B --> I[g group container]
    I --> J[child shape 1]
    I --> K[child shape 2]
    B --> L[defs reusable definitions]
    L --> M[linear gradient]
    L --> N[pattern]
    B --> O[style css styles]
```

## 4.2 SVG Rendering Pipeline ŌĆö Block-Level Functional Architecture

This block diagram explains how a browser converts the SVG source code into pixels on your screen.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[HTML file with inline svg block] --> B[Browser HTML parser]
    B --> C[XML parser detects svg namespace]
    C --> D[SVG DOM tree construction]
    D --> E[Layout engine computes viewBox mapping]
    E --> F[Rendering engine executes shape primitives]
    F --> G[Apply presentation attributes fill, stroke]
    G --> H[Compositor blends layers]
    H --> I[Final pixels on screen]
    D --> J[JavaScript DOM access]
    J --> K[Dynamic manipulation onClick, onHover]
    K --> F
```

## 4.3 Coordinate System Mapping Diagram

This diagram visualizes how the internal `viewBox` coordinate system maps onto the physical display pixels.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Physical Display 400 x 400 pixels] --> B[viewBox attribute 0 0 100 100]
    B --> C[Internal coordinate space 0 to 100 on both axes]
    C --> D[Scale factor computed as 4 pixels per internal unit]
    D --> E[Shape coordinates multiplied by scale factor]
    E --> F[Final rendered position on screen]
    A2[Element cx 50, cy 50, r 40] --> D
    F --> G[Circle at screen center with radius 160 pixels]
```

## 4.4 SVG Element Selection Decision Flow

Use this decision flow to pick the correct SVG element for any given drawing requirement.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[What do you want to draw?] --> B{Is it text?}
    B -- Yes --> C[Use text element]
    B -- No --> D{Is it a closed box?}
    D -- Yes --> E[Use rect element]
    D -- No --> F{Is it a perfect circle?}
    F -- Yes --> G[Use circle element]
    F -- No --> H{Is it an oval or egg shape?}
    H -- Yes --> I[Use ellipse element]
    H -- No --> J{Is it a single straight line?}
    J -- Yes --> K[Use line element]
    J -- No --> L{Is it a multi-sided closed shape?}
    L -- Yes --> M[Use polygon element]
    L -- No --> N{Is it a complex freeform curve?}
    N -- Yes --> O[Use path element with d attribute]
    N -- No --> P[Use polyline for open multi-line shape]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper 1]`**

**Q:** Define SVG. List any **four** advantages of using SVG over raster image formats for web graphics.

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **SVG (Scalable Vector Graphics)** is an XML-based vector graphics format recommended by the W3C for describing two-dimensional graphics on the web.

**Four advantages of SVG over raster formats:**

1.  **Infinite Scalability:** SVG images scale to any size without loss of quality because they are defined by mathematical equations, not pixels.
2.  **Smaller File Size for Simple Graphics:** For icons, logos, and diagrams, SVG files are typically much smaller than equivalent PNG/JPG images.
3.  **DOM Addressable & Scriptable:** Inline SVG elements are part of the HTML DOM, so they can be styled with CSS and manipulated with JavaScript.
4.  **Resolution Independence:** A single SVG file looks crisp on mobile screens, 4K monitors, and printed pages ŌĆö no need for multiple `@2x` or `@3x` versions.
5.  *Bonus:* Editable with any text editor, and supports animation and interactivity natively.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**`[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Model Paper 2]`**

**Q:** Differentiate between the `<circle>` and `<ellipse>` elements in SVG. Provide a sample code snippet for each.

**Model Answer:**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Difference:** The `<circle>` element uses a single radius `r`, while the `<ellipse>` element uses two radii (`rx` for the horizontal semi-axis and `ry` for the vertical semi-axis).

| Feature | `<circle>` | `<ellipse>` |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Radius definition | Single value `r` | Two values `rx`, `ry` |
| Shape produced | Always a perfect circle | Can be a circle (if `rx = ry`) or an oval |
| Center coordinate | `cx`, `cy` | `cx`, `cy` |
| Number of mandatory attributes | 1 (`r`) | 2 (`rx`, `ry`) |

**Sample Code:**

```html
<svg width="200" height="100">
    <circle cx="50" cy="50" r="40" fill="red" />
    <ellipse cx="150" cy="50" rx="40" ry="20" fill="green" />
</svg>
```

The circle has a uniform radius of 40 in all directions, while the ellipse is stretched horizontally with `rx = 40` and compressed vertically with `ry = 20`.

---

## 5.2 Part B ŌĆö Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)
**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Adapted from Past Paper]`**

**(a)** Explain the `<svg>` root element and the role of the `viewBox` attribute in detail. Illustrate your answer with a complete HTML example that uses `viewBox` to render a centered square. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** With a neat diagram and complete code, explain how to draw a triangle, a circle, and a smiley face using SVG elements. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution for (a):**

The `<svg>` element is the **root container** for any SVG illustration. It defines the canvas on which all shapes are drawn. The two most important attributes of the `<svg>` tag are:

*   `width` and `height`: Define the **physical display size** in pixels (or other CSS units).
*   `viewBox`: Defines the **internal coordinate system** as four space-separated values: `min-x min-y width height`.

**The `viewBox` attribute** decouples the logical drawing space from the physical display space. This means an SVG file with a `viewBox` of `"0 0 100 100"` can be displayed at any physical size (e.g., 200├Ś200, 400├Ś400, or 100% of its parent container) and the shapes inside will scale proportionally.

**Complete HTML Example:**

```html
<!DOCTYPE html>
<html>
<head>
    <title>SVG viewBox Demo</title>
</head>
<body>
    <svg width="300" height="300" viewBox="0 0 100 100"
         style="border: 1px solid black;">
        <rect x="25" y="25" width="50" height="50"
              fill="skyblue" stroke="navy" stroke-width="2" />
    </svg>
</body>
</html>
```

**Explanation of the example:**

*   The physical canvas is **300 ├Ś 300 pixels**.
*   The internal coordinate system is **0 to 100 on both axes** (set by `viewBox`).
*   The square is drawn from `(25, 25)` to `(75, 75)`, making it exactly **50 internal units wide and tall**, which is half of the canvas.
*   The browser computes a scale factor of `300 / 100 = 3` pixels per internal unit.
*   The rendered square on screen measures `50 ├Ś 3 = 150` pixels in both dimensions and is perfectly centered.

**Model Solution for (b):**

```html
<!DOCTYPE html>
<html>
<head>
    <title>SVG Smiley Face</title>
</head>
<body>
    <svg width="300" height="300" viewBox="0 0 200 200">
        <circle cx="100" cy="100" r="90"
                fill="yellow" stroke="black" stroke-width="3" />
        <circle cx="70" cy="80" r="10" fill="black" />
        <circle cx="130" cy="80" r="10" fill="black" />
        <path d="M 60 130 Q 100 170 140 130"
              fill="none" stroke="black" stroke-width="4"
              stroke-linecap="round" />
    </svg>
</body>
</html>
```

*   The **face** is a large yellow circle centered at `(100, 100)` with radius 90.
*   The **left eye** is a small black circle at `(70, 80)`.
*   The **right eye** is a small black circle at `(130, 80)`.
*   The **smile** is a quadratic Bezier curve (`Q` command) starting at `(60, 130)`, curving through control point `(100, 170)`, and ending at `(140, 130)`.
*   `stroke-linecap="round"` gives the smile soft, rounded ends.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) ŌĆö Alternative Choice
**`[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Adapted from Past Paper]`**

**(a)** Explain the `<path>` element in SVG. Describe the meanings of the commands `M`, `L`, `H`, `V`, `Z`, `C`, and `Q` with one example each. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Write complete HTML code to embed an inline SVG that draws: a red rectangle, a blue circle, a green triangle, and a black line. Apply different `stroke-width` and `fill` values to demonstrate styling. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution for (a):**

The `<path>` element is the **most powerful** shape in SVG. It is used to draw arbitrary shapes, including curves and complex outlines. The `d` (data) attribute contains a sequence of commands and coordinates.

| Command | Name | Meaning | Example |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| `M x y` | MoveTo | Lifts the pen and moves to (x, y) without drawing | `M 10 20` |
| `L x y` | LineTo | Draws a straight line from the current point to (x, y) | `L 50 80` |
| `H x` | Horizontal LineTo | Draws a horizontal line to absolute x coordinate | `H 100` |
| `V y` | Vertical LineTo | Draws a vertical line to absolute y coordinate | `V 150` |
| `Z` | ClosePath | Closes the current sub-path by drawing a line back to the starting point | `Z` |
| `C x1 y1 x2 y2 x y` | Cubic Bezier Curve | Draws a smooth curve using two control points | `C 10 20, 30 40, 50 60` |
| `Q x1 y1 x y` | Quadratic Bezier Curve | Draws a smooth curve using a single control point | `Q 50 100, 100 50` |

**Composite Example:** `d="M 10 10 L 90 10 L 90 90 L 10 90 Z"` draws a square from `(10, 10)` to `(90, 90)`.

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

```html
<!DOCTYPE html>
<html>
<head>
    <title>Multi-Shape SVG Demo</title>
</head>
<body>
    <svg width="400" height="300" viewBox="0 0 400 300"
         style="border: 1px solid gray;">

        <rect x="20" y="30" width="120" height="80"
              fill="red" stroke="darkred" stroke-width="3" />

        <circle cx="250" cy="70" r="50"
                fill="blue" stroke="navy" stroke-width="2" />

        <polygon points="200,150 320,150 260,260"
                 fill="green" stroke="darkgreen" stroke-width="4" />

        <line x1="20" y1="280" x2="380" y2="280"
              stroke="black" stroke-width="5" />

    </svg>
</body>
</html>
```

**Code explanation:**

*   **Red rectangle** `<rect>`: top-left at `(20, 30)`, dimensions `120 ├Ś 80`, filled red with a dark red 3-unit border.
*   **Blue circle** `<circle>`: center at `(250, 70)`, radius 50, filled blue with a navy 2-unit border.
*   **Green triangle** `<polygon>`: three vertices given by the `points` attribute, filled green with a dark green 4-unit border.
*   **Black line** `<line>`: horizontal line at y = 280, from x = 20 to x = 380, with a 5-unit black stroke.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning ŌĆö Common Pitfalls:**
> 1.  **Forgetting `</svg>` close tag:** SVG is XML. Unlike HTML5, every tag (including `<rect>`, `<circle>`) **must be properly closed** with `/>` or a closing tag. The examiner will deduct 1 mark for unclosed tags.
> 2.  **Confusing `(cx, cy)` with `(x, y)`:** A circle uses `cx` and `cy` for its **center**, while a rectangle uses `x` and `y` for its **top-left corner**. This is a classic 1-mark trap.
> 3.  **Using `fill` on a line:** The `<line>` element has no interior. Setting `fill="red"` on a line has no effect. You must use `stroke` to color a line.
> 4.  **Forgetting `viewBox` in exam diagrams:** When asked to draw a "responsive" or "scalable" graphic, the examiner expects the use of `viewBox`. Omitting it costs you the full "responsive design" mark.
> 5.  **Wrong text anchor:** In `<text>`, the `y` coordinate refers to the **baseline** of the text, not the top. Beginners often misplace text thinking `y` is the top edge.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist ŌĆö "Adding SVG Elements"**

*   **SVG = XML-based vector graphics**; sharp at any zoom level.
*   **Root tag is `<svg>`** with `width`, `height`, and (optionally but recommended) `viewBox`.
*   **`viewBox="min-x min-y w h"`** creates a virtual coordinate system independent of physical pixels.
*   **Origin `(0, 0)` is the top-left** corner of the canvas. y increases downward.
*   **Basic shapes:** `<rect>`, `<circle>`, `<ellipse>`, `<line>`, `<polyline>`, `<polygon>`, `<path>`, `<text>`.
*   **`<rect>`** uses `x, y, width, height` (top-left corner reference).
*   **`<circle>`** uses `cx, cy, r` (center reference).
*   **`<ellipse>`** uses `cx, cy, rx, ry` (center + two radii).
*   **`<line>`** uses `x1, y1, x2, y2` (two endpoints); needs `stroke`, not `fill`.
*   **`<polygon>`** auto-closes the shape; `<polyline>` does not.
*   **`<path>`** with `d` attribute is the most flexible ŌĆö supports `M`, `L`, `H`, `V`, `Z`, `C`, `Q`, `A` commands.
*   **`<text>`** is anchored by the **baseline** at coordinate `(x, y)`.
*   **Presentation attributes:** `fill` (interior), `stroke` (outline), `stroke-width` (thickness), `opacity` (transparency).
*   **XML rule:** Every SVG element must be **properly closed** with `/>` or a closing tag.
*   **Inline SVG** is part of the DOM ŌåÆ styleable with CSS, scriptable with JavaScript.
*   **Use `"none"`** for `fill` when you only want the outline of a shape visible.
*   **Grouping** with `<g>` allows shared `transform` and `fill` for multiple child shapes.
*   **KTU 2024 focus:** Inline embedding, basic shapes, `viewBox` understanding, and writing complete HTML+SVG code from scratch.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Responsive Image Markup, Table
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEITT833 - WEB DESIGNING > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Responsive Image Markup, Table # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Responsive Image Markup ŌĆö Formal Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Responsive Image Markup** is the use of HTML elements and attributes (``, `srcset`, `sizes`, ``) that allow a web browser to **automatically select and load the most appropriate image file** based on the user's device characteristics ŌĆö primarily **viewport width**, **device pixel ratio (DPR)**, and **art direction** requirements ŌĆö without serving the same oversized image to every visitor. The Web Vitals metric that this directly influences is the **Largest Contentful Paint (LCP)**. The KTU 2024 syllabus (Module 1) treats this as a *core sub-topic* under "HTML5 ŌĆö Multimedia & Document Structure." > [!NOTE] > **Key KTU Definition You Must Memorise:** > *Responsive images are images that adapt fluidly to the rendering surface of any device, optimising both visual fidelity and network payload using a combination of the `srcset`, `sizes`, and `` markup patterns.* ### Conceptual Analogy ŌĆö The Restaurant Menu Imagine you walk into a restaurant. Instead of being forced to order one **fixed-size plate** (say, a 4000-calorie platter), the waiter hands you a **smart menu**: - If you are a **light eater** (small phone, slow 3G), the kitchen sends a **small, well-presented dish** (low-resolution, compressed file). - If you are a **big eater** with a **sharp palate** (Retina laptop on Wi-Fi), the kitchen sends a **large, gourmet version** (high-DPR, high-resolution file). - If you order a **different cuisine altogether** (landscape vs portrait composition), the menu offers a **completely different dish** (art-directed crop). The **smart menu** is the **responsive image markup**, and the **kitchen** is the **browser**. The `` element is the menu itself; `srcset` is the list of dish sizes; `sizes` is your instruction to the waiter about your appetite. > [!TIP] > The phrase **"resolution switching"** is what the examiner expects when `srcset` is used. The phrase **"art direction"** is what the examiner expects when `` is used. Memorise these two phrases. --- ## 1.2 HTML Tables ŌĆö Formal Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > An **HTML Table** is a structured grid of data arranged in **rows** (``) and **columns** (formed implicitly by `` / `` cells), semantically grouped using ``, ``, and ``, and made *responsive* through CSS strategies such as `overflow-x: auto`, `table-layout: fixed`, or card-based transformations for narrow viewports. > [!NOTE] > **Key KTU Definition You Must Memorise:** > *Tables in HTML5 are intended **only for tabular data** (schedules, marks lists, product catalogues). They are **NOT** to be used for page layout ŌĆö that role is reserved for CSS Grid and Flexbox.* ### Conceptual Analogy ŌĆö The Class Register Picture a physical **school attendance register**: - The **top strip** lists the column headings: *Roll No, Name, Attendance, Marks*. ŌåÆ This is the **``**. - The **bottom strip** has a summary row: *Total Students: 60, Average: 78%*. ŌåÆ This is the **``**. - The **main body** holds individual student rows. ŌåÆ This is the **``**. - Each cell is filled with data ŌåÆ These are **``** (table data) cells. - Column headers (Roll No, Name) are **bold and centred** ŌåÆ These are **``** (table header) cells. If a teacher wants a *single studentŌĆÖs marks to span two columns* (like "Science" combining "Theory" + "Practical"), they use **`colspan`**. If a single label needs to *span two rows* (like a grouped category), they use **`rowspan`**. > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistake:** Using `` for layout (e.g., a 3-column page with a header, sidebar, content). This is a **2-mark penalty trap** in KTU valuations. Always use CSS Grid/Flexbox for layout; use tables **only** for tabular data. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Three Pillars of Responsive Image Markup The HTML5 specification offers **three coordinated techniques**. KTU expects you to know *when* to use which. ### Pillar 1 ŌĆö The Simple `` Tag (Baseline) ```html ``` - **What it does:** Loads a single, fixed image. - **When to use:** Logos, icons, small decorative graphics that do not require resolution switching. - **Limitation:** Sends the same file to a 4-inch phone and a 27-inch 4K monitor. **Wastes bandwidth** on small devices. ### Pillar 2 ŌĆö Resolution Switching with `srcset` + `sizes` (Same Image, Different Sizes) The browser picks the **best file** from a list based on device pixel ratio and viewport width. **How it works internally (decision logic):** 1. Browser reads the **`sizes`** attribute ŌåÆ it now knows the *rendered display width* of the image on the page (e.g., `100vw`, `50vw`, or a fixed `300px`). 2. Browser reads the **`srcset`** attribute ŌåÆ it sees a list of candidate image files with their **intrinsic widths** (e.g., `480w`, `960w`, `1920w`) or **pixel-density descriptors** (e.g., `1x`, `2x`). 3. Browser consults the **device pixel ratio (DPR)** and **viewport width (vw)** to compute the **effective required resolution**. 4. Browser **selects the smallest file** from `srcset` that is still large enough to avoid visible pixelation. ### Pillar 3 ŌĆö Art Direction with `` (Different Image, Different Crop) The `` element provides **multiple `` elements** that the browser can choose between based on **`media` queries**. The actual `` is the mandatory fallback. - **When to use:** When you need a *different composition* (e.g., a tightly-cropped portrait on mobile, a wide landscape on desktop). > [!TIP] > **Examiner's Heuristic:** If the question says *"same image, different resolution"* ŌåÆ answer with `srcset`/`sizes`. If it says *"different image at different breakpoints"* ŌåÆ answer with `` and `media` queries. **Mixing them up is the #1 reason students lose 3ŌĆō4 marks.** --- ## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Attribute Cheat Sheet ### Responsive Image Attributes | Attribute | Belongs To | Purpose | Possible Values / Format | |---|---|---|---| | `src` | `` | Mandatory fallback image URL | `"image.jpg"` | | `alt` | `` | Accessibility text (required) | `"Description of image"` | | `srcset` | ``, `` | Lists candidate images | `"small.jpg 480w, large.jpg 1920w"` | | `sizes` | `` | Tells browser the rendered width | `"(max-width:600px) 100vw, 50vw"` | | `media` | `` | CSS media query condition | `"(min-width:800px)"` | | `type` | `` | MIME type hint for format negotiation | `"image/webp"`, `"image/avif"` | | `loading` | `` | Lazy-load hint | `"lazy"`, `"eager"` | | `decoding` | `` | Decode timing hint | `"async"`, `"sync"`, `"auto"` | | `width`, `height` | `` | Aspect-ratio reservation (CLS) | Integers (pixels) | | `srcset` density | `` | Pixel-density descriptor (legacy) | `"image.jpg 1x, image@2x.jpg 2x"` | ### Table Elements & Attributes | Element / Attribute | Role | Mandatory? | Example | |---|---|---|---| | `
` | Root container | Yes | `
` | | `` | Header row group | Best practice | Wraps header `` | | `` | Body row group | Best practice | Wraps data ``s | | `` | Footer row group | Optional | Totals / footnotes | | `` | Table row | Yes (inside a group) | `...` | | `` | `` | | `` | | `colspan` | Cell spans N columns | Optional | `colspan="3"` | | `rowspan` | Cell spans N rows | Optional | `rowspan="2"` | | `scope` | Accessibility binding | Best practice | `scope="col"`, `scope="row"` | | `` | | `` / `` | Column styling | Optional | `` | ### Responsive Table CSS Strategies (Cheat Sheet) | Strategy | CSS Snippet | Pros | Cons | |---|---|---|---| | Horizontal scroll | `table { display: block; overflow-x: auto; }` | Simple, preserves structure | Awkward UX on phones | | Fixed layout | `table { table-layout: fixed; }` | Predictable column widths | Long text may overflow | | Stack-as-cards (mobile) | `td { display: block; }` + `::before` labels | Phone-friendly | More CSS code | | Hide columns | `tr td:nth-child(3) { display: none; }` | Reduces clutter | Data loss | --- ## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility 1. **E-Commerce Platforms (Amazon, Flipkart):** A product image is delivered at 1x, 2x, and 3x DPR. On a poor 3G connection, the user gets a 320 ├Ś 320 JPEG; on a 5G iPhone, they get a 1280 ├Ś 1280 WebP. This is `srcset` in production. 2. **News Websites (BBC, The Hindu):** A hero photograph uses `` with a tightly cropped portrait version for mobile and a wide panoramic version for desktop. This is art direction. 3. **University Result Portals:** Marks lists are rendered as `
` | Header cell | Inside `
Name` | Data cell | Yes | `Anu
` | Table title/caption | Best practice | `Marks Report
` with `` (column titles), `` (roll-numbered rows), and a `` containing the class average. A horizontal `overflow-x: auto` is applied so the table scrolls on narrow screens. 4. **Performance Budgets:** Google's Core Web Vitals *reward* sites that use responsive images with a higher LCP score, which directly impacts SEO ranking. # 3. Step-by-Step Construction & Analytical Matrix Because this is a **Web Designing** module, "derivations" are replaced with **progressive code construction** ŌĆö building responsive solutions step-by-step with full justification for every line. **No shortcuts. Every line explained.** ## 3.1 Building a Resolution-Switched `` (Full Logical Walk-through) ### Final Code ```html ``` ### Step-by-Step Logical Walk-through **Step 1 ŌĆö Establish a fallback with `src`.** The browser **always needs** a working `src` URL. If `srcset` parsing fails (very old browsers) or no candidate matches, the browser falls back to this. We pick `fallback-960.jpg` (960 px wide) as a reasonable mid-size file ŌĆö neither too heavy nor too pixelated. **Step 2 ŌĆö Provide the alternative text with `alt`.** The `alt` attribute is **mandatory for accessibility** (WCAG 2.1, KTU syllabus highlight). Screen readers read this aloud. If the image is purely decorative, use `alt=""` (empty). Here it is meaningful, so we write a real description. **Step 3 ŌĆö Reserve layout space with `width` and `height`.** Setting `width="960"` and `height="540"` establishes an **aspect ratio of 16:9**. The browser reserves the box *before* the image bytes arrive, eliminating **Cumulative Layout Shift (CLS)** ŌĆö a Core Web Vitals metric. **Step 4 ŌĆö Define candidates with `srcset`.** ```text small-480.jpg 480w, medium-960.jpg 960w, large-1920.jpg 1920w, xlarge-3840.jpg 3840w ``` The trailing descriptors `480w`, `960w`, etc. are called **width descriptors**. They tell the browser the **intrinsic pixel width** of each file. The browser will pick the file whose intrinsic width is closest to (but not less than) the **effective resolution** it needs. **Step 5 ŌĆö Declare the rendered display width with `sizes`.** ```text (max-width: 600px) 100vw, (max-width: 1024px) 80vw, 1200px ``` This is a **comma-separated list of (media-condition, length) pairs**. The browser reads top-to-bottom and uses the **first matching condition**: - If the viewport is Ōēż 600 px wide ŌåÆ the image occupies 100% of viewport width (`100vw`). - Else if the viewport is Ōēż 1024 px wide ŌåÆ the image occupies 80% of viewport width. - Otherwise (desktop, > 1024 px) ŌåÆ the image is capped at `1200px` (the CSS pixel size). **Step 6 ŌĆö Calculate the effective resolution the browser needs.** The browser multiplies the chosen `sizes` value by the device pixel ratio. $$ E_{\text{px}} \;=\; L_{\text{slot}} \times \text{DPR} $$ where $E_{\text{px}}$ is the **effective pixel width** the browser wants to download, $L_{\text{slot}}$ is the CSS pixel slot from the matched `sizes` entry, and $\text{DPR}$ is the device pixel ratio. **Numerical example 1 ŌĆö Smartphone on 3G:** - Viewport = 375 CSS px - Matched condition: `(max-width: 600px) 100vw` ŌåÆ $L_{\text{slot}} = 375$ CSS px - DPR = 2 (typical iPhone) - Required: $E_{\text{px}} = 375 \times 2 = 750$ device pixels - Browser picks: `medium-960.jpg` (960w Ōēź 750, the smallest file meeting the threshold) **Numerical example 2 ŌĆö Desktop 4K monitor:** - Viewport = 1920 CSS px - Matched condition: default ŌåÆ $L_{\text{slot}} = 1200$ CSS px - DPR = 2 (HiDPI 4K display) - Required: $E_{\text{px}} = 1200 \times 2 = 2400$ device pixels - Browser picks: `xlarge-3840.jpg` (3840w Ōēź 2400, and the file is large enough to look sharp) **Step 7 ŌĆö Add performance hints `loading` and `decoding`.** `loading="lazy"` defers the image load until it is near the viewport ŌĆö saves bandwidth for below-the-fold images. `decoding="async"` lets the browser decode the image on a separate thread, preventing main-thread jank. --- ## 3.2 Building an Art-Directed `` Block ### Final Code ```html ``` ### Step-by-Step Logical Walk-through **Step 1 ŌĆö Open ``.** This container is *invisible* ŌĆö it does not render anything by itself. Its only job is to provide a list of `` candidates. **Step 2 ŌĆö List `` elements top-to-bottom.** The browser walks the `` list **in source order** and picks the **first one whose `media` query matches AND whose `type` is supported**. This is why **WebP comes first** (modern, smaller) and JPEG comes after (universal fallback). **Step 3 ŌĆö Provide a mandatory `` fallback.** The `` is **not optional**. It is the fallback that the browser uses if no `` matches. KTU examiners will deduct marks if `` is missing. **Step 4 ŌĆö Match the `media` queries to your breakpoints.** - `>= 1200 px` ŌåÆ wide desktop hero (landscape crop) - `600 px ŌĆō 1199 px` ŌåÆ tablet hero (square-ish crop) - `< 600 px` ŌåÆ mobile hero (portrait, tightly cropped face) **Key insight:** The three image files are **different photographs** (or different crops), not just different resolutions. This is the definition of **art direction**. --- ## 3.3 Building a Semantic, Accessible, Responsive HTML Table ### Final Code ```html
Semester Results ŌĆö B.Tech CSE, 2024 Admission Batch
Roll No Student Name Web Tech Data Structures Maths Total
S001 Anu Krishnan 92 88 76 256
S002 Rahul Menon 85 91 (combined paper) 74 250
Class Average 253
``` ### Step-by-Step Logical Walk-through **Step 1 ŌĆö Wrap the table in a scrollable container.** ```html
``` with CSS: ```css .table-wrapper { overflow-x: auto; } ``` This is the **simplest responsive strategy**: on narrow screens, the table scrolls horizontally *within* its container; the page itself does not scroll sideways. **Step 2 ŌĆö Add a ``.** The `` is the **tableŌĆÖs title**. It is read aloud by screen readers and is also visible above the table. *KTU 2024 syllabus explicitly lists `` as a required element for accessibility.* **Step 3 ŌĆö Define column groups with ``.** `` lets you set widths and styles for **entire columns at once**, which is faster than styling each ``. The `style="width:10%"` declarations sum to 100%, enforcing proportional widths. **Step 4 ŌĆö Use semantic sectioning with ``, ``, ``.** | Section | Contains | Rendered Order | |---|---|---| | `` | Column headers (``) | Always at the top | | `` | Data rows | Middle | | `` | Summary / totals | Always at the bottom ŌĆö **even if placed last in HTML** | **Step 5 ŌĆö Use `` and `` for accessibility.** The `scope` attribute binds a header cell to all cells in its column or row. Screen readers use this to announce "Web Tech: 92" rather than just "92." **Step 6 ŌĆö Use `colspan` and `rowspan` for cell merging.** - `colspan="2"` on RahulŌĆÖs DSA+Maths cell ŌåÆ that cell spans **two columns**. - `colspan="5"` on the "Class Average" cell ŌåÆ that cell spans the **first five columns**, with the average value sitting in the sixth. ### Responsive Table ŌĆö Card Stacking for Mobile (Advanced) For true mobile-first design, transform the table into stacked cards below 600 px: ```css @media (max-width: 600px) { table, thead, tbody, tfoot, tr, th, td { display: block; } thead { display: none; } /* hide column headers on mobile */ td::before { content: attr(data-label) ": "; font-weight: bold; } } ``` ```html 92 88 ``` On mobile, each row becomes a "card," and `::before` pseudo-elements inject the column label so the user still knows what each value means. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Decision Flow ŌĆö How the Browser Selects a Responsive Image ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Browser encounters img tag] --> B{Does srcset exist?} B -- No --> C[Load src attribute only] B -- Yes --> D{Does sizes exist?} D -- No --> E[Assume 100vw display width] D -- Yes --> F[Evaluate sizes media queries] E --> G[Compute effective px = slot x DPR] F --> G G --> H[Pick smallest srcset candidate that meets px threshold] H --> I[Download and render that file] C --> I style A fill:#e3f2fd,stroke:#0d47a1,color:#000 style G fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17,color:#000 style H fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20,color:#000 style I fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#bf360c,color:#000 ``` ## 4.2 Block Diagram ŌĆö `` Art Direction Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TD P[picture element] --> S1[source 1 webp wide desktop] P --> S2[source 2 jpeg wide desktop] P --> S3[source 3 webp tablet] P --> S4[source 4 jpeg tablet] P --> S5[source 5 webp mobile] P --> IF[img fallback mobile jpeg] S1 --> M1{Media matches?} S2 --> M2{Media matches?} S3 --> M3{Media matches?} S4 --> M4{Media matches?} S5 --> M5{Media matches?} M1 -- Yes --> WIN1[Browser loads source 1] M1 -- No --> S2 M2 -- Yes --> WIN2[Browser loads source 2] M2 -- No --> S3 M3 -- Yes --> WIN3[Browser loads source 3] M3 -- No --> S4 M4 -- Yes --> WIN4[Browser loads source 4] M4 -- No --> S5 M5 -- Yes --> WIN5[Browser loads source 5] M5 -- No --> WINFALLBACK[Browser loads img fallback] style P fill:#e1f5fe,stroke:#01579b,color:#000 style IF fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17,color:#000 style WINFALLBACK fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#bf360c,color:#000 ``` ##
