4.3 Table DOM Tree ŌĆö Semantic Hierarchy ```mermaid flowchart TD T[table] --> CAP[caption: Semester Results] T --> CG[colgroup: column widths 10 25 15 15 15 20 percent] T --> HEAD[thead: header row group] T --> BODY[tbody: data row group] T --> FOOT[tfoot: footer row group] HEAD --> TRH[tr: header row] TRH --> TH1[th scope col: Roll No] TRH --> TH2[th scope col: Name] TRH --> TH3[th scope col: Web Tech] TRH --> TH4[th scope col: DS] TRH --> TH5[th scope col: Maths] TRH --> TH6[th scope col: Total] BODY --> TR1[tr: student S001] TR1 --> RH1[th scope row: S001] TR1 --> TD1[td: Anu] TR1 --> TD2[td: 92] TR1 --> TD3[td: 88] TR1 --> TD4[td: 76] TR1 --> TD5[td: 256] FOOT --> TRF[tr: class average] TRF --> TDF1[td colspan 5: Class Average] TRF --> TDF2[td: 253] style T fill:#bbdefb,stroke:#0d47a1,color:#000 style HEAD fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20,color:#000 style BODY fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17,color:#000 style FOOT fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#bf360c,color:#000 ``` ## 4.4 Comparison Matrix ŌĆö `srcset` vs `` vs Plain `` ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph PILLAR_A[Plain img] A1[Single file] A2[No adaptation] A3[Smallest payload overhead] end subgraph PILLAR_B[srcset plus sizes] B1[Same image different sizes] B2[Resolution switching] B3[Browser auto selects] end subgraph PILLAR_C[picture element] C1[Different images possible] C2[Art direction supported] C3[Media query driven] end PILLAR_A --> USE_A[Use for icons logos] PILLAR_B --> USE_B[Use for hero photos product images] PILLAR_C --> USE_C[Use for marketing banners art directed crops] style PILLAR_A fill:#e0e0e0,stroke:#212121,color:#000 style PILLAR_B fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20,color:#000 style PILLAR_C fill:#bbdefb,stroke:#0d47a1,color:#000 ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## ­¤ōś PART A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Differentiate between `srcset` and the `` element in HTML5. When would you prefer one over the other?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Aspect | `srcset` | `` | |---|---|---| | **Purpose** | Resolution switching ŌĆö same image, different file sizes | Art direction ŌĆö different image compositions | | **Decision basis** | Device pixel ratio and `sizes` slot width | CSS `media` queries on viewport | | **Output** | Single `` tag | Wrapper containing multiple `` and one `` | | **Preferred when** | You want the browser to choose the sharpest file for the current DPR | You want a different crop/composition per breakpoint | | **Example** | Product catalogue photo | Hero banner that needs a portrait crop on mobile | (3 marks ŌĆö 1.5 marks for the table, 1.5 marks for the example distinction) --- ### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Explain the role of the ``, ``, and `` elements in an HTML table.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** - **``** ŌĆö Provides a visible title/caption for the table. It is the **first child** of `` and is read aloud by screen readers. *Example:* ``. **(1 mark)** - **``** ŌĆö Groups one or more rows of column-header cells (``** ŌĆö Groups one or more rows containing summary data (totals, averages). It is **rendered at the bottom of the table** even if its source appears at the end of the HTML, ensuring semantic summary separation. **(1 mark)** --- ## ­¤ōŚ PART B ŌĆö Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice) > [!NOTE] > KTU 2024 Scheme ESE Part B offers **internal choice** between two questions of 14 marks. Both questions below are fully solved model answers. --- ### ­¤ö╣ Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **(a) [7 marks]** Explain the responsive image techniques in HTML5. Write a complete HTML snippet using `srcset` and `sizes` to deliver three different image resolutions (480w, 960w, 1920w) for a hero banner that occupies 100% of the viewport on mobile and 50% on desktop. **(b) [7 marks]** Design a responsive HTML table to display the marks of 5 students across 4 subjects. The table must include ``, ``, ``, `colspan`, `rowspan`, and a horizontal scroll for narrow screens. Provide the full HTML and the supporting CSS. --- #### Model Solution ŌĆö Q.A(a) [7 marks] **Conceptual explanation (3 marks):** Responsive image techniques ensure the browser downloads the **smallest sufficient** image file. HTML5 offers three tools: 1. **`srcset` attribute** ŌĆö Lists candidate image files with their **intrinsic widths** (`480w`, `960w`, `1920w`). The browser uses the device's effective resolution to choose. 2. **`sizes` attribute** ŌĆö Declares the **CSS-pixel display width** the image will occupy at various breakpoints. The browser multiplies this by the device pixel ratio to find the effective pixel requirement. 3. **`src` attribute** ŌĆö A mandatory fallback URL used by very old browsers that do not understand `srcset`. **Final code (4 marks):** ```html ``` **Walk-through:** - The fallback `src` points to the 960 px file. - `width="1920" height="800"` reserves a 12:5 aspect-ratio box to prevent layout shift. - `srcset` lists three files: 480 px, 960 px, 1920 px. - `sizes` says: below 768 px viewport, render at `100vw`; otherwise, render at `50vw` of the viewport. - On a 375 CSS-px iPhone with DPR = 3, the effective requirement is $375 \times 3 = 1125$ device pixels. Browser picks `hero-1920.jpg` because `960 < 1125 \le 1920$. - On a 1920 CSS-px desktop monitor with DPR = 1, the slot is `50vw = 960` CSS px, so $960 \times 1 = 960$ device pixels ŌåÆ browser picks `hero-960.jpg` (exactly the threshold). --- #### Model Solution ŌĆö Q.A(b) [7 marks] **HTML (5 marks):** ```html Student Marks
Semester Results
`). Content is rendered at the top of the table, and in printing/scrolling, it can be made sticky via CSS. **(1 mark)** - **`
`, `
Semester Marks ŌĆö B.Tech CSE (2024 Batch)
Roll No Name Web Tech Data Structures Mathematics Total
S101 Anu Krishnan 92 88 76 256
S102 Rahul Menon 85 91 (Combined paper) 74 250
S103 Group A
(Toppers)		 95 91 89 275
S104 93 90 87 270
S105 Meera Pillai 80 82 70 232
Class Average 256.6
``` **CSS (2 marks):** ```css .table-scroll { overflow-x: auto; } table { width: 100%; border-collapse: collapse; table-layout: fixed; font-family: Arial, sans-serif; } caption { caption-side: top; font-weight: bold; font-size: 1.1em; padding: 8px; } th, td { border: 1px solid #999; padding: 8px 10px; text-align: center; } thead th { background: #1565c0; color: #fff; } tbody tr:nth-child(even) { background: #f4f6f8; } tfoot td { background: #ffe0b2; font-weight: bold; } ``` **Explanation of key features:** - `colspan="2"` on Rahul's DSA+Maths cell merges two columns into one (1 mark). - `rowspan="2"` on the "Group A" cell makes the label span two rows for S103 and S104 (1 mark). - `` displays the class average at the bottom, regardless of source order (1 mark). - `.table-scroll { overflow-x: auto; }` enables horizontal scrolling on narrow viewports (1 mark). - `table-layout: fixed` enforces the colgroup widths (1 mark). - `caption`, `thead`, `tbody`, `tfoot` semantic grouping (1 mark). - Accessibility via `scope="col"` and `scope="row"` (1 mark). --- ### ­¤ö╣ Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **(a) [7 marks]** What is art direction in responsive web design? Build a `` element that serves a **portrait-cropped** mobile image, a **square** tablet image, and a **landscape** desktop image, each offered in both **WebP** and **JPEG** formats. **(b) [7 marks]** Discuss the various CSS strategies for making an HTML table responsive. Compare horizontal scroll, card stacking, and column hiding with code examples. --- #### Model Solution ŌĆö Q.B(a) [7 marks] **Conceptual explanation (3 marks):** **Art direction** is the practice of using *different image compositions* (not just different file sizes) for different viewports. The browser uses CSS `media` queries inside a `` element to choose the *first* `` whose media condition matches and whose MIME type is supported. The actual image rendering is done by the mandatory `` fallback inside ``. Art direction is preferred when the *subject* of the image must be re-cropped ŌĆö e.g., a group photo where faces must remain visible on small screens. **Final code (4 marks):** ```html ``` **Walk-through:** - The browser tests `` elements **top-to-bottom** (1 mark). - For a 1280 px desktop, the first two match by media; the WebP one wins on type support (1 mark). - For a 768 px tablet, the third/fourth `` match; WebP is picked if supported (1 mark). - For a 375 px mobile, none of the `media` queries match, so the browser falls through to the `` (1 mark). --- #### Model Solution ŌĆö Q.B(b) [7 marks] **Three CSS Strategies (7 marks ŌĆö 2 marks per strategy + 1 mark comparison table):** **Strategy 1 ŌĆö Horizontal Scroll Wrapper (2 marks)** The simplest, most-used approach. Wrap the table in a `
` with `overflow-x: auto`. ```css .table-scroll { overflow-x: auto; } @media (max-width: 600px) { .table-scroll table { min-width: 600px; } } ``` The `min-width: 600px` forces the table to retain its layout integrity, and the wrapper provides a horizontal scrollbar on phones. **Pros:** preserves structure. **Cons:** requires horizontal scrolling. **Strategy 2 ŌĆö Card Stacking (2 marks)** On mobile, reflow the table rows into vertically stacked "cards," with each cell labelled using the `::before` pseudo-element and the `data-label` attribute. ```css @media (max-width: 600px) { table, thead, tbody, tfoot, tr, th, td { display: block; } thead { display: none; } td { padding-left: 50%; position: relative; } td::before { content: attr(data-label); position: absolute; left: 10px; font-weight: bold; } } ``` **Pros:** native mobile UX, no scrolling. **Cons:** requires `data-label` attributes and extra CSS. **Strategy 3 ŌĆö Column Hiding (2 marks)** Hide non-essential columns on small screens using `:nth-child`. ```css @media (max-width: 600px) { td:nth-child(3), th:nth-child(3), td:nth-child(4), th:nth-child(4) { display: none; } } ``` **Pros:** preserves readability. **Cons:** *removes data* ŌĆö only use for truly non-essential columns. **Comparison Table (1 mark):** | Strategy | UX on Mobile | Data Preserved | Complexity | |---|---|---|---| | Horizontal scroll | Acceptable | 100% | Low | | Card stacking | Excellent | 100% | Medium | | Column hiding | Good | Partial | Low | --- > [!WARNING] > **­¤öź KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls ŌĆö Read This Carefully** > 1. **Forgetting `` inside ``** ŌĆö KTU deducts **2 marks** because `` is the *mandatory* fallback. The browser will not render anything without it. > 2. **Confusing `srcset` density descriptors with width descriptors** ŌĆö `image.jpg 2x` is *pixel density*; `image.jpg 480w` is *intrinsic width*. Mixing them up in the same `srcset` is a syntax error and **costs 1 mark**. > 3. **Omitting the `alt` attribute** ŌĆö A bare `` is **inaccessible** and loses 1 mark for failing WCAG/syllabus accessibility criteria. > 4. **Using `` for page layout** ŌĆö A 2-mark penalty. The examiner will specifically check for layout misuse. > 5. **Missing `scope` attribute on `` content at the top** ŌĆö Students sometimes think `` renders at its source position. It always renders at the **bottom** of the table. --- ## ­¤¦Ā Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - Ō£ģ **Resolution switching** is done with `srcset` + `sizes`; the browser selects the file. - Ō£ģ **Art direction** is done with `` + ``; the browser selects a *different image*. - Ō£ģ A `` element **must** contain an `` fallback. - Ō£ģ The `sizes` attribute tells the browser the CSS-pixel display slot; the browser multiplies by DPR to find the effective resolution. - Ō£ģ The effective resolution formula is $E_{\text{px}} = L_{\text{slot}} \times \text{DPR}$. - Ō£ģ Tables are for **tabular data only**, never for page layout. - Ō£ģ Always include ``, ``, `` to semantically group rows. - Ō£ģ Always add `scope="col"` or `scope="row"` to `
`** ŌĆö Loses 1 mark for poor accessibility. > 6. **Placing `
` for table title and accessibility. - Ō£ģ Always use `
` cells. - Ō£ģ `colspan` merges columns; `rowspan` merges rows. - Ō£ģ The simplest responsive table technique is `overflow-x: auto` on a wrapper `
`. - Ō£ģ `loading="lazy"` defers below-the-fold images; `decoding="async"` reduces main-thread jank. - Ō£ģ Setting `width` and `height` on `` prevents **Cumulative Layout Shift (CLS)**. - Ō£ģ Modern formats (WebP, AVIF) are smaller; always offer them with JPEG as a fallback. - Ō£ģ Core Web Vitals ŌĆö LCP and CLS ŌĆö are directly improved by correct responsive image markup.

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEITT833 - WEB DESIGNING > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Forms # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) An **HTML Form** is a structured interactive section of a web document, defined using the `
` element, that is specifically designed to **collect, validate, and submit user-supplied data** to a web server (or to a client-side script) for processing. According to the W3C (World Wide Web Consortium) and WHATWG HTML Living Standard specifications, a form acts as a *container* that groups a collection of interactive **form controls** (also called *widgets*) such as text fields, checkboxes, radio buttons, dropdown lists, and submit buttons, each capable of capturing a discrete piece of user information. In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, the topic "Forms" under Module 1 of OEITT833 - Web Designing, deals with how clients (browsers) package user-entered data into **name-value pairs**, which are then transmitted to a server endpoint using the HTTP protocol via methods like **GET** or **POST**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim):** > *A form is an area where users can enter information that can be sent to a server for processing. Forms can contain text fields, checkboxes, radio buttons, submit buttons, and more. The form's behavior is governed by the `action` and `method` attributes of the `` element.* ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition (Plain English) Imagine you walk into a **government office to apply for a certificate**. The clerk does not just ask you to "say your details" ŌĆö instead, they hand you a **printed form with labeled boxes**: - One box says **"Full Name"** ŌåÆ you write your name there. - One box says **"Date of Birth"** ŌåÆ you write your DOB. - A few circles say **"Gender: Male / Female / Other"** ŌåÆ you tick one. - A few squares say **"Hobbies: Reading / Music / Sports"** ŌåÆ you tick *all that apply*. - At the bottom is a **"Submit"** button and a **"Reset"** button. An **HTML form works exactly the same way**, but it is rendered in the browser. Each labeled box in the paper form corresponds to an **`` element** (or ` ``` Key attributes: `rows`, `cols`, `name`, `placeholder`, `maxlength`, `required`, `readonly`, `disabled`, `wrap`. ### 2.3.2 ` ``` Key attributes: `name`, `multiple`, `size`, `required`. The `

topics.title: Embedded Media: Iframe
topics.content: > **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEITT833 - WEB DESIGNING > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Embedded Media: Iframe # ­¤ōś SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme ŌĆō OEITT833 Web Designing** syllabus, an **Iframe (Inline Frame)** is a **HTML5 container element** used to embed another HTML document, web page, media file, or interactive application directly inside the parent (host) web page. The element is represented by the paired tag `` and is part of the *Embedded Content* category in the HTML Living Standard. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Syllabus Definition (KTU):** "An iframe is an HTML element that creates an inline browsing context, allowing the current document to display a nested, independent document whose origin, layout, and execution are isolated from the parent page." The **inline browsing context** is the technical term that distinguishes an ` ``` > [!NOTE] > The `title` attribute is **mandatory** for accessibility (WCAG 2.1 / KTU accessibility guidelines). It must describe the embedded content for screen-reader users. Missing it is one of the most common KTU valuation deductions. --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!TIP] > **Real-world Analogy ŌĆö "The Picture-in-Picture Television"** Imagine you are watching a news channel on your main TV screen. Suddenly, in the **bottom-right corner**, a small secondary window opens up showing a live cricket match. This small window is **independently running** ŌĆö it has its own audio, its own channel, its own advertisements ŌĆö yet it lives *inside* your main viewing experience. An **` ``` ### Sandbox Token Reference | Token | Effect When Applied | |---|---| | *(empty)* | Disallows **everything** (scripts, forms, popups, same-origin) | | `allow-scripts` | Permits JavaScript execution | | `allow-same-origin` | Treats content as same-origin (must be paired with caution) | | `allow-forms` | Permits form submission | | `allow-popups` | Permits `window.open()` calls | | `allow-modals` | Permits `alert()`, `confirm()`, `prompt()` | | `allow-top-navigation` | Allows the iframe to navigate the **top-level** window | | `allow-popups-to-escape-sandbox` | Popups bypass sandbox restrictions | | `allow-presentation` | Allows Presentation API | | `allow-downloads` | Permits file downloads | > [!WARNING] > **Critical Security Pitfall:** Combining `allow-scripts` + `allow-same-origin` allows the embedded page to **remove its own sandbox**. Never use this combo on untrusted content ŌĆö it is a known attack vector (KTH Key jacking, clickjacking). --- ## 2.4 The `srcdoc` Attribute ŌĆö Server-Free Embedding `srcdoc` lets you write the iframe's HTML *inline* in the parent document. The browser renders it as if it were a separate document, but **no network request is made**. ```html ``` ### When to Use `src` vs `srcdoc` | Scenario | Use `src` | Use `srcdoc` | |---|---|---| | Embed YouTube video | Ō£ģ | ŌØī | | Embed Google Map | Ō£ģ | ŌØī | | Render user-supplied HTML safely | ŌØī | Ō£ģ (with sandbox) | | Create a quick preview / sandbox demo | ŌØī | Ō£ģ | | Reduce network round-trip | ŌØī | Ō£ģ | --- ## 2.5 `loading="lazy"` ŌĆö Performance Engineering Modern browsers (Chrome 77+, Firefox 121+, Safari 17+) support **lazy loading** for offscreen iframes. This defers the network request until the iframe is near the viewport ŌĆö saving **bandwidth, CPU, and battery**. ```html ``` > [!TIP] > **KTU Engineering Utility:** Use `loading="lazy"` on all below-the-fold iframes (e.g., a YouTube embed at the bottom of a long blog post). It improves **Largest Contentful Paint (LCP)** and **Time to Interactive (TTI)** ŌĆö both key **Core Web Vitals** metrics. --- ## 2.6 Iframe vs `

topics.title: Object
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEITT833 - WEB DESIGNING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Object

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# SECTION 1 ŌĆö Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (JavaScript Object)**
> An **Object** in JavaScript is a **composite, non-primitive data type** that stores data as an **unordered collection of related data points or functionality** in the form of **keyŌĆōvalue pairs** (also called **properties**). Each key is a string (or Symbol) that acts as a unique identifier, and each value can be any valid JavaScript data type ŌĆö including primitives, arrays, functions (called *methods*), or even other nested objects.

According to the **ECMAScript (ES) Language Specification** (which governs JavaScript), an object is defined as a **member of the type `Object`** ŌĆö it is a collection of **properties**, where every property is a `(key, value)` association. JavaScript objects are **reference types**, meaning they are stored and accessed by **memory address (reference)**, not by value.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Nearly every element in a JavaScript program ŌĆö *arrays, functions, dates, regex, errors, DOM nodes* ŌĆö is internally an object. Understanding the Object is therefore the **single most important foundation** for client-side web programming.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of a JavaScript Object like a **real-world identity card or a Wikipedia infobox** ­¤ōć.

Imagine a person named **"Arjun"**:

| Aspect of Arjun | Real-world property | Object property (key ŌåÆ value) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Name | "Arjun Kumar" | `name: "Arjun Kumar"` |
| Age | 22 years | `age: 22` |
| Course | B.Tech CSE | `course: "B.Tech CSE"` |
| Action: can greet | "Says Hello" | `greet: function() { ... }` |

Here, Arjun is the **object**, his attributes (`name`, `age`, `course`) are the **properties (keys)**, and the values ("Arjun Kumar", 22, "B.Tech CSE") are the **values** associated with those keys. His actions (like `greet()`) are called **methods** ŌĆö these are simply functions stored as property values.

> **In short:** *If a real-world entity has **attributes** (data) and **behaviors** (actions), it is best modeled as an **Object** in JavaScript.*

---

## 1.3 Why Objects Matter in Web Designing

Web pages are **interactive**, **dynamic**, and **data-driven**. Objects allow web developers to:

1. **Model real-world entities** ŌĆö a user, a product, a blog post, a shopping cart.
2. **Group related data and behavior** into a single reusable unit (encapsulation).
3. **Send and receive structured data** (e.g., **JSON ŌĆö JavaScript Object Notation**) between client and server.
4. **Manipulate the DOM** (each HTML element is an object with properties and methods like `.innerHTML`, `.style`, `.addEventListener()`).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Metrics / Constants to Remember:**
> - **Property Access Complexity:** *Average-case **O(1)** (constant time) for hash-based property lookup.*
> - **JSON Payload Standard:** *Internet Engineering Task Force (IETF) **RFC 8259**.*
> - **ECMAScript Version governing modern objects:** **ES6 (ES2015)** introduced the `class` keyword, formally aligning JS with classical OOP.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# SECTION 2 ŌĆö Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of a JavaScript Object

An object is a **dictionary-like** structure where each entry is a `(key, value)` pair.

```text
        ŌöīŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöÉ
OBJECT  Ōöé  {                                  Ōöé
        Ōöé    key1 : value1 ,                  Ōöé
        Ōöé    key2 : value2 ,                  Ōöé
        Ōöé    methodName : function( ) { ... } Ōöé
        Ōöé  }                                  Ōöé
        ŌööŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöś
```

### Structural Rules (KTU 2024)

- **Keys** must be **strings** (or Symbols). JavaScript automatically converts numeric keys to strings.
- **Values** can be **any** data type: number, string, boolean, null, undefined, array, function, or another object.
- A **value that is a function** is called a **method**.
- A **value that is another object** is called a **nested object** (or *sub-object*).
- Objects are **mutable** ŌĆö properties can be added, modified, or deleted at runtime.

---

## 2.2 Methods of Object Creation (High-Yield)

JavaScript provides **four primary ways** to create objects. Examiners frequently test this.

### A. Object Literal Syntax (Most Common ŌŁÉ)
```javascript
const student = {
  name: "Ananya",
  rollNo: 45,
  branch: "CSE",
  greet: function() {
    return "Hello, I am " + this.name;
  }
};
```

### B. Using the `new Object()` Constructor
```javascript
const car = new Object();
car.brand = "Tesla";
car.speed = 200;
car.drive = function() { return "Driving at " + this.speed + " km/h"; };
```

### C. Constructor Function (Classical OOP Style)
```javascript
function Employee(id, name, salary) {
  this.id = id;
  this.name = name;
  this.salary = salary;
  this.intro = function() {
    return this.id + " - " + this.name;
  };
}
const emp1 = new Employee(101, "Ravi", 50000);
```

### D. ES6 `class` Syntax (Modern Recommended Ō£ģ)
```javascript
class Animal {
  constructor(name, sound) {
    this.name = name;
    this.sound = sound;
  }
  speak() {
    return this.name + " says " + this.sound;
  }
}
const dog = new Animal("Dog", "Woof");
```

---

## 2.3 The `this` Keyword ŌĆö Contextual Reference

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition:** `this` is a **reference variable** that points to the **object that is currently executing the code** (also called the *execution context*).

| Calling Context | Value of `this` |
| :--- | :--- |
| Method call: `obj.method()` | The `obj` itself |
| Standalone function call | Global object (`window` in browsers) |
| `new Constructor()` | The newly created object |
| Arrow function | Lexical `this` (inherited from enclosing scope) |

---

## 2.4 Prototype and Inheritance

Every JavaScript object has a hidden internal link to another object called its **prototype (`__proto__`)**. Through the **prototype chain**, objects can inherit properties and methods from their ancestors.

> **Formula (Conceptual):** 
> `Object.prototype` is the **root** of all JS objects. `Array.prototype` ŌåÆ inherits from `Object.prototype`. This forms a **single-rooted inheritance tree**.

---

## 2.5 Object Property Operations (Cheat-Sheet)

| Operation | Syntax | Result |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Access | `obj.key` or `obj["key"]` | Returns the value |
| Add | `obj.newKey = value;` | Adds a new property |
| Modify | `obj.key = newValue;` | Overwrites existing value |
| Delete | `delete obj.key;` | Removes the property |
| Check existence | `key in obj` or `obj.hasOwnProperty("key")` | Returns boolean |
| Enumerate | `for (let k in obj) { ... }` | Iterates over keys |
| Get keys | `Object.keys(obj)` | Returns array of keys |
| Get values | `Object.values(obj)` | Returns array of values |

---

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Syntax / Equation | Real-world Web Use |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Object literal | $\text{const obj} = \{ k: v, \dots \}$ | Configure web components, JSON config |
| 2 | Property access | $\text{obj.key} \equiv \text{obj["key"]}$ | Form data, API response handling |
| 3 | Method definition | $\text{method()} = \text{function attached as property}$ | Event handlers, form validation |
| 4 | `this` binding | $\text{this} = \text{calling object (in methods)}$ | DOM event targeting |
| 5 | Constructor | $\text{function Name(args)} \{ \text{this.x} = \text{args}; \}$ | User, Product, Order models |
| 6 | ES6 Class | $\text{class Name} \{ \text{constructor( )} \{ \dots \} \}$ | Reusable UI widget templates |
| 7 | Prototype link | $\text{obj}.\_{ \_proto\_\_{ }} \to \text{ParentObj}$ | Method inheritance, memory efficiency |
| 8 | JSON stringify | $\text{JSON.stringify(obj)} \to \text{string}$ | API requests, localStorage save |
| 9 | JSON parse | $\text{JSON.parse(string)} \to \text{obj}$ | API responses, config loading |
| 10 | Object spread | $\{ \dots \text{obj1}, \dots \text{obj2} \}$ | Immutable updates (React/Redux) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world Engineering Utility:** In modern full-stack web apps, a single `User` object might be created, sent to a **Node.js** server as a JSON string, stored in **MongoDB** (which is document-based ŌĆö documents are objects), and returned back to the client. **Objects are the universal data interchange format of the web.**

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# SECTION 3 ŌĆö Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example: Building a `Product` Object (Object Literal Approach)

### Problem Statement
Create a JavaScript object representing a **mobile phone product** with at least **4 properties** and **1 method**, and demonstrate **all CRUD operations** (Create, Read, Update, Delete).

### Step-by-Step Construction

**Step 1 ŌĆö Create the object using object literal syntax.**

```javascript
const product = {
  id: 101,
  name: "SmartPhone X",
  brand: "TechCorp",
  price: 24999,
  inStock: true,
  displayInfo: function() {
    return this.brand + " " + this.name + " costs Rs." + this.price;
  }
};
```
**Logic:** We use curly braces `{}` to define an object. The `displayInfo` function uses `this` to refer to the `product` object itself, allowing it to read `this.brand`, `this.name`, and `this.price`.

**Step 2 ŌĆö READ: Access properties (two equivalent syntaxes).**

```javascript
console.log(product.name);          // Dot notation   ŌåÆ "SmartPhone X"
console.log(product["price"]);      // Bracket notation ŌåÆ 24999
```
**Logic:** Dot notation is concise. Bracket notation is required when the key is dynamic (e.g., stored in a variable like `let k = "brand"; product[k];`).

**Step 3 ŌĆö READ method: Call the function property.**

```javascript
console.log(product.displayInfo());
// Output: "TechCorp SmartPhone X costs Rs.24999"
```

**Step 4 ŌĆö UPDATE: Modify an existing property.**

```javascript
product.price = 22999;
console.log(product.price);         // 22999
```
**Logic:** Assignment with the same key overwrites the previous value. Objects are mutable.

**Step 5 ŌĆö CREATE (add new property).**

```javascript
product.discount = 10;
console.log(product.discount);      // 10
```
**Logic:** Assigning a value to a previously non-existent key adds a new property to the object dynamically.

**Step 6 ŌĆö DELETE: Remove a property.**

```javascript
delete product.inStock;
console.log(product.inStock);       // undefined
```
**Logic:** The `delete` operator removes the property entirely. Accessing it afterward returns `undefined` (the property no longer exists).

**Step 7 ŌĆö VERIFY using `in` operator and `Object.keys()`.**

```javascript
console.log("price" in product);           // true
console.log(Object.keys(product));         // ["id", "name", "brand", "price", "displayInfo", "discount"]
```

### Final State of the Object

```javascript
{
  id: 101,
  name: "SmartPhone X",
  brand: "TechCorp",
  price: 22999,
  displayInfo: function() { ... },
  discount: 10
}
```

---

## 3.2 Worked Example: ES6 Class with Inheritance

### Problem Statement
Demonstrate object creation using ES6 `class`, including **inheritance** via the `extends` and `super` keywords.

### Step-by-Step Construction

**Step 1 ŌĆö Define the parent (super) class.**

```javascript
class Vehicle {
  constructor(brand, wheels) {
    this.brand = brand;
    this.wheels = wheels;
  }
  describe() {
    return this.brand + " has " + this.wheels + " wheels.";
  }
}
```
**Logic:** The `constructor` is a special method called automatically when `new Vehicle(...)` is invoked. It initializes instance properties using `this`.

**Step 2 ŌĆö Create the child class using `extends`.**

```javascript
class Car extends Vehicle {
  constructor(brand, wheels, fuelType) {
    super(brand, wheels);   // Calls the parent constructor
    this.fuelType = fuelType;
  }
  honk() {
    return this.brand + " goes Beep!";
  }
}
```
**Logic:** `extends Vehicle` sets up the prototype chain. `super(brand, wheels)` is **mandatory** before using `this` in a child constructor ŌĆö it invokes the parent's constructor to initialize inherited properties.

**Step 3 ŌĆö Instantiate and use the objects.**

```javascript
const v1 = new Vehicle("GenericBoat", 0);
console.log(v1.describe());    // "GenericBoat has 0 wheels."

const c1 = new Car("Honda", 4, "Petrol");
console.log(c1.describe());    // "Honda has 4 wheels."  (inherited method)
console.log(c1.honk());        // "Honda goes Beep!"     (own method)
```

### Prototype Chain Visualization (Conceptual Equation)

$$
\text{carObj} \to \text{Car.prototype} \to \text{Vehicle.prototype} \to \text{Object.prototype} \to \text{null}
$$

**Logic:** When JavaScript cannot find a property/method on `carObj`, it traverses up the chain, looking at `Car.prototype`, then `Vehicle.prototype`, then `Object.prototype`, until either the property is found or `null` is reached.

---

## 3.3 JSON Serialization ŌĆö Object Ōćä String Conversion

### Problem Statement
Convert a JavaScript object to a JSON string and back.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Stringify (Object ŌåÆ JSON string).**

```javascript
const user = { name: "Meera", age: 21, active: true };
const jsonStr = JSON.stringify(user);
console.log(jsonStr);
// Output: '{"name":"Meera","age":21,"active":true}'
```
**Logic:** `JSON.stringify()` traverses the object's enumerable own properties and serializes them per **RFC 8259** standards. Functions and `undefined` values are silently dropped.

**Step 2 ŌĆö Parse (JSON string ŌåÆ Object).**

```javascript
const parsedObj = JSON.parse(jsonStr);
console.log(parsedObj.name);     // "Meera"
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Note:** `JSON.parse()` will throw a `SyntaxError` if the string is malformed. Always wrap it in `try...catch` in production code.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# SECTION 4 ŌĆö Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Object Internal Architecture (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Student Object]:::obj --> B["name: Ananya"]:::prop
    A --> C["rollNo: 45"]:::prop
    A --> D["branch: CSE"]:::prop
    A --> E["greet: function"]:::method
    E --> F["this -> Student Object"]:::ref

    classDef obj fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef prop fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    classDef method fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    classDef ref fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The **Student Object** (yellow) is the container.
- **Blue nodes** are *data properties* (primitive values).
- **Green node** is a *method* (function property).
- The **pink node** indicates that inside the method, `this` refers back to the Student Object.

---

## 4.2 Prototype Inheritance Chain (Class Hierarchy)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    O["Object.prototype (Root)"]:::root
    V["Vehicle.prototype"]:::mid
    C["Car.prototype"]:::mid
    I1["veh1 instance"]:::leaf
    I2["car1 instance"]:::leaf

    V -- inherits from --> O
    C -- inherits from --> V
    I1 -- proto link --> V
    I2 -- proto link --> C

    classDef root fill:#FF6347,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFF
    classDef mid fill:#FFA500,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef leaf fill:#98FB98,stroke:#000,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- Every object ultimately inherits from `Object.prototype`.
- `Vehicle` extends `Object` (inherits `toString`, `hasOwnProperty`, etc.).
- `Car` extends `Vehicle` (inherits `describe` method).
- Instances (`veh1`, `car1`) link upward to their respective prototypes.

---

## 4.3 Object Lifecycle in a Web Application (Block Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[User Input / API Call]:::input --> B[Create JS Object]:::process
    B --> C["JSON.stringify()"]:::process
    C --> D[Send via HTTP]:::network
    D --> E[Server Database]:::storage
    E --> F[Retrieve as JSON]:::network
    F --> G["JSON.parse()"]:::process
    G --> H[Render in DOM]:::output
    H --> I[User sees updated UI]:::output

    classDef input fill:#E6E6FA,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    classDef process fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    classDef network fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    classDef storage fill:#F0E68C,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef output fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
```

**Reading the diagram:** This shows the **full lifecycle of a web object** ŌĆö from creation on the client, to serialization (JSON), to network transmission, to storage, and back to the DOM for rendering.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# SECTION 5 ŌĆö KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Model Question ŌĆō Module 1]**

### Question A1 (3 Marks)
**Q: Define an Object in JavaScript. List any four ways to create an object.**

**Model Answer:**
An object in JavaScript is a non-primitive, reference data type that stores data as an unordered collection of **keyŌĆōvalue pairs** (properties). Each key is a string identifier, and each value can be any data type, including functions (called *methods*).

The four ways to create an object are:
1. **Object Literal syntax** ŌĆö `const obj = { name: "X" };`
2. **`new Object()` constructor** ŌĆö `const obj = new Object(); obj.name = "X";`
3. **Constructor Function** ŌĆö `function F(n) { this.name = n; } const obj = new F("X");`
4. **ES6 `class` syntax** ŌĆö `class F { constructor(n) { this.name = n; } } const obj = new F("X");`

*(Marks: Definition = 1, Four ways with syntax = 2)*

---

### Question A2 (3 Marks)
**Q: Explain the difference between a *property* and a *method* of an object with an example.**

**Model Answer:**
- A **property** of an object is a keyŌĆōvalue pair where the value is **data** (number, string, boolean, array, or another object). Example: `student.name = "Anu";` ŌĆö here, `name` is the key and `"Anu"` is the property value.
- A **method** is a property of an object whose value is a **function**. Methods define the *behavior* of the object. Example: `student.greet = function() { return "Hello"; };` ŌĆö `greet` is a method.

**Key distinction:** Properties represent **state** (what an object *is*), while methods represent **behavior** (what an object *does*).

---

## 5.2 PART B ŌĆö Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

### **Question B-A (14 Marks)** [Choice 1]

> **[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Model Paper ŌĆō Module 1]**

**(a)** Define a JavaScript Object. Explain the different ways of creating objects in JavaScript with suitable examples. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Write a JavaScript program to create a class `Book` with properties `title`, `author`, and `price`. Include a method to display book details. Create two book objects and demonstrate inheritance by creating a subclass `EBook` that adds a `fileSize` property. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

A JavaScript Object is a composite data type that groups related data and functionality into keyŌĆōvalue pairs. It is a reference type, stored in heap memory and accessed by reference.

**Four ways of creating objects:**

1. **Object Literal:**
```javascript
const car = { brand: "Tesla", speed: 250 };
```

2. **`new Object()` Constructor:**
```javascript
const car = new Object();
car.brand = "Tesla"; car.speed = 250;
```

3. **Constructor Function:**
```javascript
function Car(brand, speed) {
  this.brand = brand; this.speed = speed;
}
const c1 = new Car("Tesla", 250);
```

4. **ES6 Class:**
```javascript
class Car {
  constructor(brand, speed) {
    this.brand = brand; this.speed = speed;
  }
}
const c1 = new Car("Tesla", 250);
```

#### Model Solution for (b):

```javascript
class Book {
  constructor(title, author, price) {
    this.title = title;
    this.author = author;
    this.price = price;
  }
  display() {
    return this.title + " by " + this.author + " - Rs." + this.price;
  }
}

class EBook extends Book {
  constructor(title, author, price, fileSize) {
    super(title, author, price);
    this.fileSize = fileSize;
  }
  display() {
    return super.display() + " (" + this.fileSize + "MB)";
  }
}

const b1 = new Book("JS Basics", "John", 300);
const b2 = new EBook("Advanced JS", "Jane", 500, 15);

console.log(b1.display());
// Output: JS Basics by John - Rs.300

console.log(b2.display());
// Output: Advanced JS by Jane - Rs.500 (15MB)
```

**Explanation:** `EBook` inherits from `Book` using `extends`. The `super()` call initializes the parent properties. The overridden `display()` method uses `super.display()` to extend the parent's behavior ŌĆö this is **method overriding**, a key OOP concept.

---

### **Question B-B (14 Marks)** [Choice 2]

> **[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Model Paper ŌĆō Module 1]**

**(a)** Explain the concept of the `this` keyword in JavaScript with at least three different contexts. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the prototype chain in JavaScript. How does inheritance work via prototypes? Write a constructor function `Shape` and a child constructor `Circle` to demonstrate prototypal inheritance. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

The `this` keyword refers to the **object that is currently executing the function**. Its value depends on **how** the function is called:

1. **Inside a method (object context):** `this` refers to the **object owning the method**.
```javascript
const user = {
  name: "Anu",
  greet() { return this.name; }   // 'this' = user
};
```

2. **In a constructor function (called with `new`):** `this` refers to the **newly created object**.
```javascript
function User(n) { this.name = n; }
const u = new User("Anu");   // 'this' inside = new object
```

3. **In a standalone function call:** `this` refers to the **global object** (in browsers, `window`). In **strict mode**, it is `undefined`.

> **Additional:** Arrow functions do **not** bind their own `this`; they inherit it from the enclosing (lexical) scope.

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Prototype Chain Concept:** Every JavaScript object has an internal hidden link `[[Prototype]]` (accessible as `__proto__`) pointing to another object. When accessing a property, JavaScript first looks at the object itself; if not found, it traverses up the prototype chain until `null` is reached.

**Inheritance via Prototypes:** A child constructor's prototype can be set to an instance of the parent constructor, allowing the child to inherit all parent properties and methods.

```javascript
function Shape(color) {
  this.color = color;
}
Shape.prototype.getColor = function() {
  return this.color;
};

function Circle(color, radius) {
  Shape.call(this, color);   // inherit properties
  this.radius = radius;
}
Circle.prototype = Object.create(Shape.prototype);  // inherit methods
Circle.prototype.constructor = Circle;

Circle.prototype.area = function() {
  return Math.PI * this.radius * this.radius;
};

const c = new Circle("Red", 5);
console.log(c.getColor());   // "Red"   (inherited from Shape)
console.log(c.area().toFixed(2));  // "78.54"  (own method)
```

**Explanation:** `Object.create(Shape.prototype)` sets up the chain `Circle.prototype ŌåÆ Shape.prototype ŌåÆ Object.prototype ŌåÆ null`. The `area()` method is defined on `Circle.prototype` and is accessible to all circle instances.

---

## 5.3 Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls (KTU 2024 Valuation Key Patterns):**
> 1. **Confusing `JSON.stringify()` with `JSON.parse()`** ŌĆö many students reverse these in exam answers. Remember: *stringify* = object ŌåÆ string, *parse* = string ŌåÆ object.
> 2. **Forgetting `new` keyword** with constructor functions ŌĆö without `new`, `this` will point to the global object (a major bug).
> 3. **Forgetting to call `super()` in child class constructor** ŌĆö this throws a `ReferenceError: Must call super constructor in derived class before accessing 'this'`.
> 4. **Confusing `prototype` with `__proto__`** ŌĆö `prototype` is a property of **functions/classes**; `__proto__` is a property of **instances**.
> 5. **Writing `this` inside arrow functions** expecting it to refer to the calling object ŌĆö arrow functions use **lexical** `this`, not dynamic.
> 6. **Spelling `JSON` as `JASON`** ŌĆö this is a frequent typographical error that examiners deduct marks for.
> 7. **Not closing the object literal with `;`** ŌĆö JavaScript will throw a `SyntaxError` in some contexts (e.g., after another statement).

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember Ō£©

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist (Module 1 ŌĆö Object):**

- ŌŁÉ An **Object** is a collection of **keyŌĆōvalue pairs** (properties); a value that is a function is called a **method**.
- ŌŁÉ Objects are **reference types** ŌĆö copied by reference, not by value.
- ŌŁÉ **Four creation methods:** Object literal `{}`, `new Object()`, constructor function, ES6 `class`.
- ŌŁÉ Property access: **dot notation** (`obj.key`) vs **bracket notation** (`obj["key"]`).
- ŌŁÉ `this` refers to the **calling object** in methods, the **new object** in constructors, and the **global object** in standalone function calls.
- ŌŁÉ **ES6 `class`** is *syntactic sugar* over JavaScript's existing **prototype-based inheritance** ŌĆö under the hood, classes still use prototypes.
- ŌŁÉ **Prototype chain** = `obj ŌåÆ Constructor.prototype ŌåÆ Object.prototype ŌåÆ null`.
- ŌŁÉ `JSON.stringify(obj)` ŌåÆ JSON string ; `JSON.parse(str)` ŌåÆ Object.
- ŌŁÉ `Object.keys(obj)`, `Object.values(obj)`, `Object.entries(obj)` are used for enumeration.
- ŌŁÉ `delete obj.key` removes a property; assigning to a new key adds a property.
- ŌŁÉ Web objects are exchanged between client and server using **JSON** (RFC 8259).
- ŌŁÉ **DOM elements are objects** ŌĆö `document.getElementById("x")` returns an object with properties like `.innerHTML` and methods like `.addEventListener()`.
- ŌŁÉ Arrow functions **do not have their own `this`** ŌĆö they inherit lexically.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Video and Audio
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEITT833 - WEB DESIGNING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Video and Audio

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Video and Audio in Modern Web Design

### Formal Definition
In the context of **HTML5 Web Design**, *Video* and *Audio* refer to the native multimedia embedding capabilities provided by the HTML5 specification through the dedicated `<video>` and `<audio>` elements. These elements allow browsers to natively decode, render, and control audio-visual content **without requiring third-party plugins** (like the legacy Adobe Flash, QuickTime, or Windows Media Player). The W3C HTML5 standard introduced these as first-class media elements, governed by the *HTML Living Standard* and supported by a *Media Source Extensions (MSE)* API and *WebRTC* for advanced streaming use cases.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Under the 2024 Scheme (OEITT833 ŌĆō Web Designing), Module 1 expects students to master the syntax, attributes, supported codecs, MIME types, fallback strategies, and accessibility provisions of the HTML5 `<video>` and `<audio>` tags.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Imagine you walk into a modern smart television showroom. The TV has **buttons, a remote, a volume knob, a progress bar showing where the movie is, and subtitles**. Before HTML5, embedding a video on a webpage was like asking the user to "bring their own TV" (install a plugin like Flash). With HTML5's `<video>` and `<audio>` elements, the browser **becomes the smart TV** ŌĆö playback, pause, volume, fullscreen, looping, and even captions are all built-in.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Architectural Shift:** HTML5 multimedia transitioned the web from a *plugin-dependent* ecosystem to a *browser-native* media playback model, reducing security vulnerabilities, improving performance, and enabling mobile compatibility.

### Key Technical Metrics
- **Standard Frame Rate (Cinema/Web):** **24 fps / 30 fps / 60 fps**
- **CD-Quality Audio Sample Rate:** **44.1 kHz**
- **Studio-Quality Audio Sample Rate:** **48 kHz**
- **Standard Bit-Depth for Audio:** **16-bit (CD)**, **24-bit (Studio)**
- **HD Video Resolution:** **1280├Ś720 (720p)**
- **Full HD Video Resolution:** **1920├Ś1080 (1080p)**
- **4K UHD Resolution:** **3840├Ś2160**

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Aid:** A common rule of thumb in web streaming ŌĆö *uncompressed 1080p video at 30 fps Ōēł 1.5 Gbps*. That is why **codecs** (compressors/decompressors) are absolutely critical.

---

## 1.2 The `<video>` Element ŌĆö The Foundational Building Block

The HTML5 `<video>` element embeds a video player directly into a webpage. It is a **replaced inline element** that supports multiple attributes controlling playback behavior, source files, and user interaction.

### Basic Syntax
```html
<video src="movie.mp4" controls></video>
```

### Essential Attributes (Exam-Favorite)

| Attribute | Purpose | Default Behavior |
|---|---|---|
| `src` | Specifies the URL/path of the video file | None (required if no `<source>` children) |
| `controls` | Displays browser-native playback controls | Disabled by default |
| `autoplay` | Starts playback automatically on load | Disabled (browsers may override) |
| `loop` | Restarts the video after it ends | Disabled |
| `muted` | Silences audio output by default | Disabled |
| `preload` | Hints to browser whether to preload media | `auto` |
| `poster` | URL of an image shown before playback | None |
| `width` / `height` | Sets the player dimensions in CSS pixels | Intrinsic video size |
| `playsinline` | Prevents iOS from forcing fullscreen on mobile | Disabled (iOS-specific) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Autoplay Policy (2024):** Modern browsers (Chrome 66+, Safari 11+, Firefox 66+) **require videos to be muted** for autoplay to function. This is a UX-driven policy to prevent unwanted audio.

---

## 1.3 The `<audio>` Element ŌĆö Companion to Video

The `<audio>` element operates with near-identical syntax to `<video>`, except it renders **no visual canvas** (no picture, no poster). It is used purely for sound files such as podcasts, music, sound effects, and voice-overs.

### Basic Syntax
```html
<audio src="theme.mp3" controls></audio>
```

### Attribute Compatibility
The `<audio>` element supports the same `controls`, `autoplay`, `loop`, `muted`, `preload`, and `src` attributes as `<video>`, but **excludes** `width`, `height`, and `poster` (since there is no visual frame).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Tip:** A frequent question asks ŌĆö *"Why does `<audio>` not have a `poster` attribute?"* The answer: **A poster is a visual preview image. Audio has no visual frame to preview.**

---

## 1.4 Multimedia File Formats & Codecs (The Heart of the Topic)

A **container format** is the file wrapper (`.mp4`, `.webm`, `.ogg`) that bundles compressed video, audio, and metadata. A **codec** is the algorithm that compresses/decompresses the media stream.

### Video Format Matrix

| Container | Video Codec | Audio Codec | Browser Support | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **MP4 (`.mp4`)** | **H.264 (AVC)**, H.265 (HEVC) | AAC | All modern browsers | Universal web standard |
| **WebM (`.webm`)** | **VP8, VP9, AV1** | Vorbis, Opus | Chrome, Firefox, Edge, Opera (limited Safari) | Open-source alternative |
| **Ogg (`.ogv`)** | Theora | Vorbis | Firefox, Chrome (legacy) | Open-source, older fallback |
| **AVI (`.avi`)** | Various | Various | Not natively supported (legacy) | Avoid for web |

### Audio Format Matrix

| Format | Codec | Compression | Browser Support | Quality |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **MP3 (`.mp3`)** | MPEG-1 Layer III | Lossy | Universal | Good |
| **WAV (`.wav`)** | PCM (uncompressed) | Lossless | Universal | Studio-grade, large size |
| **OGG (`.ogg`)** | Vorbis | Lossy | Firefox, Chrome, Opera | Open alternative to MP3 |
| **AAC (`.aac` / `.m4a`)** | Advanced Audio Coding | Lossy | All modern browsers | Better than MP3 at same bitrate |
| **FLAC (`.flac`)** | Free Lossless Audio Codec | Lossless | Most modern browsers | Audiophile-grade |
| **Opus (`.opus`)** | Opus | Lossy | Modern browsers | Best for real-time/VoIP |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight ŌĆö Why MP4 + H.264 is King:** The combination of **MP4 container + H.264 video codec + AAC audio codec** is supported by virtually every browser and device on Earth. This is why professional web designers **always ship MP4 as the primary source**.

---

## 1.5 MIME Types ŌĆö The Browser's Content Passport

When the server delivers a media file, it must send the correct **MIME type** in the `Content-Type` HTTP header. If the MIME type is wrong or missing, the browser may refuse to play the file.

### Critical MIME Types for Web Design

| File Extension | Correct MIME Type |
|---|---|
| `.mp4` | `video/mp4` |
| `.webm` | `video/webm` |
| `.ogv` | `video/ogg` |
| `.mp3` | `audio/mpeg` |
| `.ogg` | `audio/ogg` |
| `.wav` | `audio/wav` |
| `.m4a` / `.aac` | `audio/aac` |
| `.flac` | `audio/flac` |
| `.opus` | `audio/opus` |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often confuse `video/mp4` with `video/mpeg`. The latter is for legacy `.mpeg`/`.mpg` files ŌĆö never use it for `.mp4`. Wrong MIME types are a common cause of *"video not playing"* in real projects.

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Workflow ŌĆö How a Browser Plays HTML5 Video

Understanding the *lifecycle* of a video request is critical for both board answers and real-world debugging.

### Step-by-Step Lifecycle

1. **HTML Parsing:** The browser's HTML parser encounters the `<video>` element. It checks for `controls`, `autoplay`, `preload` attributes.
2. **Source Resolution:** The browser reads the `src` attribute *or* iterates through child `<source>` elements to identify candidate media files.
3. **MIME Type Filtering:** The browser checks the `type` attribute of each `<source>`. If the browser cannot decode the listed codec/MIME type, it skips that source and tries the next.
4. **Resource Fetching:** Based on `preload` value:
   - `none` ŌåÆ No preload, defer until user interaction
   - `metadata` ŌåÆ Preload duration, dimensions, track list only
   - `auto` ŌåÆ Browser decides (typically preloads entire file)
5. **Decoding Pipeline:** The browser's media decoder demuxes the container and feeds compressed frames to:
   - **Video Decoder** (e.g., H.264 ŌåÆ raw YUV frames)
   - **Audio Decoder** (e.g., AAC ŌåÆ raw PCM samples)
6. **Rendering:** Video frames go to a `<canvas>`-like internal surface; audio samples go to the audio output device.
7. **Playback Control:** JavaScript can interact via the DOM `HTMLMediaElement` API.
8. **Buffering:** Browser maintains a *buffer ahead* of the current playhead to prevent stalling.

> [!TIP]
> **Why This Matters in KTU Exams:** A 14-mark question may ask you to *"Explain the working of HTML5 video element with a block diagram"*. Memorizing these 8 steps with technical vocabulary (demux, codec, preload, buffer) is a guaranteed high-scoring answer.

---

## 2.2 The `<source>` Element ŌĆö Multi-Format Fallback Strategy

Because no single format is supported by **all** browsers, the W3C recommends providing multiple `<source>` children. The browser picks the **first one it can play**.

### Syntax Pattern
```html
<video controls poster="preview.jpg" width="640" height="360">
    <source src="movie.mp4" type="video/mp4">
    <source src="movie.webm" type="video/webm">
    <source src="movie.ogv" type="video/ogg">
    Your browser does not support the HTML5 video element.
</video>
```

### Evaluation Logic
The browser iterates sources **top to bottom**, evaluating the `type` attribute. If the `type` attribute is omitted, the browser probes the file (network request) ŌĆö slow. If the `type` is provided, the browser decides instantly based on its codec support matrix.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Order Matters:** Always list the most widely-supported format **first** (typically MP4) and progressive fallbacks below. Otherwise, browsers that support all formats will download the suboptimal one.

---

## 2.3 The `preload` Attribute ŌĆö Bandwidth vs. User Experience

The `preload` attribute is a **hint** to the browser, not a command. It balances three values:

| Value | Behavior | Best Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| `none` | No data is preloaded | Pages where video is unlikely to be watched; mobile data conservation |
| `metadata` | Only metadata (duration, dimensions) is fetched | Pages with many videos; bandwidth-constrained users |
| `auto` | Browser may preload the entire file | Hero videos, landing-page background media |

> [!NOTE]
> **Mobile Safari Override:** On iOS, the browser may **ignore** the `preload` attribute for `<video>` elements that are not yet visible (e.g., off-screen). This is a known optimization.

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (For Numerical / Estimation Questions)

Although web design is mostly conceptual, KTU sometimes includes calculation-based questions on **storage, bitrate, and bandwidth estimation** for media files.

| Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|
| $ \text{File Size (bits)} = \text{Bitrate} \times \text{Duration} $ | Total bits in media file | bits |
| $ \text{File Size (MB)} = \dfrac{\text{Bitrate} \times \text{Duration}}{8 \times 1024 \times 1024} $ | Convert bits to Megabytes | MB |
| $ \text{Compressed Bitrate} = \dfrac{\text{Raw Bitrate}}{\text{Compression Ratio}} $ | Effective bitrate after codec compression | Mbps |
| $ \text{Raw Video Bitrate} = W \times H \times \text{FPS} \times \text{Bit Depth} \times 3 $ | Uncompressed RGB video bitrate (Chroma subsampling omitted) | bps |
| $ \text{Compression Ratio} = \dfrac{\text{Uncompressed Size}}{\text{Compressed Size}} $ | Codec efficiency metric | Dimensionless |
| $ \text{Required Bandwidth} = \text{Stream Bitrate} \times (1 + \text{Buffer Overhead}) $ | Safe streaming throughput | Mbps |
| $ \text{Sample Size} = \text{Sample Rate} \times \text{Bit Depth} \times \text{Channels} $ | Uncompressed audio size per second | bits/sec |

### Worked Constants to Memorize
- **1 byte** = **8 bits**
- **1 KB** = **1024 bytes**
- **1 MB** = **1024 KB**
- **1 GB** = **1024 MB**
- **1 Mbps** = **10ŌüČ bps** (approx, in networking context)

---

## 2.5 The `HTMLMediaElement` JavaScript API

Both `<video>` and `<audio>` inherit from the `HTMLMediaElement` interface, exposing a rich set of properties and methods for programmatic control.

### Core Methods

| Method | Description |
|---|---|
| `play()` | Starts or resumes playback. Returns a Promise. |
| `pause()` | Pauses playback. |
| `load()` | Resets the element and reloads the source. |
| `canPlayType(type)` | Returns `"probably"`, `"maybe"`, or `""` for a given MIME type. |
| `addTextTrack()` | Adds a new text track (for captions/subtitles). |

### Core Properties

| Property | Type | Description |
|---|---|---|
| `currentTime` | seconds | Current playback position |
| `duration` | seconds | Total media length |
| `paused` | boolean | `true` if playback is paused |
| `volume` | 0.0 ŌĆō 1.0 | Audio volume level |
| `muted` | boolean | Mute state |
| `playbackRate` | number | Speed multiplier (1.0 = normal) |
| `readyState` | 0ŌĆō4 | Media readiness: HAVE_NOTHING ŌåÆ HAVE_ENOUGH_DATA |
| `networkState` | 0ŌĆō3 | Network state: NETWORK_EMPTY ŌåÆ NETWORK_LOADING ŌåÆ NETWORK_IDLE ŌåÆ NETWORK_NO_SOURCE |
| `buffered` | TimeRanges | Cached time ranges |
| `ended` | boolean | `true` if playback has completed |

### Core Events

| Event | Fires When |
|---|---|
| `play` | Playback begins (or resumes after pause) |
| `pause` | Playback is paused |
| `ended` | Playback reaches the end |
| `timeupdate` | `currentTime` changes (frequent) |
| `loadedmetadata` | Metadata (duration, dimensions) is loaded |
| `canplay` | Browser estimates it can begin playback |
| `canplaythrough` | Browser estimates it can play through without buffering |
| `error` | Loading or playback error occurs |
| `volumechange` | Volume or mute state changes |
| `waiting` | Playback halted due to buffering |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Pearl:** The question *"Differentiate between `canplay` and `canplaythrough` events"* is a classic 3-mark question. `canplay` = *sufficient data to start*. `canplaythrough` = *sufficient data to play entirely without stalling*.

---

## 2.6 Accessibility & `<track>` Element ŌĆö Inclusive Web Design

The `<track>` element is a child of `<video>` (or `<audio>`) that provides **time-synchronized text tracks** for accessibility, search, and internationalization.

### `<track>` Sub-Types

| `kind` Value | Purpose |
|---|---|
| `subtitles` | Translation of dialogue (assumes sound is heard) |
| `captions` | Transcription including sound effects, for the deaf/HoH |
| `descriptions` | Audio description of visual content for the blind |
| `chapters` | Navigational chapter titles |
| `metadata` | Script-readable metadata (timed cues) |

### Syntax Pattern
```html
<video controls>
    <source src="lecture.mp4" type="video/mp4">
    <track src="captions_en.vtt" kind="captions" srclang="en" label="English" default>
    <track src="subtitles_es.vtt" kind="subtitles" srclang="es" label="Espa├▒ol">
</video>
```

> [!NOTE]
> **WebVTT Format:** Track files use the **WebVTT (Web Video Text Tracks)** format ŌĆö a simple plain-text file with timestamps like `00:00:05.000 --> 00:00:10.000`. The `.vtt` MIME type is `text/vtt`.

### Why Accessibility Matters in KTU
- **WCAG 2.1 (Web Content Accessibility Guidelines)** mandates captions for prerecorded video.
- Captioned video improves **SEO** because search engines can index the text content of captions.
- Multi-language subtitle tracks enable **global reach** with minimal code.

---

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Derivation ŌĆö Media File Size Calculation

This derivation is a **board-exam favorite** for numerical questions on web media planning.

### Problem Statement (Standard KTU Style)
> *"A 5-minute HD video (1280├Ś720, 30 fps, 8-bit color depth) is encoded using H.264 with a compression ratio of 100:1. If the AAC audio track is 128 kbps, calculate the total file size in MB. Assume 1 MB = 1024┬▓ bytes."*

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 ŌĆö Identify the Given Parameters**
- Resolution: $W = 1280$ pixels, $H = 720$ pixels
- Frame rate: $\text{FPS} = 30$
- Bit depth: $8$ bits per color channel
- Color channels: $3$ (RGB)
- Duration: $T = 5 \text{ min} = 300 \text{ seconds}$
- Compression ratio: $CR = 100$
- Audio bitrate: $B_a = 128 \text{ kbps}$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Apply the Raw Video Bitrate Formula**

$$ \text{Raw Bitrate} = W \times H \times \text{FPS} \times \text{Bit Depth} \times \text{Channels} $$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Substitute the Values**

$$ \text{Raw Bitrate} = 1280 \times 720 \times 30 \times 8 \times 3 $$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Calculate the Product**

$$ 1280 \times 720 = 921600 $$

$$ 921600 \times 30 = 27648000 $$

$$ 27648000 \times 8 = 221184000 $$

$$ 221184000 \times 3 = 663552000 \text{ bps} $$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Apply the Compression Ratio**

$$ \text{Compressed Video Bitrate} = \dfrac{\text{Raw Bitrate}}{CR} = \dfrac{663552000}{100} = 6635520 \text{ bps} $$

**Step 6 ŌĆö Convert to kbps (Divide by 1000)**

$$ B_v = 6635.52 \text{ kbps} \approx 6.636 \text{ Mbps} $$

**Step 7 ŌĆö Calculate the Compressed Video File Size in Bits**

$$ \text{Size}_v = B_v \times T = 6635520 \times 300 = 1990656000 \text{ bits} $$

**Step 8 ŌĆö Calculate the Audio File Size in Bits**

$$ \text{Size}_a = B_a \times T = 128000 \times 300 = 38400000 \text{ bits} $$

**Step 9 ŌĆö Total File Size in Bits**

$$ \text{Size}_{\text{total}} = 1990656000 + 38400000 = 2029056000 \text{ bits} $$

**Step 10 ŌĆö Convert to Bytes (Divide by 8)**

$$ \text{Size}_{\text{total}} = \dfrac{2029056000}{8} = 253632000 \text{ bytes} $$

**Step 11 ŌĆö Convert to Megabytes (Divide by 1024┬▓)**

$$ \text{Size}_{\text{total}} = \dfrac{253632000}{1048576} \approx 241.88 \text{ MB} $$

### Final Answer
$$ \boxed{\text{Total File Size} \approx 241.88 \text{ MB} } $$

## 3.4 Comparison Matrix ŌĆö `autoplay` vs `loop` vs `muted`

| Attribute | Triggers Playback | Restarts on End | Silences Audio | Common Combo |

| `autoplay` | Ō£ģ Yes | ŌØī No | ŌØī No (unless also `muted`) | `autoplay muted loop` (background video) |

> [!NOTE]

## 3.5 Analytical Derivation ŌĆö Bandwidth Estimation for Streaming

### Problem Statement
> *"A web application serves a 10-minute 1080p video to 500 concurrent users. The video has a compressed bitrate of 5 Mbps. Calculate: (a) the per-user bandwidth requirement with 20% buffer overhead, (b) the total server-side bandwidth needed."*

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Given:**
- Duration: $T = 10 \text{ min} = 600 \text{ s}$
- Bitrate: $B = 5 \text{ Mbps}$
- Concurrent users: $N = 500$
- Buffer overhead: $O = 20\% = 0.20$

**Step 1 ŌĆö Per-User Bandwidth with Overhead**

$$ B_{\text{user}} = B \times (1 + O) $$

$$ B_{\text{user}} = 5 \times (1 + 0.20) = 5 \times 1.20 $$

$$ B_{\text{user}} = 6 \text{ Mbps} $$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Total Server Bandwidth**

$$ B_{\text{total}} = B_{\text{user}} \times N $$

$$ B_{\text{total}} = 6 \times 500 $$

$$ B_{\text{total}} = 3000 \text{ Mbps} = 3 \text{ Gbps} $$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Total Data Served in the 10-Minute Window**

$$ \text{Data} = B \times T = 5 \times 600 = 3000 \text{ Mb} = 375 \text{ MB per user} $$

### Final Answer
$$ \boxed{B_{\text{user}} = 6 \text{ Mbps}, \quad B_{\text{total}} = 3 \text{ Gbps}, \quad \text{Data/user} = 375 \text{ MB} } $$

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 HTML5 Media Playback Architecture ŌĆö Block Diagram

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the full architecture from HTML parsing to screen/audio output.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[HTML Document Parsed] --> B[Video or Audio Element Detected]
    B --> C{Read src or Source Children}
    C --> D[Check MIME Type against Browser Codec Matrix]
    D --> E{Browser Supports Format?}
    E -- No --> F[Try Next Source]
    F --> E
    E -- Yes --> G[Initiate Network Request with Range Header]
    G --> H[Server Responds with Media File Chunks]
    H --> I[Demux Container MP4 or WebM or Ogg]
    I --> J[Video Decoder H264 or VP9 or AV1]
    I --> K[Audio Decoder AAC or Opus or Vorbis]
    J --> L[Raw Video Frames YUV]
    K --> M[Raw Audio Samples PCM]
    L --> N[Render to Internal Canvas Surface]
    M --> O[Send to Audio Output Device]
    N --> P[User Views Video on Screen]
    O --> Q[User Hears Audio]
    P --> R[HTMLMediaElement Events fire: play pause timeupdate]
    Q --> R
    R --> S[JavaScript can Read or Control Playback]
```

---


## 4.3 Source Fallback Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Browser Encounters Video Element] --> ReadSources[Read Source Elements in Order]
    ReadSources --> S1[Source 1: MP4 H264 AAC]
    S1 --> Q1{Browser Supports MP4 H264 AAC?}
    Q1 -- Yes --> P1[Play MP4 Source]
    Q1 -- No --> S2[Source 2: WebM VP9 Opus]
    S2 --> Q2{Browser Supports WebM VP9 Opus?}

    S3 --> Q3{Browser Supports Ogg?}

```

## 4.4 ReadyState Progression State Machine

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> HAVE_NOTHING
    HAVE_NOTHING --> HAVE_METADATA: Metadata Loaded Duration and Dimensions Known
    HAVE_METADATA --> HAVE_CURRENT_DATA: Current Playback Position Available
    HAVE_CURRENT_DATA --> HAVE_FUTURE_DATA: Data Available for Current and Next Frames
    HAVE_FUTURE_DATA --> HAVE_ENOUGH_DATA: Sufficient Data to Play Through Without Stalling
    HAVE_ENOUGH_DATA --> [*]: Playback Completes
    HAVE_METADATA --> HAVE_NOTHING: Source Error or load Called
    HAVE_CURRENT_DATA --> HAVE_NOTHING: Source Error or load Called
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pearl:** The five `readyState` values (0ŌĆō4) map exactly to these states. Memorize: `0 = HAVE_NOTHING`, `1 = HAVE_METADATA`, `2 = HAVE_CURRENT_DATA`, `3 = HAVE_FUTURE_DATA`, `4 = HAVE_ENOUGH_DATA`.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2023]**
*Explain the role of the `controls` attribute in the HTML5 `<video>` element. What happens if it is omitted?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
The `controls` attribute is a boolean attribute of the HTML5 `<video>` and `<audio>` elements. When present, it instructs the browser to display its **native playback control interface**, which typically includes a play/pause button, a seekable progress bar, a volume slider, a fullscreen toggle, and (where supported) a closed-caption toggle.

If the `controls` attribute is **omitted**, the browser does **not** render any default controls. The video will appear as a blank or poster-only frame with no user-interactive playback affordances. Playback in this case can only be initiated programmatically via the JavaScript `play()` method triggered by a custom button or user event. For most practical web pages, omitting `controls` is undesirable unless a fully custom controls overlay is being built. **(3 Marks)**

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2022]**
*Differentiate between the `canplay` and `canplaythrough` events of the HTML5 media element.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
Both events are part of the `HTMLMediaElement` interface and signal media readiness, but they indicate **different thresholds of buffered data**:

- **`canplay` event:** Fires when the browser estimates that **enough media data has been loaded** to begin playback *at the current playback position*. However, further buffering may be required, and the user **may experience stalling** if the network is slow. This is the typical point at which developers call `play()` for best UX.

- **`canplaythrough` event:** Fires when the browser estimates that the **entire media file** can be played from start to finish **without interruption or buffering stalls**, given the current network conditions. This is a stronger guarantee and is useful for auto-playing media seamlessly.

In short: `canplay` = *can start now*. `canplaythrough` = *can finish uninterrupted*. **(3 Marks)**

---

## 5.2 PART B ŌĆö Essay Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **[KTU ESE Module Choice Pattern ŌĆö Select ANY ONE]**

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** ŌĆö Comprehensive Coverage
**[KTU University Exam ŌĆō June 2024 Model Paper]**

**(a)** Explain the HTML5 `<video>` element in detail. Discuss its important attributes such as `controls`, `autoplay`, `loop`, `muted`, `preload`, and `poster` with suitable examples. *(7 Marks)*

**(b)** Discuss the various video and audio file formats supported in HTML5. Explain the role of codecs and MIME types with examples. *(7 Marks)*

---

#### Model Solution for Part (a)

The HTML5 `<video>` element is the **standard, native mechanism** defined by the W3C HTML Living Standard for embedding video content in web pages. It eliminates the dependency on third-party plugins such as Adobe Flash, which was the dominant technology before HTML5.

**1. Basic Syntax:**
```html
<video src="clip.mp4" controls></video>
```

**2. Important Attributes:**

- **`controls` (boolean):** When present, the browser displays its built-in playback UI including play/pause, seek, volume, and fullscreen.

- **`autoplay` (boolean):** Starts playback as soon as the page loads and the video is ready. Modern browsers **mute** the video automatically for autoplay to succeed ŌĆö or require the developer to add the `muted` attribute. Example:
  ```html
  <video src="bg.mp4" autoplay muted loop></video>
  ```

- **`loop` (boolean):** Restarts the video automatically when it reaches the end, useful for background videos and ambient clips.

- **`muted` (boolean):** Silences the audio output. Required for autoplay on most modern browsers.

- **`preload` (string: `none` / `metadata` / `auto`):** Provides a hint to the browser about how much of the file to pre-fetch. `metadata` is the safest for pages with many videos.

- **`poster` (URL):** Specifies the URL of an image displayed in the video frame **before** playback begins ŌĆö analogous to a movie poster outside a cinema. Example:
  ```html
  <video src="lecture.mp4" poster="thumb.jpg" controls></video>
  ```

**3. Multiple Sources for Fallback:**
```html
<video controls poster="thumb.jpg" width="640" height="360">
    <source src="lecture.mp4" type="video/mp4">
    <source src="lecture.webm" type="video/webm">
    <source src="lecture.ogv" type="video/ogg">
    Your browser does not support HTML5 video.
</video>
```

**4. Accessibility Integration with `<track>`:**
The `<video>` element can host child `<track>` elements for captions, subtitles, and chapter markers, fulfilling WCAG accessibility mandates. **(7 Marks ŌĆö full credit breakdown: 1 for basic syntax, 2 for each of the 3 attribute groups, 1 for fallback, 1 for accessibility)**

---

#### Model Solution for Part (b)

**1. Video Containers and Codecs:**

A **container** is the file format wrapper; a **codec** is the compression algorithm. The two are independent ŌĆö the same codec can be wrapped in multiple containers.

| Container | Video Codec | Audio Codec | Browser Support |
|---|---|---|---|
| MP4 (`.mp4`) | H.264 | AAC | Universal |
| WebM (`.webm`) | VP8, VP9, AV1 | Vorbis, Opus | Chrome, Firefox, Edge, Opera |
| Ogg (`.ogv`) | Theora | Vorbis | Firefox, legacy Chrome |

**2. Audio Formats:**

- **MP3 (`audio/mpeg`):** Lossy, universally supported, the de-facto standard.
- **AAC (`audio/aac`):** Lossy, better quality than MP3 at the same bitrate, used in MP4 containers.
- **Ogg Vorbis (`audio/ogg`):** Open-source lossy alternative to MP3.
- **WAV (`audio/wav`):** Uncompressed PCM, high fidelity, large file size.
- **Opus (`audio/opus`):** Modern codec optimized for both music and speech.
- **FLAC (`audio/flac`):** Lossless compression, audiophile-grade.

**3. Role of MIME Types:**

The server must send the correct `Content-Type` HTTP header. If the MIME type is wrong, the browser will reject the file even if the codec is supported. Examples:
- `.mp4` ŌåÆ `video/mp4`
- `.webm` ŌåÆ `video/webm`
- `.mp3` ŌåÆ `audio/mpeg`
- `.ogg` ŌåÆ `audio/ogg`
- `.wav` ŌåÆ `audio/wav`

The `type` attribute on `<source>` provides a hint to the browser to **avoid downloading unsupported formats**, improving performance.

**4. Practical Recommendation:**
Use **MP4 (H.264 + AAC) as the primary format** and **WebM (VP9 + Opus) as the fallback**. This combination covers over 99% of modern browsers. **(7 Marks)**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** ŌĆö Alternative Choice
**[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the working of the HTML5 `<video>` element from page load to playback. *(7 Marks)*

**(b)** Explain the `HTMLMediaElement` JavaScript API. Discuss at least six properties and four methods with examples. *(7 Marks)*

---

#### Model Solution for Part (a)

The lifecycle of an HTML5 video from page load to playback can be described in **eight sequential stages**:

1. **HTML Parsing:** The browser parses the HTML and encounters the `<video>` element, noting its attributes (`src`, `controls`, `preload`, etc.).

2. **Source Resolution:** The browser identifies the media source from the `src` attribute or iterates through child `<source>` elements in document order.

3. **MIME Type Filtering:** For each `<source>`, the browser checks the `type` attribute against its internal codec support matrix. The first supported source is chosen.

4. **Resource Fetching:** A network request is sent (often with a `Range` header for partial content). The server responds with the media file in HTTP chunks.

5. **Demuxing:** The browser's media engine **demultiplexes** the container, separating the video track, audio track, and metadata.

6. **Decoding:** The video track is decoded by the video codec (e.g., H.264 hardware/software decoder), and the audio track is decoded by the audio codec (e.g., AAC). The output is raw YUV video frames and PCM audio samples.

7. **Rendering:** Video frames are painted to an internal canvas surface and displayed on screen. Audio samples are routed to the system's audio output device.

8. **Event Dispatch & JS Interaction:** Throughout the lifecycle, events such as `loadedmetadata`, `canplay`, `play`, `pause`, `timeupdate`, and `ended` are dispatched. JavaScript can subscribe to these events and call methods like `play()`, `pause()`, or read properties like `currentTime` to implement custom controls.

> **Block Diagram:** *(Refer to Section 4.1 Mermaid block diagram of this document for a visual representation.)* **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Part (b)

The `HTMLMediaElement` is a **DOM interface** implemented by both `<video>` and `<audio>`. It provides a rich set of properties, methods, and events for programmatic control.

**Properties (6 examples):**

1. **`currentTime` (number, seconds):** Returns or sets the current playback position.
   ```javascript
   video.currentTime = 30; // jump to 30 seconds
   ```

2. **`duration` (number, seconds):** Total length of the media in seconds. Returns `NaN` if metadata is not yet loaded.

3. **`paused` (boolean):** `true` if playback is currently paused.

4. **`volume` (number, 0.0ŌĆō1.0):** Audio volume level, where 1.0 is maximum.

5. **`muted` (boolean):** Indicates or sets the mute state.

6. **`playbackRate` (number):** Speed of playback, where 1.0 is normal, 2.0 is double speed, 0.5 is half speed.

**Methods (4 examples):**

1. **`play()`:** Starts or resumes playback. Returns a Promise that resolves when playback begins or rejects on error.
   ```javascript
   video.play().then(() => console.log("Playing"));
   ```

2. **`pause()`:** Pauses playback at the current position.

3. **`load()`:** Aborts the current load operation and restarts it from scratch ŌĆö useful when changing the `src` attribute dynamically.

4. **`canPlayType(MIME)`:** Tests if the browser can play a given MIME type. Returns `"probably"`, `"maybe"`, or `""`.
   ```javascript
   if (video.canPlayType('video/mp4') === "probably") { /* safe to use MP4 */ }
   ```

**Practical Example ŌĆö Custom Play/Pause Button:**
```javascript
const video = document.getElementById("myVideo");
const btn = document.getElementById("playBtn");
btn.addEventListener("click", () => {
    if (video.paused) { video.play(); btn.textContent = "Pause"; }
    else { video.pause(); btn.textContent = "Play"; }
});
```

This API enables designers to build custom video players matching a brand's visual identity while leveraging the browser's native decoding power. **(7 Marks)**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning ŌĆö Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Where Students Typically Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Confusing container with codec** ŌĆö Writing *"MP4 is a codec"* is wrong. MP4 is a **container**; H.264 is the **codec** inside it. Lose 1 mark.
>
> 2. **Forgetting MIME type** ŌĆö When asked to write a `<source>` tag, students often omit the `type` attribute. The `type` attribute is **strongly recommended** for performance. Lose 1 mark.
>
> 3. **Writing `autoplay` without `muted`** ŌĆö In 2024, this combination is **non-functional** in major browsers. Examiners expect awareness of modern autoplay policies. Lose 1 mark.
>
> 4. **Mixing up `poster` and `preload`** ŌĆö `poster` is a *visual thumbnail image*. `preload` is a *network bandwidth hint*. Frequently confused. Lose 1 mark.
>
> 5. **No fallback content** ŌĆö The text between `<video>...</video>` tags is the fallback shown to users on unsupported browsers. Omitting it is a **UX violation**. Lose 0.5ŌĆō1 mark.
>
> 6. **Incorrect `readyState` values** ŌĆö Students often write *"readyState returns a string"*. It is a **numeric** value from 0 to 4. Lose 1 mark.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- Ō£ģ HTML5 `<video>` and `<audio>` elements eliminate the need for third-party plugins.
- Ō£ģ The most universally supported video format is **MP4 with H.264 video + AAC audio**.
- Ō£ģ The most universally supported audio formats are **MP3, AAC, and WAV**.
- Ō£ģ Always provide **multiple `<source>` children** in MP4 ŌåÆ WebM ŌåÆ Ogg order for fallback.
- Ō£ģ The `type` attribute on `<source>` must specify the correct **MIME type** (e.g., `video/mp4`).
