- Ō£ģ `controls` enables the browser's native player UI; omit it only when building a custom UI.
- Ō£ģ `autoplay` requires `muted` in modern browsers (Chrome, Safari, Firefox).
- Ō£ģ `loop` restarts media at the end; `muted` silences audio; `preload` hints at bandwidth usage.
- Ō£ģ `poster` attribute (video only) shows a thumbnail image before playback.
- Ō£ģ The `HTMLMediaElement` interface provides `play()`, `pause()`, `load()`, `canPlayType()` methods.
- Ō£ģ Key properties: `currentTime`, `duration`, `paused`, `volume`, `muted`, `playbackRate`, `readyState`.
- Ō£ģ Key events: `play`, `pause`, `ended`, `timeupdate`, `loadedmetadata`, `canplay`, `canplaythrough`, `error`.
- Ō£ģ The `<track>` element provides **WebVTT-based** captions, subtitles, descriptions, and chapters.
- Ō£ģ Always provide fallback text inside the `<video>` or `<audio>` element for older browsers.
- Ō£ģ Formula to remember: **Compressed Bitrate = Raw Bitrate / Compression Ratio**.
- Ō£ģ Formula: **File Size (MB) = (Bitrate ├Ś Duration) / (8 ├Ś 1024 ├Ś 1024)**.
- Ō£ģ For accessibility, always add **captions** using `<track kind="captions" srclang="en">`.
- Ō£ģ WebVTT is the standard format for caption files with `.vtt` extension and `text/vtt` MIME type.

> **Final Exam Mantra:** *"MP4 + AAC + H.264 + `controls` + fallback sources + captions = a professional, accessible, browser-compatible web media experience."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Canvas
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEITT833 - WEB DESIGNING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Canvas

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **HTML5 `<canvas>` element** is a resolution-dependent bitmap canvas that is used to render **2D shapes, bitmaps, animations, and interactive graphics** dynamically at runtime through a scripting API (JavaScript). It was introduced in the **HTML5 specification (W3C/WHATWG, 2014)** as a successor to plug-in-based graphics (Flash, Silverlight) and serves as the foundational low-level drawing primitive for browser-based rendering, charting libraries, game engines, and image-manipulation tools.

The element itself is a *blank container*. The actual drawing capability is exposed through a **rendering context**, obtained via the JavaScript method:

```javascript
canvas.getContext("2d");
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** Per KTU 2024 OEITT833 Module 1, students must be able to (a) declare a canvas element with proper `width` and `height` attributes, (b) obtain the 2D rendering context, and (c) draw basic geometric primitives using the documented Canvas 2D API.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine an **artist's blank sheet of paper pinned to a wall** ŌĆö that sheet is your `<canvas>`. The HTML element creates the paper, but it has no drawings on it. Now imagine you are handed a **paintbrush, a set of colored paints, and a ruler** ŌĆö that is the **JavaScript Canvas API**.

- The paper does nothing on its own ŌĆö it just exists.
- Every line, color, or shape only appears **after you (JavaScript) actively tell it what to draw**.
- If you make a mistake, you cannot *undo* in the traditional sense ŌĆö you must **paint over** or **clear** a region.

This active, imperative model contrasts with **SVG (Scalable Vector Graphics)**, where the picture is described declaratively in XML and the browser maintains the DOM. Canvas is *fire-and-forget pixels* ŌĆö fast but harder to inspect.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Distinction:** Canvas = **raster** (pixel-based, resolution dependent). SVG = **vector** (mathematical shapes, infinitely scalable). For most KTU board questions, you are expected to know when to prefer one over the other.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Coordinate System

| Property | Value |
|---|---|
| **Default Canvas Size** | **300 ├Ś 150 pixels** (W3C specification default) |
| **Origin (0,0)** | **Top-left corner** of the canvas |
| **X-axis** | Increases toward the right |
| **Y-axis** | Increases **downward** (NOT upward, unlike math class) |
| **Rendering API** | Imperative, immediate-mode (drawn pixels cannot be queried back) |
| **Coordinate Unit** | 1 unit = 1 CSS pixel (by default) |
| **Rotation Convention** | **Radians**, clockwise from positive X-axis |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Canvas Rendering Pipeline

The Canvas API operates in a strict logical sequence. Understanding this pipeline is critical for KTU exam answers because questions often test *why* something renders incorrectly.

**Logical Steps (in order):**

1. **HTML Parsing** ŌĆö The browser encounters `<canvas width="500" height="400"></canvas>` and allocates a bitmap surface in GPU/CPU memory of those exact dimensions.
2. **Context Acquisition** ŌĆö JavaScript calls `canvas.getContext("2d")`. This returns a `CanvasRenderingContext2D` object ŌĆö the *paintbrush handle*.
3. **State Setup** ŌĆö Properties like `fillStyle`, `strokeStyle`, `lineWidth`, `font`, `globalAlpha` are configured. These constitute the *current drawing state*.
4. **Path Construction** ŌĆö A *path* is a sequence of sub-paths (lines, arcs, curves). The context builds an invisible *current path* using `beginPath()`, `moveTo()`, `lineTo()`, `arc()`, etc.
5. **Stroke / Fill** ŌĆö Once the path is complete, `stroke()` paints the outline, `fill()` paints the interior. The path is then closed internally.
6. **Pixel Rasterization** ŌĆö The browser converts vector path commands into pixel colors on the bitmap.
7. **Compositing** ŌĆö The canvas bitmap is composited onto the page during the next browser paint cycle.

> [!TIP]
> **Why It Matters:** Calling `fillStyle = "red"` AFTER `fill()` has no effect on the already-painted pixels. Students frequently lose marks in KTU exams by misordering state setup vs. drawing commands. Always: **set state ŌåÆ build path ŌåÆ stroke/fill.**

### 2.2 The 'Why' Behind Each Step

- **Why a separate context object?** It encapsulates stateful drawing operations, allowing multiple canvases on one page with independent styles.
- **Why `beginPath()`?** Without it, every new sub-path (like a fresh `lineTo`) would connect back to the previous drawing via an implicit line ŌĆö producing "spider-web" artifacts.
- **Why path then fill?** A single path can be filled, stroked, *and* clipped, allowing reuse. It also avoids redundant computation.

### 2.3 KTU Formula / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Syntax | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Canvas declaration | `<canvas id="c" width="500" height="400">` | Dimensions in **pixels** |
| Get 2D context | `ctx = canvas.getContext("2d")` | Returns `CanvasRenderingContext2D` |
| Filled rectangle | `ctx.fillRect(x, y, w, h)` | `(x, y)` is **top-left corner** |
| Outlined rectangle | `ctx.strokeRect(x, y, w, h)` | Uses `strokeStyle` color |
| Clear region | `ctx.clearRect(x, y, w, h)` | Makes pixels transparent |
| Straight line segment | `ctx.lineTo(x, y)` | Pairs with `moveTo(x0, y0)` |
| Arc (circle/partial) | `ctx.arc(x, y, r, startAngle, endAngle, anticlockwise)` | Angles in **radians** |
| Degree ŌåÆ Radian | `radians = degrees ├Ś (ŽĆ / 180)` | ŽĆ Ōēł **3.14159265** |
| Radian ŌåÆ Degree | `degrees = radians ├Ś (180 / ŽĆ)` | |
| Fill text | `ctx.fillText(str, x, y)` | Uses `font`, `fillStyle`, `textAlign` |
| Translation (move origin) | `ctx.translate(tx, ty)` | Cumulative ŌĆö order matters |
| Rotation | `ctx.rotate(angle)` | Angle in **radians**, about current origin |
| Scaling | `ctx.scale(sx, sy)` | `sx`, `sy` are multipliers |
| State save / restore | `ctx.save()` / `ctx.restore()` | Stack-based, LIFO |
| Linear gradient | `ctx.createLinearGradient(x0,y0, x1,y1)` | Define color stops |
| Radial gradient | `ctx.createRadialGradient(x0,y0,r0, x1,y1,r1)` | Inner & outer circles |
| Draw image | `ctx.drawImage(img, dx, dy, dw, dh)` | Image must be loaded |
| Animation loop | `requestAnimationFrame(callback)` | ~60 fps, browser-synced |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Angle Warning:** In Canvas, **all rotation and arc angles are in RADIANS**, not degrees. The only place degrees naturally appear is text orientation. Always convert using the formula above before calling `arc()` or `rotate()`.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The Canvas API underpins a vast ecosystem:

- **Data Visualization** ŌĆö Chart.js, ECharts, D3.js render graphs on Canvas for dashboards.
- **Web Games** ŌĆö Phaser, PixiJS, and most browser-based 2D games use Canvas (or its WebGL counterpart) for the render loop.
- **Image Editors** ŌĆö Photoshop Express, Canva's web editor, and Figma's bitmap layer use Canvas-based pixel manipulation.
- **Computer Vision in Browser** ŌĆö TensorFlow.js renders prediction overlays onto `<canvas>` elements.
- **PDF / Barcode Generation** ŌĆö jsPDF and JsBarcode rasterize directly to Canvas before exporting.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Examples

> [!NOTE]
> The "derivations" here are **logical step-by-step traces of Canvas drawing operations** ŌĆö analogous to algebraic derivation in math subjects, but applied to the Canvas 2D API. Each transition explicitly states *what changed in state* and *what pixels resulted*.

---

### 3.1 Worked Example A ŌĆö Drawing a Concentric Circle Pattern

**Problem Statement:**
Using the HTML5 Canvas API, draw three concentric circles centered at the canvas center (250, 250), with radii **50 px**, **100 px**, and **150 px**, in the colors **red**, **green**, and **blue** respectively. The canvas size is **500 ├Ś 500**.

#### Step 1 ŌĆö Declare the HTML structure

```html
<!DOCTYPE html>
<html>
<head><title>Canvas Demo</title></head>
<body>
  <canvas id="myCanvas" width="500" height="500"
          style="border:1px solid #000;">
    Your browser does not support the HTML5 Canvas element.
  </canvas>

  <script>
    // JavaScript will be added in the next steps
  </script>
</body>
</html>
```

*Reasoning:* The `width` and `height` attributes define the bitmap resolution. The `border` is a CSS visual aid only and does not affect the drawing surface. The text inside `<canvas>` is a **fallback** rendered only if Canvas is unsupported.

#### Step 2 ŌĆö Acquire the rendering context

```javascript
let canvas  = document.getElementById("myCanvas");
let ctx     = canvas.getContext("2d");
```

*Reasoning:* `getElementById` locates the DOM node. `getContext("2d")` returns the **only officially standardized drawing context for 2D Canvas**. (WebGL contexts use `"webgl"` or `"webgl2"`.)

#### Step 3 ŌĆö Compute the center coordinates

```javascript
let cx = canvas.width  / 2;   // = 250
let cy = canvas.height / 2;   // = 250
```

*Reasoning:* Dividing the canvas dimensions by 2 yields the geometric center in **pixel units**. This separates *data* (geometry) from *drawing* (presentation).

#### Step 4 ŌĆö Draw the outermost circle (radius 150, blue)

```javascript
ctx.beginPath();
ctx.arc(cx, cy, 150, 0, 2 * Math.PI, false);
ctx.fillStyle = "blue";
ctx.fill();
```

*Reasoning trace:*
- `beginPath()` discards any previous path ŌåÆ starts fresh.
- `arc(cx, cy, 150, 0, 2*Math.PI, false)` ŌåÆ a full revolution. Since a full circle = $2\pi$ radians, we use `2 * Math.PI`. The `false` flag means *clockwise*, but for a closed circle the direction is irrelevant.
- `fillStyle = "blue"` sets the *paint color* in the current state.
- `fill()` rasterizes the interior pixels in blue.

#### Step 5 ŌĆö Draw the middle circle (radius 100, green)

```javascript
ctx.beginPath();
ctx.arc(cx, cy, 100, 0, 2 * Math.PI, false);
ctx.fillStyle = "green";
ctx.fill();
```

*Reasoning:* Same as Step 4 with different radius and color. The new `beginPath()` is **critical** ŌĆö without it, the new arc would *connect* to the previous circle via a stray straight line.

#### Step 6 ŌĆö Draw the innermost circle (radius 50, red)

```javascript
ctx.beginPath();
ctx.arc(cx, cy, 50, 0, 2 * Math.PI, false);
ctx.fillStyle = "red";
ctx.fill();
```

#### Step 7 ŌĆö Complete program listing

```javascript
let canvas = document.getElementById("myCanvas");
let ctx    = canvas.getContext("2d");

let cx = canvas.width  / 2;
let cy = canvas.height / 2;

function drawCircle(x, y, r, color) {
    ctx.beginPath();
    ctx.arc(x, y, r, 0, 2 * Math.PI, false);
    ctx.fillStyle = color;
    ctx.fill();
}

drawCircle(cx, cy, 150, "blue");
drawCircle(cx, cy, 100, "green");
drawCircle(cx, cy,  50, "red");
```

*Refactoring reasoning:* Wrapping the draw logic into a function reduces repetition, isolates the *state-mutation pattern*, and is the standard idiom for KTU practical exams.

---

### 3.2 Worked Example B ŌĆö Smiley Face Using Paths

**Problem Statement:** Draw a yellow filled circle (face), two black filled small circles (eyes), and a curved path (smile) on a 400├Ś400 canvas centered at (200, 200).

#### Step 1 ŌĆö Face base (yellow filled disc, radius 150)

```javascript
ctx.beginPath();
ctx.arc(200, 200, 150, 0, 2 * Math.PI, false);
ctx.fillStyle = "#FFEB3B";
ctx.fill();

ctx.strokeStyle = "#000";
ctx.lineWidth   = 3;
ctx.stroke();
```

#### Step 2 ŌĆö Left eye (small filled circle)

```javascript
ctx.beginPath();
ctx.arc(155, 165, 15, 0, 2 * Math.PI, false);
ctx.fillStyle = "#000";
ctx.fill();
```

#### Step 3 ŌĆö Right eye (small filled circle)

```javascript
ctx.beginPath();
ctx.arc(245, 165, 15, 0, 2 * Math.PI, false);
ctx.fillStyle = "#000";
ctx.fill();
```

#### Step 4 ŌĆö Smile (arc path)

For the smile, we want only a *partial arc* (e.g., the lower half of a circle). Using radians:

```javascript
ctx.beginPath();
// Start angle: 0 rad (right side)
// End angle:   Math.PI rad (left side), going clockwise
ctx.arc(200, 200, 80, 0, Math.PI, false);
ctx.strokeStyle = "#000";
ctx.lineWidth   = 4;
ctx.stroke();
```

*Reasoning:* The arc spans **ŽĆ radians = 180┬░**, giving the lower semicircle. Note that the smile center is the *same* as the face center, but with a smaller radius (80) so the curve sits inside the face. We use `stroke()` (not `fill()`) because we want an *outline* curve.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix ŌĆö Method Categories

| Category | Primary Methods | Output Type | When to Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Rectangles** | `fillRect`, `strokeRect`, `clearRect` | Pre-built shapes | UI panels, charts bars |
| **Paths** | `beginPath`, `moveTo`, `lineTo`, `arc`, `closePath`, `fill`, `stroke` | Custom geometry | Polygons, custom shapes |
| **Curves** | `quadraticCurveTo`, `bezierCurveTo`, `arc` | Smooth shapes | Splines, organic shapes |
| **Text** | `fillText`, `strokeText`, `measureText` | Glyph rasterization | Labels, dynamic text |
| **Images** | `drawImage` | Bitmap blitting | Sprites, photos |
| **Gradients** | `createLinearGradient`, `createRadialGradient` | Color interpolation | Backgrounds, charts |
| **Transforms** | `translate`, `rotate`, `scale`, `setTransform` | Coordinate remapping | Rotated labels, zoom |
| **Compositing** | `globalAlpha`, `globalCompositeOperation` | Blend modes | Layered effects, transparency |
| **Animation** | `requestAnimationFrame`, state changes | Time-based redraw | Games, dashboards |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Golden Rule:** Every property that begins with `ctx.` (like `fillStyle`, `lineWidth`, `globalAlpha`) is part of the **drawing state**, which can be saved with `ctx.save()` and restored with `ctx.restore()`. The state is stored on a *stack*, so restores happen in **Last-In-First-Out** order.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Canvas Lifecycle Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[HTML Parser encounters canvas element] --> B[Allocate bitmap of width x height pixels]
    B --> C[Canvas element rendered as empty rectangle]
    C --> D[JavaScript runs script block]
    D --> E[document.getElementById finds canvas node]
    E --> F[getContext 2d returns rendering context]
    F --> G[Configure state: fillStyle strokeStyle lineWidth font]
    G --> H[Begin path with beginPath]
    H --> I[Build subpaths with moveTo lineTo arc]
    I --> J{Apply paint operation}
    J -->|fill| K[Rasterize interior pixels]
    J -->|stroke| L[Rasterize boundary pixels]
    J -->|clip| M[Restrict future drawing to path region]
    K --> N[Composite onto page]
    L --> N
    M --> N
    N --> O[Wait for next frame or user event]
    O --> P{New drawing needed?}
    P -->|Yes| G
    P -->|No| Q[Idle]
```

### 4.2 Canvas Coordinate System Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Canvas[500 x 500 Pixel Canvas]
        direction LR
        O[Origin 0,0 Top Left] -->|X axis increases| X[500, 0 Bottom Right]
        O -->|Y axis increases| Y[0, 500 Top Right is incorrect]
        O2[Center 250, 250]
    end
    X -.->|Y axis down| BR[500, 500 Bottom Right]
    Y -.->|Y axis down| TR[0, 500 Bottom Left]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Visual Interpretation:** In Canvas, (0,0) is the **top-left corner**. The Y-axis points **downward**. This is the opposite of the standard Cartesian math convention where Y points up. This is the **single most common source of off-by-coordinate bugs** in student Canvas code.

### 4.3 Save/Restore State Stack Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Initial state global] --> B[ctx.save snapshot1]
    B --> C[Change fillStyle to red]
    C --> D[Change lineWidth to 5]
    D --> E[Draw shape using red 5px outline]
    E --> F[ctx.save snapshot2]
    F --> G[Change fillStyle to blue]
    G --> H[Draw shape using blue fill]
    H --> I[ctx.restore restores to snapshot2]
    I --> J[State now: fillStyle red, lineWidth 5]
    J --> K[ctx.restore restores to snapshot1]
    K --> L[State now: original global]
```

*Interpretation:* Each `save()` pushes the **entire drawing state** onto a stack. Each `restore()` pops the most recent snapshot. This is essential when drawing complex scenes where temporary state changes (e.g., a rotated label) must be reverted without affecting other elements.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. `[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023, Model Paper Set A]`**
**Define the HTML5 `<canvas>` element. List any FOUR drawing methods supported by its 2D rendering context.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The HTML5 `<canvas>` element is a **resolution-dependent bitmap container** introduced in HTML5 that allows dynamic, scriptable rendering of 2D shapes, images, and animations via JavaScript. It is a low-level, immediate-mode drawing surface where pixels are committed once `stroke()` or `fill()` is called and cannot be individually inspected later.

The Canvas 2D context (`CanvasRenderingContext2D`) supports dozens of methods. Four important drawing methods are:

1. **`fillRect(x, y, w, h)`** ŌĆö Draws a filled rectangle.
2. **`strokeRect(x, y, w, h)`** ŌĆö Draws the outline of a rectangle.
3. **`arc(x, y, r, startAngle, endAngle, anticlockwise)`** ŌĆö Adds an arc to the current path.
4. **`fillText(text, x, y)`** ŌĆö Rasterizes text at the given baseline coordinates.

*(+1 mark for correct definition, +2 marks for accurate listing with one-line descriptions.)*

---

**Q2. `[KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2024, Supplementary Model]`**
**Differentiate between `fill()` and `stroke()` methods of the Canvas API. What state properties control their appearance?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | `fill()` | `stroke()` |
|---|---|---|
| **Purpose** | Paints the **interior** of the current path | Paints the **outline/boundary** of the current path |
| **Color source** | `fillStyle` property | `strokeStyle` property |
| **Other state** | `globalAlpha` | `lineWidth`, `lineCap`, `lineJoin`, `miterLimit`, `globalAlpha` |
| **Effect on text** | `fillText()` rasterizes glyph interiors | `strokeText()` outlines glyphs |
| **Typical use** | Solid shapes, filled text | Outlined shapes, wireframes |

*(+1 mark for conceptual difference, +1 mark for state property identification, +1 mark for typical use.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B ŌĆö Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

### Question A (14 Marks)

**`[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2024, Expected Pattern]`**

**(a)** Explain the **HTML5 Canvas coordinate system** in detail. How does it differ from the standard Cartesian coordinate system used in mathematics? With the help of a properly formatted JavaScript snippet, demonstrate how to draw a **200 ├Ś 100 pixel rectangle** filled with the color **green** at position **(50, 75)** on a **400 ├Ś 300** canvas. (7 Marks)

**(b)** Explain the concepts of **Canvas translation, rotation, and scaling**. Write a complete JavaScript program that draws a **square rotated by 45 degrees** around its own center on a 300 ├Ś 300 canvas. State and justify the use of `save()` and `restore()` in your program. (7 Marks)

---

#### Model Solution ŌĆö Q.A(a) (7 Marks)

**Step 1 ŌĆö Coordinate System Explanation (3 Marks):**

The HTML5 Canvas coordinate system has its **origin (0, 0)** at the **top-left corner** of the canvas element. The **X-axis** increases horizontally toward the right, and the **Y-axis** increases vertically **downward**. Each unit corresponds to one **CSS pixel** of the canvas bitmap (by default, when CSS sizing matches attribute sizing).

**Differences from Cartesian coordinates:**

| Property | Cartesian Math | Canvas 2D |
|---|---|---|
| Origin | Bottom-left (by convention) | **Top-left** |
| Y direction | Up is positive | **Down is positive** |
| Angle measure | Usually degrees | **Radians** |
| Rotation sense | Counter-clockwise positive | **Clockwise positive** |

These differences mean that drawing "above" a point in math becomes "with a smaller Y value" in Canvas, and rotation by 30┬░ clockwise visually equals rotation by $+\frac{\pi}{6}$ radians.

**Step 2 ŌĆö HTML setup (1 Mark):**

```html
<canvas id="c1" width="400" height="300"
        style="border:1px solid black;"></canvas>
```

**Step 3 ŌĆö JavaScript drawing code (3 Marks):**

```javascript
let canvas = document.getElementById("c1");
let ctx    = canvas.getContext("2d");

// Set fill style to green
ctx.fillStyle = "#00AA00";   // standard HTML green

// Draw the filled rectangle
ctx.fillRect(50, 75, 200, 100);
```

*Explanation:* `fillRect(50, 75, 200, 100)` interprets its arguments as `(x, y, width, height)` in **pixel units**. So the top-left of the rectangle sits at (50, 75), and its bottom-right corner sits at (50+200, 75+100) = **(250, 175)**. The interior 200├Ś100 = 20,000 pixels are rasterized in the fillStyle color.

---

#### Model Solution ŌĆö Q.A(b) (7 Marks)

**Step 1 ŌĆö Concept Explanation (2 Marks):**

- **Translation** ŌĆö `ctx.translate(tx, ty)` *moves the origin* of the coordinate system. After translating, every subsequent drawing command is interpreted relative to the *new* origin. Useful for placing reusable shapes at different locations.
- **Rotation** ŌĆö `ctx.rotate(angle)` rotates the coordinate system by `angle` **radians clockwise** about the current origin. Important: rotations accumulate.
- **Scaling** ŌĆö `ctx.scale(sx, sy)` multiplies the unit length along each axis. `scale(2, 2)` makes everything draw twice as large.
- **save()/restore()** ŌĆö These methods push/pop the *entire drawing state* (including transformations, styles, line widths) onto an internal stack. They prevent transformation leakage between independent drawing operations.

**Step 2 ŌĆö Program (5 Marks):**

```javascript
let canvas = document.getElementById("rot");
let ctx    = canvas.getContext("2d");

let size = 100;          // side length of the square
let cx   = 150;          // canvas center X
let cy   = 150;          // canvas center Y

ctx.save();              // PUSH state 1 (clean canvas)

// Step A: move origin to desired rotation pivot
ctx.translate(cx, cy);

// Step B: rotate by 45 degrees = ŽĆ/4 radians
ctx.rotate(Math.PI / 4);

// Step C: draw square centered on the (now translated/rotated) origin
//         Square corners: (-size/2, -size/2) to (size/2, size/2)
ctx.fillStyle = "#1976D2";
ctx.fillRect(-size / 2, -size / 2, size, size);

// Optional outline for clarity
ctx.strokeStyle = "#000";
ctx.lineWidth   = 2;
ctx.strokeRect(-size / 2, -size / 2, size, size);

ctx.restore();           // POP state 1 ŌĆö back to original orientation
```

**Justification of save()/restore():**

Without `ctx.save()` before translation/rotation and `ctx.restore()` afterward, **any subsequent drawing on this canvas (e.g., drawing an axis label, a second shape) would inherit the rotation**, producing sideways text and further rotated geometry. The pair isolates the rotation to the single square, restoring the original coordinate system for whatever is drawn next.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning (Part B):** A common mistake students make is computing rotation around the wrong pivot. Rotating around `(0,0)` instead of the *center* of the shape will cause the square to swing in a large arc instead of spinning in place. The fix is to **translate first, then rotate, then draw centered on the new origin**. Marks are awarded specifically for this correct ordering ŌĆö failing to demonstrate it costs 2ŌĆō3 marks.

---

### Question B ŌĆö Alternative Choice (14 Marks)

**`[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2024, Expected Pattern]`**

**(a)** What are **Canvas paths**? Explain the role of `beginPath()`, `moveTo()`, `lineTo()`, and `closePath()` with a clear example. Write a JavaScript program to draw a **hollow star (5-pointed outline only)** on a 400 ├Ś 400 canvas. (7 Marks)

**(b)** Explain the concept of **Canvas gradients** (linear and radial). Write a JavaScript program that fills a 300 ├Ś 200 rectangle with a **linear gradient transitioning from red (left) to blue (right)** and fills a **circle of radius 80 centered at (150, 100)** with a **radial gradient transitioning from white (center) to black (edge)**. (7 Marks)

---

#### Model Solution ŌĆö Q.B(a) (7 Marks)

**Conceptual Explanation (2 Marks):**

A **path** in Canvas is an abstract sequence of geometric sub-paths (straight lines, arcs, B├®zier curves). It exists *in the context's internal memory* until a paint operation (`fill()` or `stroke()`) commits it to pixels. Key methods:

- **`beginPath()`** ŌĆö Discards the current path and starts a fresh one. Essential to prevent unwanted "spider web" lines connecting consecutive drawings.
- **`moveTo(x, y)`** ŌĆö Lifts the "pen" and moves it to `(x, y)` without drawing a line.
- **`lineTo(x, y)`** ŌĆö Draws a straight line from the *current pen position* to `(x, y)` and updates the pen position.
- **`closePath()`** ŌĆö Draws a straight line from the current position back to the *most recent* `moveTo` location, closing the sub-path.

**Star Geometry (2 Marks):**

A 5-pointed star consists of **10 vertices** alternating between an **outer radius** $R$ and an **inner radius** $r$. If $R = 150$ and $r = 60$, centered at canvas center $(200, 200)$, the i-th vertex angle is:

$$\theta_i = -\frac{\pi}{2} + i \cdot \frac{\pi}{5}, \quad i = 0, 1, 2, \ldots, 9$$

The $i=0$ vertex points straight up ($-\pi/2$ rotation).

The vertex coordinates are:

$$x_i = cx + \rho_i \cos(\theta_i)$$
$$y_i = cy + \rho_i \sin(\theta_i)$$

where $\rho_i = R$ if $i$ is even, else $\rho_i = r$.

**Complete JavaScript Program (3 Marks):**

```javascript
let canvas = document.getElementById("starCanvas");
let ctx    = canvas.getContext("2d");

let cx = 200, cy = 200;
let R  = 150, r  = 60;
let points = 5;

ctx.beginPath();

for (let i = 0; i < 2 * points; i++) {
    let radius   = (i % 2 === 0) ? R : r;
    let angle    = -Math.PI / 2 + i * Math.PI / points;
    let x        = cx + radius * Math.cos(angle);
    let y        = cy + radius * Math.sin(angle);

    if (i === 0) {
        ctx.moveTo(x, y);     // lift pen for the very first vertex
    } else {
        ctx.lineTo(x, y);     // draw line to next vertex
    }
}

ctx.closePath();             // connect final vertex back to moveTo

ctx.strokeStyle = "#FF9800"; // orange outline
ctx.lineWidth   = 3;
ctx.stroke();                // paint outline only ŌĆö hollow star

// Optional: also fill the interior
ctx.fillStyle = "rgba(255, 152, 0, 0.25)";
ctx.fill();
```

---

#### Model Solution ŌĆö Q.B(b) (7 Marks)

**Gradient Theory (2 Marks):**

A **gradient** in Canvas is a smooth color transition defined by **color stops** along a geometric axis.

- **`createLinearGradient(x0, y0, x1, y1)`** ŌĆö Defines a gradient that proceeds along the straight line from point $(x_0, y_0)$ to point $(x_1, y_1)$. Colors interpolate linearly between stops.
- **`createRadialGradient(x0, y0, r0, x1, y1, r1)`** ŌĆö Defines a gradient between two concentric circles (inner at $(x_0, y_0)$ with radius $r_0$, outer at $(x_1, y_1)$ with radius $r_1$).

After creation, color stops are added with `gradient.addColorStop(offset, color)` where `offset Ōłł [0, 1]`. The gradient is then assigned to `fillStyle` or `strokeStyle`.

**Complete Program (5 Marks):**

```javascript
let canvas = document.getElementById("gradCanvas");
let ctx    = canvas.getContext("2d");

// ---- (1) Linear gradient rectangle (red -> blue, left to right) ----
let linGrad = ctx.createLinearGradient(0, 0, 300, 0);
linGrad.addColorStop(0,    "red");
linGrad.addColorStop(0.5,  "purple");    // intermediate stop (optional)
linGrad.addColorStop(1,    "blue");

ctx.fillStyle = linGrad;
ctx.fillRect(0, 0, 300, 200);

// ---- (2) Radial gradient circle (white center -> black edge) ----
let radGrad = ctx.createRadialGradient(
    150, 100, 0,       // inner circle (degenerate at center, r=0)
    150, 100, 80       // outer circle (centered same, r=80)
);
radGrad.addColorStop(0,   "white");
radGrad.addColorStop(0.7, "#888888");    // mid gray transition
radGrad.addColorStop(1,   "black");

ctx.fillStyle = radGrad;
ctx.beginPath();
ctx.arc(150, 100, 80, 0, 2 * Math.PI, false);
ctx.fill();
```

**Explanation of the Radial Gradient:** Both the inner and outer circle centers are placed at the *same* point $(150, 100)$. The inner radius is **0** (a degenerate point that becomes the white "core"), while the outer radius is **80** (the circle's actual boundary, becoming pure black). Color stops at offsets 0, 0.7, and 1 produce a smooth radial fade.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Part B):** Students frequently write `ctx.fillStyle = "createLinearGradient(...)"` (treating it as a string) instead of assigning the *gradient object* directly. Canvas gradients are **objects**, not strings. The valid assignment is `ctx.fillStyle = linGrad;` with no quotes. Examiners specifically check this ŌĆö writing it incorrectly costs a full mark in the code-output column.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

**Rapid Revision Checklist (Module 1 ŌĆö Canvas):**

- **Definition** ŌĆö `<canvas>` is an HTML5 bitmap element; it is *blank* until JavaScript draws on it via `getContext("2d")`.
- **Coordinate System** ŌĆö Origin at **top-left**, **Y-axis points down**, units are **CSS pixels**.
- **Default Size** ŌĆö **300 ├Ś 150** if `width`/`height` attributes are omitted.
- **Angle Units** ŌĆö All `arc()` and `rotate()` calls use **radians**. Convert with `radians = degrees ├Ś ŽĆ / 180`.
- **Path Lifecycle** ŌĆö Always: `beginPath() ŌåÆ moveTo() ŌåÆ lineTo()/arc() ... ŌåÆ stroke()` **OR** `fill()`.
- **fillStyle vs strokeStyle** ŌĆö `fillStyle` controls interior color (used by `fill()`), `strokeStyle` controls outline color (used by `stroke()`).
- **State Management** ŌĆö Use `ctx.save()` and `ctx.restore()` to isolate transformations and styles.
- **Transform Order** ŌĆö When drawing rotated shapes: **translate ŌåÆ rotate ŌåÆ draw** around the new origin.
- **Gradients** ŌĆö Linear and radial gradients are **objects**, assigned directly to `fillStyle` (no quotes).
- **Animation** ŌĆö Use `requestAnimationFrame()` for ~60 fps smooth loops; mutate state and redraw each frame.
- **Canvas vs SVG** ŌĆö Canvas = raster, imperative, fast, not DOM-inspectable. SVG = vector, declarative, scalable, DOM-inspectable.
- **Resolution Dependence** ŌĆö Canvas bitmap does **not** auto-scale. To support Retina displays, scale the canvas dimensions by `devicePixelRatio` and apply a compensating `ctx.scale(dpr, dpr)`.
- **Security Note** ŌĆö Canvas pixels may be tainted by cross-origin images, causing `getImageData()` to throw a security error.

**Single-Line Mantra for Last-Minute Revision:**
> *"Canvas = blank paper + JavaScript paintbrush; set state, build path, fill or stroke ŌĆö always in radians, always save before transforming."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Floating and Positioning: Normal Flow, Floating, Fancy Text Wrap with CSS Shapes, Positioning Basics, Relative Positioning, Absolute Positioning, Fixed Positioning
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEITT833 - WEB DESIGNING > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Floating and Positioning: Normal Flow, Floating, Fancy Text Wrap with CSS Shapes, Positioning Basics, Relative Positioning, Absolute Positioning, Fixed Positioning ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview In CSS (Cascading Style Sheets), **layout** is the process of placing elements on a web page. Before Flexbox and Grid became dominant, two foundational techniques governed page layout: the **Normal Flow** (the default), and the two override systems ŌĆö **Floating** and **Positioning**. Mastering these three pillars is mandatory for the KTU OEITT833 examination because they form the conceptual base upon which modern layout systems are built. ### 1.1 Normal Flow (The Default Layout) **Formal Definition:** *Normal flow* is the default rendering model of CSS in which **block-level elements** (e.g., `
`, `

`, `

`) are stacked vertically one after another, occupying the full available width, while **inline elements** (e.g., ``, ``, ``) flow horizontally within the line, wrapping at the parent's edge. > [!NOTE] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Normal flow is the *baseline* against which `float`, `position: relative`, `position: absolute`, and `position: fixed` deviate. Examiner questions frequently test what happens *when you don't specify* any of these properties. **Conceptual Analogy:** Think of a **newspaper article printed in a single narrow column**. Sentences (inline elements) read left-to-right and wrap automatically. Paragraphs (block elements) start on a new line. This is exactly normal flow. ### 1.2 Floating (The "Push to the Side" Mechanism) **Formal Definition:** The `float` CSS property **removes an element from normal flow** and pushes it to the **left or right edge** of its containing block, allowing subsequent content (typically text) to **wrap around it**. Valid values are `left`, `right`, `none`, `inherit`, and `inline-start`/`inline-end`. **Conceptual Analogy:** Imagine a **photograph dropped into a paragraph of a magazine**. The text naturally flows *around* the photo. The photo has been "floated" ŌĆö it's lifted out of the line and pushed to the side. ### 1.3 Fancy Text Wrap with CSS Shapes **Formal Definition:** The **CSS Shapes Module** (Level 1 / Level 2) extends `float` by allowing wrapped content to follow *non-rectangular* contours using properties like `shape-outside`, `shape-image-threshold`, and `shape-margin`. **Conceptual Analogy:** A **circular badge** in a magazine article ŌĆö text wraps around the *curve* of the circle, not a square box. ### 1.4 Positioning Basics **Formal Definition:** The `position` property selects a **positioning scheme** for an element. The five values are `static` (default ŌĆö normal flow), `relative`, `absolute`, `fixed`, and `sticky`. Each value changes how the four offset properties (`top`, `right`, `bottom`, `left`) and the element's containing block behave. ### 1.5 Relative, Absolute, and Fixed Positioning - **Relative Positioning:** The element is shifted *relative to where it would have been* in normal flow, but **its original space is preserved**. - **Absolute Positioning:** The element is **removed from normal flow entirely** and positioned relative to its nearest *positioned ancestor* (an ancestor whose `position` is anything other than `static`). - **Fixed Positioning:** The element is **removed from normal flow** and positioned relative to the **viewport** (browser window). It does *not* move on scroll. **Conceptual Analogy:** - *Relative* ŌåÆ A person who **steps one step aside** in a queue; the queue (normal flow) still has the gap. - *Absolute* ŌåÆ A person who **leaves the queue** and stands at a fixed spot on a marked floor tile (the containing block). - *Fixed* ŌåÆ A person who **leaves the queue** and stands at a fixed spot **on the building itself** (the viewport), staying in the same screen position even as the queue scrolls past. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Pattern:** A common 3-mark question asks to differentiate between `relative`, `absolute`, and `fixed`. Memorize the *containing block* and the *effect on normal flow* for each. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet ### 2.1 The Three Layout Pillars ŌĆö Conceptual Breakdown #### A. Normal Flow ŌĆö Step-by-Step Logic 1. The browser reads the HTML top-to-bottom. 2. Each **block element** generates a new line box that is as wide as the parent and as tall as its content. 3. Each **inline element** is placed on the current line, sharing horizontal space. 4. No element is taken "out of the line" ŌĆö everything occupies its natural slot. #### B. Floating ŌĆö Step-by-Step Logic 1. Apply `float: left;` (or `right`) to an element. 2. The element is **shifted** to the specified edge of its containing block. 3. The element is taken out of normal flow **vertically** ŌĆö siblings *below* it will reflow into the vacated space. 4. **Non-floated inline content** (text) wraps around the floated element on the opposite side. 5. **Non-floated block content** (a `
`) ignores the float *unless* `clear` is applied or the float is contained (clearfix). 6. The `clear` property (`left`, `right`, `both`, `none`) forces an element to start *below* the specified floated elements. #### C. Positioning ŌĆö Step-by-Step Logic 1. Default `position: static` ŌåÆ element obeys normal flow; offset properties are ignored. 2. `position: relative` ŌåÆ element stays in normal flow but can be **offset** by `top/right/bottom/left`; **its space is reserved**; it becomes a *positioning context* for absolutely positioned descendants. 3. `position: absolute` ŌåÆ element is **removed from normal flow**; positioned relative to the nearest positioned ancestor; if none exists, relative to the **initial containing block** (the viewport-equivalent root). 4. `position: fixed` ŌåÆ element is **removed from normal flow**; positioned relative to the **viewport**; it scrolls *with* the page movement but stays pinned; ignores ancestor transforms in most practical cases. 5. Use `z-index` to control the **stacking order** of positioned elements (only works on positioned elements ŌĆö `position: static` ignores it). ### 2.2 CSS Shapes ŌĆö The "Fancy Wrap" Logic 1. Set `float: left;` on the image (a float is required for shapes to take effect). 2. Apply `shape-outside: circle(50%)` (or `ellipse()`, `polygon()`, `url(image.png)`). 3. Wrapped inline content follows the contour, not the bounding box. 4. `shape-image-threshold` defines the alpha transparency cutoff (0.0 to 1.0). 5. `shape-margin` adds padding between the shape and the wrapped text. ### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet ŌĆö Property Reference | Property | Allowed Values | Effect on Normal Flow | Containing Block | Common Use Case | |---|---|---|---|---| | `position: static` | Default | Stays in normal flow | N/A | Default layout | | `position: relative` | + `top/right/bottom/left` | **Stays in flow** (space reserved) | Itself | Nudge icons, tooltip anchors | | `position: absolute` | + `top/right/bottom/left` | **Removed from flow** | Nearest positioned ancestor | Modals, dropdowns, badges | | `position: fixed` | + `top/right/bottom/left` | **Removed from flow** | **Viewport** | Sticky navbars, chat widgets | | `float: left/right` | N/A | **Partially removed** | Parent block | Image wrap, columns | | `clear: left/right/both` | N/A | Forces below floats | N/A | Fix parent collapse | | `shape-outside` | `circle()`, `ellipse()`, `polygon()`, `url()` | Requires float | Parent block | Magazine-style wraps | | `z-index` | Integer (can be negative) | Stack order | N/A | Layered UI | > [!NOTE] > **KTU Pitfall:** Many students incorrectly think `float` removes the element *completely* from flow. In reality, the floated element still occupies a line box that other inline content wraps around ŌĆö only its **block-level siblings** act as if it isn't there (unless cleared). ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Relative positioning** is the invisible "anchor" behind tooltips, dropdowns, and form validation bubbles. - **Absolute positioning** underpins nearly every modal dialog, image-overlay caption, and custom toast notification in production. - **Fixed positioning** is the workhorse of "sticky" navigation bars (pre-`position: sticky` era) and back-to-top buttons. - **CSS Shapes** power editorial sites like *The New York Times* and *Medium* for magazine-style article layouts. ## 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Demonstrations Because this module is **declarative CSS** (not algebra), the "derivations" below are **layout transformations** ŌĆö each step explicitly shows the *before* and *after* state of a rendered element. ### 3.1 Demonstration 1: Normal Flow ŌåÆ Float Layout **HTML Source:** ```html

This paragraph wraps around the image in normal flow.

This paragraph also wraps until cleared.

``` **Step-by-step Transformation:** 1. **Initial state (Normal Flow):** The `` and both `

` tags stack vertically as block boxes. 2. **Apply `float: left;` to `.floated`:** The image is lifted to the left edge. Its original block-level slot is **vacated**. 3. **First `

` reacts:** Inline text content wraps to the **right of the image**. 4. **Second `

` (`.next`) reacts:** Without `clear`, it also wraps. To force it *below* the image, add `clear: left;`. **Resulting CSS:** ```css .floated { float: left; margin-right: 10px; } .next { clear: left; } ``` **The Containing Block Equation:** $$ \text{Containing Block of a Float} = \text{Nearest Block-level Ancestor} $$ ### 3.2 Demonstration 2: Relative Positioning Offset **The Offset Equation (CSS Specification):** $$ \text{Visible Position} = \text{Normal Flow Position} + (top, right, bottom, left) $$ **Worked Numerical Example:** Given an element with `position: relative; top: 20px; left: 30px;`: 1. **Normal flow position:** Element is at coordinates $(0, 0)$ of its parent. 2. **Apply offset:** The visible top-left corner moves to $(30, 20)$. 3. **The original slot at $(0, 0)$ is preserved** ŌĆö siblings are not affected. $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Visual Top} &= \text{Flow Top} + top \\ &= 0 + 20 = 20 \text{ px} \\ \text{Visual Left} &= \text{Flow Left} + left \\ &= 0 + 30 = 30 \text{ px} \end{aligned} $$ ### 3.3 Demonstration 3: Absolute Positioning ŌĆö Containing Block Resolution **The Containing Block Resolution Algorithm:** $$ \text{Containing Block} = \begin{cases} \text{Viewport (initial CB)} & \text{if no positioned ancestor exists} \\ \text{Nearest ancestor with } position \in \{relative, absolute, fixed, sticky\} & \text{otherwise} \end{cases} $$ **Step-by-step Trace:** Given the following structure: ```html

Hello
``` 1. `.child` is `position: absolute`. The browser must find its containing block. 2. Search ancestors: `.parent` has `position: relative` ŌåÆ **stop here**. 3. `.child`'s containing block = `.parent`. 4. `top: 10px` means the top edge of `.child` is **10px from the top padding edge of `.parent`**. 5. `left: 20px` means the left edge of `.child` is **20px from the left padding edge of `.parent`**. **Counter-example (fallback to viewport):** If `.parent` were `position: static` and `.grandparent` were also `static`, then `.child`'s containing block becomes the **initial containing block (the viewport-equivalent HTML root)**. ### 3.4 Demonstration 4: Fixed Positioning Persistence Equation $$ \text{Fixed Element Position} = \text{Viewport-relative Offset} \quad (\text{invariant under scroll}) $$ **Step-by-step Behavior:** 1. Apply `position: fixed; top: 0; left: 0;` to a navbar. 2. Element is removed from normal flow. 3. As the user scrolls the page by $\Delta y$ pixels, the navbar's screen coordinates **do not change** ŌĆö it remains pinned to $(0, 0)$ of the viewport. 4. Content scrolling beneath it passes *behind* it (controlled by `z-index`). ### 3.5 Demonstration 5: CSS Shapes ŌĆö Geometric Wrap **HTML:** ```html

Curved text follows the circle's contour...

``` **CSS:** ```css .shape-img { float: left; width: 200px; height: 200px; shape-outside: circle(50%); shape-margin: 15px; } ``` **Trace:** 1. `float: left` is **mandatory** ŌĆö without it, `shape-outside` is ignored. 2. `circle(50%)` uses the element's own dimensions to define a circle at 50% radius. 3. `shape-margin: 15px` pushes wrapped text **15px away** from the shape edge, creating a buffer. 4. The text now wraps along a **circular contour**, not a rectangular box. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Trap:** Students often write `shape-outside` on a non-floated element. The CSS specification **requires** the element to be floated for `shape-outside` to take effect. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Diagram 1: The CSS Layout Decision Tree This flowchart shows how the browser decides where to place a given element. ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Element Encountered] --> B{position property set?} B -- No, default static --> C[Normal Flow] B -- Yes, value is --> D{Which value?} D -- relative --> E[Stay in flow + offset by top/right/bottom/left] D -- absolute --> F[Remove from flow + position to nearest positioned ancestor] D -- fixed --> G[Remove from flow + position to viewport] D -- sticky --> H[Hybrid: relative until scroll threshold, then fixed] C --> I{float property set?} I -- No --> J[Default block stack] I -- Yes, left or right --> K[Shift to edge + wrap inline content] K --> L{shape-outside set?} L -- No --> M[Rectangular wrap] L -- Yes --> N[Geometric / image-based wrap] ``` ### 4.2 Diagram 2: Containing Block Resolution Chain A block-level architecture showing how the containing block is resolved for an absolutely positioned element. ```mermaid flowchart TD R[Viewport / Initial Containing Block] --> G1[Grandparent: position static] G1 --> P1[Parent: position static] P1 --> C1[Child: position absolute] C1 --> Q1[Result: CB = Viewport] R2[Viewport / Initial Containing Block] --> G2[Grandparent: position static] G2 --> P2[Parent: position relative] P2 --> C2[Child: position absolute] C2 --> Q2[Result: CB = Parent] ``` ### 4.3 Diagram 3: Float Layout ŌĆö Box Interaction Map ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Parent Container] --> B[Floated Image: float left] A --> C[Paragraph 1: inline wraps right of image] A --> D[Paragraph 2: clear left - drops below image] B -. occupies .-> E[Left edge of parent] C -. flows around .-> B D -. forced below .-> B ``` ### 4.4 Diagram 4: Positioning Scheme Comparison Block ```mermaid flowchart TD subgraph StaticFlow["STATIC (Default)"] S1[In normal flow] S2[No offset properties work] S3[No z-index effect] end subgraph RelativePos["RELATIVE"] R1[In normal flow + offset] R2[Space reserved] R3[Anchor for absolute children] end subgraph AbsolutePos["ABSOLUTE"] A1[Removed from normal flow] A2[Positioned to nearest positioned ancestor] A3[No space reserved] end subgraph FixedPos["FIXED"] F1[Removed from normal flow] F2[Positioned to viewport] F3[Stays on scroll] end ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### Part A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam ŌĆō Model Question]** Define the term **"normal flow"** in CSS. How does a floated element interact with the normal flow of its sibling elements? **Model Answer (3 marks):** *Normal flow* is the default CSS rendering scheme in which **block-level elements** (e.g., `
`, `

`) are laid out vertically, each occupying the full available width of the parent, while **inline elements** (e.g., ``, ``) flow horizontally within the line and wrap at the parent's edge. When an element is given `float: left` or `float: right`, it is **partially removed from normal flow** ŌĆö it is pushed to the specified edge of its containing block, and **subsequent inline content wraps around it**. However, **block-level siblings** of the floated element will reflow into the space as if the floated element were not there, unless the `clear` property is applied. **(3 marks)** --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam ŌĆō Model Question]** Differentiate between `position: relative`, `position: absolute`, and `position: fixed` based on their containing block and effect on normal flow. **Model Answer (3 marks):** - **`position: relative`:** The element remains in normal flow, its original space is **preserved**, and it can be offset using `top/right/bottom/left` relative to **itself**. - **`position: absolute`:** The element is **removed from normal flow**, no space is reserved for it, and it is positioned relative to its **nearest positioned ancestor** (or the initial containing block if none exists). - **`position: fixed`:** The element is **removed from normal flow** and is positioned relative to the **viewport**; it stays pinned to the screen even when the page is scrolled. **(3 marks)** --- ### Part B ŌĆö Full-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > **KTU Pattern:** Each Part B question has internal choice. You must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B. Each carries sub-parts of 7 + 7 = 14 marks. --- #### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam ŌĆō Model Question]** **(a)** Explain the CSS **Floating** mechanism with its `float` and `clear` properties. Write a complete HTML + CSS example showing an image floated to the left with text wrapping around it, and another paragraph forced below the image using `clear`. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Explain **CSS Shapes** with the `shape-outside` property. Provide a working example demonstrating circular text wrap with `circle()`, and mention the role of `shape-margin` and `shape-image-threshold`. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **(a) Floating Mechanism (7 marks):** The `float` property removes an element from normal flow and positions it to the left or right edge of its containing block, allowing **inline content** to wrap around it. Values include `left`, `right`, `none`, `inherit`. The `clear` property stops wrapping by forcing the element to appear **below** the specified floated elements (`left`, `right`, or `both`). **HTML:** ```html

This is the first paragraph. The text wraps neatly around the floated image on the right.

This paragraph is forced below the image.

``` **CSS:** ```css .float-img { float: left; width: 150px; margin: 0 12px 8px 0; } .new-section { clear: left; } ``` **Explanation of flow:** 1. `.float-img` is floated left ŌåÆ it moves to the left edge of `.article`. 2. The first `

` text wraps to the **right** of the image. 3. `.new-section` uses `clear: left` to **break the wrap** and start on a new line below the image. **(7 marks)** --- **(b) CSS Shapes (7 marks):** CSS Shapes extend floating by allowing text to wrap around **non-rectangular contours** such as circles, ellipses, polygons, or image alpha channels. The `shape-outside` property defines the wrap contour. It **requires the element to be floated**; otherwise it is ignored. **Key properties:** - `shape-outside: circle(50%)` ŌĆö wraps text around a circle of 50% radius of the element's box. - `shape-margin: 15px` ŌĆö adds a 15px buffer between the shape and the wrapped text. - `shape-image-threshold: 0.5` ŌĆö when using an image-based shape, this defines the alpha cutoff (0.0 = fully transparent, 1.0 = fully opaque). **Example:** ```css .circle-img { float: left; width: 200px; height: 200px; shape-outside: circle(50%); shape-margin: 15px; border-radius: 50%; } ``` **Explanation of flow:** 1. The image is floated left and given a circular clip via `border-radius`. 2. `shape-outside: circle(50%)` declares the **wrap contour** as a circle. 3. Wrapped text in the adjacent paragraph follows the curve, not the rectangular bounding box. 4. `shape-margin` keeps the text comfortably away from the curve. **(7 marks)** --- #### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam ŌĆō Model Question]** **(a)** Explain the CSS **`position` property** and its values. With a labeled diagram, illustrate how **relative positioning** differs from **absolute positioning** in terms of containing block and space reservation. **(7 marks)** **(b)** What is **fixed positioning**? How does it differ from absolute positioning? Provide a real-world use case (e.g., a sticky navigation bar) with a complete HTML + CSS implementation. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **(a) The `position` Property and Relative vs. Absolute (7 marks):** The `position` property selects the **positioning scheme** for an element. It accepts five values: `static` (default), `relative`, `absolute`, `fixed`, and `sticky`. When `position` is anything other than `static`, the four offset properties ŌĆö `top`, `right`, `bottom`, `left` ŌĆö become active, and the element can be layered using `z-index`. **Relative vs. Absolute Comparison:** | Aspect | `position: relative` | `position: absolute` | |---|---|---| | Normal flow | **Remains** in normal flow | **Removed** from normal flow | | Space reservation | **Original space preserved** | **No space reserved** | | Containing block | Itself | Nearest positioned ancestor | | Use case | Small nudges, anchors | Modals, tooltips, overlays | **Diagram:** ``` RELATIVE: ABSOLUTE: [ ][ ][ ] [ ][ ][ ] [ ][R][ ] [A] [ ][ ][ ] [ ][ ][ ] (R stays in slot) (A leaves no gap, overlays) ``` **Explanation:** 1. A relatively positioned element is shifted by `top/left/...` from its **own normal position**, but its **original slot remains** in the layout ŌĆö siblings are not reflowed. 2. An absolutely positioned element is **completely removed** from normal flow; it leaves no gap. Its position is computed relative to the **nearest ancestor with a `position` value other than `static`**. 3. If no such ancestor exists, the browser falls back to the **initial containing block** (the viewport's root). **(7 marks)** --- **(b) Fixed Positioning vs. Absolute + Real-World Use Case (7 marks):** **Definition:** `position: fixed` removes the element from normal flow and positions it **relative to the viewport** (the visible browser window). The element **stays in the same screen location** as the user scrolls the page. **Difference from Absolute:** - **Absolute** is positioned relative to the *nearest positioned ancestor* ŌĆö it can move with the page on scroll. - **Fixed** is positioned relative to the *viewport* ŌĆö it **never moves** on scroll (in typical cases). **Real-World Use Case: Sticky Top Navigation Bar** **HTML:** ```html

My Website

Lots of scrollable content here...

``` **CSS:** ```css .topbar { position: fixed; top: 0; left: 0; width: 100%; background: #333; color: white; padding: 12px 20px; z-index: 1000; } .content { margin-top: 60px; /* prevent overlap with fixed bar */ padding: 20px; } ``` **Explanation of behavior:** 1. `.topbar` is fixed to `top: 0; left: 0;` ŌåÆ it pins to the top-left of the **viewport**. 2. As the user scrolls, the bar **stays visible** at the top of the screen. 3. `z-index: 1000` ensures the bar sits **above** other content. 4. `.content` uses `margin-top: 60px` so the first paragraph is not hidden beneath the bar. **(7 marks)** --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning ŌĆö Common Mark-Losing Mistakes:** > 1. **Forgetting to add `float` when using `shape-outside`** ŌĆö many students lose 2 marks here. `shape-outside` is a no-op without a float. > 2. **Confusing containing block for `absolute`** ŌĆö students often say "viewport" when the correct answer is "nearest *positioned* ancestor." Read carefully: if no positioned ancestor exists, the fallback is the *initial containing block*, not strictly the viewport. > 3. **Saying `relative` removes the element from flow** ŌĆö this is the single most common error. Relative positioning **preserves** the original space. > 4. **Omitting the `clear` property in float questions** ŌĆö without `clear`, a block-level sibling will slide up next to the float and break the expected layout. Examiners deduct 1ŌĆō2 marks for this. > 5. **Writing code without indentation or comments** ŌĆö for 7-mark sub-parts, the code must be syntactically clean and accompanied by 2ŌĆō3 lines of explanation. --- ### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) - Ō£ģ **Normal flow** is the default: blocks stack vertically, inlines flow horizontally. - Ō£ģ **`float: left/right`** partially removes an element from flow; inline content wraps around it. - Ō£ģ **`clear: left/right/both`** forces an element to appear below floated siblings. - Ō£ģ **`shape-outside`** requires a float to take effect; supports `circle()`, `ellipse()`, `polygon()`, `url()`. - Ō£ģ **`shape-margin`** adds buffer space; **`shape-image-threshold`** controls alpha cutoff (0.0 to 1.0). - Ō£ģ **`position: static`** is the default ŌĆö ignores offset properties and `z-index`. - Ō£ģ **`position: relative`** keeps the element in flow; offsets from its normal position; becomes a positioning context. - Ō£ģ **`position: absolute`** removes the element from flow; positions to the **nearest positioned ancestor** (or initial CB). - Ō£ģ **`position: fixed`** removes the element from flow; positions to the **viewport**; does not scroll. - Ō£ģ **`z-index`** works only on positioned elements (non-static). - Ō£ģ Always test layouts in a **modern browser** ŌĆö older browsers (pre-2017) may have incomplete `shape-outside` support.

topics.title: CSS Layout with Flexbox and Grid
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEITT833 - WEB DESIGNING > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** CSS Layout with Flexbox and Grid ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) **CSS Layout with Flexbox and Grid** refers to the two primary modern CSS (Cascading Style Sheets) layout modules used in the **W3C (World Wide Web Consortium)** standard to arrange HTML elements in a two-dimensional or one-dimensional responsive space, replacing older techniques like `float`, `table`, and `positioning hacks`. > [!IMPORTANT] > **CSS Flexible Box Layout Module (Flexbox)** is a **one-dimensional** layout model that distributes space between items in an interface along a **single axis** (either row or column). **CSS Grid Layout Module** is a **two-dimensional** layout model that handles both rows and columns simultaneously. | Module | Dimension | Primary Use Case | Specification Status | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Flexbox** | 1-D (Main + Cross axis) | Component-level alignment, nav bars, cards | W3C Recommendation | | **CSS Grid** | 2-D (Rows + Columns) | Page-level layout, complex dashboards | W3C Recommendation | ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **The Bookshelf Analogy:** Imagine you are arranging books on a shelf. > - **Flexbox** is like organizing books in a **single row** ŌĆö you decide the order, spacing, and whether they should grow to fill empty space. You can only work along *one line at a time*. > - **CSS Grid** is like designing a **complete bookcase with multiple rows AND columns** ŌĆö you pre-define the slots (compartments), and you place each book exactly in the cell you want. ### 1.3 The Pre-Layout Era: Box Model & Display Property (Foundation Concept) Before Flexbox and Grid, layout depended on the **CSS Box Model**: $$ \text{Total Element Width} = \text{Width} + 2 \times \text{Padding} + 2 \times \text{Border} + 2 \times \text{Margin} $$ **Standard Display Values (Pre-Flex/Grid):** - `block` ŌĆö Stacks vertically, takes full width (e.g., `
`, `

`). - `inline` ŌĆö Flows horizontally, ignores width/height (e.g., ``, ``). - `inline-block` ŌĆö Flows horizontally *but* accepts width/height. - `none` ŌĆö Completely removes the element from the page. > [!TIP] > **Why Flexbox/Grid exist:** `float`-based layouts broke with responsive design. `display: flex` and `display: grid` activate powerful, content-aware layout engines built into the browser ŌĆö no third-party libraries required. --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Flexbox Architecture When you set `display: flex` on a parent element, that element becomes a **flex container**, and its direct children become **flex items**. Two axes are automatically generated: - **Main Axis** ŌĆö Defined by `flex-direction`. Items flow along this axis by default. - **Cross Axis** ŌĆö Perpendicular to the main axis. ### 2.2 Flex Container Properties (Step-by-Step Logic) **A. `flex-direction`** ŌĆö Establishes the main axis. | Value | Main Axis Direction | Visual Flow | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | `row` *(default)* | Left ŌåÆ Right | Horizontal, normal | | `row-reverse` | Right ŌåÆ Left | Horizontal, reversed | | `column` | Top ŌåÆ Bottom | Vertical, normal | | `column-reverse` | Bottom ŌåÆ Top | Vertical, reversed | **B. `flex-wrap`** ŌĆö Controls whether items wrap to new lines. - `nowrap` *(default)* ŌĆö All items on one line (may overflow). - `wrap` ŌĆö Items wrap to next line (top-to-bottom). - `wrap-reverse` ŌĆö Items wrap to next line (bottom-to-top). **C. `flex-flow`** ŌĆö Shorthand for `flex-direction` + `flex-wrap`. ```css .container { flex-flow: row wrap; } ``` **D. `justify-content`** ŌĆö Aligns items along the **MAIN axis**. | Value | Effect | | :--- | :--- | | `flex-start` | Pack items to the start | | `flex-end` | Pack items to the end | | `center` | Center items | | `space-between` | First item at start, last at end, equal gaps | | `space-around` | Equal gaps around each item | | `space-evenly` | Perfectly equal gaps everywhere | **E. `align-items`** ŌĆö Aligns items along the **CROSS axis** (single line). | Value | Effect | | :--- | :--- | | `stretch` *(default)* | Fill the cross axis | | `flex-start` | Pack to cross-start | | `flex-end` | Pack to cross-end | | `center` | Center on cross axis | | `baseline` | Align text baselines | **F. `align-content`** ŌĆö Aligns **multiple wrapped lines** on the cross axis (only works when `flex-wrap: wrap` and items wrap). ### 2.3 Flex Item Properties **A. The Flex Sizing Triad ŌĆö The Most Important Concept** The `flex` shorthand combines three properties: $$ \boxed{\text{flex: } \underbrace{\text{grow}}_{\text{positive free space}} \;\; \underbrace{\text{shrink}}_{\text{negative free space}} \;\; \underbrace{\text{basis}}_{\text{initial size}}} $$ | Property | Default | Meaning | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | `flex-grow` | `0` | How much item **grows** relative to siblings when there is extra space | | `flex-shrink` | `1` | How much item **shrinks** relative to siblings when space is tight | | `flex-basis` | `auto` | The **initial main size** before free space is distributed | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Common Shorthand Patterns:** > - `flex: 0 1 auto` (default) ŌĆö Item does not grow, can shrink, sized by content. > - `flex: 1` ŌåÆ equivalent to `flex: 1 1 0%` ŌĆö Item grows AND shrinks equally, ignores content size. **Most useful for equal-width columns.** > - `flex: auto` ŌåÆ `flex: 1 1 auto`. > - `flex: none` ŌåÆ `flex: 0 0 auto` ŌĆö Fully inflexible. **B. `order`** ŌĆö Changes the visual order of items without altering HTML. Default is `0`. **C. `align-self`** ŌĆö Overrides the container's `align-items` for a single item. ### 2.4 The CSS Grid Architecture When you set `display: grid` on a parent, it becomes a **grid container**, and its children become **grid items** automatically placed into a defined track system. ### 2.5 Grid Container Properties **A. Defining the Track Structure ŌĆö The Foundation** | Property | Purpose | Example | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | `grid-template-columns` | Defines column track sizes | `grid-template-columns: 200px 1fr 2fr;` | | `grid-template-rows` | Defines row track sizes | `grid-template-rows: 100px auto;` | | `grid-template-areas` | Named area layout | `"header header" "sidebar main"` | | `grid-template` | Shorthand for all three | ŌĆö | **B. The `fr` Unit (Fractional Unit)** The `fr` unit distributes **available space** proportionally after fixed-size tracks are reserved. > [!NOTE] > **Example:** In a 1200px container, `grid-template-columns: 1fr 2fr 1fr;` yields: > - Total fr = **4** > - Available space = $1200 - \text{fixed tracks}$ > - Column 1 = $\frac{1}{4} \times \text{available}$ > - Column 2 = $\frac{2}{4} \times \text{available}$ > - Column 3 = $\frac{1}{4} \times \text{available}$ **C. The `repeat()` Function and Special Sizing** ```css grid-template-columns: repeat(3, 1fr); /* 3 equal columns */ grid-template-columns: repeat(auto-fit, minmax(250px, 1fr)); /* Responsive */ ``` | Function | Purpose | | :--- | :--- | | `repeat(n, size)` | Repeats a track definition n times | | `minmax(min, max)` | Defines a size range (min ŌåÆ max) | | `auto-fit` / `auto-fill` | Auto-creates tracks based on container width | **D. Gaps (Gutters)** | Property | Effect | | :--- | :--- | | `column-gap` | Space **between columns** | | `row-gap` | Space **between rows** | | `gap` *(shorthand)* | Sets both: `gap: row-gap column-gap;` | **E. Alignment in Grid** | Property | Scope | Axis | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | `justify-items` | All items in cells | Inline (horizontal) | | `align-items` | All items in cells | Block (vertical) | | `justify-content` | Whole grid in container | Inline (horizontal) | | `align-content` | Whole grid in container | Block (vertical) | | `place-items` | Shorthand for both `justify-items` + `align-items` | ŌĆö | ### 2.6 Grid Item Placement Properties **A. Line-Based Placement** Items are placed using grid line numbers (lines start at **1**): ```css .item { grid-column-start: 1; grid-column-end: 3; /* Spans 2 columns */ grid-row: 2 / 4; /* Shorthand: starts at line 2, ends at line 4 */ } ``` **B. Shorthand: `grid-column` and `grid-row`** $$ \text{grid-column: } \underbrace{\text{start-line}}_{\text{column-start}} \; / \; \underbrace{\text{end-line}}_{\text{column-end}} $$ **C. Named Area Placement (`grid-area`)** ```css .container { grid-template-areas: "header header header" "sidebar main main" "footer footer footer"; } .header { grid-area: header; } .sidebar { grid-area: sidebar; } .main { grid-area: main; } .footer { grid-area: footer; } ``` ### 2.7 KTU High-Yield Flex Sizing Formula Sheet > [!TIP] > **The Ultimate Free-Space Distribution Formula (Flexbox):** After `flex-basis` is set, **remaining free space** $\Delta$ is calculated, then distributed based on `flex-grow` ratios. $$ \boxed{\text{Item Final Size} = \text{flex-basis} + \left( \Delta \times \frac{\text{flex-grow}_i}{\sum \text{flex-grow}} \right)} $$ Where: $$ \Delta = \text{Container Main Size} - \sum \text{flex-basis}_i $$ ### 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Flexbox**: Mobile-first UI components, navigation bars, form controls, card decks, equal-height columns. - **CSS Grid**: Magazine-style layouts, dashboards with sidebars, photo galleries, calendar UIs, complex enterprise portals. - **Combined Use** (Industry Best Practice): Grid for **outer page skeleton** ŌåÆ Flexbox for **inner component alignment**. --- ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation 1: Flexbox Free-Space Distribution (Exhaustive) **Problem Statement:** A flex container has width **900px** with `flex-direction: row`. It contains 3 items with the following properties: - Item A: `flex: 2 1 100px` - Item B: `flex: 1 1 200px` - Item C: `flex: 1 1 100px` **Find the final width of each item.** --- **Step 1: Decompose the `flex` shorthand into individual properties.** $$ \text{Item A:} \quad \text{grow}_A = 2, \quad \text{shrink}_A = 1, \quad \text{basis}_A = 100 \text{ px} $$ $$ \text{Item B:} \quad \text{grow}_B = 1, \quad \text{shrink}_B = 1, \quad \text{basis}_B = 200 \text{ px} $$ $$ \text{Item C:} \quad \text{grow}_C = 1, \quad \text{shrink}_C = 1, \quad \text{basis}_C = 100 \text{ px} $$ **Step 2: Sum the flex-bases to find initial occupied space.** $$ \sum \text{basis} = 100 + 200 + 100 = 400 \text{ px} $$ **Step 3: Calculate the remaining free space $\Delta$.** $$ \Delta = \text{Container Width} - \sum \text{basis} $$ $$ \Delta = 900 - 400 = 500 \text{ px} $$ **Step 4: Calculate the sum of all `flex-grow` values.** $$ \sum \text{grow} = 2 + 1 + 1 = 4 $$ **Step 5: Compute each item's share of the free space using the ratio formula.** $$ \text{Share}_i = \Delta \times \frac{\text{grow}_i}{\sum \text{grow}} $$ For Item A: $$ \text{Share}_A = 500 \times \frac{2}{4} = 500 \times 0.5 = 250 \text{ px} $$ For Item B: $$ \text{Share}_B = 500 \times \frac{1}{4} = 500 \times 0.25 = 125 \text{ px} $$ For Item C: $$ \text{Share}_C = 500 \times \frac{1}{4} = 500 \times 0.25 = 125 \text{ px} $$ **Step 6: Compute each item's final width.** $$ \text{Final Width}_i = \text{basis}_i + \text{Share}_i $$ For Item A: $$ \text{Width}_A = 100 + 250 = 350 \text{ px} $$ For Item B: $$ \text{Width}_B = 200 + 125 = 325 \text{ px} $$ For Item C: $$ \text{Width}_C = 100 + 125 = 225 \text{ px} $$ **Step 7: Verify the total width equals the container width (Sanity Check).** $$ \text{Total} = 350 + 325 + 225 = 900 \text{ px} \quad \checkmark $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Result:** The three items will render with widths **350px, 325px, and 225px** respectively, distributed in a **2:1:1 ratio** along the main axis. --- ### 3.2 Derivation 2: CSS Grid `fr` Unit Distribution **Problem Statement:** A grid container has width **1200px**. The CSS is: ```css grid-template-columns: 200px 1fr 2fr 1fr; gap: 20px; ``` **Find the width of each column and the total consumed horizontal space.** --- **Step 1: Identify the fixed track and fractional tracks.** - Fixed track: **200px** (Column 1) - Fractional tracks: **1fr, 2fr, 1fr** (Columns 2, 3, 4) **Step 2: Calculate total `fr` units.** $$ \text{Total fr} = 1 + 2 + 1 = 4 \text{ fr} $$ **Step 3: Account for gaps.** There are **3 gaps** between 4 columns. With `gap: 20px`: $$ \text{Total Gap Width} = 3 \times 20 = 60 \text{ px} $$ **Step 4: Calculate available space for fractional distribution.** $$ \text{Available Space} = \text{Container Width} - \text{Fixed Tracks} - \text{Total Gaps} $$ $$ \text{Available Space} = 1200 - 200 - 60 = 940 \text{ px} $$ **Step 5: Distribute available space across fractional tracks.** For Column 2 (1fr): $$ \text{Width}_2 = 940 \times \frac{1}{4} = 235 \text{ px} $$ For Column 3 (2fr): $$ \text{Width}_3 = 940 \times \frac{2}{4} = 470 \text{ px} $$ For Column 4 (1fr): $$ \text{Width}_4 = 940 \times \frac{1}{4} = 235 \text{ px} $$ **Step 6: Final column widths.** | Column | Track Definition | Final Width | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | 1 | `200px` | **200 px** | | 2 | `1fr` | **235 px** | | 3 | `2fr` | **470 px** | | 4 | `1fr` | **235 px** | | **Sum** | ŌĆö | **1140 px** | **Step 7: Sanity check including gaps.** $$ \text{Total} = 200 + 235 + 470 + 235 + 60 \text{ (gaps)} = 1200 \text{ px} \quad \checkmark $$ > [!NOTE] > **Key Insight:** The `fr` unit distributes only the **leftover** space after fixed tracks and gaps have been subtracted from the container. --- ### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Flexbox vs. CSS Grid | Criterion | Flexbox | CSS Grid | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Dimensions** | 1-D (one axis at a time) | 2-D (rows AND columns) | | **Layout Source** | Content-driven (items ask for space) | Container-driven (template defines space) | | **Best For** | Component-level | Page-level / structural | | **Track System** | No explicit tracks | Explicit row/column tracks | | **Overlap Support** | No (items stack along axis) | Yes (`grid-area` overlap) | | **Item Source Order Independence** | Yes (`order` property) | Yes (line/area placement) | | **Browser Support** | Excellent (all modern) | Excellent (all modern) | | **Learning Curve** | Moderate | Steeper | | **Keyword vs Value** | Item-first | Layout-first | | **Typical Use** | Nav bar, form row, card row | Dashboard, magazine, calendar | --- ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Flexbox Architecture Flow Diagram ```mermaid graph TD A[FLEX CONTAINER display: flex] --> B[Establishes Two Axes] B --> C[MAIN AXIS via flex-direction] B --> D[CROSS AXIS Perpendicular] C --> C1[flex-direction: row / row-reverse] C --> C2[flex-direction: column / column-reverse] A --> E[Distribution Properties] E --> E1[justify-content along MAIN axis] E --> E2[align-items along CROSS axis] E --> E3[align-content for WRAPPED lines] A --> F[FLEX ITEMS Direct Children] F --> F1[flex-grow ratio] F --> F2[flex-shrink ratio] F --> F3[flex-basis initial size] F --> F4[order visual reorder] F --> F5[align-self override] style A fill:#1e40af,stroke:#0f172a,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff style B fill:#0891b2,stroke:#0f172a,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff style C fill:#059669,stroke:#0f172a,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff style D fill:#059669,stroke:#0f172a,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff style E fill:#7c3aed,stroke:#0f172a,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff style F fill:#dc2626,stroke:#0f172a,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff ``` ### 4.2 CSS Grid Architecture Flow Diagram ```mermaid graph TD A[GRID CONTAINER display: grid] --> B[Explicit Track System] B --> B1[grid-template-columns] B --> B2[grid-template-rows] B --> B3[grid-template-areas named zones] A --> C[Sizing Functions] C --> C1[fr unit proportional] C --> C2[repeat n pattern] C --> C3[minmax min max range] C --> C4[auto-fit auto-fill responsive] A --> D[Gap Gutter Control] D --> D1[row-gap vertical] D --> D2[column-gap horizontal] D --> D3[gap shorthand] A --> E[Alignment Layer] E --> E1[justify-items inline] E --> E2[align-items block] E --> E3[justify-content grid horizontal] E --> E4[align-content grid vertical] A --> F[GRID ITEMS Auto-placed or Explicit] F --> F1[grid-column-start end] F --> F2[grid-row-start end] F --> F3[grid-area placement] F --> F4[z-index layering overlap] style A fill:#1e40af,stroke:#0f172a,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff style B fill:#0891b2,stroke:#0f172a,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff style C fill:#059669,stroke:#0f172a,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff style D fill:#7c3aed,stroke:#0f172a,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff style E fill:#ea580c,stroke:#0f172a,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff style F fill:#dc2626,stroke:#0f172a,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff ``` ### 4.3 Decision Flow: When to Use Flexbox vs Grid ```mermaid graph TD Q1{Layout Requirement} --> Q2{One axis or Two axes} Q2 -->|One axis| FLEX[Use FLEXBOX] Q2 -->|Two axes| Q3{Page skeleton or component} Q3 -->|Page skeleton| GRID[Use CSS GRID] Q3 -->|Component| Q4{Need to overlap items} Q4 -->|Yes| GRID Q4 -->|No| FLEX FLEX --> F1[Nav bar Form row Card list] GRID --> G1[Dashboard Magazine Calendar Gallery] style Q1 fill:#0f172a,stroke:#0f172a,color:#ffffff style Q2 fill:#0f172a,stroke:#0f172a,color:#ffffff style Q3 fill:#0f172a,stroke:#0f172a,color:#ffffff style Q4 fill:#0f172a,stroke:#0f172a,color:#ffffff style FLEX fill:#2563eb,stroke:#0f172a,color:#ffffff style GRID fill:#16a34a,stroke:#0f172a,color:#ffffff style F1 fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#0f172a,color:#0f172a style G1 fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#0f172a,color:#0f172a ``` ### 4.4 Visual Schematic: Flex Sizing Triad (Block-Level Functional Map) ```mermaid graph LR A[Available Container Width 900px] --> B[Subtract Sum of flex-basis values 400px] B --> C[Free Space DELTA = 500px] C --> D[Divide DELTA by Sum of flex-grow 4] D --> E[Unit Share = 125px] E --> F[Item A 2 units = 250px added] E --> G[Item B 1 unit = 125px added] E --> H[Item C 1 unit = 125px added] F --> I[Item A final = 100 + 250 = 350px] G --> J[Item B final = 200 + 125 = 325px] H --> K[Item C final = 100 + 125 = 225px] I --> L[Total 900px verified] J --> L K --> L style A fill:#1e3a8a,stroke:#0f172a,color:#ffffff style B fill:#0e7490,stroke:#0f172a,color:#ffffff style C fill:#0d9488,stroke:#0f172a,color:#ffffff style D fill:#059669,stroke:#0f172a,color:#ffffff style E fill:#65a30d,stroke:#0f172a,color:#ffffff style F fill:#ca8a04,stroke:#0f172a,color:#ffffff style G fill:#ca8a04,stroke:#0f172a,color:#ffffff style H fill:#ca8a04,stroke:#0f172a,color:#ffffff style I fill:#dc2626,stroke:#0f172a,color:#ffffff style J fill:#dc2626,stroke:#0f172a,color:#ffffff style K fill:#dc2626,stroke:#0f172a,color:#ffffff style L fill:#7c2d12,stroke:#0f172a,color:#ffffff ``` --- ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ### ­¤ōś PART A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Differentiate between CSS Flexbox and CSS Grid layout models in terms of dimensionality and use cases.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** - **Flexbox** is a **one-dimensional** layout model that distributes items along a single axis (main or cross). It is content-driven and ideal for **component-level layouts** such as navigation bars, form rows, and card decks. - **CSS Grid** is a **two-dimensional** layout model that handles **rows and columns simultaneously**. It is container-driven (template-based) and ideal for **page-level structural layouts** like dashboards, magazines, and calendars. - Flexbox is best for aligning content within a known space; Grid is best for defining that space first. - *(Scoring: 1 mark for definition each, 1 mark for dimensionality + use case comparison.)* --- **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Explain the CSS `flex` shorthand property with a suitable example.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** The `flex` shorthand is a combination of three properties: ```css .item { flex: ; } ``` - `flex-grow` ŌĆö Factor by which item grows relative to siblings when extra space is available. - `flex-shrink` ŌĆö Factor by which item shrinks when space is limited. - `flex-basis` ŌĆö Initial main-axis size of the item before free space distribution. **Example:** `flex: 1 1 0%;` makes the item grow, shrink, and ignore content size ŌåÆ perfect for equal-width columns in a flex container. *(Scoring: 1 mark for syntax, 1 mark for each property explanation, 1 mark for example.)* --- ### ­¤ōŚ PART B ŌĆö Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each ŌĆö Module Internal Choice) --- #### Ō£Å’ĖÅ QUESTION A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **(a)** Design a responsive 3-column layout using **CSS Flexbox** where all three columns have **equal width** and are vertically centered. Provide the complete HTML and CSS code. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Explain `justify-content` and `align-items` with all possible values using a labeled diagram. **(7 Marks)** --- **Model Solution:** **(a) 3-Column Equal-Width Flexbox Layout (7 Marks):** ```html Flexbox 3-Column Layout

Column 1
Column 2
Column 3
``` **Explanation of key lines:** - `display: flex` ŌĆö Activates the flex layout on `.container`. - `flex: 1` on each column ŌĆö Equivalent to `flex: 1 1 0%`, ensuring **equal distribution** of space. - `align-items: center` ŌĆö Vertically centers items on the cross axis. - `gap: 10px` ŌĆö Adds uniform spacing between columns (modern CSS). --- **(b) `justify-content` vs `align-items` (7 Marks):** | Property | Axis | Purpose | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | `justify-content` | **Main Axis** | Distributes free space along the row/column direction | | `align-items` | **Cross Axis** | Aligns items perpendicular to the main axis | **All values of `justify-content`:** -
