}); }; } ``` **Engineering Insight:** The polyfill wraps **XMLHttpRequest** (legacy synchronous-feeling API) inside a **Promise** (modern async pattern), so user code can use `fetch().then()` uniformly. ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix ŌĆö Common Polyfills by Category | Category | Feature | Native Since | Popular Polyfill | |----------|---------|--------------|------------------| | **Array Methods** | `Array.from()` | ES6 (2015) | `es6-shim`, custom | | **Array Methods** | `Array.prototype.includes()` | ES7 (2016) | `array-includes` | | **String Methods** | `String.prototype.startsWith()` | ES6 (2015) | `string.prototype.startswith` | | **String Methods** | `String.prototype.padStart()` | ES8 (2017) | `string.prototype.padstart` | | **Number Methods** | `Number.isInteger()` | ES6 (2015) | `number-isinteger` | | **Object Methods** | `Object.assign()` | ES6 (2015) | `object.assign` | | **APIs** | `fetch()` | 2015 | `whatwg-fetch` | | **APIs** | `Promise` | ES6 (2015) | `es6-promise`, Bluebird | | **APIs** | `localStorage` | HTML5 (2009) | `store2` | | **CSS** | `flexbox` | 2012 | `flexibility.js` | | **HTML5 Shiv** | `
`, `
` | HTML5 | `html5shiv` | # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Polyfill Execution Flow Diagram ```mermaid flowchart TD nodeA[Web Page Loads in Browser] --> nodeB{Browser Engine
Parses Script} nodeB --> nodeC{Feature Detection:
Does native API exist?} nodeC -->|Yes - Native Present| nodeD[Use Native API
Skip Polyfill] nodeC -->|No - Missing| nodeE[Load Polyfill Script] nodeE --> nodeF[Define Replica Function
on Prototype/Global] nodeF --> nodeG[Polyfill Ready in Runtime] nodeG --> nodeH[Application Code Calls Feature] nodeD --> nodeH nodeH --> nodeI{Which Path Used?} nodeI -->|Native| nodeJ[Execute Native Implementation] nodeI -->|Polyfill| nodeK[Execute Replica Logic] nodeJ --> nodeL[Return Result to Caller] nodeK --> nodeL nodeL --> nodeM[Application Continues Normally] style nodeA fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0066cc style nodeC fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#856404 style nodeE fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#721c24 style nodeG fill:#d4edda,stroke:#155724 style nodeM fill:#d1ecf1,stroke:#0c5460 ``` ## 4.2 Browser Compatibility Layer Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph LAYER_A[Application Code Layer] nodeApp[Modern JavaScript Code
Uses ES6+ APIs] end subgraph LAYER_B[Compatibility Middleware] nodeP1[Feature Detection Engine] nodeP2[Polyfill Library
es6-shim core-js] nodeP3[Modernizr Library
CSS Feature Tests] end subgraph LAYER_C[Browser Runtime Layer] nodeB1[Modern Browser
Chrome 120] nodeB2[Legacy Browser
IE11] nodeB3[Mobile Webview
Old Android] end nodeApp --> nodeP1 nodeP1 -->|Native Exists| nodeB1 nodeP1 -->|Missing| nodeP2 nodeP2 -->|Adds Missing API| nodeB2 nodeP3 -->|CSS Feature Query| nodeB3 nodeB1 --> nodeResult[Consistent Output] nodeB2 --> nodeResult nodeB3 --> nodeResult style nodeApp fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#084298 style nodeP2 fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#856404 style nodeB2 fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#721c24 style nodeResult fill:#d4edda,stroke:#155724 ``` ## 4.3 Polyfill Decision Tree ```mermaid flowchart TD nodeStart[New Web Feature Released] --> nodeQ1{Can we wait
for adoption?} nodeQ1 -->|Yes - Wait 2-3 years| nodeWait[Use Native Only
No Polyfill] nodeQ1 -->|No - Need now| nodeQ2{Target audience
includes old browsers?} nodeQ2 -->|No| nodeUse[Use Native
Progressive Enhancement] nodeQ2 -->|Yes| nodeQ3{Stable polyfill
available?} nodeQ3 -->|No| nodeFallback[Implement Fallback
Alternative Code Path] nodeQ3 -->|Yes| nodeQ4{Polyfill size
acceptable?} nodeQ4 -->|Too large| nodeQ5{Can we lazy-load
via feature detection?} nodeQ5 -->|Yes| nodeLazy[Conditional Async Load] nodeQ5 -->|No| nodeFallback nodeQ4 -->|Yes| nodeLoad[Include Polyfill
in Build Pipeline] nodeLoad --> nodeTest[Test in Target Browsers] nodeLazy --> nodeTest nodeFallback --> nodeTest nodeTest --> nodeDeploy[Deploy Application] style nodeStart fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0066cc style nodeLoad fill:#d4edda,stroke:#155724 style nodeFallback fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#856404 style nodeDeploy fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#084298 ``` ## 4.4 Polyfill Loading Strategy Comparison ```mermaid flowchart TD subgraph STRATEGY_A[Eager Loading Strategy] nodeE1[HTML Head Loads polyfill.js] --> nodeE2[Polyfill executes before app code] nodeE2 --> nodeE3[All features available immediately] end subgraph STRATEGY_B[Lazy Loading Strategy] nodeL1[App code checks feature] --> nodeL2{Feature missing?} nodeL2 -->|Yes| nodeL3[Dynamic script tag injection] nodeL3 --> nodeL4[Load polyfill on-demand] nodeL2 -->|No| nodeL5[Skip polyfill loading] end subgraph STRATEGY_C[Build-Time Strategy] nodeB1[Babel / Webpack / Vite] --> nodeB2[Analyze target browser list] nodeB2 --> nodeB3[Auto-inject required polyfills] nodeB3 --> nodeB4[Tree-shake unused polyfills] end style nodeE1 fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#721c24 style nodeL1 fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#856404 style nodeB1 fill:#d4edda,stroke:#155724 ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## ­¤ōØ PART A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1: Define Polyfill. Give two examples. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Model Answer (3 Marks):** A **polyfill** is a piece of JavaScript code that implements a web technology feature on browsers that do not natively support it. The term was coined by Remy Sharp in 2009. **Two Examples:** 1. **`html5shiv`** ŌĆö Polyfill that enables HTML5 elements like `
`, `
`, `
` to be recognized and styled by Internet Explorer 8 and earlier. 2. **`es6-shim`** ŌĆö A collection of polyfills that replicate ECMAScript 6 (ES2015) features like `Promise`, `Map`, `Set`, and `Array.from()` in older browsers. --- ### Question 2: Differentiate between Polyfill, Shim, and Fallback. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Model Answer (3 Marks):** | Term | Definition | Example | |------|------------|---------| | **Polyfill** | Code that *replicates* a missing API so the same code works in legacy browsers | `Array.prototype.includes` polyfill | | **Shim** | A *generic* library that intercepts API calls and normalizes behavior across browsers | `es5-shim` | | **Fallback** | Code that provides an *alternative* approach (different API) when a feature is unsupported | Using AJAX with `XMLHttpRequest` if `fetch` is not available | --- ## ­¤ōØ PART B ŌĆö Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each ŌĆö Module Internal Choice) ### **Question A: 14 Marks** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]` #### **Part (a) ŌĆö 7 Marks** **Explain the concept of polyfills in web design. Discuss why feature detection is preferred over browser sniffing when implementing polyfills.** **Model Solution:** **Concept of Polyfills (4 Marks):** A polyfill is JavaScript code that replicates a modern web API in browsers that lack native support. It allows developers to use cutting-edge features while maintaining backward compatibility. Polyfills are essential for **progressive enhancement** strategies. **Feature Detection vs. Browser Sniffing (3 Marks):** - **Feature Detection** tests whether the API actually exists in the runtime: `if (window.fetch) {...}` - **Browser Sniffing** parses the `navigator.userAgent` string to guess browser capabilities. - Feature detection is **reliable, future-proof, and immune to user-agent spoofing**, making it the **W3C-recommended approach**. #### **Part (b) ŌĆö 7 Marks** **Write a complete polyfill for `String.prototype.startsWith()` with proper feature detection. Explain each step.** **Model Solution:** ```javascript if (!String.prototype.startsWith) { String.prototype.startsWith = function(searchString, position) { position = position || 0; return this.substr(position, searchString.length) === searchString; }; } ``` **Step-by-Step Explanation:** 1. **Feature Detection (Line 1):** Checks if the method already exists natively. 2. **Parameter Handling (Line 3):** `position` defaults to `0` (start of string). 3. **Substring Extraction (Line 4):** `substr()` extracts a portion of the original string from `position` of length equal to `searchString`. 4. **Strict Comparison (Line 4):** Returns `true` if extracted substring matches the search string exactly. 5. **Return Type:** Boolean ŌĆö `true` if the string starts with `searchString`, else `false`. --- ### **Question B: 14 Marks** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]` #### **Part (a) ŌĆö 7 Marks** **List and explain any five commonly used polyfills in modern web development.** **Model Solution:** 1. **html5shiv** ŌĆö Enables HTML5 elements (`
`, `
`, `
`) to be styled in IE8 and below. 2. **Respond.js** ŌĆö Polyfills CSS3 Media Queries for IE6-8. 3. **es6-shim** ŌĆö Provides ES6 methods like `Array.from()`, `Object.assign()` to ES5 browsers. 4. **fetch polyfill** (whatwg-fetch) ŌĆö Implements the `fetch()` API using `XMLHttpRequest` for older browsers. 5. **Picturefill** ŌĆö Polyfills the `` element for responsive images in legacy browsers. #### **Part (b) ŌĆö 7 Marks** **Explain the role of Modernizr in feature detection. How does it integrate with polyfills?** **Model Solution:** **Modernizr's Role (4 Marks):** Modernizr is a JavaScript library that runs **automated feature detection** on page load. It adds CSS classes to the `` element like `flexbox`, `no-flexbox`, `canvas`, `no-canvas`, allowing developers to apply **conditional CSS** and **conditional JavaScript logic**. **Integration with Polyfills (3 Marks):** Developers often combine Modernizr with polyfills: ```javascript Modernizr.load({ test: Modernizr.fetch, nope: 'fetch-polyfill.js' // Load only if missing }); ``` This pattern ensures polyfills are **loaded lazily** only when needed, optimizing performance. --- > [!WARNING] > **­¤ö┤ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls** > > 1. **Missing Feature Detection Guard (ŌĆō2 Marks):** Writing a polyfill *without* the `if (!FeatureName)` check is the **#1 reason students lose marks**. Examiners will deduct 2 marks immediately. > 2. **Confusing Polyfill with Fallback (ŌĆō2 Marks):** A polyfill must replicate the *same* API, not an alternative. Writing `if (!fetch) { use XMLHttpRequest() }` is a **fallback**, not a polyfill. > 3. **Using Browser Sniffing (ŌĆō3 Marks):** Using `navigator.userAgent.indexOf('MSIE')` is **outdated and incorrect**. Always use feature detection. > 4. **No Explanation of Steps (ŌĆō2 Marks):** For 14-mark questions, every polyfill code block must be **followed by step-by-step explanation** of each line. > 5. **Ignoring Edge Cases (ŌĆō1 Mark):** Failing to handle `NaN`, `null`, or sparse arrays in array polyfills will cost a mark. --- ## ­¤Ä» Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist ŌĆö KTU Module 3 Polyfills** ### ­¤öæ Core Definitions - Ō£ģ **Polyfill** = JavaScript code that replicates missing browser APIs - Ō£ģ **Coined by** Remy Sharp in 2009 (from "Polyfilla" spackling paste) - Ō£ģ **Purpose** = Enable modern features on legacy browsers - Ō£ģ **Principle** = Same code works across all browser versions ### ­¤øĀ’ĖÅ Three-Step Polyfill Pattern 1. Ō£ģ **Feature Detection** ŌĆö `if (!FeatureName)` guard clause 2. Ō£ģ **Define Replica** ŌĆö Add the function to `Prototype` or global 3. Ō£ģ **Transparent Execution** ŌĆö Caller unaware of polyfill ### ­¤ōŖ Key Differentiations (Memorize for 3-mark Qs) - Ō£ģ Polyfill = **same API replicated** - Ō£ģ Shim = **generic compatibility layer** - Ō£ģ Fallback = **different alternative code** - Ō£ģ Shiv = **HTML5 element support specifically** ### ­¤Ä» Mandatory Best Practices - Ō£ģ Always use **feature detection**, NEVER browser sniffing - Ō£ģ Use **Modernizr** for centralized detection - Ō£ģ Use **conditional loading** to avoid unnecessary polyfill overhead - Ō£ģ Use **build tools** (Babel, Webpack) for automatic polyfill injection - Ō£ģ Test polyfills in **real legacy browsers** (BrowserStack, etc.) ### ­¤ōÜ Essential Polyfills to Remember - Ō£ģ `Array.prototype.includes()` ŌĆö ES7 method - Ō£ģ `String.prototype.startsWith()` ŌĆö ES6 method - Ō£ģ `fetch()` API ŌĆö Replaces XMLHttpRequest - Ō£ģ `Promise` object ŌĆö Async pattern - Ō£ģ `Object.assign()` ŌĆö Object merging - Ō£ģ `html5shiv` ŌĆö HTML5 element support in IE ### ­¤ö¼ SameValueZero Algorithm - Ō£ģ Used by `includes()` ŌĆö treats `NaN === NaN` as `true` - Ō£ģ Differentiates from `===` (which says `NaN !== NaN`) - Ō£ģ Essential edge case for array polyfills ### ­¤īÉ Real-World Tools - Ō£ģ **Modernizr** ŌĆö Feature detection library - Ō£ģ **core-js** ŌĆö Modular polyfill library - Ō£ģ **es6-shim** / **es5-shim** ŌĆö ECMAScript polyfills - Ō£ģ **Babel `@babel/preset-env`** ŌĆö Automatic polyfill via `useBuiltIns`

topics.title: JavaScript Libraries, jQuery
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEITT833 - WEB DESIGNING > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** JavaScript Libraries, jQuery # ­¤ōś SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 JavaScript Libraries ŌĆö Formal Definition A **JavaScript Library** is a pre-written, reusable collection of JavaScript functions, objects, and methods that encapsulates common tasks (such as DOM manipulation, AJAX calls, animation, and event handling) into a single distributable file. Libraries allow developers to leverage tested, optimized code instead of writing everything from scratch. According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme (OEITT833 ŌĆō Web Designing)** syllabus, the concept of libraries is positioned as a productivity-multiplier for client-side scripting. A library exposes a public **API (Application Programming Interface)** that the developer invokes using a defined calling convention. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Distinction (Board Favourite):** > - **Library** ŌåÆ A collection of reusable functions *you* call when you need them. (e.g., jQuery, Lodash) > - **Framework** ŌåÆ A scaffolding that *calls your code* (Inversion of Control). (e.g., Angular, React sometimes classified as libraries too) > > The classic line: *"You call a Library. A Framework calls you."* ### Popular JavaScript Libraries (Reference Table) | Library | Primary Use | |---|---| | **jQuery** | DOM manipulation, AJAX, animations | | **Lodash / Underscore** | Utility functions (arrays, objects) | | **D3.js** | Data visualization / SVG | | **Chart.js** | Canvas-based charts | | **Three.js** | 3D WebGL graphics | | **Moment.js / Day.js** | Date/Time manipulation | | **Axios** | Promise-based HTTP client | ## 1.2 jQuery ŌĆö The Core Subject **jQuery** is a fast, lightweight, feature-rich **JavaScript library** released in **August 2006** by **John Resig**. It simplifies HTML document traversal and manipulation, event handling, animation, and AJAX interactions with a concise, cross-browser-compatible syntax. The slogan of jQuery is: **"Write less, do more."** > [!NOTE] > **jQuery at a Glance** > - **File size:** ~30 KB minified, ~87 KB uncompressed > - **Version covered in KTU 2024:** jQuery 3.x > - **License:** MIT (open-source, free for commercial use) > - **Motto:** *"Write less, do more."* > - **Official site:** `https://jquery.com` ## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy ŌĆö "The Swiss Army Knife of Web Pages" > [!TIP] > **­¤¦░ The Swiss Army Knife Analogy** > Imagine you are a carpenter (the developer) and you need to build a wooden table (the webpage). Without a Swiss Army knife, you would have to forge every single tool ŌĆö hammer, saw, screwdriver ŌĆö from raw metal. That is **vanilla JavaScript**. > > A **JavaScript library like jQuery** is the Swiss Army knife handed to you on day one. It has pre-built blades (functions) for the most common tasks: cutting (DOM manipulation), screwing (event binding), measuring (CSS access), etc. You just flip the right tool open and use it. > > You don't need to *understand metallurgy* (low-level JavaScript internals) to fix the chair leg. You just call the function. ## 1.4 Why jQuery? ŌĆö The Problem It Solved (Pre-2006 Era) Before jQuery, every browser had its **own quirky way** of handling the DOM: ```text Internet Explorer : document.getElementById("x").innerText Netscape / Firefox : document.getElementById("x").textContent Safari / Chrome : document.getElementById("x").textContent ``` jQuery provided a **single unified API** that worked the same in **all major browsers** ŌĆö this was revolutionary and is still cited in exams as the prime reason for its adoption. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Highlight ŌĆö Key Features of jQuery** > 1. **HTML/DOM manipulation** > 2. **CSS manipulation** > 3. **HTML event methods** > 4. **Effects and animation** > 5. **AJAX** (Asynchronous JavaScript And XML) > 6. **Utilities** (e.g., `$.each()`, `$.map()`) > 7. **Cross-browser compatibility** # ­¤ōś SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet ## 2.1 Including jQuery in a Web Page There are **two official methods** to integrate jQuery: ### Method 1 ŌĆö Download and Host Locally ```html ``` ### Method 2 ŌĆö Use a CDN (Content Delivery Network) ŌĆö Most Common in KTU Practicals ```html ``` > [!TIP] > **Best Practice (KTU Practical):** Always load jQuery from a CDN with a local fallback: > ```html > > > ``` ## 2.2 The jQuery Syntax ŌĆö The Heart of the Library The entire jQuery philosophy is condensed into one master equation: $$\text{jQuery Syntax} = \$(\text{selector}).\text{action}()$$ Or expanded: $$\underbrace{\$}_{\text{Alias for } jQuery}(\underbrace{\text{selector}}_{\text{Find HTML element(s)}}) \cdot \underbrace{action()}_{\text{Perform task on element(s)}}$$ ### Step-by-step Anatomy 1. **`$` sign** ŌåÆ A shortcut/alias for the global function `jQuery`. So `jQuery("p")` is identical to `$("p")`. 2. **Selector** ŌåÆ A CSS-like query string that finds HTML element(s). 3. **Action** ŌåÆ A jQuery method that performs an operation on the selected element(s). > [!NOTE] > **Exam Definition (Write verbatim):** > *The jQuery syntax is a combination of the dollar sign `$` and a selector followed by an action. The `$()` factory function returns a jQuery object wrapping the matched DOM elements, on which chained methods are invoked.* ## 2.3 The Document Ready Event ŌĆö Mandatory Boilerplate ```javascript $(document).ready(function(){ // jQuery code goes here ŌĆö runs when DOM is fully loaded }); // Shorthand version (most commonly used in KTU exams) $(function(){ // jQuery code goes here }); ``` > [!WARNING] > **Why not just put `




``` **Exhaustive Line-by-Line Walkthrough:** | Line | Code | Why | |---|---|---| | 1-3 | HTML head + jQuery CDN | Load jQuery library | | 4-7 | CSS rules for `.error` and `.invalid` | Visual feedback | | 9-19 | Form HTML with two inputs and error spans | The form being validated | | 21 | `$(document).ready(function(){...})` | Wait for DOM ready | | 22 | `$("#regForm").submit(function(event){...})` | Bind submit handler; `event` is the form submission event | | 23 | `event.preventDefault()` | **CRITICAL** ŌĆö stops the browser's default form submission | | 24 | `var valid = true` | Flag to track overall validity | | 27-31 | Username validation block | Show error if empty, mark input red | | 32-36 | Email validation block | Use regex to verify format | | 38-40 | Success path | Show confirmation, normally do AJAX submit here | # ­¤ōś SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram ŌĆö jQuery Architecture and Execution Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Web Browser Loads HTML] --> B[Encounters script tag with jQuery] B --> C{Download Method} C -->|CDN| D1[Fetch from code.jquery.com] C -->|Local| D2[Load from /js/jquery-3.7.1.min.js] D1 --> E[jQuery Library Parsed] D2 --> E E --> F[DOMContentLoaded Fires] F --> G[$(document).ready handler runs] G --> H[Selectors find target elements] H --> I[Return jQuery wrapped object] I --> J{Action Category} J -->|DOM Manipulation| K1[text, html, val, attr] J -->|CSS| K2[css, addClass, removeClass] J -->|Events| K3[click, hover, submit, keydown] J -->|Effects| K4[hide, show, fade, slide, animate] J -->|AJAX| K5[$.ajax, $.get, $.post, $.getJSON] K1 --> L[Updated DOM rendered to screen] K2 --> L K3 --> M[Event handler attached to element] K4 --> N[Animation runs via internal timer loop] K5 --> O[HTTP Request sent to server] O --> P[Server response received] P --> Q[Callback function executed] Q --> L ``` ## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram ŌĆö The `$(selector).action()` Master Pattern ```mermaid flowchart LR A["Dollar Sign $"] --> B["Alias for jQuery Function"] B --> C["Receives a Selector String"] C --> D{"Selector Type"} D -->|CSS-style| E1["#id .class element [attr]"] D -->|jQuery-specific| E2[":first :last :even :odd :hidden :visible"] E1 --> F["DOM Traversal Occurs"] E2 --> F F --> G["jQuery Object Created with matched elements"] G --> H["Method chained on object"] H --> I{"Method Category"} I -->|Getter| J1["Returns value"] I -->|Setter| J2["Modifies element"] I -->|Chained| J3["Returns same object for next call"] J1 --> K[Result available to JavaScript] J2 --> L[Browser re-renders] J3 --> M[Next method invoked on result] ``` ## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram ŌĆö AJAX Request-Response Cycle in jQuery ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant U as User participant B as Browser DOM participant J as jQuery Library participant X as XMLHttpRequest Engine participant S as Web Server U->>B: Click event on button B->>J: Trigger handler via $(btn).click() J->>X: $.get(url, callback) called X->>S: HTTP GET request to data.txt S-->>X: HTTP 200 OK with file content X-->>J: Response data delivered J->>J: Execute success callback function J->>B: $(sel).html(data) injects content B-->>U: User sees updated page Note over J,B: No page reload occurred Note over U,S: Process is asynchronous ``` ## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram ŌĆö jQuery Effect Chain on a Single Element ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Element Selected] --> B{Initial State} B -->|Visible| C1[.fadeOut 600ms] B -->|Hidden| C2[.fadeIn 600ms] C1 --> D1[opacity 1 to 0] D1 --> E1[display: none] C2 --> D2[display: block] D2 --> E2[opacity 0 to 1] E1 --> F[Callback fires] E2 --> F F --> G[Next chained method runs] H[.animate params 500] --> I[Step 1: Read current CSS value] I --> J[Step 2: Calculate intermediate value] J --> K[Step 3: Apply via setInterval approx 13ms] K --> L{More frames} L -->|Yes| J L -->|No| M[Final value applied] M --> N[Callback fires] ``` # ­¤ōś SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## ­¤ōØ PART A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Q1. [KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2023, Model QP]** **Define a JavaScript library. List any four popular JavaScript libraries with their primary use.** #### Model Answer (3 Marks): A **JavaScript library** is a pre-packaged collection of JavaScript functions, methods, and objects designed to simplify common web development tasks such as DOM manipulation, AJAX, animation, and event handling. (1 Mark) **Four popular JavaScript libraries:** (2 Marks) 1. **jQuery** ŌĆö DOM manipulation, AJAX, animation, cross-browser compatibility. 2. **Lodash** ŌĆö Utility functions for arrays, objects, strings, and functions. 3. **D3.js** ŌĆö Data-driven visualizations using SVG. 4. **Chart.js** ŌĆö Canvas-based charts and graphs. 5. *(Alt: Moment.js, Axios, Three.js)* --- ### **Q2. [KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2022, Model QP]** **Explain the jQuery syntax with a suitable example. What is the role of the `$` sign?** #### Model Answer (3 Marks): The **jQuery syntax** is built on the master pattern: `$(selector).action()`. (┬Į Mark) - The **`$` sign** is a shortcut alias for the global `jQuery` function. Writing `$("p")` is identical to writing `jQuery("p")`. (1 Mark) - The **selector** finds one or more HTML elements using CSS-like syntax. (┬Į Mark) - The **action** is a jQuery method that operates on the selected element(s). (┬Į Mark) **Example:** ```javascript $(document).ready(function(){ $("p").click(function(){ $(this).hide(); }); }); ``` This hides any `

` element when it is clicked. (┬Į Mark) --- ## ­¤ōØ PART B ŌĆö 14 Mark Questions (ESE Module Choice) > **Instructions (KTU 2024 ESE Pattern):** Answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full. Each sub-part carries 7 marks. --- ### **ŌØō Question A (14 Marks)** #### **(a) [7 Marks] Explain the different types of jQuery selectors with examples. Demonstrate how to bind a click event to dynamically added elements using event delegation.** #### Model Answer (a): **jQuery Selectors (5 Marks for the listing + 2 Marks for event delegation):** | Selector | Syntax | Purpose | |---|---|---| | Element selector | `$("p")` | Selects all `

` elements | | ID selector | `$("#header")` | Selects the element with given `id` | | Class selector | `$(".btn")` | Selects all elements with given `class` | | Descendant | `$("div p")` | All `

` nested inside any `

` | | Child | `$("ul > li")` | Direct `
  • ` children of `
      ` | | Attribute | `$("[type='text']")` | Elements whose attribute matches | | `:first` / `:last` | `$("li:first")` | First / last matching element | | `:even` / `:odd` | `$("tr:even")` | Even- or odd-indexed matches | | `:input` | `$(":input")` | All form input elements | | Multiple | `$("h1, h2, p")` | Multiple selector groups | **Event Delegation with `.on()` (2 Marks):** When elements are added dynamically (e.g., list items appended via AJAX), direct binding does not work. The delegated pattern is: ```javascript $("#parent").on("click", ".child", function(){ alert("Child clicked: " + $(this).text()); }); ``` This binds the handler to the **parent** which always exists, and the event is matched against the **child** selector at click time. This is the only correct way to handle events on dynamically injected DOM nodes. --- #### **(b) [7 Marks] Write a complete jQuery program that fetches a list of users from a JSON API (`https://jsonplaceholder.typicode.com/users`) using `$.getJSON` and displays their names in an unordered list.** #### Model Answer (b): ```html User List

      User List

        ``` **Explanation of Key Parts (7 Marks breakdown):** | Component | Marks | Purpose | |---|---|---| | CDN inclusion | 1 | Loads jQuery library | | Button + `
          ` HTML | 1 | Provides UI target | | `$.getJSON` call | 2 | Sends HTTP GET, parses response as JSON | | `$.each()` iteration | 2 | Loops over the array of user objects | | `.html()` injection + `.fail()` handler | 1 | Builds list and handles errors | **Sample JSON response element used:** ```json { "id": 1, "name": "Leanne Graham", "email": "Sincere@april.biz" } ``` Only `user.name` is extracted and wrapped in `
        • ` tags. --- ### **ŌØō Question B (14 Marks) ŌĆö Alternative Choice** #### **(a) [7 Marks] Compare JavaScript and jQuery. List at least six differences. When would you prefer vanilla JavaScript over jQuery?** #### Model Answer (a): **Comparison Table (5 Marks):** | Aspect | Vanilla JavaScript | jQuery | |---|---|---| | **File size** | 0 KB (built-in) | ~30 KB minified | | **DOM access** | `document.getElementById()` | `$("#id")` | | **Cross-browser code** | Often required manual fixes | Built-in handling | | **AJAX** | `XMLHttpRequest` (verbose) | `$.ajax` / `$.get` / `$.post` | | **Animations** | Manual with `requestAnimationFrame` | Built-in `.animate()`, `.fadeIn()` | | **Learning curve** | Steeper for beginners | Easier for beginners | | **Performance** | Faster (no extra layer) | Slightly slower (wrapper layer) | | **Modern features** | Supports latest ES6+ standards | Frozen at ES5 era | | **Modern apps** | Standard for React/Vue/Angular | Considered legacy | **When to prefer vanilla JavaScript (2 Marks):** 1. **Modern SPA frameworks** (React, Angular, Vue) ŌĆö they use their own DOM abstractions. 2. **Performance-critical apps** ŌĆö extra layer overhead is not acceptable. 3. **No external dependencies** ŌĆö required for restricted environments (e.g., browser extensions). 4. **Learning fundamentals** ŌĆö better for understanding how the web really works. --- #### **(b) [7 Marks] Demonstrate jQuery's event handling with a complete program that changes the background colour of a `
          ` to a random colour on every mouse-enter and resets it on mouse-leave. Use `.hover()` and `.css()`.** #### Model Answer (b): ```html Random Colour Hover
          Hover over me!
          ``` **Explanation (7 Marks breakdown):** | Step | Code | Marks | |---|---|---| | Storing original colour | `var originalColor = $("#box").css("background-color");` | 1 | | `.hover(handlerIn, handlerOut)` | Binds both events in one call | 2 | | Generating random RGB | `Math.random() * 256` per channel, `Math.floor()` to integer | 2 | | Applying with `.css()` | `$(this).css("background-color", "rgb(...)")` | 1 | | Resetting on leave | Reusing `originalColor` | 1 | --- ## ŌÜĀ’ĖÅ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks:** > > 1. **Forgetting `$(document).ready()`** ŌĆö Code runs before DOM exists, `$("#x")` returns empty. ŌåÆ **Lose 2-3 marks.** > 2. **Using `|` inside markdown tables** ŌĆö Breaks the table syntax in exam answer sheets. Use `\vert` or `\mid` instead. > 3. **Confusing `.text()` and `.html()`** ŌĆö `.text()` escapes HTML, `.html()` renders it. Examiners test this. > 4. **Calling `.click()` without a handler** ŌĆö `$("#x").click()` (no arg) **triggers** the click; `$("#x").click(function(){...})` **binds** it. > 5. **Using `$` without including the library** ŌĆö Console error: `$ is not defined`. Always mention CDN/local script. > 6. **AJAX without error handling** ŌĆö Always use `.fail()` or `.done()`/`.always()` for production quality. > 7. **Event binding on dynamic elements without `.on()` delegation** ŌĆö Click handlers silently fail on AJAX-injected DOM. > 8. **Mixing up `$.get` and `$.post`** ŌĆö GET is for retrieval (idempotent), POST is for data submission. > 9. **Forgetting `event.preventDefault()` in form submit handlers** ŌĆö Form does a full page reload, defeating the purpose. --- ## Ō£ģ Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!TIP] > **Rapid Revision Checklist ŌĆö jQuery (Module 3)** - ­¤ōī jQuery is a **JavaScript library**, not a framework. Motto: *"Write less, do more."* - ­¤ōī Released by **John Resig** in **August 2006**. - ­¤ōī Master syntax: `$(selector).action()` ŌĆö `$` is alias for `jQuery`. - ­¤ōī Always wrap code in `$(document).ready(function(){...})` or `$(function(){...})`. - ­¤ōī **Selectors** mimic CSS: `$("p")`, `$("#id")`, `$(".class")`, `$("[attr]")`, `$("parent > child")`. - ­¤ōī **Events**: `.click()`, `.dblclick()`, `.hover(in,out)`, `.focus()`, `.blur()`, `.submit()`, `.change()`. - ­¤ōī **Effects**: `.hide()`, `.show()`, `.toggle()`, `.fadeIn/Out/Toggle()`, `.slideUp/Down/Toggle()`, `.animate()`. - ­¤ōī **DOM manipulation**: `.text()`, `.html()`, `.val()`, `.attr()`, `.css()`, `.append()`, `.prepend()`, `.remove()`, `.empty()`. - ­¤ōī **Class methods**: `.addClass()`, `.removeClass()`, `.toggleClass()`, `.hasClass()`. - ­¤ōī **AJAX**: `$.ajax(settings)`, `$.get(url, callback)`, `$.post(url, data, callback)`, `$.getJSON(url, callback)`, `$(sel).load(url)`. - ­¤ōī **Event delegation** for dynamic content: `$(parent).on(event, childSelector, handler)`. - ­¤ōī **Chaining** works because most jQuery methods return the same jQuery object. - ­¤ōī **CDN link** (latest stable): `https://code.jquery.com/jquery-3.7.1.min.js`. - ­¤ōī jQuery is still shipped by default in **WordPress** and many legacy enterprise systems. - ­¤ōī Modern alternatives for new projects: **React, Vue, Angular** ŌĆö but jQuery concepts (selectors, events, AJAX) are **universal**.

topics.title: jQuery Use
topics.content: > **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEITT833 - WEB DESIGNING > - **Module:** Module Module 3 > - **Topic:** jQuery Use # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **jQuery** is a fast, lightweight, and feature-rich **JavaScript library** released in 2006 by John Resig. It is designed to simplify client-side HTML scripting ŌĆö particularly **DOM (Document Object Model) manipulation, event handling, animation, and AJAX interactions** ŌĆö by wrapping common JavaScript tasks into concise, cross-browser-compatible method calls. In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme (OEITT833 - Web Designing)**, jQuery is treated as a foundational JavaScript enhancement layer that abstracts verbose native JavaScript into a chained, selector-based syntax governed by the motto: **"Write less, do more."** > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 3 ŌĆö jQuery Use):** > The student must be able to: > - Include jQuery in a webpage (CDN / Local). > - Apply jQuery selectors, events, and effects. > - Perform DOM manipulation and traversal. > - Use jQuery AJAX for asynchronous data exchange. > - Distinguish jQuery from native JavaScript and modern frameworks. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine you are a **chef in a large, messy kitchen (the web browser)**. The raw ingredients (HTML elements) and the cooking tools (native JavaScript APIs) are powerful but scattered across many drawers, and the recipes (your code) require dozens of steps for even a simple dish. **jQuery acts like a master toolkit on a single tray.** Instead of searching for the right knife, peeler, and pan individually, you reach for one organized toolset that does the slicing, peeling, and cooking in a single motion. The chef (developer) writes one short instruction, and the toolkit (jQuery) handles the rest ŌĆö even if different kitchens (browsers like Chrome, Firefox, Safari) have slightly different layouts. | Concept | Real-World Analogy | Technical Role | |---|---|---| | **HTML** | Raw ingredients | Static structure | | **Native JavaScript** | Individual kitchen tools | Verbose, browser-specific scripting | | **jQuery** | Master organized toolkit | Simplified, unified API | | **Selector** | A label on a tray | Identifies target elements | | **Event** | A customer ringing the bell | User/browser interaction | | **Effect** | Plating and presentation | Visual animations | | **AJAX** | Kitchen-to-kitchen delivery | Asynchronous data fetching | > [!NOTE] > **Key Fact:** jQuery is **not** a separate programming language. It is a **library** written entirely in JavaScript. After being processed by the browser, every jQuery line is converted into equivalent native JavaScript under the hood. ## 1.3 Physical / Library Constants and Standard Metrics - **Latest stable jQuery version (as of syllabus period):** **3.7.1** (~**~30 KB** minified, **~90 KB** uncompressed). - **License:** **MIT License** ŌĆö open-source, free for commercial use. - **Selector engine:** **Sizzle** (built into jQuery). - **Standard CDN providers:** Google CDN, Microsoft CDN, jsDelivr, cdnjs. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet ## 2.1 Why jQuery? The Operational Rationale Before jQuery, developers had to write lengthy code to handle **cross-browser inconsistencies**. For example, fetching an element by ID in Internet Explorer versus Firefox used different APIs. jQuery normalizes this. Its operational pillars are: 1. **Selector Engine (Sizzle):** Uses CSS-style selectors to target DOM elements. 2. **Chaining:** Multiple methods can be called on the same element in a single statement. 3. **Implicit Iteration:** Methods automatically apply to all matched elements. 4. **Cross-Browser Abstraction:** One API works across all major browsers. 5. **AJAX Wrapper:** Simplifies `XMLHttpRequest` into a few lines. 6. **Effect Engine:** Built-in animation utilities. ## 2.2 Including jQuery in a Web Page There are **two standard ways** to include jQuery in a webpage, both of which are KTU-exam relevant. ### Method A ŌĆö CDN (Content Delivery Network) The recommended method for production websites. The library is loaded from a remote server. ```html ``` ### Method B ŌĆö Local Download The library file is downloaded and stored in the project's directory. ```html ``` > [!NOTE] > **Best Practice:** Always place the ` ``` **2. Local download:** The jQuery file (`jquery-3.7.1.min.js`) is downloaded and placed inside the project folder. ```html ``` **The `$(document).ready()` function:** This function ensures that the jQuery code runs **only after the entire HTML document has been fully loaded and parsed by the browser**. Without it, the code may try to access DOM elements that do not yet exist, resulting in errors. ```javascript $(document).ready(function(){ // All jQuery code goes here $("p").hide(); }); ``` **Shorthand syntax:** `$(function(){ ... });` > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why it matters:** Placing the `

topics.title: Finding Elements
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEITT833 - WEB DESIGNING > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Finding Elements # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) In the context of **Web Designing** under the **Document Object Model (DOM)** paradigm, **Finding Elements** refers to the systematic process of **locating, selecting, and retrieving specific HTML (or XML) element nodes** from a web page's tree-structured document representation, using standardized **JavaScript DOM API methods**. The DOM treats every HTML tag, attribute, and text node as a programmable object, and "finding" an element is the gateway operation that allows scripts to dynamically read, modify, style, or delete content on a web page. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** "Finding Elements" is classified under **JavaScript DOM Manipulation** and encompasses the use of legacy DOM methods (`getElementById`, `getElementsByClassName`, `getElementsByTagName`, `getElementsByName`) as well as modern **Selectors API** methods (`querySelector`, `querySelectorAll`) to traverse the document tree and obtain references to element nodes for subsequent manipulation. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine a **massive library** containing millions of books (your web page's HTML elements). You walk in needing a specific book. How do you find it? - **Using the Library's Unique Catalog ID** ŌåÆ `getElementById()` (you know the exact unique ID) - **Asking for all books on a specific shelf labeled "Science"** ŌåÆ `getElementsByClassName()` (a class acts like a shelf label, and many books can share it) - **Requesting every book whose title starts with "History"** ŌåÆ `getElementsByTagName()` (a tag is like the title category) - **Using the advanced search bar with CSS-style filters** ŌåÆ `querySelector()` / `querySelectorAll()` (most powerful, accepts CSS selectors) > [!NOTE] > The DOM element-finding methods are the **"search engines"** of client-side JavaScript. Without them, your script would be blindfolded inside a webpage, unable to interact with anything. ## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Terminology - **HTMLCollection**: A *live*, array-like collection of elements (auto-updates when DOM changes). - **NodeList**: A *static* (mostly) collection of nodes returned by `querySelectorAll`. - **CSS Selector**: A pattern string used to match elements (e.g., `#id`, `.class`, `tag`). - **Time Complexity** of finding: Generally **O(n)** for legacy methods, **O(n)** for querySelector (with selector engine optimization), where *n* = number of DOM nodes. - **Null Return**: `getElementById` and `querySelector` return **`null`** if no match is found ŌĆö a common source of runtime errors. > [!NOTE] > **Why this matters for KTU:** Every dynamic website feature ŌĆö form validation, animations, AJAX updates, event handling ŌĆö **starts with finding an element**. It is the single most frequently assessed foundational concept in Web Designing papers. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Method Sheet ## 2.1 The Document Object Model (DOM) Tree ŌĆö Foundation for Finding The browser parses your HTML and builds a **tree structure** where every element becomes a *node*. JavaScript can only find elements if it understands this tree. ``` document ŌööŌöĆŌöĆ Ōö£ŌöĆŌöĆ Ōöé ŌööŌöĆŌöĆ , <meta>, <link>... ŌööŌöĆŌöĆ <body> Ōö£ŌöĆŌöĆ <h1> Ōö£ŌöĆŌöĆ <p> ŌööŌöĆŌöĆ <div> Ōö£ŌöĆŌöĆ <span> ŌööŌöĆŌöĆ <a> ``` ## 2.2 The Five Primary Element-Finding Methods ### A. `document.getElementById(id)` - **Purpose:** Finds the **single element** whose `id` attribute exactly matches the given string. - **Returns:** A single `Element` object OR `null`. - **Constraint:** IDs must be **unique** within a valid HTML document. - **Speed:** Fastest method (browsers maintain a direct id-to-element index). ### B. `document.getElementsByClassName(className)` - **Purpose:** Finds **all elements** that have the specified CSS class. - **Returns:** A *live* `HTMLCollection` (array-like, but not a true `Array`). - **Note:** Passing `"foo bar"` finds elements with **both** classes (space-separated). ### C. `document.getElementsByTagName(tagName)` - **Purpose:** Finds **all elements** with a given tag name (e.g., `"p"`, `"div"`). - **Returns:** A *live* `HTMLCollection`. - **Wildcard:** Passing `"*"` returns **every element** in the document. ### D. `document.getElementsByName(name)` - **Purpose:** Finds elements by their `name` attribute (commonly used for form elements). - **Returns:** A *live* `NodeList`. ### E. `querySelector()` and `querySelectorAll()` ŌĆö The Modern Powerhouses - **Purpose:** Accept **any valid CSS selector** string. - **`querySelector(selector)`** ŌåÆ returns the **first matching element** or `null`. - **`querySelectorAll(selector)`** ŌåÆ returns a *static* `NodeList` of **all matches**. - **Examples of selectors:** - `"#header"` ŌåÆ element with id="header" - `".active"` ŌåÆ elements with class="active" - `"ul li:first-child"` ŌåÆ first `<li>` inside any `<ul>` - `"input[type='text']"` ŌåÆ all text inputs - `"div > p.warning"` ŌåÆ `<p class="warning">` that is a direct child of a `<div>` ## 2.3 The "Why" and "How" Behind the Logic | Step | Why It Matters | How It Works Internally | |------|---------------|------------------------| | 1. Parsing | Browser converts HTML into a tree | HTML Parser ŌåÆ Tokenizer ŌåÆ Tree Builder | | 2. Indexing | Browsers pre-build lookup tables | Hash map for IDs; inverted index for classes | | 3. Querying | Script requests elements | Tree walk OR index lookup | | 4. Caching | Returned collections may be live or static | Live = re-queried; Static = snapshot | | 5. Manipulation | Once found, the element can be modified | `.textContent`, `.style`, `.setAttribute()`... | ## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Method Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > Memorize this table ŌĆö it is the **single most tested reference** for Finding Elements in KTU exams. | Method | Selector Input | Return Type | Live/Static | Returns If Not Found | |--------|---------------|-------------|-------------|----------------------| | `getElementById` | id string | `Element` | N/A (single) | `null` | | `getElementsByClassName` | class string | `HTMLCollection` | **Live** | Empty collection `[]` | | `getElementsByTagName` | tag name string | `HTMLCollection` | **Live** | Empty collection `[]` | | `getElementsByName` | name string | `NodeList` | **Live** | Empty collection `[]` | | `querySelector` | CSS selector | `Element` | N/A (single) | `null` | | `querySelectorAll` | CSS selector | `NodeList` | **Static** | Empty `NodeList` | > [!NOTE] > **Live vs Static ŌĆö A High-Yield Distinction:** > - A **Live** collection (e.g., from `getElementsByClassName`) **automatically reflects DOM changes**. If you add a new element with that class, it appears in the collection. > - A **Static** collection (from `querySelectorAll`) is a **snapshot** ŌĆö it does NOT update when the DOM changes. ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Form Validation:** Finding `<input>` elements to read their values and display error messages. - **Single-Page Applications (SPAs):** React, Vue, Angular internally use element-finding techniques (often via Virtual DOM) to update the UI. - **Browser Extensions:** Inject scripts that find and modify page elements. - **Web Scraping / Automation Tools:** Locate elements by CSS selectors (Puppeteer, Selenium). - **Accessibility Tools:** Find elements missing ARIA attributes to report issues. <!-- SECTION_2_END --> <!-- SECTION_3_START --> # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Code Examples ## 3.1 Example 1: Finding by ID (Foundation Method) **HTML Setup:** ```html <h1 id="title">Welcome</h1> ``` **JavaScript:** ```javascript // Step 1: Locate the element let heading = document.getElementById("title"); // Step 2: Verify it was found if (heading !== null) { // Step 3: Read its current content let currentText = heading.textContent; console.log("Original text:", currentText); // Step 4: Modify the content heading.textContent = "Hello, KTU Student!"; // Step 5: Style the element heading.style.color = "blue"; heading.style.fontSize = "32px"; } else { console.log("Element with id 'title' not found."); } ``` **Output trace:** ``` Original text: Welcome [Browser displays]: Hello, KTU Student! (in blue, 32px) ``` > [!NOTE] > **Why `null` checking matters:** If the element doesn't exist, accessing `.textContent` on `null` throws a **`TypeError`**. KTU examiners often award marks for proper null-checks. ## 3.2 Example 2: Finding by Class Name (Multiple Results) **HTML Setup:** ```html <ul> <li class="item">Apple</li> <li class="item">Banana</li> <li class="item special">Cherry</li> <li class="item">Date</li> </ul> ``` **JavaScript:** ```javascript // Step 1: Get all elements with class "item" let items = document.getElementsByClassName("item"); // Step 2: items is a live HTMLCollection console.log("Total items:", items.length); // Output: 4 // Step 3: Iterate using a classic for loop for (let i = 0; i < items.length; i++) { items[i].style.fontWeight = "bold"; } // Step 4: Get elements with BOTH classes "item" AND "special" let special = document.getElementsByClassName("item special"); console.log("Special items:", special.length); // Output: 1 // Step 5: Convert HTMLCollection to a true Array if needed let itemsArray = Array.from(items); itemsArray.forEach(el => console.log(el.textContent)); ``` ## 3.3 Example 3: Finding by Tag Name **HTML Setup:** ```html <p>First paragraph.</p> <p>Second paragraph.</p> <p>Third paragraph.</p> ``` **JavaScript:** ```javascript // Step 1: Find all <p> elements let paragraphs = document.getElementsByTagName("p"); // Step 2: Change the text of the second paragraph paragraphs[1].textContent = "Modified second paragraph."; // Step 3: Apply CSS class to all paragraphs for (let p of paragraphs) { p.classList.add("highlighted"); } ``` ## 3.4 Example 4: Modern querySelector & querySelectorAll **HTML Setup:** ```html <div class="container"> <h2>News</h2> <p class="lead">Breaking news item 1.</p> <p>Regular news item 2.</p> <p>Regular news item 3.</p> <input type="text" name="search" placeholder="Search..."> </div> ``` **JavaScript:** ```javascript // Step 1: First element matching a CSS selector let lead = document.querySelector(".container .lead"); console.log(lead.textContent); // Output: "Breaking news item 1." // Step 2: All elements matching a CSS selector let allParagraphs = document.querySelectorAll(".container p"); console.log("Found", allParagraphs.length, "paragraphs."); // Output: Found 3 paragraphs. // Step 3: Attribute-based selector let searchInput = document.querySelector("input[type='text']"); searchInput.value = "Default search term"; // Step 4: Pseudo-class selector (first child) let firstP = document.querySelector(".container p:first-child"); firstP.style.color = "red"; // Step 5: Chained/descendant selector let nested = document.querySelectorAll("div.container > p"); nested.forEach((el, idx) => console.log(`P${idx}:`, el.textContent)); ``` ## 3.5 Example 5: The Live vs Static Collection Distinction **This is a classic KTU question pattern:** ```javascript // Setup: three <li> items with class "fruit" let fruits = document.getElementsByClassName("fruit"); // LIVE collection let fruitsStatic = document.querySelectorAll(".fruit"); // STATIC NodeList console.log("Live length:", fruits.length); // 3 console.log("Static length:", fruitsStatic.length); // 3 // Add a new fruit dynamically let newFruit = document.createElement("li"); newFruit.className = "fruit"; newFruit.textContent = "Mango"; document.getElementById("fruitList").appendChild(newFruit); console.log("Live length after add:", fruits.length); // 4 (UPDATED!) console.log("Static length after add:", fruitsStatic.length); // 3 (UNCHANGED) ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **Derivation Logic:** The live collection is a **reference** to the live DOM query, while the static NodeList is a **frozen snapshot** at the time of the query. This is *why* `querySelectorAll` is preferred in React-style frameworks that re-render the DOM ŌĆö it prevents unexpected mutations. ## 3.6 Example 6: Comprehensive Form Validation Using Multiple Find Methods ```javascript function validateForm() { // Find by ID let username = document.getElementById("username"); let errorBox = document.getElementById("error-msg"); // Find all inputs by tag name let allInputs = document.getElementsByTagName("input"); // Find by querySelectorAll let requiredFields = document.querySelectorAll("input[required]"); // Step 1: Clear previous error errorBox.textContent = ""; // Step 2: Loop through required fields for (let field of requiredFields) { if (field.value.trim() === "") { errorBox.textContent = `Field "${field.name}" is required.`; field.style.borderColor = "red"; return false; } } // Step 3: Use getElementsByName for radio buttons let genders = document.getElementsByName("gender"); let genderSelected = Array.from(genders).some(r => r.checked); if (!genderSelected) { errorBox.textContent = "Please select a gender."; return false; } return true; } ``` <!-- SECTION_3_END --> <!-- SECTION_4_START --> # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Hierarchy of DOM Element-Finding Methods ```mermaid graph TD A[Document Object Model] --> B[Finding Elements API] B --> C[Legacy DOM Methods] B --> D[Modern Selectors API] C --> C1[getElementById] C --> C2[getElementsByClassName] C --> C3[getElementsByTagName] C --> C4[getElementsByName] D --> D1[querySelector] D --> D2[querySelectorAll] C1 --> E1[Returns: Single Element or null] C2 --> E2[Returns: Live HTMLCollection] C3 --> E3[Returns: Live HTMLCollection] C4 --> E4[Returns: Live NodeList] D1 --> F1[Returns: First Match or null] D2 --> F2[Returns: Static NodeList] E1 --> G[Manipulation Phase] E2 --> G E3 --> G E4 --> G F1 --> G F2 --> G G --> H[textContent] G --> I[innerHTML] G --> J[style] G --> K[setAttribute] ``` ## 4.2 Element-Finding Workflow Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart TD Start([Script Execution Begins]) --> Parse[Browser Parses HTML into DOM Tree] Parse --> Build[DOM Tree with Node References] Build --> Call{Script Calls Find Method} Call -->|getElementById| M1[Hash Table Lookup by ID] Call -->|getElementsByClassName| M2[Scan for Class Attribute Match] Call -->|getElementsByTagName| M3[Scan for Tag Name Match] Call -->|querySelector| M4[Run CSS Selector Engine] Call -->|querySelectorAll| M5[Run CSS Selector Engine Collect All] M1 --> R1{Match Found?} M2 --> R2{Build Live HTMLCollection} M3 --> R3{Build Live HTMLCollection} M4 --> R6{Match Found?} M5 --> R7[Build Static NodeList] R1 -->|Yes| Out1[Return Element] R1 -->|No| Null1[Return null] R2 --> Out2[Return HTMLCollection] R3 --> Out3[Return HTMLCollection] R6 -->|Yes| Out4[Return First Element] R6 -->|No| Null2[Return null] R7 --> Out5[Return NodeList] Out1 --> Manip[Perform DOM Manipulation] Out2 --> Manip Out3 --> Manip Out4 --> Manip Out5 --> Manip Null1 --> Err[Handle TypeError Risk] Null2 --> Err Manip --> End([Updated Web Page Rendered]) Err --> End ``` ## 4.3 Live vs Static Collection ŌĆö Memory State Diagram ```mermaid graph LR subgraph Live_Collection[Live HTMLCollection] L1[Reference to DOM Query] L2[Auto-sync with DOM] end subgraph Static_Collection[Static NodeList] S1[Frozen Snapshot at T0] S2[Fixed list of nodes] end DOM[DOM Tree] -->|Always Queries| Live_Collection DOM -->|Snapshotted Once| Static_Collection Live_Collection --> L3[Length updates if DOM changes] Static_Collection --> S3[Length stays constant] L3 --> Manip1[Mutation reflects immediately] S3 --> Manip2[Mutation NOT reflected] ``` ## 4.4 CSS Selector Anatomy for querySelector ```mermaid graph LR A[querySelector String] --> B[Selector Engine] B --> C{Selector Type} C -->|ID| D[#myId] C -->|Class| E[.myClass] C -->|Tag| F[div] C -->|Attribute| G[input type text] C -->|Pseudo| H[hover nth-child] C -->|Combinator| I[div > p + span] C -->|Group| J[h1, h2, h3] ``` <!-- SECTION_4_END --> <!-- SECTION_5_START --> # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Q1. [KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023, Model Paper]** **Differentiate between `getElementById()` and `querySelector()`. Mention the return type and behavior when no element is found.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Aspect | `getElementById()` | `querySelector()` | |--------|--------------------|-------------------| | Selector Type | Accepts only an `id` string | Accepts any valid CSS selector | | Return Type | Single `Element` object | Single `Element` object (first match) | | When Not Found | Returns `null` | Returns `null` | | Speed | Fastest (uses internal id index) | Slightly slower (runs selector engine) | | Flexibility | Low | High (supports combinators, pseudo-classes) | > Both return `null` if no match is found; both return a single `Element` object on success. --- ### **Q2. [KTU University Exam ŌĆō June 2024, Expected Pattern]** **Explain the difference between a "live" `HTMLCollection` and a "static" `NodeList` with an example.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** A **live HTMLCollection** (returned by `getElementsByClassName`/`getElementsByTagName`) **automatically reflects changes** made to the DOM after the collection was created. A **static NodeList** (returned by `querySelectorAll`) is a **frozen snapshot** that does not update. **Example:** ```javascript let liveItems = document.getElementsByClassName("item"); let staticItems = document.querySelectorAll(".item"); let newEl = document.createElement("div"); newEl.className = "item"; document.body.appendChild(newEl); console.log(liveItems.length); // 4 (UPDATED) console.log(staticItems.length); // 3 (UNCHANGED) ``` --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each ŌĆö Internal Choice) ### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023 Style]** **(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **Document Object Model (DOM) tree** and describe how it enables element finding in JavaScript. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Write a JavaScript program that uses **all five** element-finding methods (`getElementById`, `getElementsByClassName`, `getElementsByTagName`, `querySelector`, and `querySelectorAll`) on a sample HTML page containing a form, list, and headings. Display the results in the console. **(7 marks)** --- #### **Model Solution for Q.A (a):** The **Document Object Model (DOM)** is a **tree-structured, platform- and language-neutral interface** that allows programs and scripts to dynamically access and update the content, structure, and style of a document. The W3C defines the DOM as: > *"The Document Object Model provides a standard set of objects for representing HTML and XML documents, a standard model of how these objects can be combined, and a standard interface for accessing and manipulating them."* **Tree Structure (ASCII Representation):** ``` document | <html> / \ <head> <body> | / | \ <title> <h1> <ul> <form> | | | text <li>├Ś3 <input>├Ś2 ``` **How it enables element finding:** 1. The browser's **HTML parser** converts source code into a tree of **node objects**. 2. JavaScript accesses this tree via the global `document` object. 3. Each element is an `Element` node with properties like `id`, `className`, `tagName`, and methods like `querySelector`. 4. Finding methods traverse the tree (or use internal indexes) to return references to nodes. 5. Once a node reference is obtained, the script can read (`textContent`) or modify (`style`, `innerHTML`) it. **Key benefit:** Element finding is the **first step** of any dynamic web interaction ŌĆö without locating nodes, no event handling, validation, or animation is possible. --- #### **Model Solution for Q.A (b):** **HTML Setup (`index.html`):** ```html <!DOCTYPE html> <html> <head><title>DOM Demo

Student Directory

  • Alice
  • Bob
  • Charlie
``` **JavaScript (`script.js`):** ```javascript // 1. getElementById ŌĆö finds the single h1 let title = document.getElementById("main-title"); console.log("1. By ID:", title.textContent); // Output: 1. By ID: Student Directory // 2. getElementsByClassName ŌĆö finds all .student li elements let students = document.getElementsByClassName("student"); console.log("2. By Class - count:", students.length); // Output: 2. By Class - count: 3 for (let s of students) console.log(" -", s.textContent); // Output: - Alice // - Bob // - Charlie // 3. getElementsByTagName ŌĆö finds all
  • tags let listItems = document.getElementsByTagName("li"); console.log("3. By Tag - count:", listItems.length); // Output: 3. By Tag - count: 3 // 4. querySelector ŌĆö finds the first .topper let topper = document.querySelector(".topper"); console.log("4. querySelector:", topper.textContent); // Output: 4. querySelector: Charlie // 5. querySelectorAll ŌĆö finds all inputs of type="text" let textInputs = document.querySelectorAll("input[type='text']"); console.log("5. querySelectorAll - count:", textInputs.length); // Output: 5. querySelectorAll - count: 1 textInputs[0].value = "Enter your name here"; ``` > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Students commonly **forget that `getElementsByClassName` returns a live HTMLCollection, not an array**. Calling array methods like `.map()` or `.forEach()` directly on it will throw a TypeError. Always wrap with `Array.from()` or use a `for` loop. Marks are lost here every semester. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) [Alternative Choice ŌĆō KTU Pattern]** **(a)** Compare and contrast `getElementsByClassName()` and `querySelectorAll()`. Discuss their return types, live/static nature, and a real-world use case for each. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Write a JavaScript function `highlightActiveSection()` that uses element-finding methods to: (i) find the navigation bar by ID, (ii) find all anchor tags inside it by tag name, (iii) add the class `active` to the anchor whose `href` matches the current `window.location.pathname`, and (iv) log the total number of anchors found. **(7 marks)** --- #### **Model Solution for Q.B (a):** | Feature | `getElementsByClassName()` | `querySelectorAll()` | |---------|----------------------------|----------------------| | **Selector Input** | A single class name or space-separated list of class names | Any valid CSS selector string | | **Return Type** | `HTMLCollection` (live) | `NodeList` (static) | | **Live/Static** | **Live** ŌĆö auto-updates on DOM changes | **Static** ŌĆö snapshot at query time | | **Performance** | Slightly faster (no selector parsing) | Slightly slower (parses CSS selector) | | **Iteration** | `for` loop or `Array.from()` | Supports `.forEach()` natively | | **Empty Result** | Empty `HTMLCollection` (length 0) | Empty `NodeList` (length 0) | **Real-World Use Cases:** - **`getElementsByClassName`:** A real-time chat application where new messages are dynamically added with class `message`. Using the live collection ensures your "scroll to bottom" or "notification badge" logic sees new messages immediately. - **`querySelectorAll`:** A static report-generation script that takes a snapshot of all `.data-row` elements, processes them, and exports to CSV. The static nature prevents mid-iteration data corruption if rows are added/removed during processing. --- #### **Model Solution for Q.B (b):** **HTML Setup:** ```html
    Home About Contact
    ``` **JavaScript Solution:** ```javascript function highlightActiveSection() { // (i) Find the navigation bar by ID let nav = document.getElementById("main-nav"); if (nav === null) { console.log("Navigation bar not found."); return; } // (ii) Find all anchor tags inside the nav using tag name let links = nav.getElementsByTagName("a"); // (iv) Log total count console.log("Total anchors found:", links.length); // Get current path let currentPath = window.location.pathname; // (iii) Loop and add 'active' class to the matching link for (let link of links) { if (link.getAttribute("href") === currentPath) { link.classList.add("active"); console.log("Active link set to:", link.textContent); } else { link.classList.remove("active"); } } } // Invoke the function highlightActiveSection(); ``` **Sample Output (if current page is `/about`):** ``` Total anchors found: 3 Active link set to: About ``` > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout ŌĆö Q.B(b):** Many students compare `link.href` directly with `currentPath`. However, `link.href` returns the **fully resolved URL** (e.g., `"http://localhost:5500/about"`), while `pathname` returns just `"/about"`. **Always use `getAttribute("href")`** for an exact string match, or compare both after resolving. This is one of the most common mistakes flagged by KTU evaluators. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist ŌĆö "Finding Elements"** - Ō£ģ **DOM is a tree** ŌĆö every HTML element is a node accessible via `document`. - Ō£ģ **Five core methods:** `getElementById`, `getElementsByClassName`, `getElementsByTagName`, `getElementsByName`, `querySelector`, `querySelectorAll`. - Ō£ģ **`getElementById` and `querySelector` return** `null` if no match ŌĆö *always null-check before accessing properties*. - Ō£ģ **Legacy methods return live** `HTMLCollection`/`NodeList` ŌĆö they update with the DOM. - Ō£ģ **`querySelectorAll` returns a static** `NodeList` ŌĆö fixed at query time, supports `.forEach()`. - Ō£ģ **`querySelector` accepts full CSS selectors** ŌĆö IDs, classes, attributes, pseudo-classes, combinators. - Ō£ģ **HTMLCollection is not a true Array** ŌĆö use `Array.from()` to use array methods like `.map()`. - Ō£ģ **For finding a single element:** prefer `getElementById` (fastest) or `querySelector` (most flexible). - Ō£ģ **For finding multiple elements:** prefer `querySelectorAll` (more modern, supports `.forEach()`). - Ō£ģ **Element finding is Step 1** ŌĆö it must precede any DOM manipulation (`.textContent`, `.style`, `.innerHTML`, etc.). - Ō£ģ **KTU frequently tests:** Live vs Static distinction, return types, null-handling, and code that combines multiple methods. - Ō£ģ **Common pitfall:** `document.getElementById` requires *no* `#` prefix; `querySelector` *requires* the `#` for IDs. - Ō£ģ **Attribute selectors** use `[]`, e.g., `input[type="email"]` ŌĆö a high-yield querySelectorAll pattern.

topics.title: jQuery Selection
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEITT833 - WEB DESIGNING > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** jQuery Selection # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!NOTE] > **Definition (KTU Syllabus Aligned)** > **jQuery Selection** refers to the mechanism by which the jQuery library locates, retrieves, and groups together one or more elements from the Document Object Model (DOM) of an HTML page. The selection process is primarily executed through the global factory function **`jQuery()`**, conventionally aliased as **`$()`**, which accepts a CSS-style selector string (or a DOM element/function) as its argument and returns a **jQuery object** ŌĆö a wrapper collection (a jQuery-wrapped array-like structure) of the matched DOM nodes. In strict KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, jQuery selection is the foundational operation upon which the entire library is built ŌĆö it precedes all DOM manipulation, event binding, animation, and AJAX operations. | Parameter | Value / Description | |---|---| | **Core Function** | `jQuery()` / `$()` | | **Input Type** | Selector string, HTML string, DOM element, or function | | **Return Type** | jQuery object (array-like wrapper of 0 or more DOM nodes) | | **Pseudo-class support** | `:first`, `:last`, `:even`, `:odd`, `:eq(n)`, `:gt(n)`, `:lt(n)`, `:animated`, `:visible`, `:hidden`, `:input`, `:checked`, `:selected` | | **Library Size (minified)** | ~**30 KB** (gzipped) | | **Current Stable Line** | jQuery **3.7.x** (as of KTU 2024 syllabus window) | --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy ŌĆö The "Universal Remote" Mental Model > [!IMPORTANT] > **Intuition Builder ŌĆö Think of jQuery Selection as a Universal Remote Control** Imagine your HTML page is a **large hotel building** with hundreds of rooms (the DOM elements: `

`, `

`, ``, etc.). Before you can perform any action (change lights, open curtains, adjust temperature), you first need to **point your remote at the specific room(s)** you want to control. - The **remote control itself** = the `$()` function. - The **room number you type into the remote** = the **selector** (e.g., `"#lobby"`, `".bedroom"`, `"p"`). - The **rooms the remote now points to** = the **jQuery object** (a wrapper holding the actual rooms). - **Button presses on the remote** = jQuery methods (`.hide()`, `.css()`, `.fadeIn()`). If you type `#header` on the remote, the remote highlights every element whose `id` equals `header`. If you type `div.note`, it highlights every `
` with `class="note"`. The remote never gives you the raw rooms ŌĆö it gives you a **handle** (jQuery object) that *knows about* the rooms, so you can give commands uniformly, even if only one room or a hundred rooms match. > [!NOTE] > **Key Insight for Exam Writing:** The selector never *returns* the raw DOM node ŌĆö it always returns a **jQuery object**. This is why every subsequent method like `.html()`, `.val()`, or `.css()` is callable directly on the result of `$()`. Forgetting this distinction is one of the most common valuation errors. --- ## 1.3 The jQuery Object vs. The Raw DOM Element | Property | Raw DOM Element | jQuery Object | |---|---|---| | Returned by | `document.getElementById("x")` | `$("#x")` | | Type | Native browser object | JavaScript array-like wrapper (jQuery-internal class) | | Has jQuery methods (`.fadeIn`, `.html`)? | ŌØī No | Ō£ģ Yes | | Has native methods (`.innerHTML`, `.style`)? | Ō£ģ Yes | ŌÜĀ’ĖÅ Only via `.get(0)` or `[0]` | | Indexed access | N/A (single element) | `$("p")[0]` ŌåÆ first `

` DOM node | | Length property | N/A | `$("p").length` ŌåÆ number of matches | ```html ``` The wrapper-jQuery-object pattern is what allows **method chaining** ŌĆö the most celebrated feature of jQuery syntax ŌĆö because every jQuery method returns the jQuery object itself. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Selector Cheat Sheet ## 2.1 The Selection Pipeline ŌĆö Internal Mechanics When you call `$("selector")`, jQuery executes the following four-step internal pipeline: 1. **Tokenization** ŌĆö The selector string is parsed into tokens (e.g., `"div.note > p:first"` ŌåÆ `[element:div, class:note, child-combinator, element:p, pseudo:first]`). 2. **Native Engine Dispatch** ŌĆö jQuery delegates the actual matching to one of three engines, depending on browser capability: - **`Sizzle`** ŌĆö jQuery's bundled selector engine (still ships with jQuery 3.x as the **fallback**). - **`querySelectorAll`** ŌĆö modern native browser API used when available (the **fast path**). - Legacy `getElementById`, `getElementsByTagName`, `getElementsByClassName` ŌĆö used for trivially optimizable selectors. 3. **DOM Traversal** ŌĆö The engine walks the DOM tree from the **document root** (or from a specified context) and collects matching nodes into an ordered list. 4. **jQuery Wrapping** ŌĆö The matched nodes are pushed into a new jQuery object instance. `this` inside the object is set to the matched set, `this.length` is set to the match count, and `this.context` is set to the search root. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why does this matter for exams?** Because **selector specificity and complexity directly affect runtime performance**. A selector like `$("#nav .item a")` is fast (it can short-circuit at the ID). A selector like `$(":not(div):has(a)")` forces the engine to walk the entire tree ŌĆö KTU questions on jQuery often test this trade-off. --- ## 2.2 The "Five Families" of jQuery Selectors KTU 2024 Scheme Module 3 organizes jQuery selection into the following hierarchical families. You must be fluent in all five for the ESE. ### Family 1 ŌĆö **Basic CSS Selectors** | Syntax | Selects | Example | |---|---|---| | `$("tag")` | All elements of a tag | `$("p")` ŌåÆ all `

` | | `$("#id")` | The element with that ID | `$("#logo")` ŌåÆ element with `id="logo"` | | `$(".class")` | All elements with that class | `$(".btn")` ŌåÆ all elements with `class="btn"` | | `$("*")` | Every element in the context | `$("body *")` ŌåÆ all descendants of `` | | `$("tag.class")` | AND combination | `$("p.intro")` ŌåÆ `

` with class `intro` | | `$("tag, tag, .class")` | OR combination | `$("h1, h2, .title")` | ### Family 2 ŌĆö **Hierarchy (Combinator) Selectors** | Combinator | Symbol | Meaning | Example | |---|---|---|---| | Descendant | space | Any nested level | `$("div p")` ŌåÆ `

` inside any `

` | | Child | `>` | Direct child only | `$("ul > li")` ŌåÆ `
  • ` whose parent is `
      ` | | Adjacent sibling | `+` | The very next sibling | `$("h1 + p")` ŌåÆ `

      ` immediately after `

      ` | | General sibling | `~` | Any following sibling | `$("h1 ~ p")` ŌåÆ all `

      ` siblings after `

      ` | ### Family 3 ŌĆö **Basic Filter Pseudo-classes** (Indexed) These filter a matched set by **position**. | Syntax | Selects | Example | |---|---|---| | `:first` | First match | `$("li:first")` | | `:last` | Last match | `$("li:last")` | | `:even` | Even indices (0, 2, 4ŌĆ”) | `$("tr:even")` | | `:odd` | Odd indices (1, 3, 5ŌĆ”) | `$("tr:odd")` | | `:eq(n)` | The one at index `n` (0-based) | `$("li:eq(2)")` ŌåÆ 3rd `
    • ` | | `:gt(n)` | All with index greater than `n` | `$("li:gt(2)")` ŌåÆ 4th, 5th, ŌĆ” | | `:lt(n)` | All with index less than `n` | `$("li:lt(2)")` ŌåÆ 1st and 2nd | | `:not(sel)` | Negation | `$("input:not(:checked)")` | ### Family 4 ŌĆö **Attribute Selectors** | Syntax | Selects | Example | |---|---|---| | `[attr]` | Has the attribute | `$("[title]")` | | `[attr="v"]` | Exact match | `$("[type='text']")` | | `[attr!="v"]` | No such attribute OR not equal | `$("[type!='submit']")` | | `[attr^="v"]` | Starts with | `$("[href^='https']")` | | `[attr$="v"]` | Ends with | `$("[src$='.png']")` | | `[attr*="v"]` | Contains substring | `$("[class*='btn']")` | | `[attr~="v"]` | Whitespace-separated word match | `$("[class~='active']")` | | `[attr\|="v"]` | Exact OR prefix-followed-by-hyphen | `$("[lang\|='en']")` | ### Family 5 ŌĆö **Form Selectors** | Syntax | Selects | |---|---| | `:input` | All ``, `
