topics.content: > **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEITT833 - WEB DESIGNING > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Getting Element Content ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) In the **jQuery JavaScript Library**, *Getting Element Content* refers to the set of **DOM accessor (getter) methods** that retrieve the textual, structural, attribute, or property information of one or more matched HTML elements from the Document Object Model without altering the underlying document. The four canonical jQuery getter methods are: 1. `$(selector).html()` ŌĆö Retrieves the **inner HTML** (markup + text) of the first matched element. 2. `$(selector).text()` ŌĆö Retrieves the **combined text content** of all matched elements (markup stripped). 3. `$(selector).val()` ŌĆö Retrieves the current **value** of form elements (``, `

topics.title: Updating Elements
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEITT833 - WEB DESIGNING > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Updating Elements # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition In the context of **Web Designing (OEITT833 ŌĆō Module 3: JavaScript & jQuery for Dynamic Web Pages)**, **Updating Elements** refers to the systematic process of **modifying the content, attributes, properties, and styling of existing HTML Document Object Model (DOM) nodes** at runtime, without requiring a full page reload. This operation constitutes one of the three pillars of DOM manipulation (the other two being *Retrieval* and *Deletion*). According to the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for OEITT833, updating elements is formally defined as: > *"The act of changing the text, HTML markup, attribute values, CSS properties, or form values of one or more selected DOM elements using JavaScript native methods (such as `innerHTML`, `textContent`, `setAttribute()`) or jQuery methods (such as `.html()`, `.text()`, `.attr()`, `.css()`, `.val()`)."* The **DOM (Document Object Model)** is a W3C-standard, tree-structured, object-oriented representation of an HTML or XML document, where every element, attribute, and text node is exposed as a programmable object. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight ŌĆö Module 3 Outcome (CO3):** > *"Apply JavaScript and jQuery techniques to manipulate DOM elements, handle events, and update content dynamically for responsive web design."* > The topic "Updating Elements" is a direct assessor of CO3 and carries significant weightage in End Semester Evaluations (ESE). ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine a **physical notice board mounted on a college corridor wall**. 1. The **frame of the board** is fixed once installed ŌĆö this is your **HTML structure** (`
`, `

`, ``, etc.). 2. The **paper notices pinned on the board** can be **swapped out, rewritten, restyled, or repositioned** at any time ŌĆö these are the **updatable elements**. 3. The **janitor (JavaScript / jQuery engine)** is the worker who, upon hearing instructions from the **Principal (user event / code logic)**, walks up to the board and changes its contents. | Physical Board | Web Equivalent | |----------------|----------------| | Notice board frame | HTML `

` | | Pinned paper | Inner text/HTML content | | Marker color (red/blue) | CSS style (color, font) | | Title heading on the paper | HTML attribute (e.g., `title="..."`) | | Janitor's instruction slip | JavaScript / jQuery code line | > [!NOTE] > Just as the janitor does not dismantle the entire corridor wall to change a notice, the JavaScript engine does **not reload the entire web page** to update a DOM element ŌĆö it surgically modifies only the targeted node. This is the essence of **dynamic, asynchronous web updating**. ## 1.3 Why "Updating Elements" is a Core Web Skill Modern web applications (Gmail, Facebook, e-commerce carts, real-time dashboards) are **Single Page Applications (SPAs)** in spirit. They rely almost entirely on element updating techniques to: - Reflect user input into the UI in real time. - Update cart totals, scores, message counts, stock availability. - Inject server-fetched data (via `fetch()` / `AJAX`) into predefined placeholder elements. - Provide smooth UX without disruptive page reloads. The performance metric of such updates is measured by the time taken between the user action and the visual re-render, which is targeted to be under **100 ms** for a perceived "instant" feel (per Google's **RAIL** performance model). > [!TIP] > **Key KTU Term to Memorize:** The four axes of updating an element are ŌĆö *Content* (text/HTML), *Attribute* (id, class, src, href), *Style* (CSS properties), and *Value* (form input values). Most exam questions in KTU 2024 map directly to one of these four axes. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Four Dimensions of Element Updating Every update operation on a DOM element in JavaScript / jQuery falls into exactly one (or a combination) of the following four categories. KTU examiners love asking classification questions on this. ### Dimension A ŌĆö Updating **Content** (Text / Inner HTML) - **JavaScript Native:** - `element.innerHTML = "..."` ŌåÆ sets the HTML *markup* inside the element (parses tags). - `element.textContent = "..."` ŌåÆ sets only *plain text* (safe, no tag parsing, XSS-safe). - `element.innerText = "..."` ŌåÆ like `textContent` but respects CSS visibility (slower). - **jQuery:** - `$(selector).html("...")` ŌåÆ jQuery equivalent of `innerHTML`. - `$(selector).text("...")` ŌåÆ jQuery equivalent of `textContent`. ### Dimension B ŌĆö Updating **Attributes** (id, class, src, href, title, data-*) - **JavaScript Native:** - `element.setAttribute("name", "value")` - `element.getAttribute("name")` - `element.removeAttribute("name")` - Direct property access: `element.id = "newId"`, `element.className = "newClass"`, `element.src = "new.jpg"` - **jQuery:** - `$(selector).attr("name", "value")` ŌåÆ set an attribute - `$(selector).attr({name1: val1, name2: val2})` ŌåÆ set multiple attributes (object overload) ### Dimension C ŌĆö Updating **Styles** (CSS properties) - **JavaScript Native:** - `element.style.property = "value"` (e.g., `element.style.color = "red"`, `element.style.backgroundColor = "blue"`) - Note: CSS property names with hyphens are converted to **camelCase** in JS. - **jQuery:** - `$(selector).css("property", "value")` - `$(selector).css({property1: val1, property2: val2})` ŌåÆ set multiple CSS properties ### Dimension D ŌĆö Updating **Form Values** - **JavaScript Native:** - `inputElement.value = "new value"` - **jQuery:** - `$(selector).val("new value")` - `$(selector).val()` (getter form) ## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Method Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > Memorize the following table verbatim. It is the **single highest-yield revision sheet** for "Updating Elements" in KTU ESE. | Axis of Update | JavaScript Native Method | jQuery Method | Return Type | Parses HTML Tags? | Safe from XSS? | |----------------|--------------------------|---------------|-------------|-------------------|----------------| | Set HTML content | `el.innerHTML = "..."` | `$sel.html("...")` | String | Ō£ģ Yes | ŌØī No | | Set plain text | `el.textContent = "..."` | `$sel.text("...")` | String | ŌØī No | Ō£ģ Yes | | Get text | `el.textContent` | `$sel.text()` | String | ŌØī No | Ō£ģ Yes | | Set attribute | `el.setAttribute("k","v")` | `$sel.attr("k","v")` | undefined / jQuery obj. | N/A | N/A | | Remove attribute | `el.removeAttribute("k")` | `$sel.removeAttr("k")` | undefined | N/A | N/A | | Set single CSS prop | `el.style.color = "red"` | `$sel.css("color","red")` | String | N/A | N/A | | Set multiple CSS props | ŌØī (chain needed) | `$sel.css({...})` | jQuery object | N/A | N/A | | Set form value | `input.value = "..."` | `$sel.val("...")` | String / Array | N/A | N/A | | Add CSS class | `el.classList.add("c")` | `$sel.addClass("c")` | undefined | N/A | N/A | | Remove CSS class | `el.classList.remove("c")` | `$sel.removeClass("c")` | undefined | N/A | N/A | | Toggle CSS class | `el.classList.toggle("c")` | `$sel.toggleClass("c")` | Boolean | N/A | N/A | | Check class presence | `el.classList.contains("c")` | `$sel.hasClass("c")` | Boolean | N/A | N/A | ## 2.3 The Chaining Paradigm (jQuery's Killer Feature) jQuery's `$(selector).action1().action2().action3()` chain is the reason it became ubiquitous before vanilla JS matured. Each setter method in jQuery returns the **same jQuery wrapped set**, allowing fluid pipelines: ```javascript $("#userBox") .text("Hello, Anu") .css({ "color": "white", "background": "navy", "padding": "10px" }) .attr("title", "Greeting box") .addClass("active-highlight"); ``` > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Utility:** In real-time **Live Scoreboards**, **Stock Tickers**, and **IoT Dashboards**, element updaters are invoked inside a `setInterval()` or a WebSocket `onmessage` handler ŌĆö typically **every 1 to 5 seconds**. The efficiency of native DOM methods versus jQuery becomes critical at scale (jQuery adds ~30 KB of overhead; modern vanilla JS is preferred for performance-critical apps). ## 2.4 The "Why" Behind Each Method - **Why `textContent` is XSS-safe:** It treats the input as a literal string and does not invoke the HTML parser. So `` is shown as text rather than executed. - **Why CSS properties are camelCase in JS:** The CSS Object Model (CSSOM) uses **DOM Level 2 Style interfaces**, where `background-color` becomes `backgroundColor` to be a valid JavaScript identifier. - **Why jQuery overwrites the entire content:** Both `.html()` and `.text()` are *destructive setters* ŌĆö they replace, not append. For appending, use `.append()` / `.prepend()`. - **Why attribute names remain kebab-case in jQuery:** jQuery mirrors the HTML attribute name directly (e.g., `.attr("data-id", 42)`), unlike JS property access (`.dataset.id = 42`). # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Worked Example 1 ŌĆö Updating a Paragraph's Text (Vanilla JavaScript) **Problem Statement:** A `

` initially contains the text *"LoadingŌĆ”"*. Update it to display *"Welcome, KTU Student!"* and color the text **green** when a button is clicked. **Step 1 ŌĆö HTML Skeleton (provided as basis):** ```html

LoadingŌĆ”

``` **Step 2 ŌĆö Logical Flow:** The click event must: 1. Locate the element by its `id`. 2. Mutate its text content. 3. Mutate its CSS `color` property. 4. Optionally mutate its `class` to leverage pre-defined CSS. **Step 3 ŌĆö JavaScript Implementation with Exhaustive Line-by-Line Reasoning:** ```javascript // STEP 3.1 ŌĆö Hook the event listener to the button's click event. document.getElementById("goBtn").addEventListener("click", function () { // STEP 3.2 ŌĆö Grab a reference to the

element using its unique id. // getElementById returns a single live HTMLElement object. const para = document.getElementById("greeting"); // STEP 3.3 ŌĆö Replace its inner text using textContent (safe from HTML parsing). // This completely overwrites the previous "LoadingŌĆ”" text. para.textContent = "Welcome, KTU Student!"; // STEP 3.4 ŌĆö Update the CSS color property using the style object. // Note: "color" has no hyphen, so camelCase conversion is NOT needed here. para.style.color = "green"; // STEP 3.5 ŌĆö Add a CSS class for additional styling (e.g., bold, font-size). // classList.add is idempotent: adding an existing class is a no-op. para.classList.add("highlighted"); // STEP 3.6 ŌĆö Update a custom data-* attribute (demonstration of setAttribute). para.setAttribute("data-status", "updated"); }); ``` **Step 4 ŌĆö Validation Logic:** - After clicking, the DOM tree's `

` node now contains: - text node: `"Welcome, KTU Student!"` - inline style: `color: green;` - class attribute: `"highlighted"` - data attribute: `data-status="updated"` **Step 5 ŌĆö DOM State Visualization (before vs after):** $$ \text{DOM}_{\text{before}} = \text{

} \to \text{TextNode("LoadingŌĆ”")} $$ $$ \text{DOM}_{\text{after}} = \text{

} \to \text{TextNode("Welcome, KTU Student!")} $$ ## 3.2 Worked Example 2 ŌĆö Multi-Element Update Using jQuery **Problem Statement:** A list of 5 student names is rendered in `

  • ` elements. On clicking a "Highlight Toppers" button, every `
  • ` whose text equals `"Anu"` or `"Rahul"` should turn **yellow**, get `class="topper"`, and have their `title` attribute set to `"Distinction"`. **Step 1 ŌĆö HTML Skeleton:** ```html
    • Anu
    • Jobin
    • Rahul
    • Meera
    • Sreya
    ``` **Step 2 ŌĆö jQuery Implementation:** ```javascript // STEP 2.1 ŌĆö Ensure DOM is fully loaded before binding (prevents null reference errors). $(document).ready(function () { // STEP 2.2 ŌĆö Bind the click event to the highlight button. $("#highlightBtn").on("click", function () { // STEP 2.3 ŌĆö Use the :contains() pseudo-selector to filter
  • elements by text. // The selector returns a jQuery collection of matching elements. const toppers = $("#studentList li:contains('Anu'), #studentList li:contains('Rahul')"); // STEP 2.4 ŌĆö Apply CSS, class, and attribute updates in a single chain. toppers .css("background-color", "yellow") // Dimension C ŌĆö Style update .addClass("topper") // Dimension B ŌĆö Class (attribute) update .attr("title", "Distinction") // Dimension B ŌĆö Custom attribute update .text(function (i, oldText) { // Dimension A ŌĆö Content update with callback return oldText + " ŌŁÉ"; // Append a star emoji to the existing name }); }); }); ``` **Step 3 ŌĆö Analytical Trace:** $$ \text{Selected Set} = \{\text{li}_1\text{(Anu)}, \text{li}_3\text{(Rahul)}\} $$ $$ \text{After chain execution, each } \text{li}_i \in \text{Selected Set} \text{ has:} $$ $$ \begin{aligned} \text{background-color} &= \text{yellow} \\ \text{class} &\leftarrow \text{classList} \cup \{\text{"topper"}\} \\ \text{title} &= \text{"Distinction"} \\ \text{textContent} &= \text{oldName} + \text{" ŌŁÉ"} \end{aligned} $$ > [!NOTE] > **The `.text(function)` callback** is a frequently tested jQuery pattern. It receives the **index `i`** and the **current text `oldText`** of each element, allowing element-specific transformations. KTU may ask: *"Differentiate between `.text("string")` and `.text(function(i, old){...})`."* ## 3.3 Worked Example 3 ŌĆö Updating Form Input Value Live (Pure JS) **Problem Statement:** A text input has a default value of `""`. As the user types, a sibling `` should mirror the typed characters in **UPPERCASE**, character by character. **Step 1 ŌĆö HTML Skeleton:** ```html ``` **Step 2 ŌĆö JavaScript Implementation:** ```javascript // STEP 2.1 ŌĆö Reference the input element using querySelector (CSS-style selector). const inputBox = document.querySelector("#nameInput"); const mirrorSpan = document.querySelector("#mirror"); // STEP 2.2 ŌĆö Listen to the 'input' event (fires on EVERY keystroke, unlike 'change'). inputBox.addEventListener("input", function () { // STEP 2.3 ŌĆö Read the current value of the input (getter form). const raw = inputBox.value; // STEP 2.4 ŌĆö Transform to uppercase using String.prototype.toUpperCase(). const transformed = raw.toUpperCase(); // STEP 2.5 ŌĆö Write the transformed value back into the span (setter form). mirrorSpan.textContent = transformed; // STEP 2.6 ŌĆö Conditionally update style based on length (dimension C). if (transformed.length > 8) { mirrorSpan.style.color = "red"; mirrorSpan.style.fontWeight = "bold"; } else { mirrorSpan.style.color = "black"; mirrorSpan.style.fontWeight = "normal"; } }); ``` **Step 3 ŌĆö The Getter vs Setter Duality (A KTU Favourite):** | Method | Getter Form (Read) | Setter Form (Write) | |--------|--------------------|---------------------| | `el.textContent` | `el.textContent` ŌåÆ returns String | `el.textContent = "x"` ŌåÆ returns undefined | | `$(sel).text()` | `$(sel).text()` ŌåÆ returns String | `$(sel).text("x")` ŌåÆ returns jQuery object (chainable) | | `$(sel).html()` | `$(sel).html()` ŌåÆ returns String | `$(sel).html("x")` ŌåÆ returns jQuery object (chainable) | | `$(sel).val()` | `$(sel).val()` ŌåÆ returns value | `$(sel).val("x")` ŌåÆ returns jQuery object | | `$(sel).attr("k")` | `$(sel).attr("k")` ŌåÆ returns value | `$(sel).attr("k","v")` ŌåÆ returns jQuery object | | `$(sel).css("k")` | `$(sel).css("k")` ŌåÆ returns value | `$(sel).css("k","v")` ŌåÆ returns jQuery object | > [!TIP] > **Examiner's Trick:** Most students lose marks by writing `el.textContent("hello")` (calling it as a function). Always remember: in **vanilla JavaScript**, `textContent` is a **property** (use `=`); in **jQuery**, `.text()` is a **method** (use parentheses). # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Architecture of an "Update Operation" ŌĆö Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart TD A[User Action or Timer Event] --> B{Selector Engine} B -->|Vanilla JS| C1[document.querySelector / getElementById] B -->|jQuery| C2[$ Sizzle Engine] C1 --> D[Target DOM Node Object] C2 --> D D --> E{Dimension of Update} E -->|Content| F1[innerHTML / textContent / .html() / .text()] E -->|Attribute| F2[setAttribute / .attr() / classList] E -->|Style| F3[style.property / .css()] E -->|Form Value| F4[input.value / .val()] F1 --> G[DOM Tree Mutation] F2 --> G F3 --> G F4 --> G G --> H[Browser Rendering Engine] H --> I[Layout / Paint / Composite] I --> J[User Sees Updated UI] G --> K[Optional Event Dispatch] K --> L[MutationObserver / Custom Event] ``` ## 4.2 Getter vs Setter Decision Flow ```mermaid flowchart LR Q1{Is the method called with arguments?} -->|No| R1[Getter Mode: READ current value] Q1 -->|Yes| R2[Setter Mode: WRITE new value] R1 --> S1[Returns String or Array] R2 --> S2[Returns undefined or jQuery wrapped set] S2 --> S3[Triggers DOM reflow and repaint] ``` ## 4.3 jQuery Chaining Pipeline for Bulk Updates ```mermaid flowchart LR P1[Selector $] --> P2[Step1 Filter] P2 --> P3[Step2 Update Text] P3 --> P4[Step3 Update CSS] P4 --> P5[Step4 Update Attribute] P5 --> P6[Step5 Add Class] P6 --> P7[End of Chain] P7 --> P8[Same jQuery Object Returned for Each Step] ``` ## 4.4 Mapping of the Four Update Dimensions to Real-World UI Components ```mermaid flowchart TD D1[Axis 1: Content Update] --> D1A[Live News Ticker] D1 --> D1B[Search Auto-complete Dropdown] D1A --> D1C[Uses innerHTML or .html to inject rich text] D1B --> D1C D2[Axis 2: Attribute Update] --> D2A[Profile Picture src change after upload] D2 --> D2B[Anchor href change after login] D2A --> D2C[Uses setAttribute src or .attr src] D2B --> D2C D3[Axis 3: Style Update] --> D3A[Dark Mode toggle button] D3 --> D3B[Hover effect on table rows] D3A --> D3C[Uses style background or .css background] D3B --> D3C D4[Axis 4: Form Value Update] --> D4A[Quantity stepper in cart] D4 --> D4B[Checkbox check all in table] D4A --> D4C[Uses input.value or .val to set new numeric] D4B --> D4C ``` > [!NOTE] > **Block Diagram Interpretation:** Each block represents a functional stage in the lifecycle of an "Update Elements" operation, from the trigger event to the final visible UI change. The **DOM Tree Mutation** stage is the heart of the operation ŌĆö it is where the actual in-memory representation of the document is altered, and the browser's rendering engine subsequently performs **Layout ŌåÆ Paint ŌåÆ Composite** to display the change. # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question A1 **`[KTU University Exam ŌĆö May 2024]`** *Explain the difference between `innerHTML` and `textContent` in JavaScript with a suitable example.* **Model Answer (3 Marks):** - `innerHTML` (1 mark): Property that gets or sets the **HTML markup** contained within an element. It **parses** the assigned string as HTML, so any tags within it are rendered. Example: `div.innerHTML = "Hi";` makes the text bold. - `textContent` (1 mark): Property that gets or sets the **plain text** content. It treats the input as a literal string; HTML tags are not parsed. Example: `div.textContent = "Hi";` displays the literal text `Hi`. - Security note (1 mark): `textContent` is **safe from Cross-Site Scripting (XSS) attacks**, whereas `innerHTML` is vulnerable because it executes any ` ``` **Explanation of each line (briefly for marks):** - `getElementById` (0.5 mark): Retrieves a reference to each element. - `addEventListener` (0.5 mark): Binds the click event handler to the button. - `inputEl.value` (0.5 mark): **Read** operation ŌĆö getter form of the `value` property. - `outputEl.textContent = ...` (1 mark): **Write** operation ŌĆö content setter. - `outputEl.style.backgroundColor = ...` (1.5 marks): **Write** operation ŌĆö CSS setter using camelCase. - `outputEl.setAttribute("title", "Updated")` (1 mark): **Write** operation ŌĆö attribute setter. - Conditional logic and `length` check (1 mark). - Proper HTML structure with ``, ``, `` (1 mark). --- ## 5.3 ŌÜĀ’ĖÅ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:** > 1. **Confusing property vs method** ŌĆö Writing `el.textContent("hi")` (parentheses) instead of `el.textContent = "hi"`. JavaScript setters are **properties**, not methods. > 2. **Hyphen vs camelCase in JS** ŌĆö Writing `el.style.background-color` will throw a `SyntaxError`. Correct form: `el.style.backgroundColor`. > 3. **Forgetting the jQuery `$` wrapper** ŌĆö Writing `("p").text("hi")` instead of `$("p").text("hi")`. The `$` is the jQuery function and is **mandatory**. > 4. **Using `innerHTML` for user input** ŌĆö Vulnerable to XSS. Examiners deduct 1 mark if security is ignored when user input is rendered. > 5. **Forgetting to load jQuery CDN** ŌĆö Code using `$` placed **before** the `

topics.title: Changing Content
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEITT833 - WEB DESIGNING > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Changing Content ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme OEITT833 (Web Designing)** syllabus, **"Changing Content"** refers to the dynamic modification of the textual, structural, and attribute-level content of an HTML document after it has been initially loaded by the browser. This is accomplished primarily through the **Document Object Model (DOM) API**, which exposes every element of an HTML page as a programmable object in memory. Formally, *Content Modification* is defined as: > The runtime alteration of node properties ŌĆö such as `innerHTML`, `textContent`, `innerText`, and `attributes` ŌĆö of existing DOM elements using JavaScript, without requiring a full page reload from the server. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Module 3 of OEITT833 explicitly tests the student's ability to *select* elements (covered in prior topics) and then **modify their content** using core DOM properties. Questions frequently appear in ESE demanding the distinction between `innerHTML` and `textContent`. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine you have printed a **newspaper**. The news is static ŌĆö printed in ink and unchangeable. Now imagine a **digital notice board in a shopping mall**. The content displayed on it can be updated every few seconds from a central control room without anyone touching the screen physically. - The **printed newspaper** is analogous to a **static HTML page** (you must rewrite and re-print the entire page to change anything). - The **digital notice board** is analogous to a **DOM-manipulated HTML page** ŌĆö JavaScript is the "central control room" that swaps the displayed text, images, or styles instantly. ### 1.3 The Four Standard Content-Change Channels The KTU syllabus recognizes four canonical techniques to change content: 1. **`element.innerHTML`** ŌĆö Reads or replaces the **HTML markup** inside an element. 2. **`element.textContent`** ŌĆö Reads or replaces the **plain text** (no HTML parsing). 3. **`element.innerText`** ŌĆö Similar to `textContent`, but respects CSS visibility (renders only visible text). 4. **`document.write()`** ŌĆö Writes content directly into the document stream (legacy technique, rarely recommended). > [!NOTE] > **Why does this matter?** Without content-change APIs, every interaction (a button click, form submission, live clock) would require a full HTTP round-trip to the server ŌĆö making modern web apps impossibly slow. Content modification is the **engine of interactivity**. ### 1.4 Key Standard Metrics (Bolded) - **DOM (Document Object Model):** A W3C-standard tree-structured representation of any HTML/XML document. - **Node:** Every element, attribute, and piece of text in the DOM tree is a *node*. - **Element Node:** A node that represents an HTML tag (e.g., `

`, `

`). - **Text Node:** A node that contains the raw textual data inside an element. - **Cross-Site Scripting (XSS):** A security vulnerability introduced by careless use of `innerHTML` with untrusted input. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet ### 2.1 Operational Breakdown of Each Content-Change Method #### 2.1.1 The `innerHTML` Property - **What it does:** Returns the HTML markup (including tags) contained within an element as a string. When assigned a value, it **parses the string as HTML** and replaces all child nodes. - **Why use it:** When you need to inject a **chunk of structured HTML** (e.g., a list, a table row, formatted text). - **How it works internally:** 1. Browser takes the assigned string. 2. Hands it to the **HTML parser**. 3. Parser generates new DOM nodes. 4. Old child nodes are **detached and discarded**. 5. New nodes are **appended** as children of the element. ```javascript document.getElementById("box").innerHTML = "Updated!"; ``` #### 2.1.2 The `textContent` Property - **What it does:** Returns *all* the text inside an element, including hidden text, without any HTML tags. When assigned, it sets plain text ŌĆö **HTML in the string is rendered as literal characters**, not parsed. - **Why use it:** When you want **security and predictability** ŌĆö perfect for displaying user-supplied data safely. - **How it works:** Directly replaces the text node, bypassing the HTML parser. ```javascript document.getElementById("box").textContent = "Updated!"; // The user literally sees: Updated! ``` #### 2.1.3 The `innerText` Property - **What it does:** Like `textContent`, but aware of CSS ŌĆö it skips elements that are `display: none` and respects rendered line breaks. - **Why use it:** When you want to read what the user actually *sees* on screen (e.g., for a copy-to-clipboard feature). - **Performance warning:** Triggers a **layout reflow** because it must compute visibility. #### 2.1.4 The `document.write()` Method - **What it does:** Writes an HTML string directly into the document **while it is still loading**. - **Why avoid it:** Calling it *after* page load **completely wipes the entire document** and replaces it ŌĆö destroying event listeners and the DOM tree. KTU examiners often deduct marks for using this in modern code. ### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet | Method / Property | Reads HTML Tags? | Parses HTML on Write? | Safe from XSS? | Affects Performance | Use Case | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | `innerHTML` | Ō£ģ Yes (returns markup) | Ō£ģ Yes | ŌØī No ŌĆö **unsafe with user input** | High (full subtree rebuild) | Injecting structured HTML | | `textContent` | ŌØī No (returns plain text only) | ŌØī No (treated as text) | Ō£ģ **Yes ŌĆö safest option** | Low (single text node) | User input, plain text updates | | `innerText` | ŌØī No | ŌØī No | Ō£ģ Yes | High (forces reflow) | Copying visible text | | `document.write()` | N/A | Ō£ģ Yes | ŌØī No | Very High | **Deprecated ŌĆö avoid** | ### 2.3 The Cross-Site Scripting (XSS) Threat ŌĆö Engineering Reality > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why this matters in industry:** In 2024, XSS remains the **#1 most reported web vulnerability** (OWASP Top 10). When a KTU question asks "Why is `textContent` preferred over `innerHTML`?", the answer is *security*. **Real-World Engineering Utility:** - **Live chat widgets** (WhatsApp Web, Slack) ŌåÆ use `textContent` for incoming messages. - **Code editors** (CodePen, JSFiddle) ŌåÆ use `innerHTML` because the input is *trusted* developer code that must render with formatting. - **Banking dashboards** ŌåÆ exclusively use `textContent` for displaying transaction amounts to prevent injection attacks. ### 2.4 The `attributes` API ŌĆö Changing Non-Textual Content Beyond text, KTU Module 3 also covers **changing element attributes** (the values inside HTML tags like `src`, `href`, `class`, `id`): ```javascript let img = document.getElementById("logo"); img.setAttribute("src", "newlogo.png"); // Change image source img.setAttribute("alt", "Updated Logo"); // Change alt text let current = img.getAttribute("src"); // Read current source img.removeAttribute("alt"); // Remove attribute img.hasAttribute("alt"); // Boolean check ``` Direct property access is also allowed for standard HTML attributes: ```javascript img.src = "newlogo.png"; // Equivalent to setAttribute("src", ...) ``` > [!NOTE] > **Exam Tip:** KTU examiners often ask: *"What is the difference between `setAttribute()` and direct property assignment?"* ŌĆö Direct property access works only for **standard DOM properties**, whereas `setAttribute()` works on **any custom or non-standard attribute** (e.g., `data-*` attributes). ## 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Walkthrough ### 3.1 Worked Example: Safe vs Unsafe Content Update **Problem Scenario:** A user types their name into a form field. We must display it on the page in real-time. **HTML Skeleton:** ```html

Your name will appear here.

``` #### Method A ŌĆö Unsafe (using `innerHTML`) ```javascript document.getElementById("nameInput").oninput = function() { let value = document.getElementById("nameInput").value; document.getElementById("output").innerHTML = value; }; ``` **Step-by-step execution trace:** 1. The user types ``. 2. `nameInput.value` captures the raw string. 3. `innerHTML` receives the string and **parses it as HTML**. 4. The browser **executes** the injected ``. 2. `nameInput.value` captures the raw string. 3. `textContent` receives the string and **treats it as plain text**. 4. The browser renders the literal characters: ``. 5. Ō£ģ **Safe** ŌĆö no script execution; user sees their own input as text. ### 3.2 Formal Derivation: Reflow and Repaint Cost While not algebraic, KTU 2024 Scheme values conceptual mathematical reasoning. The cost of a content change can be conceptualized as: $$ T_{\text{total}} = T_{\text{parse}} + T_{\text{layout}} + T_{\text{paint}} + T_{\text{composite}} $$ Where: $$ T_{\text{layout}} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} C_i \cdot D_i $$ - $T_{\text{parse}}$ = Time to tokenize and build the new DOM nodes. - $T_{\text{layout}}$ = Reflow time, summed over all $n$ affected elements. - $C_i$ = Complexity factor of element $i$ (child count, style complexity). - $D_i$ = Depth factor ŌĆö how nested element $i$ is in the DOM tree. - $T_{\text{paint}}$ = Time to rasterize the visual changes to the framebuffer. - $T_{\text{composite}}$ = Time for the GPU to combine layers into the final frame. **Interpretation for `innerHTML` vs `textContent`:** - `innerHTML` rebuilds the entire subtree ŌåÆ high $n$ ŌåÆ high $T_{\text{layout}}$. - `textContent` modifies a single text node ŌåÆ $n = 1$, no new parsing ŌåÆ minimal $T_{\text{total}}$. - `innerText` is the slowest because it forces a *synchronous* layout to compute visibility before returning a value. ### 3.3 Step-by-Step Attribute Change Operation **Problem:** Toggle an image between two sources when a button is clicked. ```javascript let img = document.getElementById("banner"); let btn = document.getElementById("toggleBtn"); btn.onclick = function() { let currentSrc = img.getAttribute("src"); // Step 1: Read current value if (currentSrc === "day.jpg") { // Step 2: Conditional swap img.setAttribute("src", "night.jpg"); img.setAttribute("alt", "Night View"); } else { img.setAttribute("src", "day.jpg"); img.setAttribute("alt", "Day View"); } // Step 3: Verify (optional) console.log("New source:", img.getAttribute("src")); }; ``` **Derivation of the swap logic:** Let $S$ denote the current `src` attribute. The new source $S'$ is defined as: $$ S' = \begin{cases} \text{night.jpg}, & \text{if } S = \text{day.jpg} \\ \text{day.jpg}, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases} $$ This piecewise function maps the two states deterministically and ensures idempotent toggling on repeated clicks. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 DOM Tree Representation of an HTML Document The following Mermaid diagram illustrates how a typical HTML page is structured as a tree of nodes, which is the foundation upon which all content-change operations act. ```mermaid graph TD docA[Document] --> htmlA[html] htmlA --> headA[head] headA --> titleA[title: My Page] htmlA --> bodyA[body] bodyA --> divA[div id container] divA --> h1A[h1: Welcome] divA --> pA[p id output] pA --> textNodeA[Text Node: Hello World] divA --> btnA[button id updateBtn] btnA --> btnTextA[Text Node: Click Me] ``` ### 4.2 Flow of a Content-Change Operation The complete lifecycle of dynamically updating content in a web page: ```mermaid flowchart TD startA([User Interaction: Click / Input / Timer]) --> selectA[Step 1: Select Target Element via DOM API] selectA --> decideA{Step 2: Choose Modification Method} decideA -->|Structured HTML needed| innerA[Use innerHTML - Parses String] decideA -->|Plain Text Needed| textA[Use textContent - Safe Fast] decideA -->|Change Attribute| attrA[Use setAttribute - Modify Property] innerA --> warnA[Security Check: Is Input Trusted?] textA --> applyA[Step 3: Apply Change to DOM Tree] attrA --> applyA warnA -->|Untrusted Input| sanitizeA[Escape Input or Use textContent] warnA -->|Trusted Input| applyA sanitizeA --> applyA applyA --> browserA[Step 4: Browser Engine Recalculates Styles] browserA --> paintA[Step 5: Paint and Composite to Screen] paintA --> userA([User Sees Updated Content]) ``` ### 4.3 Comparison Matrix: `innerHTML` vs `textContent` ```mermaid graph LR subgraph Input["Input String: b Hello b"] inputNode[Raw String] end inputNode --> innerPath["innerHTML Path"] inputNode --> textPath["textContent Path"] innerPath --> innerParse[HTML Parser Invoked] innerParse --> innerResult[DOM Nodes Created: b element b] textPath --> textDirect[Text Node Created Directly] textDirect --> textResult[Literal Text Displayed: b Hello b] ``` ### 4.4 Hierarchy of Content-Change APIs by Safety ```mermaid graph TB safest["SAFEST: textContent"] --> safe["SAFE: innerText"] safe --> moderate["MODERATE: setAttribute for known attributes"] moderate --> risky["RISKY: innerHTML with sanitized input"] risky --> danger["DANGEROUS: innerHTML with raw user input"] danger --> deprecated["DEPRECATED: document.write after page load"] ``` > [!NOTE] > **Visual Reading:** The diagram above represents an inverse safety hierarchy. The further down you go, the higher the risk of XSS, performance degradation, and unintended side-effects. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023]` **Differentiate between `innerHTML` and `textContent` properties in JavaScript DOM manipulation. Which one is safer and why?** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** | Aspect | `innerHTML` | `textContent` | |---|---|---| | **Reads** | Returns the HTML markup (with tags) inside an element. | Returns only the plain text (no tags). | | **Writes** | Parses the assigned string as HTML and replaces children. | Sets the assigned string as literal text; HTML tags are displayed as characters. | | **Performance** | Slower (re-parses entire subtree). | Faster (modifies a single text node). | | **Security** | **Vulnerable to XSS** when used with untrusted input. | **Safe** ŌĆö no HTML parsing occurs. | **Conclusion:** `textContent` is safer because it never interprets its input as HTML, thereby neutralizing any embedded ` ``` If an attacker enters: ```html ``` The browser parses this as valid HTML, attempts to load the broken image `src="x"`, fires the `onerror` event handler, and **executes the attacker's JavaScript**. The user's session cookie is now exposed. **How `textContent` Prevents It:** If we replace the same line with: ```javascript document.getElementById("comment").textContent = userInput; ``` The browser does **not parse HTML**. The text node is set to the literal string ``. The user sees the raw markup as plain text on screen, and **no script execution occurs**. This is the foundational defense against XSS at the DOM level. **Additional Defenses:** Input sanitization libraries (DOMPurify), Content Security Policy headers, and escaping HTML entities are complementary defenses ŌĆö but `textContent` is the simplest and most reliable client-side mitigation. **(Marks: 1 for XSS definition, 2 for innerHTML exploit example, 2 for textContent defense explanation, 2 for additional context.)** --- **Model Solution for Q.B(b):** **Complete Program:** ```html Live Counter

Counter Application

Current Value: 0

``` **Step-by-Step Logic Explanation:** 1. **State Initialization:** The variable `count` is declared in the global script scope and initialized to `0`. It persists across multiple button clicks because the script runs only once at page load. 2. **Element Selection:** `getElementById` retrieves references to the display `` and both buttons. 3. **Increment Operation:** On each click of the Increment button, `count` is incremented by `1`. The new value is written to the `` using `textContent`. 4. **Reset Operation:** Sets `count` back to `0` and updates the display. 5. **Why `textContent`?** The counter value is always an integer generated by our code (never user input), so `textContent` is used for **clarity, safety, and performance** ŌĆö demonstrating best practice as per KTU 2024 guidelines. **Test Trace:** - Initial state ŌåÆ Display shows `0`. - Click Increment ŌåÆ Display shows `1`. - Click Increment twice ŌåÆ Display shows `3`. - Click Reset ŌåÆ Display shows `0`. **(Marks: 1 for HTML skeleton, 1 for state initialization, 2 for event handlers, 2 for textContent usage and explanation, 1 for trace.)** --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 ESE ŌĆö Changing Content** - **Core Properties to Memorize:** `innerHTML`, `textContent`, `innerText`, `document.write()`, `setAttribute()`, `getAttribute()`, `removeAttribute()`, `hasAttribute()`. - **Golden Rule:** Use `textContent` for **user-supplied data**; use `innerHTML` only for **trusted, developer-controlled HTML**. - **`innerHTML` returns the markup including tags; `textContent` returns only text without tags.** - **`innerText` is CSS-aware and triggers layout reflow; `textContent` is not.** Avoid `innerText` in performance-critical paths. - **`document.write()` after page load DESTROYS the entire document.** Never use it in modern code. - **`setAttribute()` works on any attribute**, including custom `data-*` attributes; direct property access (e.g., `img.src`) works only for standard DOM properties. - **CSS properties in JavaScript use camelCase:** `border-color` becomes `borderColor`, `background-color` becomes `backgroundColor`. - **XSS Prevention Hierarchy:** `textContent` (safest) ŌåÆ `innerHTML` with sanitization ŌåÆ `innerHTML` raw (dangerous). - **Every content-change operation MUST begin with a DOM selection step** (`getElementById`, `querySelector`, etc.). - **Performance Cost:** `innerHTML` > `innerText` > `textContent` (in terms of speed; lower is faster). - **Common KTU Question Pattern:** "Differentiate between X and Y" (always prepare at least 3 distinguishing points). - **Real-World Connection:** Live counters, chat widgets, notification toasts, dynamic form validation messages ŌĆö all rely on these content-change APIs. - **Examiner Pet Topic:** The relationship between `innerHTML` and XSS appears in nearly every KTU ESE paper for this module. - **Code Style Tip:** Use `addEventListener("click", function() { ... })` instead of `element.onclick = ...` if attaching multiple handlers is required ŌĆö but for KTU answers, `onclick` is simpler and acceptable.

topics.title: Inserting Elements
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEITT833 - WEB DESIGNING > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Inserting Elements # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) In the context of **HTML5** and the **Document Object Model (DOM)**, **Inserting Elements** refers to the systematic process of creating, adding, or embedding new HTML elements, text nodes, or content into an existing web page ŌĆö either at **design-time** (statically, while authoring the `.html` file) or at **run-time** (dynamically, through client-side scripting using JavaScript and the DOM API). According to the KTU 2024 Scheme (OEITT833 ŌĆō Web Designing, Module 3), the concept encompasses: > **Insertion** is the act of placing a new node (Element Node, Text Node, or Attribute Node) into the hierarchical tree structure of the DOM at a specified position relative to an existing parent or sibling node. The three fundamental insertion contexts recognized by the W3C DOM Level 3 specification are: | # | Insertion Context | Position | KTU Terminology | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | **As the last child** of a parent | End of children list | **Append** | | 2 | **Before an existing child** | Specific index | **Prepend / Insert Before** | | 3 | **As text/content** of a node | Replaces inner content | **innerHTML Injection** | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Under the NEP 2020 aligned outcome-based framework, students are expected to demonstrate **CO3 (Apply)** ŌĆö *"Apply DOM manipulation techniques to dynamically insert, modify, and remove HTML elements using JavaScript."* This makes "Inserting Elements" a **high-weightage, high-frequency** topic in both **Continuous Evaluation (CE)** and **End Semester Examinations (ESE)**. --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition (Plain English Explanation) Imagine you have a **college notice board** that is currently empty: - The **notice board** = your HTML page (``) - Each **notice pinned on the board** = an HTML element (e.g., `

`, `

`, `

`) - The **peon who pins new notices** = the **JavaScript engine** inside the browser When the principal wants to add a new notice, the peon can: 1. **Glue a new notice at the very bottom** ŌåÆ `appendChild()` (append) 2. **Slide a notice between two existing ones** ŌåÆ `insertBefore()` (prepend/insert) 3. **Erase everything and rewrite the whole board with new notices** ŌåÆ `innerHTML = "..."` 4. **Use a printer to print directly on the board** ŌåÆ `document.write()` This is exactly what "Inserting Elements" means ŌĆö **controlling how new content is added to a web page, and where**. > [!NOTE] > **Plain English Summary for First-Time Learners:** > "Inserting Elements" = *Adding new HTML pieces into your webpage, either by writing them directly in the code (static) or by using JavaScript to add them while the page is open in a browser (dynamic).* --- ## 1.3 Key Terms and Their Standard Definitions (Per KTU Board Standards) | Term | Standard Definition (KTU Style) | Category | |---|---|---| | **DOM (Document Object Model)** | A tree-structured, platform-neutral interface that represents an HTML/XML document as a logical hierarchy of nodes. | Foundational | | **Element Node** | A node in the DOM tree that represents an HTML element (e.g., `

`, `

`). | Node Type | | **Text Node** | A node that contains only textual content (no child elements). | Node Type | | **Parent Node** | A node that has one or more child nodes. | Relationship | | **Child Node** | A node that is a direct descendant of another node. | Relationship | | **Sibling Node** | Nodes that share the same parent. | Relationship | | **innerHTML** | A property that gets or sets the HTML markup contained within an element. | Property | | **insertAdjacentHTML()** | A DOM method that parses a text string as HTML and inserts it at a specified position. | Method | | **DOMString** | A UTF-16 string used to represent HTML/XML content. | Data Type | > [!TIP] > **Remember:** In KTU theory answers, always begin with a **one-line formal definition**, then explain with an **analogy**, and end with a **diagram or table**. This is the proven 14-mark answer structure valued by KTU board examiners. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Classification of Element Insertion Methods In KTU 2024 Scheme, insertion methods are officially classified into **two broad categories**: ### **Category A: Static / Design-Time Insertion** *(No scripting involved ŌĆö done directly in the HTML source code)* This is the **default** method used by every beginner. Elements are typed directly inside the `.html` file between the `` tags. ```html Static Insertion

Welcome to KTU Web Designing

This paragraph is statically inserted.

``` ### **Category B: Dynamic / Run-Time Insertion (JavaScript-Driven)** This is the **exam-favorite** topic. It is subdivided into: #### **B.1 ŌĆö innerHTML Property** The `innerHTML` property is the **most common and simplest** way to insert content. It **replaces** the entire inner HTML of an element with the provided string. ``` element.innerHTML = "

New paragraph

"; ``` **Characteristics:** - Parses the string as **HTML markup**. - **Replaces** all existing children of the target element. - High performance for large content blocks. - ŌÜĀ’ĖÅ **Security Warning:** Vulnerable to **XSS (Cross-Site Scripting)** attacks if user input is directly inserted. #### **B.2 ŌĆö document.write() Method** A legacy method that writes HTML output **directly to the document stream**. ``` document.write("

This is written by JS

"); ``` **Important Rule:** Works only **while the document is being parsed**. Calling it after the page has finished loading will **erase the entire page**. > [!WARNING] > KTU Examiners frequently deduct marks for using `document.write()` in **modern web design** answers. Always note in your answer: *"document.write() is discouraged in modern web development as it overwrites the entire document after page load."* #### **B.3 ŌĆö DOM API Methods (createElement + appendChild / insertBefore)** The **most structured, W3C-recommended** method. It follows a strict 3-step protocol: ``` Step 1: CREATE ŌåÆ document.createElement("p") Step 2: CUSTOMIZE ŌåÆ element.textContent = "Hello" Step 3: INSERT ŌåÆ parent.appendChild(element) ``` **Why this method is preferred in production-grade code:** - Type-safe (no string parsing). - Immune to XSS attacks when `textContent` is used. - Allows event listener attachment on the new element. - Maintains DOM tree integrity. #### **B.4 ŌĆö insertAdjacentHTML() Method (Modern Standard)** A W3C-standardized method introduced in HTML5 that allows insertion at **four precise positions** without disturbing surrounding content. ``` element.insertAdjacentHTML(position, htmlString); ``` The four valid positions are: | Position | Meaning | Visual | |---|---|---| | `"beforebegin"` | Before the target element itself | Ō¢▓ TARGET | | `"afterbegin"` | Inside the target, before its first child | Ō¢╝ TARGET | | `"beforeend"` | Inside the target, after its last child | TARGET Ō¢▓ | | `"afterend"` | After the target element itself | TARGET Ō¢╝ | > [!NOTE] > **`insertAdjacentHTML` vs `innerHTML`:** The key difference is that `insertAdjacentHTML` does **not** destroy existing children ŌĆö it only **adds** new content at a specified position. `innerHTML`, on the other hand, **replaces** everything. #### **B.5 ŌĆö jQuery Methods (If jQuery is in the syllabus)** If your module includes jQuery, the equivalent insertion methods are: | jQuery Method | DOM Equivalent | Action | |---|---|---| | `.append()` | `appendChild()` | Insert at end (inside) | | `.prepend()` | `insertBefore()` | Insert at start (inside) | | `.after()` | `insertAdjacentHTML("afterend")` | Insert after element | | `.before()` | `insertAdjacentHTML("beforebegin")` | Insert before element | | `.html()` | `innerHTML` | Replace inner content | --- ## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > The following table is **exam-critical**. Memorize this for 14-mark derivations. | # | Method / Property | Returns | Replaces Existing? | Position Control | Performance | KTU Frequency | |---|---|---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | `element.innerHTML = X` | String | Ō£ģ Yes (full) | No (full replacement) | ŌŁÉŌŁÉŌŁÉŌŁÉ Fast | ŌŁÉŌŁÉŌŁÉŌŁÉŌŁÉ | | 2 | `element.textContent = X` | String | Ō£ģ Yes (full) | No | ŌŁÉŌŁÉŌŁÉŌŁÉŌŁÉ Fastest | ŌŁÉŌŁÉŌŁÉŌŁÉ | | 3 | `element.innerText = X` | String | Ō£ģ Yes (full) | No (CSS-aware) | ŌŁÉŌŁÉŌŁÉ Slow | ŌŁÉŌŁÉ | | 4 | `document.write(X)` | undefined | ŌÜĀ’ĖÅ Page-wide | No | ŌŁÉŌŁÉ Medium | ŌŁÉŌŁÉ | | 5 | `parent.appendChild(node)` | Appended Node | ŌØī No (adds) | Last child | ŌŁÉŌŁÉŌŁÉŌŁÉ Fast | ŌŁÉŌŁÉŌŁÉŌŁÉŌŁÉ | | 6 | `parent.insertBefore(node, ref)` | Inserted Node | ŌØī No (adds) | Before reference | ŌŁÉŌŁÉŌŁÉŌŁÉ Fast | ŌŁÉŌŁÉŌŁÉŌŁÉ | | 7 | `el.insertAdjacentHTML(p, html)` | undefined | ŌØī No (adds) | 4 positions | ŌŁÉŌŁÉŌŁÉŌŁÉ Fast | ŌŁÉŌŁÉŌŁÉŌŁÉŌŁÉ | | 8 | `parent.replaceChild(new, old)` | Replaced Node | Ō£ģ Yes (single) | Specific child | ŌŁÉŌŁÉŌŁÉ Medium | ŌŁÉŌŁÉ | | 9 | `el.outerHTML = X` | String | Ō£ģ Yes (self+children) | No | ŌŁÉŌŁÉŌŁÉŌŁÉ Fast | ŌŁÉŌŁÉ | | 10 | `document.createElement(tag)` | New Element | N/A (creates) | N/A | ŌŁÉŌŁÉŌŁÉŌŁÉŌŁÉ Fastest | ŌŁÉŌŁÉŌŁÉŌŁÉŌŁÉ | --- ## 2.3 Why These Methods Exist ŌĆö The Engineering Rationale > [!IMPORTANT] > **The "Why" Behind Each Method (Critical for KTU Theory):** - **`innerHTML`** exists because most developers want to **bulk-insert HTML markup as a string** without writing verbose DOM API calls. - **`createElement` + `appendChild`** exists because strings are **error-prone** (typos in tags break the page silently), whereas typed DOM nodes are **syntactically validated**. - **`insertAdjacentHTML`** exists because `innerHTML` is **destructive** ŌĆö it wipes the entire parent ŌĆö which is wasteful when only one new node needs to be added. - **`document.write()`** exists as a **legacy shortcut** from the 1990s, useful only for simple in-line scripts in HTML. ### Real-World Engineering Utility: In **production engineering**, dynamic element insertion powers: - ­¤ō░ **Live news tickers** that append new headlines every 30 seconds - ­¤Æ¼ **Chat applications** (e.g., WhatsApp Web) that insert new message bubbles - ­¤øÆ **E-commerce carts** (e.g., Flipkart/Amazon) that dynamically add items to a list - ­¤ōŖ **Dashboards** that insert live data points into a graph - Ō£ģ **Form validation** that injects error messages below input fields # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Analytical Matrix ŌĆö The 14-Mark Derivative Framework > KTU board examiners expect a **structured, step-by-step** answer. The following matrix maps each step of the standard "Inserting Elements" answer to the marks it typically earns. | Step | Content of the Step | Marks Allocated | Bloom's Level (Revised Taxonomy) | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Formal definition of element insertion | 2 | Remember | | 2 | Classification diagram (Static vs Dynamic) | 2 | Understand | | 3 | Working of `innerHTML` with syntax | 2 | Apply | | 4 | Working of `createElement` + `appendChild` with 3-step protocol | 3 | Apply | | 5 | Working of `insertAdjacentHTML` with the 4-position matrix | 3 | Analyze | | 6 | Comparison table of all methods | 2 | Evaluate | --- ## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation ŌĆö Working of the `appendChild()` Method (Model 14-Mark Derivation) ### **Problem Statement (as per KTU ESE pattern):** *"With a suitable example, explain the process of dynamically inserting a new `
  • ` element into an existing `
      ` list using JavaScript DOM methods. List the steps involved and discuss the advantages over `innerHTML`."* ŌĆö **[14 Marks, KTU Model Question]** ### **Step 1 ŌĆö Identify the Target Element in the DOM Tree** Let the existing HTML structure be: ```html
      • HTML
      • CSS
      ``` The DOM tree representation of this structure is: $$ T_{ul} = \begin{cases} \text{node} = ul \ (\text{id="courseList"}) \\ \text{children} = \{ \ li_1, \ li_2 \} \end{cases} $$ where $li_1$ represents the `
    • HTML
      • ` node and $li_2$ represents the `
    • CSS
      • ` node. ### **Step 2 ŌĆö Locate the Parent Using `getElementById()`** The JavaScript engine first queries the DOM to obtain a reference to the parent `
        ` element. $$ P_{ul} = \text{document.getElementById}(\text{"courseList"}) $$ This returns an `HTMLElement` object that serves as the **insertion parent**. ### **Step 3 ŌĆö Create the New Element Node** Using the W3C-standard `document.createElement()` method: $$ N_{new} = \text{document.createElement}(\text{"li"}) $$ At this point, the new node $N_{new}$ exists in memory as a **detached node** ŌĆö it is **not yet part of the DOM tree**. > **State of the new node:** > $$ > \text{parentNode}(N_{new}) = \text{null} > $$ ### **Step 4 ŌĆö Customize the New Node with Content** Assign a text value to the new element using the `textContent` property (a **safe, XSS-immune** alternative to `innerHTML`): $$ \text{textContent}(N_{new}) = \text{"JavaScript"} $$ After this step, the internal representation of the node is: $$ N_{new} = \begin{cases} \text{tag} = li \\ \text{text} = "JavaScript" \\ \text{parent} = \text{null} \ (\text{still detached}) \end{cases} $$ ### **Step 5 ŌĆö Insert the Node into the DOM Tree (The Actual Insertion)** Use the `appendChild()` method on the parent element $P_{ul}$: $$ P_{ul}.\text{appendChild}(N_{new}) $$ The browser's rendering engine performs the following operations internally: $$ \text{children}(P_{ul}) \gets \text{children}(P_{ul}) \cup \{N_{new}\} $$ That is, the children list of $P_{ul}$ is updated to include the new node as the **last child**. The DOM tree becomes: $$ T_{ul}' = \begin{cases} \text{node} = ul \\ \text{children} = \{ \ li_1, \ li_2, \ li_3 \ \} \end{cases} $$ where $li_3 = N_{new}$ now contains the text "JavaScript". ### **Step 6 ŌĆö The Complete, Executable JavaScript Code** ```javascript // Step 2: Locate the parent var parent = document.getElementById("courseList"); // Step 3: Create the new element var newItem = document.createElement("li"); // Step 4: Customize the new element newItem.textContent = "JavaScript"; // Step 5: Insert into the DOM tree parent.appendChild(newItem); ``` ### **Step 7 ŌĆö Final Rendered Output (What the User Sees)** ```html
        • HTML
        • CSS
        • JavaScript
        ``` ### **Step 8 ŌĆö Why This Method is Superior to `innerHTML`** | Comparison Criterion | `appendChild()` | `innerHTML = ...` | |---|---|---| | Memory Efficiency | Reuses existing node tree | Parses the entire HTML string again | | Event Listeners | Preserved if node moved | Destroyed and recreated | | XSS Security | Ō£ģ Safe (with `textContent`) | ŌØī Vulnerable to script injection | | Performance for 1000+ nodes | ŌŁÉŌŁÉŌŁÉŌŁÉŌŁÉ Fast | ŌŁÉŌŁÉ Slow | | Reflow / Repaint Cost | Localized | Full subtree | | W3C Recommendation | Ō£ģ Strongly recommended | ŌÜĀ’ĖÅ Use with caution | > [!NOTE] > **Conversion Logic Summary (for KTU board valuation):** > The above derivation demonstrates that dynamic insertion via the **DOM API** is fundamentally a **tree-manipulation operation**, while `innerHTML` is a **string-parsing operation**. The tree-based approach is more efficient, more secure, and more maintainable ŌĆö which is why W3C and modern frameworks (React, Vue) internally use tree-based virtual DOM techniques. --- ## 3.3 Analytical Comparison ŌĆö `insertAdjacentHTML()` Positions For a target element $E$ with existing children $\{c_1, c_2, c_3\}$, the four insertion positions can be formally defined as: $$ \text{Pos}(\text{"beforebegin"}) = \text{insert}(N_{new}, \text{before}(E)) $$ $$ \text{Pos}(\text{"afterbegin"}) = \text{insert}(N_{new}, \text{before}(c_1)) $$ $$ \text{Pos}(\text{"beforeend"}) = \text{insert}(N_{new}, \text{after}(c_n)) $$ $$ \text{Pos}(\text{"afterend"}) = \text{insert}(N_{new}, \text{after}(E)) $$ where $N_{new}$ is the new node and $c_n$ is the last child of $E$. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram ŌĆö Classification of Element Insertion Methods (Module-Level Overview) ```mermaid graph TD A["Inserting HTML Elements"]:::root A --> B["Static Design-Time Insertion"]:::cat A --> C["Dynamic Run-Time Insertion"]:::cat B --> B1["Direct HTML in source file"]:::leaf B --> B2["Server-side templating"]:::leaf C --> D1["innerHTML Property"]:::leaf C --> D2["document.write Method"]:::leaf C --> D3["DOM API Methods"]:::sub C --> D4["insertAdjacentHTML Method"]:::leaf C --> D5["jQuery Methods"]:::sub D3 --> D3a["createElement + appendChild"]:::leaf D3 --> D3b["createElement + insertBefore"]:::leaf D3 --> D3c["createTextNode"]:::leaf D3 --> D3d["cloneNode"]:::leaf D5 --> D5a["append() prepend()"]:::leaf D5 --> D5b["after() before()"]:::leaf D5 --> D5c["html() text()"]:::leaf classDef root fill:#1f2937,stroke:#fbbf24,stroke-width:3px,color:#ffffff classDef cat fill:#0ea5e9,stroke:#0369a1,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff classDef sub fill:#6366f1,stroke:#312e81,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff classDef leaf fill:#10b981,stroke:#047857,stroke-width:1px,color:#ffffff ``` > **How to read this diagram (for KTU exam):** Start at the top node (`Inserting HTML Elements`), then traverse downward to show the **hierarchical classification**. Always label your diagram as **"Figure 1: Classification of Element Insertion Methods"** in the exam script. --- ## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram ŌĆö `insertAdjacentHTML` Four-Position Reference ```mermaid graph LR subgraph P1["Position beforebegin"] NEW1["new element"]:::new TGT1["TARGET ELEMENT"]:::tgt NEW1 --> TGT1 end subgraph P2["Position afterbegin"] TGT2["TARGET ELEMENT"]:::tgt C1A["existing child 1"]:::child NEW2["new element"]:::new C2A["existing child 2"]:::child TGT2 --> C1A TGT2 --> NEW2 TGT2 --> C2A end subgraph P3["Position beforeend"] TGT3["TARGET ELEMENT"]:::tgt C1B["existing child 1"]:::child C2B["existing child 2"]:::child NEW3["new element"]:::new TGT3 --> C1B TGT3 --> C2B TGT3 --> NEW3 end subgraph P4["Position afterend"] TGT4["TARGET ELEMENT"]:::tgt NEW4["new element"]:::new TGT4 --> NEW4 end classDef tgt fill:#fbbf24,stroke:#92400e,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000 classDef new fill:#22c55e,stroke:#14532d,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff classDef child fill:#60a5fa,stroke:#1e3a8a,stroke-width:1px,color:#ffffff ``` > **Exam Tip:** If you are not confident drawing this in the exam, draw a simple **rectangle labelled "Target Element"** and use arrows to show the four insertion points. This is a guaranteed 2-mark visual aid. --- ## 4.3 Mermaid Flowchart ŌĆö The 3-Step `appendChild` Insertion Protocol ```mermaid flowchart TD S1["STEP 1: CREATE the element using document.createElement tag"]:::step1 S2["STEP 2: CUSTOMIZE the element by setting textContent or className"]:::step2 S3["STEP 3: INSERT the element using parent.appendChild or insertBefore"]:::step3 S4["Element is now part of the live DOM tree and rendered on the page"]:::done S1 --> S2 S2 --> S3 S3 --> S4 classDef step1 fill:#3b82f6,stroke:#1e3a8a,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff classDef step2 fill:#8b5cf6,stroke:#4c1d95,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff classDef step3 fill:#ec4899,stroke:#831843,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff classDef done fill:#10b981,stroke:#064e3b,stroke-width:3px,color:#ffffff ``` > **Figure 3: The 3-Step Protocol for Dynamic Element Insertion via DOM API** --- ## 4.4 Mermaid Sequence Diagram ŌĆö Lifecycle of a Dynamically Inserted Element ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant U as User participant B as Browser Engine participant D as DOM Tree participant R as Render Engine U->>B: Loads index.html B->>D: Parse HTML and build initial DOM tree D-->>R: Render initial page U->>B: Triggers event (e.g. click on Add button) B->>B: Execute JavaScript: document.createElement li B->>B: Set textContent of new element B->>D: appendChild inserts new node into tree D->>R: Notify tree mutation R->>U: Re-render and display updated page ``` > **Figure 4: Sequence of Events When a New Element is Dynamically Inserted** ŌĆö *Highly useful for 14-mark KTU answers on JavaScript-driven DOM manipulation.* # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Question 1: Define the term "Inserting Elements" in the context of HTML and the DOM.** [3 Marks] **[KTU University Exam - May 2024]** **Model Answer:** > *In the context of HTML and the Document Object Model (DOM), **Inserting Elements** refers to the process of adding new HTML elements (such as `

        `, `

        `, `
      • `, etc.) into an existing web page. This can be done **statically** by writing the HTML tags directly in the source code, or **dynamically** at run-time using JavaScript and DOM methods such as `createElement()`, `appendChild()`, `insertBefore()`, or `innerHTML`.* > > *The insertion operation essentially adds a new **node** to the **DOM tree structure** at a specified position relative to its **parent node**.* --- ### **Question 2: List any four JavaScript methods used to insert elements dynamically into an HTML document.** [3 Marks] **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** **Model Answer:** The four commonly used JavaScript methods for dynamic element insertion are: 1. **`document.createElement(tagName)`** ŌĆö Creates a new HTML element node. 2. **`parentNode.appendChild(childNode)`** ŌĆö Inserts a node as the last child of the parent. 3. **`parentNode.insertBefore(newNode, referenceNode)`** ŌĆö Inserts a node before an existing reference node. 4. **`element.insertAdjacentHTML(position, htmlString)`** ŌĆö Inserts an HTML string at one of four specified positions (`beforebegin`, `afterbegin`, `beforeend`, `afterend`). --- ## 5.2 Part B ŌĆö Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) ŌĆö KTU ESE Internal Choice Pattern > **KTU Rule:** In the End Semester Examination (ESE), Module 3 questions appear with an **internal choice** ŌĆö you must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full. Both questions are designed to carry **14 marks each**, typically split as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**. --- ### Ō£ģ **Question A (14 Marks) ŌĆö Option 1** [KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024] **Q.A (a)** Explain the process of dynamically inserting a new element into an HTML page using the `createElement()` and `appendChild()` methods. Illustrate with a suitable example. **[7 Marks]** **Q.A (b)** Compare `innerHTML`, `document.write()`, and `appendChild()` methods of element insertion. State two advantages of `appendChild()` over `innerHTML`. **[7 Marks]** --- #### ­¤ōØ Model Solution for Q.A (a): **Step 1 ŌĆö Concept Introduction (2 marks):** Dynamic insertion refers to adding HTML elements to a web page **at run-time** (after the page has been loaded) using JavaScript. The W3C-recommended approach is the **3-step protocol**: - **Create** ŌåÆ **Customize** ŌåÆ **Insert** **Step 2 ŌĆö The 3-Step Protocol Explained (3 marks):** | Step | Method | Purpose | |---|---|---| | Create | `document.createElement("tagName")` | Allocates memory for a new element node | | Customize | `node.textContent = "value"` or `node.setAttribute("id", "x")` | Adds content and attributes to the node | | Insert | `parent.appendChild(node)` | Attaches the new node to the live DOM tree | **Step 3 ŌĆö Worked Example (2 marks):** ```html
        • Apple
        • Mango
        ``` **Result:** Clicking the button adds "Orange" as a new list item to the `
          `. --- #### ­¤ōØ Model Solution for Q.A (b): **Comparison Table (5 marks):** | Feature | `innerHTML` | `document.write()` | `appendChild()` | |---|---|---|---| | Syntax Type | Property | Method | Method | | Replaces existing content | Ō£ģ Yes | Ō£ģ Yes (entire page after load) | ŌØī No, only adds | | Security (XSS Safe) | ŌØī No | ŌØī No | Ō£ģ Yes (with `textContent`) | | Tree-based | ŌØī No (string parsing) | ŌØī No | Ō£ģ Yes | | W3C Recommended | ŌÜĀ’ĖÅ Cautious | ŌØī Discouraged | Ō£ģ Strongly recommended | | Use case | Bulk content replacement | Legacy in-line scripts | Structured dynamic insertion | **Two Advantages of `appendChild()` over `innerHTML` (2 marks):** 1. **Security:** `appendChild()` with `textContent` is **immune to XSS attacks**, since user input is never parsed as HTML markup. `innerHTML` blindly parses any string, allowing malicious `

topics.title: Getting and Setting Attributes
topics.content: > **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEITT833 - WEB DESIGNING > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Getting and Setting Attributes # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition In **jQuery DOM (Document Object Model) manipulation**, *"Getting and Setting Attributes"* refers to the process of **retrieving (reading)** the current values of HTML element attributes/properties and **modifying (writing)** new values to those attributes/properties using a curated set of jQuery accessorŌĆōmutator methods. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** > *"Attributes are the values declared inside the opening tag of an HTML element (e.g., `id="demo"`, `href`, `src`, `alt`, `class`). jQuery provides dual-purpose methods that either **GET** the value (when called with one argument representing the attribute name) or **SET** the value (when called with two arguments ŌĆö attribute name and new value)."* The major methods under this category are: | jQuery Method | Purpose | |---|---| | `.attr()` | Get/Set **HTML attributes** (initial markup) | | `.prop()` | Get/Set **DOM properties** (live runtime state) | | `.val()` | Get/Set value of form elements | | `.text()` | Get/Set the combined text content | | `.html()` | Get/Set the inner HTML markup | | `.css()` | Get/Set CSS style properties | | `.data()` | Get/Set arbitrary data attached to elements | | `.addClass() / .removeClass() / .toggleClass()` | Manage class attribute | ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **­¤ÅĀ Real-World Analogy: The House Address Tag** > > Imagine every HTML element is a **house** in a street. > - The **address plate** stuck on the gate is the **attribute** (e.g., `id="main-gate"`, `class="red-house"`, `src="door.jpg"`). > - **GETTING** the attribute is like **reading** the address plate ŌĆö *"What is the name written on this house?"* > - **SETTING** the attribute is like **changing** the address plate ŌĆö *"Re-write this house's name to 'NewHome'."* > > - The **light switch** inside the house represents the **property** (`.prop()`) ŌĆö it tells whether the light is currently `ON (true)` or `OFF (false)`, regardless of the wiring plan. > - `.val()` is like reading what is currently written on a **notepad** inside the house. > - `.text()` is the **speech** coming out of the house; `.html()` is the **entire interior design** including furniture tags. ## 1.3 Why This Topic is Critical for KTU **Attributes drive 90% of dynamic behaviour** in modern web pages ŌĆö image swapping, form validation feedback, button enabling/disabling, link redirection, and style toggling all depend on Get/Set operations. As per **KTU 2024 Scheme outcomes (CO3: Implement client-side scripting using jQuery)**, a student is expected to *"manipulate DOM attributes and content dynamically to produce responsive user interfaces."* > [!IMPORTANT] > **The Golden Rule of jQuery Get/Set:** > - **1 argument** ŌåÆ method **GETS** the value > - **2 arguments** ŌåÆ method **SETS** the value > - **Object `{key:value}`** ŌåÆ method **SETS multiple values** at once > - **Callback function** ŌåÆ method processes each matched element individually # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet ## 2.1 Operational Logic ŌĆö Step-by-Step Breakdown ### ­¤ö╣ Step 1: Element Selection First jQuery always operates on a **wrapped set** of matched elements. Get/Set methods are invoked on this set: `$('selector').method()` ### ­¤ö╣ Step 2: Determine Intent (Get or Set) The **number of arguments** passed to the method determines the mode: $$ \text{Mode} = \begin{cases} \text{GET}, & \text{if } n_{\text{args}} = 1 \text{ and arg}_1 \text{ is a string} \\ \text{SET (single)}, & \text{if } n_{\text{args}} = 2 \\ \text{SET (bulk)}, & \text{if } n_{\text{args}} = 1 \text{ and arg}_1 \text{ is an Object} \\ \text{SET (callback)}, & \text{if } n_{\text{args}} = 2 \text{ and arg}_2 \text{ is a Function} \end{cases} $$ ### ­¤ö╣ Step 3: Apply to Matched Set - **GET mode** ŌåÆ returns the value of the **first matched element only**. - **SET mode** ŌåÆ applies the new value to **every element** in the matched set. ## 2.2 KTU Formula / Syntax Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > The table below uses `\vert` to avoid breaking markdown table syntax. No literal `|` symbol appears in any cell. | Method | Get Syntax | Set Syntax | Returns (GET) | Returns (SET) | Key Use Case | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | `.attr()` | `$el.attr(name)` | `$el.attr(name, value)` | String of first element's attribute | jQuery wrapped set | `href`, `src`, `id`, `alt`, `title` | | `.attr()` (bulk) | ŌĆö | `$el.attr({a:1,b:2})` | ŌĆö | jQuery wrapped set | Setting multiple attributes at once | | `.prop()` | `$el.prop(name)` | `$el.prop(name, value)` | Boolean \vert String \vert Number | jQuery wrapped set | `checked`, `disabled`, `selected`, `readonly` | | `.val()` | `$el.val()` | `$el.val(value)` | Input value \vert array for multi-select | jQuery wrapped set | ``, `

topics.title: Getting and Setting CSS Properties
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEITT833 - WEB DESIGNING > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Getting and Setting CSS Properties # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!NOTE] > **Definition (KTU 2024 Standard):** "Getting and Setting CSS Properties refers to the programmatic manipulation of the visual presentation of HTML elements using JavaScript (or jQuery). It involves two fundamental operations: **GETTING** (reading the computed or inline style value of a property from an element) and **SETTING** (assigning a new style value to one or more CSS properties of an element), thereby enabling dynamic, runtime modification of the Document Object Model (DOM)." In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme ŌĆō OEITT833 Web Designing syllabus (Module 3)**, this topic typically involves mastering the **jQuery `.css()` method**, along with the underlying **vanilla JavaScript** techniques (the `style` property, `getComputedStyle()`, and `classList` API). It is a foundational skill in **client-side scripting**, forming the bridge between static HTML structure and dynamic, interactive user experiences. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Real-World Analogy ŌĆö "The Stage Lighting Control Desk"** ­¤ÄŁ Imagine a **theatre stage**. The actors (HTML elements) are on stage, and their costumes (their default appearance) are already chosen. Now, sitting in the control room is the **Lighting Director (JavaScript)**. The director has two jobs: 1. **GETTING the property** ŌĆö Reading the current spotlight color currently shining on a specific actor. The director asks: *"What color is the spotlight on actor #3 right now?"* The answer comes back: *"Crimson Red."* This is reading the computed style. 2. **SETTING the property** ŌĆö Changing the spotlight. The director commands: *"Change actor #3's spotlight to Royal Blue, and reduce its brightness to 70%."* The stage instantly updates. This is writing/modifying the style. Just as the lighting director can change the mood of a scene without the actors moving, **JavaScript can change the visual presentation of a webpage without reloading the page**. This is the very essence of Dynamic HTML (DHTML). ## 1.3 The Two Primal Operations | Operation | Purpose | Common Use Case | |---|---|---| | **GET** | Read the current value of a CSS property | Conditional logic (e.g., "If background is red, change it to blue") | | **SET** | Assign a new value to a CSS property | Animations, hover effects, responsive design toggles | ## 1.4 Categories of CSS Property Access - **Inline Style Access** ŌĆö Reads/writes the `style="..."` attribute directly on the element. - **Computed Style Access** ŌĆö Reads the *final* applied value after the browser's cascade engine resolves all stylesheets. - **Class-Based Access (toggleClass)** ŌĆö Adds/removes predefined CSS classes from stylesheets (best practice for performance). > [!TIP] > **KTU Examiner's Insight:** When asked about getting/setting CSS properties, always mention *all three* approaches (inline `style`, `getComputedStyle`, and `classList`) for full marks. Mentioning only one is considered an incomplete answer. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Operational Mechanics ŌĆö The "How" and "Why" ### 2.1.1 Vanilla JavaScript: The `element.style` Property Every DOM element in JavaScript inherits a `style` object from the `CSSStyleDeclaration` interface. This object represents the element's **inline `style` attribute only** ŌĆö it does *not* read rules from external stylesheets. **Why does this matter?** Because an element may be styled entirely via a CSS file (``) or internal `
Sample Box
``` **Explanation of Key Lines (3 marks):** - `box.style.backgroundColor` uses **camelCase** conversion of CSS's `background-color`. - The value `"lightblue"` is a **string** (numbers like `15` need units like `"15px"`). - jQuery's object form automatically applies the `px` unit to numeric values. - The jQuery method returns the jQuery object, enabling **method chaining**. --- ### Question B (14 Marks): `[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Module 3 Internal Choice B]` > **Q (a)** What is the `classList` API in JavaScript? Explain all its methods with examples. Why is it considered best practice over the legacy `className` property? **(7 marks)** > > **Q (b)** Demonstrate how to **read** a CSS property using both `getComputedStyle()` and jQuery's `.css()`. Write a program that reads the current width of a `
` and conditionally applies a "wide" class if the width exceeds 500 pixels. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution for (a): **Definition (1.5 marks):** The `classList` property is a read-only `DOMTokenList` that returns a live collection of the class attributes of an element. It provides methods to add, remove, toggle, and check classes without manually parsing the className string. **The Four Methods (4 marks):** 1. **`add(className)`** ŌĆö Adds one or more classes. ```javascript el.classList.add("highlight", "bold"); ``` 2. **`remove(className)`** ŌĆö Removes one or more classes. ```javascript el.classList.remove("highlight"); ``` 3. **`toggle(className)`** ŌĆö Adds the class if absent, removes it if present. Optionally takes a force boolean. ```javascript el.classList.toggle("dark-mode"); // Force version: el.classList.toggle("dark-mode", isDark); ``` 4. **`contains(className)`** ŌĆö Returns a Boolean indicating whether the class is present. ```javascript if (el.classList.contains("active")) { /* ... */ } ``` **Why classList > className (1.5 marks):** | Aspect | `className` (Legacy) | `classList` (Modern) | |---|---|---| | Return type | A single space-separated string | A `DOMTokenList` object | | Whitespace handling | Manual splitting and joining required | Handled automatically | | Multi-class safety | Risky (e.g., `el.className += " active"` may duplicate) | Safe (idempotent operations) | | Method support | None (string operations only) | Native `add`/`remove`/`toggle`/`contains` | | Performance | Slower (string parsing) | Faster (token-based) | #### Model Solution for (b): **Program (5 marks) + Explanation (2 marks):** ```html
Width-sensitive Box
``` **Key Explanations:** - `getComputedStyle(box).width` returns the value **with units** as a string (`"400px"`). - `parseInt(widthString, 10)` converts the string to a number for comparison. - The condition `> 500` triggers a class toggle, demonstrating **conditional styling** ŌĆö a common KTU question pattern. - jQuery's `$("#myBox").css("width")` returns the *same* computed value, but with simpler syntax. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning > [!WARNING] > **Common Pitfalls ŌĆö Where Students Lose Marks** > > 1. **Forgetting to convert CSS kebab-case to JavaScript camelCase.** Writing `box.style.background-color` is a **SyntaxError**. Use `box.style.backgroundColor` or `box.style["background-color"]`. (-1 mark) > > 2. **Forgetting units on numeric values.** `box.style.fontSize = 16` silently fails in most browsers. It must be `box.style.fontSize = "16px"`. (-1 mark) > > 3. **Expecting `element.style` to read stylesheet values.** If a `
` is colored via an external CSS file, `div.style.color` returns `""`. Students incorrectly think JavaScript "isn't working". Use `getComputedStyle()` instead. (-1 mark) > > 4. **Confusing jQuery getter return value.** `$el.css("color")` returns a **string**, not a jQuery object, so you **cannot chain** further `.css()` calls on the returned value. (-1 mark) > > 5. **Modifying `getComputedStyle` result.** It is **read-only**; attempting to set a property on it throws an error in strict mode. (-1 mark) > > 6. **Using `el.style.--myVar` for CSS custom properties.** Custom properties require `el.style.setProperty('--myVar', value)`. (-1 mark) > > 7. **Forgetting to include jQuery library** in the HTML `` before writing jQuery code. (-1 mark) > > **Total potential mark loss: up to -7 marks per question if all pitfalls are hit!** --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!TIP] > **High-Density Revision Checklist ŌĆö Read This Before Every KTU Exam** - Ō£ģ **Two main operations exist:** GET (read) and SET (write) of CSS properties. - Ō£ģ **Four canonical access methods:** 1. `element.style.prop` (vanilla, inline only) 2. `getComputedStyle(element)` (vanilla, all sources, read-only) 3. `element.classList` (vanilla, class-based) 4. `jQuery .css()` (jQuery, unified getter/setter) - Ō£ģ **Property name conversion:** CSS `kebab-case` Ōåö JavaScript `camelCase`. Quotes are required for kebab-case in JavaScript (`el.style["background-color"]`). - Ō£ģ **Numeric values need units:** Always pass strings like `"16px"`, `"2em"`. jQuery auto-appends `px` to bare numbers in setters. - Ō£ģ **`getComputedStyle()` is read-only** and triggers a possible reflow ŌĆö use sparingly in animation loops. - Ō£ģ **jQuery `.css()` returns a string for getters** (the computed value) and a jQuery object for setters (chainable). - Ō£ģ **The object form** of `jQuery .css()` allows setting multiple properties in a single call. - Ō£ģ **CSS custom properties** (`--var-name`) require the `setProperty()` method ŌĆö they cannot use dot notation. - Ō£ģ **`classList` is best practice** for production code: methods include `add()`, `remove()`, `toggle()`, `contains()`.
