It avoids the fragility of `className` string manipulation. - Ō£ģ **Inline styles have the highest specificity** (1000), beating all selectors except `!important`. - Ō£ģ **jQuery setter adds inline styles** under the hood ŌĆö the actual mutation is identical to vanilla `element.style.prop = val`. - Ō£ģ **Browser compatibility note for KTU:** `getComputedStyle` works in IE9+; `classList` works in IE10+; jQuery `.css()` works in all browsers (IE6+). - Ō£ģ **Exam mantra:** When asked "how to set a CSS property?", always answer: *"Use `element.style.prop = value` (vanilla) or `$el.css(prop, val)` (jQuery) ŌĆö both apply the property as an inline style."* - Ō£ģ **Performance ranking (fastest to slowest):** `classList` toggle ŌåÆ `element.style` direct ŌåÆ jQuery `.css()` ŌåÆ `getComputedStyle()` (forces reflow). - Ō£ģ **For KTU coding questions:** Always include `

topics.title: Using .each()
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEITT833 - WEB DESIGNING > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Using .each() # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition In **jQuery**, the **`.each()`** method is a generic iterator function used to traverse (iterate over) jQuery objects and execute a callback function for every matched DOM element. According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme OEITT833 syllabus (Module 3 ŌĆö jQuery Effects & Traversing)**, `.each()` falls under the *jQuery Traversing* family of methods, alongside `.parent()`, `.children()`, `.find()`, and `.siblings()`. > [!NOTE] > **Official Definition (jQuery API):** *"`each()` ŌĆö Iterate over a jQuery object, executing a function for each matched element."* Formally, the iteration signature is written as: $$ \forall \, e_i \in S_{jQuery} \;\; \Rightarrow \;\; \text{callback}(i, e_i) $$ where $S_{jQuery}$ is the matched jQuery set, $i$ is the zero-based integer index, and $e_i$ is the raw DOM element reference at that position. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Syllabus Highlight:** KTU expects students to know the difference between **`.each()` (jQuery iterator)** and **`$.each()` (Generic utility iterator)**, as well as the difference between **`.each()`** and the **native `for` loop**. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Plain English Intuition) Imagine a **classroom with 60 students**, and the teacher wants to mark attendance one by one. The teacher walks down each row, taps each student on the shoulder, and says *"Say your name."* Each student obeys the same instruction, but each in their **own turn**. - The **classroom** = the jQuery matched set (e.g., all `
  • ` items). - The **teacher's instruction** = the callback function. - **Each student** = one DOM element. - The **index of the student in the row** = the `index` argument. - **Returning `false`** = a student saying *"Stop, I don't want to continue!"* (breaks the loop). ## 1.3 Context within KTU Module 3 Module 3 of OEITT833 (Web Designing) typically covers: | Sub-Topic | KTU Weightage | Tools Used | |---|---|---| | jQuery Syntax & Selectors | High | `$()`, CSS selectors | | jQuery Events | High | `.click()`, `.on()` | | **jQuery Effects** | **Medium** | `.fadeIn()`, `.slideUp()` | | **jQuery Traversing (incl. `.each()`)** | **High** | **`.each()`, `.find()`, `.parent()`** | | jQuery AJAX (intro) | Medium | `$.ajax()`, `$.get()` | The `.each()` method is the **Swiss Army knife** of jQuery iteration ŌĆö it lets a single block of code operate on **multiple elements simultaneously** without writing repetitive statements. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Anatomy of the `.each()` Method The `.each()` method has **two distinct forms** in jQuery: ### Form A ŌĆö Instance Method (Most Common) ```javascript $(selector).each(function(index, element) { // 'this' refers to the current DOM element // 'index' is the integer position (0, 1, 2, ...) // 'element' is the raw DOM node }); ``` ### Form B ŌĆö Utility Function ```javascript $.each(object, function(index, value) { // Iterates over arrays AND plain objects }); ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Trap:** Many students write `$('.each')(function...)` ŌĆö this is **wrong**. The `$('.each')` is a selector looking for elements with class `each`. The correct syntax is `$(selector).each(callback)`. ## 2.2 Operational Logic ŌĆö Step-by-Step The jQuery engine executes `.each()` in the following structured way: 1. **Selector Resolution:** jQuery first resolves the selector `$('p')` into a wrapped set $S = \{e_1, e_2, \ldots, e_n\}$. 2. **Callback Binding:** The function supplied to `.each()` is stored as the iterator handler. 3. **Sequential Traversal:** A `for`-loop internally visits $e_1, e_2, \ldots, e_n$ in DOM order. 4. **Context Provision:** For each iteration, `this` is bound to $e_i$, and parameters `(i, e_i)` are passed. 5. **Return Value Check:** - Returning `false` ŌåÆ **stops** iteration early (`break`). - Returning `true` ŌåÆ **continues** to the next element (`continue`). - Returning nothing / `undefined` ŌåÆ **continues** normally. 6. **Completion:** Once all elements are visited, `.each()` returns the original jQuery object, enabling **method chaining**. ## 2.3 KTU Formula / Syntax Cheat Sheet | # | Syntax Form | Returns | Breaks On | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | `$(sel).each(cb)` | jQuery object (chainable) | `return false` | | 2 | `$.each(arr, cb)` | The iterated object | `return false` | | 3 | `$.each(obj, cb)` | The iterated object | `return false` | | 4 | `$(sel).each((i, el) => {...})` | jQuery object (ES6 arrow) | `return false` | > [!NOTE] > **Chainability formula:** Because `.each()` returns the original wrapped set, you can write: > `$('p').each(...).css('color','red').fadeOut(500);` > This single line iterates, modifies CSS, then animates ŌĆö all on the same set. ## 2.4 Real-World Web Engineering Utility - **Form validation:** Iterating through every `` to check `value.length > 0`. - **Dynamic lists:** Highlighting every third `
  • ` in a navigation menu. - **AJAX rendering:** Drawing a `forEach`-style loop over a JSON array of products to build HTML cards. - **Animation sequencing:** Staggering fade-ins across a grid of image tiles. ## 2.5 `.each()` vs. Native `for` Loop ŌĆö The Comparison Table | Feature | `$(sel).each()` | Native `for` / `for...of` | |---|---|---| | Iterates over | jQuery wrapped set | Arrays, NodeLists, objects | | `this` context | Current DOM element | Not automatic | | Supports `return false` | Yes (built-in break) | Must use `break` keyword | | Chainable | Yes | No | | Performance on huge sets | Slower (function call overhead) | Faster | | Required for KTU exam? | **Yes** | Recommended for comparison | # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix > [!IMPORTANT] > **Exhaustive Walkthrough Mandate:** Below, every line of code is dissected line-by-line, mapping the underlying logic, the jQuery engine action, and the visual DOM outcome. --- ## 3.1 Worked Example 1 ŌĆö Numbering List Items **HTML Source:** ```html
    • Home
    • About
    • Contact
    ``` **jQuery Code:** ```javascript $('#menu li').each(function(index, element) { $(element).prepend((index + 1) + '. '); }); ``` ### Line-by-Line Logical Deduction **Line 1:** `$('#menu li').each(...)` The selector engine resolves `#menu li` into the set $S = \{ \text{li}_1, \text{li}_2, \text{li}_3 \}$. The wrapped jQuery object now points to three `
  • ` nodes. **Line 2:** `function(index, element)` This is the callback signature. jQuery will pass: - `index = 0` (for the first iteration) - `element` = the raw DOM node of `
  • Home
    • ` **Line 3:** `$(element).prepend((index + 1) + '. ');` - `(index + 1)` produces `1` on the first iteration. - The string `'. '` is concatenated. - `$(element)` re-wraps the raw DOM node into a jQuery object. - `.prepend()` inserts `"1. "` as the **first child** of the `
  • `. ### Mathematical Iteration Trace $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Iteration 1:} \quad & i = 0, \; e_1 = \text{li}_1 \;\Rightarrow\; \text{prepend("1. ")} \\ \text{Iteration 2:} \quad & i = 1, \; e_2 = \text{li}_2 \;\Rightarrow\; \text{prepend("2. ")} \\ \text{Iteration 3:} \quad & i = 2, \; e_3 = \text{li}_3 \;\Rightarrow\; \text{prepend("3. ")} \\ \end{aligned} $$ **Resulting HTML after execution:** $$ \text{
  • 1. Home
    • , \quad
  • 2. About
    • , \quad
  • 3. Contact
    • } $$ --- ## 3.2 Worked Example 2 ŌĆö Highlighting Every Even Item ```javascript $('table tr').each(function(index) { if (index % 2 === 0) { $(this).css('background-color', '#f0f0f0'); } }); ``` ### Logical Breakdown The conditional check uses the modulo operator: $$ \text{even}(i) \;\Longleftrightarrow\; (i \bmod 2) = 0 $$ Inside the loop, `this` refers to the current DOM `` element. Wrapping it in `$()` re-engages jQuery methods like `.css()`. --- ## 3.3 Worked Example 3 ŌĆö Early Termination with `return false` ```javascript $('.product').each(function(index) { if ($(this).hasClass('out-of-stock')) { alert('Stopping at index ' + index); return false; // breaks the loop } $(this).addClass('processed'); }); ``` ### Mathematical Interpretation of Early Exit Let $k$ be the first index where `out-of-stock` exists. The iteration collapses to: $$ \{ e_0, e_1, \ldots, e_{k-1}, e_k \} $$ Elements beyond $e_k$ are **never visited** ŌĆö equivalent to a `break` statement in C/Java. --- ## 3.4 Worked Example 4 ŌĆö `$.each()` Over a Plain Array ```javascript var colors = ['red', 'green', 'blue']; $.each(colors, function(index, value) { console.log(index + ' => ' + value); }); ``` ### Output Trace $$ \begin{aligned} & 0 \;\Rightarrow\; \text{red} \\ & 1 \;\Rightarrow\; \text{green} \\ & 2 \;\Rightarrow\; \text{blue} \\ \end{aligned} $$ --- ## 3.5 Worked Example 5 ŌĆö Iterating Over a JSON Object ```javascript var student = { name: 'Anu', age: 20, course: 'CSE' }; $.each(student, function(key, value) { console.log(key + ': ' + value); }); ``` ### Output Trace $$ \begin{aligned} & \text{name : Anu} \\ & \text{age : 20} \\ & \text{course : CSE} \\ \end{aligned} $$ > [!NOTE] > When iterating an **object**, the first argument becomes the **property name (key)**, not a numeric index. --- ## 3.6 Analytical Matrix ŌĆö Common `.each()` Patterns | # | Pattern | Code Skeleton | Purpose | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Indexing | `(index + 1)` | Numbering items | | 2 | Zebra striping | `if (i % 2 === 0)` | Alternating row colors | | 3 | Early break | `return false;` | Stop on condition | | 4 | Skip iteration | `return true;` | Equivalent to `continue` | | 5 | Read values | `$(this).val()` | Extract form data | | 6 | Modify attribute | `$(this).attr('id', newId)` | Dynamic IDs | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics > [!IMPORTANT] > All diagrams below use **alphanumeric-only** node identifiers and **clean uppercase labels** to comply with Mermaid v10+ rendering safeguards. ## 4.1 Flowchart ŌĆö Internal Execution of `.each()` ```mermaid flowchart TD A[START Selector Resolution] --> B[Resolve S equals open curly bracket e1 comma e2 comma dot dot dot comma en close curly bracket] B --> C{Set not empty question mark} C -- NO --> Z[END return empty wrapped set] C -- YES --> D[Initialize i equals 0] D --> E[Bind this equals element ei] E --> F[Execute callback i comma ei] F --> G{Return value check} G -- return false --> Z G -- return true or undefined --> H[Increment i equals i plus 1] H --> I{i less than n question mark} I -- YES --> E I -- NO --> J[Return original jQuery set] J --> K[END Enables method chaining] ``` ## 4.2 Block Diagram ŌĆö Two Forms of `each()` ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph FORM_A["FORM A INSTANCE METHOD"] A1[Selector Dollar sign open paren sel close paren] --> A2[.each open paren callback close paren] A2 --> A3[Iterates jQuery wrapped set] A3 --> A4[Use case DOM manipulation] end subgraph FORM_B["FORM B UTILITY FUNCTION"] B1[Dollar sign dot each open paren obj comma cb close paren] --> B2[Iterates arrays or objects] B2 --> B3[Use case Generic data traversal] end FORM_A --> RESULT[Result type is jQuery object chainable] FORM_B --> RESULT2[Result type is iterated object] ``` ## 4.3 Sequence Diagram ŌĆö AJAX + `.each()` Pattern (KTU Favourite) ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant U as User Browser participant J as jQuery Engine participant S as Server API participant D as DOM U->>J: Click button triggers AJAX J->>S: GET slash products S-->>J: Returns JSON array of 5 products loop For each product i equals 0 to 4 J->>J: $.each open paren products comma cb close paren J->>D: append li with product name end J-->>U: Rendered list visible on screen ``` ## 4.4 State Diagram ŌĆö Return Value Behaviour Inside `.each()` ```mermaid stateDiagram-v2 [*] --> Visiting Visiting --> ContinueNext: return undefined OR return true Visiting --> StopLoop: return false Visiting --> Visiting: Implicit next element ContinueNext --> Visiting StopLoop --> [*] ``` ## 4.5 Comparison Block ŌĆö `.each()` vs `$.each()` ```mermaid flowchart TB Q{What are you iterating question mark} -->|DOM elements| R1[Use Dollar sign open paren sel close paren .each open paren cb close paren] Q -->|Array or plain object| R2[Use Dollar sign dot each open paren obj comma cb close paren] R1 --> S1[First arg is numeric index] R1 --> S2[this refers to DOM element] R2 --> S3[First arg is index OR key] R2 --> S4[this refers to value] ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** > 1. **Confusing `$('.each')` with `$(...).each()`** ŌĆö a single misplaced dot costs you **2 marks** instantly. > 2. **Forgetting that `.each()` is chainable** ŌĆö KTU loves asking *"What does `.each()` return?"*. Answer: the original jQuery object. > 3. **Using `return false` vs `return true`** ŌĆö many students think `return false` is the natural "continue" (it's the opposite ŌĆö it **breaks**). > 4. **Forgetting to re-wrap DOM in `$()`** ŌĆö `element.text()` fails because raw DOM nodes don't have jQuery methods. --- ## ­¤ōś PART A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. **[KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2024]** **Define the jQuery `.each()` method. State its two forms with one-line examples.** **Model Answer:** The `.each()` method is a jQuery iterator that loops over a set of matched elements (or any collection) and executes a callback function once per element. It has two forms: - **Instance form:** `$('p').each(function(i, el){ ... })` ŌĆö used on jQuery wrapped DOM sets. - **Utility form:** `$.each(arr, function(i, val){ ... })` ŌĆö used on arrays and plain JavaScript objects. *(3 marks ŌĆö 1 for definition, 2 for the two forms)* --- ### Q2. **[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023]** **Explain the role of `return false` inside a `.each()` callback. What is the equivalent in a native `for` loop?** **Model Answer:** `return false` inside a `.each()` callback **terminates the iteration early** ŌĆö no further elements are visited. It is **functionally equivalent** to the `break` statement in a native `for` or `while` loop in C/Java/JavaScript. *Example:* When searching for a specific product in a list, you can `return false` once found to skip unnecessary iterations. *(3 marks ŌĆö 1 for explanation, 1 for equivalence, 1 for example)* --- ## ­¤ōŚ PART B ŌĆö Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each ŌĆö Internal Choice) ### Ō£ģ Question A (Choice 1) ŌĆö 14 Marks > **[KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2024, Module 3]** > **(a)** Explain the jQuery `.each()` method in detail. Discuss its syntax, parameters, return value, and chainability. **(7 marks)** > **(b)** Write a jQuery program that uses `.each()` to dynamically add a "Delete" button to every paragraph in a `
    `. When clicked, the corresponding paragraph should be removed. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution ŌĆö Part (a) The jQuery `.each()` method is used to iterate over a jQuery wrapped set, executing a callback for each matched element. Its syntax is: ```javascript $(selector).each(function(index, element) { // code block }); ``` **Parameters:** - `index` ŌĆö zero-based integer counter (0, 1, 2, ŌĆ”). - `element` ŌĆö the raw DOM element at that position. **Special `this` keyword:** Inside the callback, `this` is automatically bound to the current DOM element ŌĆö equivalent to `element`. This allows direct access without re-wrapping. **Return Value:** `.each()` returns the **original jQuery object**, which means the method is **chainable**. Example: ```javascript $('li').each(function(){ /* logic */ }).fadeOut(500); ``` **Breaking the loop:** Returning `false` exits the loop; returning `true` (or nothing) continues to the next element. **Utility form `$.each()`:** Used for iterating arrays and plain objects ŌĆö first arg becomes the key/index, second becomes the value. *(7 marks: 2 for syntax, 2 for params & `this`, 2 for return/chainability, 1 for utility form)* #### Model Solution ŌĆö Part (b) **HTML Setup:** ```html

    Paragraph One

    Paragraph Two

    Paragraph Three

    ``` **jQuery Program:** ```javascript $(document).ready(function() { $('#content p').each(function() { var $para = $(this); var $btn = $(''); $btn.click(function() { $para.fadeOut(400, function() { $para.remove(); }); }); $para.append($btn); }); }); ``` **Explanation of Logic:** 1. `$('#content p').each(...)` wraps all `

    ` inside `#content` and iterates. 2. `var $para = $(this);` caches the jQuery-wrapped paragraph (avoids re-wrapping later). 3. `var $btn = $('');` creates a new `

topics.title: Events
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEITT833 - WEB DESIGNING > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Events # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned) > [!NOTE] > **Definition (JavaScript Event)** > An **event** in web designing is a specific action or occurrence detected by the browser, usually as a result of user interaction with the web page (such as clicking a button, pressing a key, moving the mouse, or resizing the window), or as a result of the system state changing (such as a page finishing loading). JavaScript provides a mechanism to **listen** for these events and execute a block of code (an **event handler** or **event listener**) in response. In the **Document Object Model (DOM)** model endorsed by the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for OEITT833, every element on a web page is a node capable of receiving **events**. The JavaScript engine registers these events in a queue and dispatches them to the appropriate **event handlers** defined by the programmer. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (OEITT833 ŌĆō Module 3)** > The events topic is the **bridge between static HTML/CSS and dynamic JavaScript behavior**. It is the foundation of client-side interactivity. Mastery of this topic is required for understanding DOM manipulation, form validation, AJAX, and modern framework paradigms (React, Vue). --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition Imagine a **doorbell in a house**: | Real-World Element | Web Designing Equivalent | |---|---| | The house | The **web page (DOM tree)** | | A visitor pressing the doorbell | A **user action (event)** ŌĆö e.g., a click | | The doorbell mechanism that detects the press | The **event listener** (e.g., `addEventListener`) | | The sound the bell makes | The **event handler** ŌĆö the JavaScript function that runs | | The homeowner deciding which ring tone plays | The **event object** ŌĆö carries details (which button? where? when?) | | The owner's policy of "only ring once" | **Event propagation** rules (bubbling/capturing) | So, in plain English: **Events are how the user "talks" to the web page, and JavaScript is how the page "listens" and "responds."** --- ## 1.3 Categories of Events (Official KTU Classification) Events are officially grouped into the following categories in the KTU 2024 scheme: 1. **Mouse Events** ŌĆö `click`, `dblclick`, `mouseover`, `mouseout`, `mousedown`, `mouseup`, `mousemove` 2. **Keyboard Events** ŌĆö `keydown`, `keyup`, `keypress` 3. **Form Events** ŌĆö `submit`, `change`, `focus`, `blur`, `input` 4. **Window / Document Events** ŌĆö `load`, `resize`, `scroll`, `unload` 5. **Touch Events** (mobile) ŌĆö `touchstart`, `touchend`, `touchmove` 6. **Clipboard Events** ŌĆö `copy`, `paste`, `cut` 7. **Drag Events** ŌĆö `drag`, `drop`, `dragstart`, `dragend` --- ## 1.4 The Event Object ŌĆö Key Properties > [!NOTE] > **Definition (Event Object)** > When an event is triggered, the browser automatically creates an **Event object** containing information about the event. This object is passed as the argument to the event handler function. Key properties (must be memorized for KTU exams): | Property | Meaning | Example | |---|---|---| | `type` | Name of the event | `"click"` | | `target` | Element that triggered the event | ` ``` **How it works:** - The HTML attribute `onclick` directly contains JavaScript code. - When the user clicks, the browser wraps the code into a function and executes it. - `this` inside the inline handler refers to the element. **Why use it?** Quick, simple, visible in markup. **Why avoid it?** Mixes HTML and JavaScript ŌĆö breaks separation of concerns. #### **Method 2: Traditional Property Method** ```html ``` **How it works:** - The DOM property `onclick` is assigned a function reference. - Only **one handler** can be assigned per event type using this method. - Reassigning **overwrites** the previous handler. #### **Method 3: `addEventListener()` (Modern Standard)** ```html ``` **How it works:** - The method takes three arguments: `eventType`, `listenerFunction`, `useCapture` (optional, default `false`). - **Multiple handlers** can be registered for the same event. - Handlers are removed using `removeEventListener()`. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Frequently Asked Point:** `addEventListener` supports multiple handlers per event, while the property method (`element.onclick = ...`) does not. --- ### 2.1.3 Event Propagation: Bubbling vs. Capturing When an event occurs on a nested element, the event travels through the DOM tree in **two phases**: 1. **Capturing Phase (Trickling Down)** ŌĆö Event travels from the outermost ancestor (`window` ŌåÆ `document` ŌåÆ `html` ŌåÆ `body` ŌåÆ ... ŌåÆ target's parent). 2. **Target Phase** ŌĆö Event reaches the target element. 3. **Bubbling Phase** ŌĆö Event bubbles back up from the target to the outermost ancestor. By default, `addEventListener` listens in the **bubbling phase** (`useCapture = false`). ```html
``` ```javascript document.getElementById("parent").addEventListener("click", () => { console.log("Parent clicked"); }, false); // Bubbling phase document.getElementById("child").addEventListener("click", () => { console.log("Child clicked"); }); ``` **Click on button output:** ``` Child clicked Parent clicked ``` > [!NOTE] > **Mnemonic for KTU exams:** *"Trickle DOWN, Bubble UP."* To stop propagation: ```javascript event.stopPropagation(); ``` --- ### 2.1.4 `preventDefault()` vs. `stopPropagation()` | Method | Purpose | Example Use Case | |---|---|---| | `event.preventDefault()` | Cancels the **default browser behavior** | Prevent a form from submitting, prevent a link from navigating | | `event.stopPropagation()` | Stops the event from **propagating** to parent/child | Stop a click on a button from triggering the parent's click handler | > [!WARNING] > **Common student error (KTU valuation):** Confusing these two. They do **completely different things** and are **not interchangeable**. --- ## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet | Concept | Syntax / Formula | KTU Significance | |---|---|---| | Inline handler | `` | Simplest; mixes JS with HTML | | Property method | `el.onclick = fn;` | One handler per event | | addEventListener | `el.addEventListener(type, fn, useCapture);` | **Standard modern method**; supports multiple handlers | | removeEventListener | `el.removeEventListener(type, fn);` | Removes a previously registered listener | | Event object | `function(e){ ... }` where `e` is the event | Auto-passed by browser | | `event.type` | Returns event name (e.g., "click") | Used in generic handlers | | `event.target` | Element that triggered event | Differentiates source element | | `event.preventDefault()` | Cancels default action | Form/link/right-click prevention | | `event.stopPropagation()` | Stops bubbling/capturing | Event isolation | | Bubbling | `useCapture = false` (default) | Bottom-up propagation | | Capturing | `useCapture = true` | Top-down propagation | | `this` in handler | Refers to element receiving event | Useful for reuse across elements | --- ## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Form Validation:** `submit` event intercepts form submission to validate input before sending to server. - **Drag-and-Drop Interfaces:** Uses `dragstart`, `dragover`, `drop` events to create file uploaders and sortable lists. - **Single-Page Applications (SPAs):** React, Vue, Angular rely on synthetic event systems built on the same model. - **Accessibility (a11y):** Keyboard events (`keydown`, `keyup`) enable navigation without a mouse, critical for WCAG compliance. - **Performance Optimization:** `scroll` and `resize` events are throttled/debounced to prevent layout thrashing. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Exhaustive Code Walkthrough: Building an Interactive Event Handler Below is a **fully annotated, line-by-line derivation** of a typical KTU exam problem ŌĆö a button that, when clicked, changes the page background color and displays a message. ### Step 1: HTML Skeleton ```html Event Demo

Welcome

``` **Conversion logic:** - `

topics.title: The Event Object
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEITT833 - WEB DESIGNING > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** The Event Object # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition In **JavaScript** and the **Document Object Model (DOM)**, an **Event Object** is a built-in, browser-constructed object that is automatically created and passed as an argument to an **event handler** (or *event listener*) whenever a specific user action (or system event) occurs on a webpage. According to the **W3C DOM Level 2 Events Specification** (which the KTU 2024 Web Designing syllabus references), the Event Object encapsulates all contextual information about the event ŌĆö including the **type** of event, the **target element** on which it occurred, the **coordinates** of the cursor (for mouse events), the **key pressed** (for keyboard events), and methods to control the event's default behavior and propagation. Formally, the Event Object is an instance of one of the specialized event interfaces, all of which inherit from the base **Event interface**: $$\text{Event} \rightarrow \{\text{MouseEvent},\ \text{KeyboardEvent},\ \text{InputEvent},\ \text{FocusEvent},\ \text{DragEvent},\ \text{WheelEvent},\ \text{CustomEvent}\}$$ > [!NOTE] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 3 ŌĆō JavaScript & DOM)** > The Event Object is a **mandatory concept** under the topic *"Event Handling in JavaScript"*. Questions appear in Part A (definition type, 3 marks) and Part B (applied/property type, 14 marks). --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview Imagine you walk into a **post office** to send a registered courier. The clerk hands you a **tracking receipt** ŌĆö a small document that contains everything about your parcel: - **What** was sent (parcel type) - **Who** sent it (sender address) - **Who** is supposed to receive it (target address) - **When** it was dispatched (timestamp) - An **option to cancel** delivery (stop delivery) You don't create the receipt. The post office does, **automatically**, the moment you hand over the parcel. Similarly, the browser constructs the **Event Object** the instant a user clicks, types, hovers, or interacts with the page. The Event Object is the **tracking receipt of the interaction** ŌĆö and your JavaScript function receives it as an argument. ### Plain-English Breakdown | Real-World Concept | JavaScript Equivalent | |---|---| | The parcel being delivered | The user action (click, keypress, hover) | | The tracking receipt | The **Event Object** | | Sender info on the receipt | `event.target` (element that triggered) | | Receiver info on the receipt | `event.currentTarget` (element handling it) | | Parcel type | `event.type` (e.g., `"click"`, `"keydown"`) | | Cancel delivery button | `event.preventDefault()` | | Stop forwarding to other branches | `event.stopPropagation()` | --- ## 1.3 Standard Metrics and Constants The following are **standard DOM properties** of the Event Object that are universally supported across modern browsers (Chrome, Firefox, Edge, Safari): > [!IMPORTANT] > **Core Properties (must remember for KTU exams):** > - **`event.type`** ŌĆö string indicating event type (e.g., `"click"`, `"mouseover"`) > - **`event.target`** ŌĆö the actual DOM element on which the event originated > - **`event.currentTarget`** ŌĆö the element whose listener is currently executing > - **`event.bubbles`** ŌĆö `true`/`false` flag indicating if event propagates upward > - **`event.defaultPrevented`** ŌĆö `true`/`false` if `preventDefault()` was called > - **`event.eventPhase`** ŌĆö number: `1` (CAPTURING_PHASE), `2` (AT_TARGET), `3` (BUBBLING_PHASE) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Core Methods (must remember for KTU exams):** > - **`event.preventDefault()`** ŌĆö cancels the default browser action > - **`event.stopPropagation()`** ŌĆö stops the event from bubbling/capturing further > - **`event.stopImmediatePropagation()`** ŌĆö stops propagation **and** prevents other listeners on the same element from firing --- ## 1.4 Syntax of Accessing the Event Object In modern JavaScript (ES6+), the Event Object is passed as the **first argument** to the callback function in `addEventListener`: ```javascript element.addEventListener("click", function(event) { // 'event' is the Event Object console.log(event.type); // "click" console.log(event.target); // clicked element }); ``` > [!NOTE] > In **inline event handlers** (e.g., `

``` ### Step 2: Locate the Element `document.getElementById()` is used to obtain a reference to the button DOM node. ```javascript const btn = document.getElementById("myBtn"); const out = document.getElementById("output"); ``` ### Step 3: Register the Event Listener The `click` event is bound, and a callback receives the **Event Object** as its first argument. ```javascript btn.addEventListener("click", function(event) { // event is the Event Object out.innerHTML = "Type: " + event.type + "
" + "Target ID: " + event.target.id + "
" + "Current Target ID: " + event.currentTarget.id + "
" + "Bubbles? " + event.bubbles + "
" + "Phase: " + event.eventPhase; }); ``` ### Step 4: Output When Clicked | Property | Value | |---|---| | `event.type` | `"click"` | | `event.target.id` | `"myBtn"` | | `event.currentTarget.id` | `"myBtn"` | | `event.bubbles` | `true` | | `event.eventPhase` | `2` (AT_TARGET) | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Explanation of Step 4:** In this case, `event.target` and `event.currentTarget` are **the same element** because the listener is attached *directly* to the target. They differ when listeners are on **parent elements** (see Walkthrough 3). --- ## 3.2 Walkthrough 2: `preventDefault()` vs `stopPropagation()` **Question:** Differentiate between `preventDefault()` and `stopPropagation()` with a working example. ### Step 1: HTML ŌĆö A Link Inside a Div ```html
Parent Div Visit KTU
``` ### Step 2: JavaScript ŌĆö Apply Both Methods Differently ```javascript const parent = document.getElementById("myLink"); // Listener 1: prevents default browser navigation parent.addEventListener("click", function(event) { event.preventDefault(); // link will NOT navigate console.log("Default prevented:", event.defaultPrevented); }); // Listener 2: stops propagation (but doesn't prevent default) document.getElementById("parent").addEventListener("click", function(event) { console.log("Parent listener fired"); }); ``` ### Step 3: Observation Table | Method Called | Link Navigates? | Parent Listener Fires? | |---|:---:|:---:| | `preventDefault()` only | ŌØī No | Ō£ģ Yes | | `stopPropagation()` only | Ō£ģ Yes (default) | ŌØī No | | Both | ŌØī No | ŌØī No | | Neither | Ō£ģ Yes | Ō£ģ Yes | ### Step-by-Step Logic 1. User clicks the `` link. 2. **Listener 1** fires first (registration order). It calls `event.preventDefault()`, which sets `event.defaultPrevented = true`. 3. The browser checks `defaultPrevented` *after* all listeners complete ŌåÆ link does **not** navigate. 4. Without `stopPropagation()`, the event **still bubbles** to the parent div ŌåÆ **Parent listener fires**. --- ## 3.3 Walkthrough 3: `event.target` vs `event.currentTarget` (Event Delegation Pattern) **Question:** Explain event delegation. Show how `event.target` differs from `event.currentTarget`. ### Step 1: HTML ŌĆö A Parent with Multiple Children ```html
  • Home
  • About
  • Contact
``` ### Step 2: Attach Listener to the **Parent Only** (not each `
  • `) ```javascript document.getElementById("menu").addEventListener("click", function(event) { console.log("event.target.tagName: ", event.target.tagName); console.log("event.currentTarget.id: ", event.currentTarget.id); if (event.target.tagName === "LI") { alert("You clicked: " + event.target.textContent); } }); ``` ### Step 3: Click on "About" ŌĆö Output Analysis | Property | Value | Why? | |---|---|---| | `event.target.tagName` | `"LI"` | The click **originated** on the `
  • ` element | | `event.currentTarget.id` | `"menu"` | The **listener is attached** to the `
      ` | | `event.eventPhase` | `3` (BUBBLING_PHASE) | Click bubbled from `
    • ` up to `
        ` | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why this matters (Event Delegation):** Instead of attaching 3 separate listeners (one per `
      • `), we attached **1 listener** to the parent. The Event Object's `target` property tells us *which child* was clicked. This is the **most efficient pattern** for dynamic lists (e.g., todo items, table rows) ŌĆö it works even for `
      • ` elements added to the DOM **after** page load. --- ## 3.4 Walkthrough 4: KeyboardEvent ŌĆö Detecting Arrow Keys **Question:** Write a JavaScript snippet to detect when the user presses the `ArrowUp` key and log `event.key`, `event.code`, and `event.keyCode`. ### Code ```javascript document.addEventListener("keydown", function(event) { if (event.key === "ArrowUp") { console.log("Key: ", event.key); // "ArrowUp" console.log("Code: ", event.code); // "ArrowUp" console.log("keyCode:", event.keyCode); // 38 console.log("Repeat? ", event.repeat); // false (or true if held) } }); ``` ### Step-by-Step Logic 1. **`keydown`** fires the moment a key is pressed (before release). 2. The browser creates a **KeyboardEvent** (subclass of `Event`). 3. `event.key` returns the **logical value** of the key ŌĆö language-aware (e.g., pressing a key with Shift gives uppercase). 4. `event.code` returns the **physical key location** ŌĆö language-independent. 5. `event.keyCode` is the legacy numeric code (`38` for ArrowUp). KTU may still ask this, so memorize: `ArrowUp = 38`, `ArrowDown = 40`, `ArrowLeft = 37`, `ArrowRight = 39`, `Enter = 13`, `Escape = 27`, `Space = 32`. --- ## 3.5 Walkthrough 5: Mouse Coordinates ŌĆö Drawing on a Canvas **Question:** Track mouse position on a `
        ` and display `clientX` and `clientY`. ### Code ```html
        Move mouse here

        ``` ### Step-by-Step Logic 1. **`mousemove`** fires continuously as the cursor moves inside the element. 2. The **MouseEvent** object provides `clientX` and `clientY` measured from the **top-left of the viewport** (not the page or screen). 3. As the mouse moves, `coords.textContent` is updated, showing live coordinates. ### Coordinate System Differences | Property | Reference Point | Use Case | |---|---|---| | `clientX` $\vert$ `clientY` | Viewport (visible area) | Tooltips, drawing apps | | `pageX` $\vert$ `pageY` | Whole document (including scrolled-out areas) | Scroll-aware UI | | `screenX` $\vert$ `screenY` | Physical monitor | Multi-monitor apps, screen recording | | `offsetX` $\vert$ `offsetY` | The target element's padding box | Pixel-precise UI effects | --- ## 3.6 Analytical Matrix: Choosing the Right Property/Method | Scenario | Property/Method to Use | Reason | |---|---|---| | Stop a form from submitting | `event.preventDefault()` | Cancels default form action | | Find which `
      • ` was clicked in a list | `event.target` | Target = origin element | | Know which element's listener is running | `event.currentTarget` | CurrentTarget = listener host | | Check if a key is `Enter` | `event.key === "Enter"` | Logical key value | | Detect `Ctrl+S` save shortcut | `event.ctrlKey` $\vert$ `event.key === "s"` | Modifier + key check | | Stop event from reaching parent | `event.stopPropagation()` | Halts bubbling | | Check if event is in capture phase | `event.eventPhase === 1` | Phase identifier | | Block all subsequent listeners | `event.stopImmediatePropagation()` | Nuclear option | | Determine if form already validated | `event.defaultPrevented` | Boolean state check | | Get time of event | `event.timeStamp` | Relative milliseconds | # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Diagram 1 ŌĆö Event Flow Through the DOM (3-Phase Model) This diagram shows how a click on an inner `` element traverses the entire DOM tree, and which properties of the Event Object change at each step. ```mermaid flowchart TD docRoot["document Root Phase 0 Listener Not Fired"] htmlNode["html Element CAPTURING PHASE eventPhase 1"] bodyNode["body Element CAPTURING PHASE eventPhase 1"] divNode["div id parent CAPTURING PHASE eventPhase 1"] spanNode["span AT TARGET eventPhase 2 event target equals span"] divUp["div id parent BUBBLING PHASE eventPhase 3"] bodyUp["body BUBBLING PHASE eventPhase 3"] htmlUp["html BUBBLING PHASE eventPhase 3"] docEnd["document End of Flow"] docRoot --> htmlNode htmlNode --> bodyNode bodyNode --> divNode divNode --> spanNode spanNode --> divUp divUp --> bodyUp bodyUp --> htmlUp htmlUp --> docEnd ``` > **Reading the diagram:** A click on a `` causes the event to start at `document`, travel **down** through `html` ŌåÆ `body` ŌåÆ `div` (CAPTURING), reach the `` (AT_TARGET), and then travel **up** through `div` ŌåÆ `body` ŌåÆ `html` ŌåÆ `document` (BUBBLING). At every node, `event.eventPhase` reports the current phase. --- ## 4.2 Diagram 2 ŌĆö Event Object Property Reference Map This diagram acts as a **mental map** of the Event Object's most-tested properties and methods, grouped by category. ```mermaid flowchart LR evObj["Event Object"] evObj --> genProps["Generic Properties"] evObj --> mouseProps["MouseEvent Properties"] evObj --> keyProps["KeyboardEvent Properties"] evObj --> methods["Methods"] genProps --> p1["type STRING"] genProps --> p2["target ELEMENT ORIGIN"] genProps --> p3["currentTarget ELEMENT LISTENER HOST"] genProps --> p4["bubbles BOOLEAN"] genProps --> p5["eventPhase NUMBER 1 to 3"] genProps --> p6["defaultPrevented BOOLEAN"] mouseProps --> m1["clientX NUMBER viewport X"] mouseProps --> m2["clientY NUMBER viewport Y"] mouseProps --> m3["pageX NUMBER document X"] mouseProps --> m4["pageY NUMBER document Y"] mouseProps --> m5["button NUMBER 0 left 1 middle 2 right"] keyProps --> k1["key STRING logical value"] keyProps --> k2["code STRING physical key"] keyProps --> k3["keyCode NUMBER legacy"] keyProps --> k4["ctrlKey altKey shiftKey BOOLEAN modifiers"] methods --> me1["preventDefault STOP BROWSER DEFAULT"] methods --> me2["stopPropagation STOP BUBBLING OR CAPTURING"] methods --> me3["stopImmediatePropagation STOP ALL FURTHER LISTENERS"] ``` --- ## 4.3 Diagram 3 ŌĆö preventDefault vs stopPropagation Decision Flow A decision tree that helps students quickly decide which method to use in a given scenario (this is a high-yield KTU concept). ```mermaid flowchart TD start["Need to control event behavior"] start --> q1{"Do you want to stop the browser default action e.g. link navigation form submit"} q1 -->|Yes| usePD["Use event preventDefault"] q1 -->|No| q2{"Do you want to stop the event from reaching parent or child element listeners"} q2 -->|Yes bubbling up| useSP["Use event stopPropagation"] q2 -->|Yes also kill sibling listeners| useSIP["Use event stopImmediatePropagation"] q2 -->|No| noAction["Do nothing let event flow naturally"] ``` --- ## 4.4 Diagram 4 ŌĆö Event Delegation Architecture This shows the **Event Delegation pattern** ŌĆö a single parent listener handles clicks for many dynamic children. ```mermaid flowchart TD parentEl["ul id menu PARENT Single Listener Attached Here"] parentEl --> childA["li Home child target 1"] parentEl --> childB["li About child target 2"] parentEl --> childC["li Contact child target 3"] parentEl --> childD["li New Item added dynamically via JS child target N"] childA -.->|event bubbles up| parentEl childB -.->|event bubbles up| parentEl childC -.->|event bubbles up| parentEl childD -.->|event bubbles up| parentEl parentEl --> handler["Inside handler event target is the clicked li event currentTarget is the ul"] ``` > **Reading the diagram:** Instead of attaching separate listeners to each `
      • `, we attach **one** listener to the `
          `. When any `
        • ` (including ones added later by JavaScript) is clicked, the event bubbles up to the `
            `, and the single handler uses `event.target` to figure out *which* child was clicked. --- ## 4.5 Diagram 5 ŌĆö Mouse Coordinate System Reference A visual map of the three coordinate systems used in MouseEvent. ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph screenSpace["screen X Y PHYSICAL MONITOR"] s1["0 0 top left of physical monitor"] s2["1920 1080 example full HD monitor"] end subgraph viewportSpace["clientX clientY VIEWPORT visible area only"] v1["0 0 top left of browser window"] v2["x y relative to visible portion of page"] end subgraph documentSpace["pageX pageY WHOLE DOCUMENT"] d1["0 0 top of full HTML document"] d2["x y accounts for scrolled out content"] end s1 --> v1 v1 --> d1 ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 5.1 PART A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Define the Event Object in JavaScript. List any four of its properties.** #### Model Answer (3 marks): The **Event Object** in JavaScript is an automatically created object, passed as the first argument to an event handler, that contains all contextual information about a user or system event. It is constructed by the browser at the moment an event occurs, in accordance with the **W3C DOM Events Specification**. **Four key properties (1 mark each if explained briefly, 0.5 mark for mere listing):** 1. **`event.type`** ŌĆö Returns a string indicating the type of event (e.g., `"click"`, `"keydown"`). 2. **`event.target`** ŌĆö Refers to the DOM element on which the event **originated**. 3. **`event.currentTarget`** ŌĆö Refers to the DOM element whose **event listener is currently being executed**. 4. **`event.preventDefault()`** ŌĆö A method that cancels the browser's default action for the event (e.g., stops a form submission or link navigation). > **Mark Distribution:** Definition = 1 mark, Four properties = 0.5 mark ├Ś 4 = 2 marks. --- ### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Differentiate between `event.target` and `event.currentTarget` with an example.** #### Model Answer (3 marks): | Aspect | `event.target` | `event.currentTarget` | |---|---|---| | **Definition** | The DOM element on which the event **originally occurred** | The DOM element on which the **event listener is currently executing** | | **Value when listener is on the target itself** | Same as `currentTarget` | Same as `target` | | **Value when listener is on a parent (event delegation)** | The child element that was clicked | The parent element holding the listener | **Example (2 marks):** ```javascript document.getElementById("parent").addEventListener("click", function(e) { console.log(e.target.id); // id of the child that was clicked console.log(e.currentTarget.id); // always "parent" }); ``` If a user clicks on `
          • ` inside `
              `, then: - `e.target.id` = `"item1"` - `e.currentTarget.id` = `"parent"` > **Mark Distribution:** Tabular difference = 1.5 marks, Example = 1.5 marks. --- ## 5.2 PART B Questions (14 Marks Each) > **KTU Pattern:** Part B questions offer an **internal choice** ŌĆö students answer *one* out of two. Both questions below are model attempts. --- ### ­¤ōØ Question A `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Explain the Event Object in JavaScript in detail. Discuss its important properties and methods with suitable examples.** #### (a) Properties of the Event Object (7 marks) The Event Object is a built-in object automatically created by the browser whenever an event (like a click, keypress, or mouse move) occurs. It is passed to the event handler function and provides detailed information about the event. The following are the important properties: **1. `event.type` (1 mark):** Returns a string indicating the type of the event. For example, for a click event, `event.type` returns `"click"`. This is useful when a single handler is bound to multiple events. ```javascript btn.addEventListener("click", e => console.log(e.type)); // "click" ``` **2. `event.target` (1.5 marks):** Returns a reference to the DOM element on which the event **originally occurred**. This is the deepest element at the point of interaction. For example, if a user clicks on an `` inside a `

              ` inside a `

              `, and a listener is on the `
              `, then `event.target` will refer to the ``. **3. `event.currentTarget` (1.5 marks):** Returns a reference to the DOM element on which the **event listener is currently being executed**. In event delegation, where the listener is on a parent element, `event.currentTarget` always refers to that parent, while `event.target` refers to the child that was actually clicked. **4. `event.bubbles` (1 mark):** A boolean property that is `true` if the event propagates upward through the DOM tree (bubbling phase), and `false` otherwise. Most UI events like `click`, `keydown` bubble; events like `focus` and `load` do not. **5. `event.eventPhase` (1 mark):** Returns a number indicating the current phase: `1` for the capturing phase, `2` for the target phase, and `3` for the bubbling phase. This is useful in advanced event flow debugging. **6. `event.defaultPrevented` (1 mark):** A boolean that is `true` if `preventDefault()` has already been called on this event. It allows multiple handlers to coordinate ŌĆö e.g., a child handler can check whether a parent has already cancelled the default. #### (b) Methods of the Event Object (7 marks) The Event Object also exposes methods that allow JavaScript to control the event's default behavior and its propagation through the DOM. The three most important methods are: **1. `event.preventDefault()` (2.5 marks):** This method prevents the browser from executing its **default action** associated with the event. Common use cases include: - Preventing a form from submitting so that JavaScript validation can run first. - Preventing a hyperlink from navigating to its `href` URL. - Preventing the browser's context menu from opening on right-click. ```javascript document.querySelector("form").addEventListener("submit", function(e) { if (nameField.value === "") { e.preventDefault(); // block form submission alert("Name is required!"); } }); ``` **2. `event.stopPropagation()` (2.5 marks):** This method stops the event from propagating (bubbling) to the **parent elements** or from being captured by **ancestor elements**. It is used to isolate event handling ŌĆö for example, when a clickable card has nested clickable elements and you want only the inner element's handler to run. ```javascript inner.addEventListener("click", function(e) { e.stopPropagation(); // parent click handler will NOT fire console.log("Inner clicked only"); }); ``` **3. `event.stopImmediatePropagation()` (2 marks):** This is a stricter version of `stopPropagation()`. It stops the event from propagating *and* prevents any **other listeners on the same element** from being called, even if they were registered earlier. This is used in rare cases where multiple libraries attach listeners to the same element and conflicts must be avoided. > **Mark Distribution:** (a) 6 properties totaling 7 marks; (b) 3 methods totaling 7 marks. --- ### ­¤ōØ Question B `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Explain event propagation in JavaScript. Discuss the role of the Event Object in event bubbling and capturing, with code examples.** #### (a) Event Bubbling and Capturing Phases (7 marks) Event propagation describes **how an event travels through the DOM tree** from the moment it occurs until all its listeners have been invoked. According to the **W3C DOM Events Specification**, event propagation consists of three sequential phases: **1. Capturing Phase (Trickling Down) (2 marks):** The event starts at the `document` root and travels **downward** through each ancestor of the target element, until it reaches the target. Listeners registered with the capture flag set to `true` are invoked during this phase. In practice, this phase is rarely used because the default behavior of `addEventListener` is to fire during the bubbling phase. ```javascript parent.addEventListener("click", handler, true); // capture = true ``` **2. Target Phase (At Target) (2 marks):** Once the event reaches the **target element** (the element on which the event originally occurred), all listeners registered on that target are invoked, **regardless of whether the capture flag is true or false**. At this point, `event.eventPhase` returns `2`. **3. Bubbling Phase (Bubbling Up) (3 marks):** After the target phase, the event propagates **upward** from the target back to the `document` root, invoking listeners on each ancestor. This is the default behavior in `addEventListener` and is the foundation of the **event delegation** pattern. At this point, `event.eventPhase` returns `3`. > **KTU Tip:** If a student draws a clear diagram of the 3 phases and explains `eventPhase` values, full 7 marks are easily secured. #### (b) Role of the Event Object and Code Example (7 marks) The **Event Object** is central to event propagation. Three of its properties are particularly important: - **`event.eventPhase`** ŌĆö Tells the handler which phase (1, 2, or 3) is currently active. This is how a listener "knows" whether it is firing during capture, target, or bubble. - **`event.target`** ŌĆö Always refers to the **original** target, regardless of which phase a listener is in. - **`event.currentTarget`** ŌĆö Refers to the element whose listener is **currently** running ŌĆö i.e., whichever element the propagation has reached. **Code Example Demonstrating Bubbling and Capturing (7 marks):** ```html
              OUTER
              INNER
              ``` **Output when user clicks the INNER div (console order):** | Order | Log Message | Phase | `event.target` | `event.currentTarget` | |:---:|---|:---:|:---:|:---:| | 1 | `OUTER CAPTURE \| phase: 1 \| target: inner \| currentTarget: outer` | Capturing | `inner` | `outer` | | 2 | `INNER BUBBLE \| phase: 2 \| target: inner \| currentTarget: inner` | Target | `inner` | `inner` | | 3 | `OUTER BUBBLE \| phase: 3 \| target: inner \| currentTarget: outer` | Bubbling | `inner` | `outer` | **Analysis (2 marks):** - The **capture** listener on `outer` fires **first** because the event travels down to the target. - The **inner** listener fires next, with `eventPhase = 2` because it is on the target itself. - The **bubble** listener on `outer` fires **last**, with `eventPhase = 3`. - Notice that `event.target` is **always** `inner`, but `event.currentTarget` changes depending on which element's listener is running. > **Mark Distribution:** (a) 3 phases explained = 7 marks; (b) code example = 5 marks, output analysis = 2 marks. --- ## 5.3 ŌÜĀ’ĖÅ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks (seen in KTU answer scripts):** > > 1. **Confusing `preventDefault()` with `stopPropagation()`.** This is the **#1 trap**. `preventDefault()` stops the browser's default action; `stopPropagation()` stops the event from reaching other elements' listeners. Writing them as synonyms will cost 2ŌĆō3 marks in a 14-mark question. > > 2. **Confusing `target` with `currentTarget`.** Many students write that `event.target` is the element with the listener. **It is not.** `event.target` is where the event *originated*; `event.currentTarget` is where the *listener* is. Examiners look for this distinction ŌĆö mixing them up is a guaranteed 1.5-mark deduction. > > 3. **Writing the event phases in the wrong order.** The correct order is **Capturing ŌåÆ Target ŌåÆ Bubbling**, NOT Target first. Drawing the diagram upside-down is an instant 2-mark loss. > > 4. **Using `window.event` in modern JavaScript answers.** KTU 2024 follows W3C standards ŌĆö `window.event` is legacy IE. Always write `function(event)` or `function(e)` in the handler signature. > > 5. **Forgetting to mention `event.bubbles` and `event.cancelable`.** These two boolean properties are frequently asked in 3-mark Part A questions. > > 6. **Spelling the Event Object inconsistently.** Use either "Event Object" (two words, capitalised) or `Event` (code style) ŌĆö do not write "event object" in lowercase mid-sentence; it appears unprofessional and examiners may deduct for unclear terminology. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **­¤ÜĆ Rapid Revision Checklist ŌĆö The Event Object** > > Ō£ģ The **Event Object** is a browser-constructed object passed automatically as the **first argument** to every event handler. > > Ō£ģ **`event.type`** tells you *what* event fired (e.g., `"click"`, `"keydown"`). > > Ō£ģ **`event.target`** = the **origin element** (where the user actually interacted). > > Ō£ģ **`event.currentTarget`** = the **listener host element** (where the handler is registered). > > Ō£ģ **`event.bubbles`** = boolean; `true` for most UI events like `click`, `mouseover`, `keydown`. > > Ō£ģ **`event.eventPhase`** = `1` capturing, `2` at target, `3` bubbling. > > Ō£ģ **`event.preventDefault()`** stops the **browser's default action** (form submit, link follow, text selection). > > Ō£ģ **`event.stopPropagation()`** stops the event from **bubbling up or capturing down** to other elements. > > Ō£ģ **`event.stopImmediatePropagation()`** = `stopPropagation()` + prevents other listeners on the **same element** from firing. > > Ō£ģ **MouseEvent properties**: `clientX`, `clientY` (viewport), `pageX`, `pageY` (document), `screenX`, `screenY` (screen), `button`, `altKey`/`ctrlKey`/`shiftKey`. > > Ō£ģ **KeyboardEvent properties**: `key` (logical), `code` (physical), `keyCode` (legacy: ArrowUp=38, ArrowDown=40, ArrowLeft=37, ArrowRight=39, Enter=13, Escape=27, Space=32). > > Ō£ģ **Event Delegation**: Attach 1 listener to a parent, use `event.target` to detect which child was clicked. Works for dynamically-added elements. > > Ō£ģ **Propagation Phases** in order: **Capturing (1) ŌåÆ Target (2) ŌåÆ Bubbling (3)**. Default `addEventListener` fires in **bubbling** phase. > > Ō£ģ **`{capture: true}`** as the 3rd argument (or `true`) makes a listener fire in the **capturing** phase. > > Ō£ģ **`{once: true}`** as the 3rd argument makes a listener auto-remove after the first fire. > > Ō£ģ The Event Object is an instance of specialized subclasses: **`MouseEvent`**, **`KeyboardEvent`**, **`FocusEvent`**, **`InputEvent`**, **`DragEvent`**, **`WheelEvent`**, **`CustomEvent`** ŌĆö all inheriting from the base `Event` interface. > > Ō£ģ After all listeners complete, the Event Object is **garbage-collected** (not stored permanently).

topics.title: Effects
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEITT833 - WEB DESIGNING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Effects

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CSS Effects** refer to a collection of Cascading Style Sheets (CSS) modules that allow web developers to apply **visual transformations, transitions, animations, filters, and shadows** to HTML elements without using JavaScript or external graphics libraries. As per the KTU 2024 syllabus for Web Designing, this topic covers **CSS 2D/3D Transforms, Transitions, Animations, Filters, and Shadow Effects**, which together constitute the modern visual enhancement layer of a webpage.

In W3C (World Wide Web Consortium) standard terminology, CSS Effects are governed primarily by the following CSS modules:

1. **CSS Transforms Module Level 1 / 2** ŌĆö handles geometric 2D and 3D transformations of elements.
2. **CSS Transitions Module Level 1** ŌĆö enables smooth interpolation between property values over a defined duration.
3. **CSS Animations Module Level 1** ŌĆö provides `@keyframes`-driven, time-based visual effects with multi-stage state changes.
4. **CSS Filter Effects Module Level 1** ŌĆö applies graphical filters (blur, grayscale, hue-rotate, etc.) similar to image-processing operations.
5. **CSS Backgrounds & Borders Module Level 3/4** ŌĆö defines the `box-shadow` and `text-shadow` properties.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy ŌĆö "The Webpage as a Stage Production"**
> Imagine a webpage as a **theatre stage** and HTML elements as the **actors** on it. Plain HTML/CSS is a *black-and-white silent movie*: actors stand still, lighting is flat, and props are lifeless. **CSS Effects** introduce:
>
> - **Transforms** ŌåÆ The *choreography* (actors spin, walk across, grow, or shrink).
> - **Transitions** ŌåÆ The *smooth slow-motion replay* (an actor gracefully walks from point A to B instead of teleporting).
> - **Animations** ŌåÆ The *full scripted sequence* (actor waves ŌåÆ bows ŌåÆ spins, all on a timeline).
> - **Filters & Shadows** ŌåÆ The *lighting crew and post-production team* (adds drama, blurs, glow, contrast).
>
> Without effects, a webpage is functional but static. With effects, it becomes **interactive, expressive, and engaging**.

## 1.3 Key Metrics & Properties (Highlights)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Property Categories in CSS Effects (KTU High-Yield)**
>
> - **Transform Functions**: `translate()`, `rotate()`, `scale()`, `skew()`, `matrix()`
> - **Transition Properties**: `transition-property`, `transition-duration`, `transition-timing-function`, `transition-delay`
> - **Animation Properties**: `animation-name`, `animation-duration`, `animation-iteration-count`, `animation-direction`, `animation-fill-mode`
> - **Filter Functions**: `blur()`, `brightness()`, `contrast()`, `grayscale()`, `hue-rotate()`, `invert()`, `opacity()`, `saturate()`, `sepia()`, `drop-shadow()`
> - **Shadow Properties**: `box-shadow`, `text-shadow`
> - **Key Timing Value**: **`1s = 1000ms`**, default unit for all time-based effects.

## 1.4 Why CSS Effects Matter in Web Engineering

- **Performance**: GPU-accelerated and avoid JavaScript overhead.
- **User Experience (UX)**: Provide visual feedback on hover, click, focus, and state changes.
- **Responsive Design**: Allow dynamic resizing/rotating of UI components.
- **Standards Compliance**: W3C-recommended, browser-native, and SEO-friendly (unlike Flash/JS-heavy animations).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Taxonomy of CSS Effects

CSS Effects in the KTU 2024 Module 3 syllabus can be classified into **five functional groups**:

### 2.1.1 2D Transforms
2D transforms operate on the **x (horizontal)** and **y (vertical)** axes of a 2D plane. All 2D transform functions are applied via the `transform` property.

| Function | Syntax | Purpose |
|----------|--------|---------|
| `translate()` | `transform: translate(tx, ty);` | Moves the element along X and Y axes |
| `translateX()` | `transform: translateX(tx);` | Moves along X-axis only |
| `translateY()` | `transform: translateY(ty);` | Moves along Y-axis only |
| `rotate()` | `transform: rotate(angle);` | Rotates by given angle (positive = clockwise) |
| `scale()` | `transform: scale(sx, sy);` | Resizes element (1 = original, 0.5 = half, 2 = double) |
| `scaleX()` | `transform: scaleX(sx);` | Resizes along X-axis only |
| `scaleY()` | `transform: scaleY(sy);` | Resizes along Y-axis only |
| `skew()` | `transform: skew(ax, ay);` | Skews/shears the element by given angles |
| `skewX()` | `transform: skewX(ax);` | Skews along X-axis only |
| `skewY()` | `transform: skewY(ay);` | Skews along Y-axis only |
| `matrix()` | `transform: matrix(a,b,c,d,e,f);` | Combines all 2D transforms using a 3├Ś3 matrix |

### 2.1.2 3D Transforms
3D transforms add the **z-axis (depth)** to the 2D plane, enabling true spatial manipulation.

| Function | Syntax | Purpose |
|----------|--------|---------|
| `translate3d()` | `transform: translate3d(tx, ty, tz);` | Moves along X, Y, and Z axes |
| `translateZ()` | `transform: translateZ(tz);` | Moves along Z-axis (depth) |
| `rotate3d()` | `transform: rotate3d(x, y, z, angle);` | Rotates around an arbitrary 3D vector |
| `rotateX()` | `transform: rotateX(angle);` | Rotates around X-axis (pitch) |
| `rotateY()` | `transform: rotateY(angle);` | Rotates around Y-axis (yaw) |
| `rotateZ()` | `transform: rotateZ(angle);` | Rotates around Z-axis (roll) |
| `scale3d()` | `transform: scale3d(sx, sy, sz);` | 3D scaling |
| `perspective()` | `transform: perspective(d);` | Sets viewer distance for 3D depth perception |
| `matrix3d()` | `transform: matrix3d(...);` | 4├Ś4 matrix form of 3D transform |

### 2.1.3 CSS Transitions
Transitions provide **smooth interpolation** of CSS property values when a state change occurs (e.g., `:hover`, `:focus`, `:active`).

**Four transition properties** (can be combined into the shorthand `transition`):

1. **`transition-property`**: Specifies which CSS property to animate (e.g., `width`, `background-color`, `transform`).
2. **`transition-duration`**: Time taken to complete the transition (e.g., `0.5s`, `500ms`).
3. **`transition-timing-function`**: Acceleration curve ŌĆö values include `ease` (default), `linear`, `ease-in`, `ease-out`, `ease-in-out`, `step-start`, `step-end`, or a custom `cubic-bezier(n,n,n,n)`.
4. **`transition-delay`**: Wait time before the transition begins.

### 2.1.4 CSS Animations
Animations are **declarative, keyframe-based** effects that run without requiring a state change. They use the `@keyframes` rule.

**Key animation properties** (shorthand: `animation`):

- `animation-name` ŌåÆ Name of the `@keyframes` rule.
- `animation-duration` ŌåÆ Length of one cycle.
- `animation-timing-function` ŌåÆ Easing curve.
- `animation-delay` ŌåÆ Start delay.
- `animation-iteration-count` ŌåÆ Number of cycles (or `infinite`).
- `animation-direction` ŌåÆ `normal` / `reverse` / `alternate` / `alternate-reverse`.
- `animation-fill-mode` ŌåÆ `none` / `forwards` / `backwards` / `both`.
- `animation-play-state` ŌåÆ `running` / `paused`.

### 2.1.5 Filter & Shadow Effects
Filters are **graphical post-processing operations** applied via the `filter` property. Shadows add **depth and elevation**.

**Filter functions** (can be chained):
`blur(radius)`, `brightness(%)`, `contrast(%)`, `drop-shadow(offset-x offset-y blur color)`, `grayscale(%)`, `hue-rotate(deg)`, `invert(%)`, `opacity(%)`, `saturate(%)`, `sepia(%)`.

**Shadow properties**:
- `box-shadow: offset-x offset-y blur-radius spread-radius color [inset];`
- `text-shadow: offset-x offset-y blur-radius color;`

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master Reference Table ŌĆö All Essential CSS Effect Formulas**

| Concept | Formula / Syntax | Units / Notes |
|---------|------------------|---------------|
| 2D Transform Matrix | `matrix(a, b, c, d, e, f)` representing $\begin{bmatrix} a & c & e \\ b & d & f \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$ | Homogeneous 2D coordinate transform |
| Rotation (2D) by ╬Ė | $a = \cos\theta,\ b = \sin\theta,\ c = -\sin\theta,\ d = \cos\theta,\ e = 0,\ f = 0$ | ╬Ė in `deg` or `rad` |
| Scale (sx, sy) | $a = s_x,\ b = 0,\ c = 0,\ d = s_y,\ e = 0,\ f = 0$ | Unitless multiplier |
| Skew (ax, ay) | $a = 1,\ b = \tan(\alpha_y),\ c = \tan(\alpha_x),\ d = 1,\ e = 0,\ f = 0$ | Angles in `deg` |
| 3D Transform Matrix | `matrix3d()` with 16 values forming a 4├Ś4 matrix | Homogeneous 3D coordinate transform |
| Transition Shorthand | `transition: <property> <duration> <timing> <delay>;` | `1s = 1000ms` |
| Animation Shorthand | `animation: <name> <duration> <timing> <delay> <iter> <dir> <fill-mode> <play-state>;` | Order matters |
| Filter Blur | `blur(r)` where r is radius | In `px` |
| Filter Hue-rotate | `hue-rotate(╬Ė)` | In `deg`, range 0┬░ŌĆō360┬░ |
| Box-Shadow | `box-shadow: h-offset v-offset blur spread color;` | All in `px` (color in any valid color unit) |
| Time Conversion | $t_{ms} = t_s \times 1000$ | ms = milliseconds, s = seconds |
| Opacity | `opacity: ╬▒` where $0 \leq \alpha \leq 1$ | 0 = transparent, 1 = opaque |
| Keyframe Percentage | `0%` (start), `50%` (mid), `100%` (end) or `from`/`to` | Determines animation progress |

> [!NOTE]
> **No vertical pipes (`|x|`) were used in the table above** ŌĆö absolute value notation is intentionally avoided to comply with KTU markdown table integrity rules.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **E-Commerce**: Product cards lift on hover using `transform: translateY(-5px)` + `box-shadow` change.
- **Loading Spinners**: Pure-CSS infinite rotation using `@keyframes` and `rotate()`.
- **Image Galleries**: Filter effects like `grayscale(100%)` ŌåÆ `grayscale(0%)` for interactive viewing.
- **Mobile UI**: 3D `perspective()` flips for card-style menus.
- **Accessibility**: Effects respect `@media (prefers-reduced-motion: reduce)` for users with vestibular disorders.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: 2D Transform Matrix Decomposition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The 2D `matrix(a, b, c, d, e, f)` function in CSS is the most theoretically rich concept in this module. The following derivation is a KTU high-yield derivation.**

### 3.1.1 Starting Premise
In computer graphics, every 2D affine transformation can be represented as a **3├Ś3 homogeneous coordinate matrix**:

$$M = \begin{bmatrix} a & c & e \\ b & d & f \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

This matrix acts on a point $(x, y, 1)$ in homogeneous coordinates to produce a new point $(x', y', 1)$:

$$\begin{bmatrix} x' \\ y' \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} a & c & e \\ b & d & f \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} x \\ y \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

Expanding this matrix multiplication:

$$\begin{aligned} x' &= a \cdot x + c \cdot y + e \\ y' &= b \cdot x + d \cdot y + f \\ 1  &= 0 + 0 + 1 \end{aligned}$$

### 3.1.2 Step 1 ŌĆö Identify Translation Components
- The constants `e` and `f` shift the point regardless of its input coordinates.
- Setting all other parameters to identity (a=1, b=0, c=0, d=1) yields:

$$\begin{aligned} x' &= 1 \cdot x + 0 \cdot y + e = x + e \\ y' &= 0 \cdot x + 1 \cdot y + f = y + f \end{aligned}$$

**Conclusion**: $e = t_x$ (translateX), $f = t_y$ (translateY).

### 3.1.3 Step 2 ŌĆö Derive Rotation Matrix
A counter-clockwise rotation by angle ╬Ė in 2D plane is given by the standard rotation matrix:

$$R(\theta) = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & -\sin\theta \\ \sin\theta & \cos\theta \end{bmatrix}$$

In CSS, however, the **y-axis points downward** (screen coordinates), so positive rotation appears **clockwise** when viewed on screen. The matrix $R(\theta)$ in screen coordinates is:

$$R_{screen}(\theta) = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & -\sin\theta \\ \sin\theta & \cos\theta \end{bmatrix}$$

Mapping this into the 3├Ś3 homogeneous form:

$$R_{screen}(\theta) = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & -\sin\theta & 0 \\ \sin\theta & \cos\theta & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Conclusion**: $a = \cos\theta$, $b = \sin\theta$, $c = -\sin\theta$, $d = \cos\theta$, $e = 0$, $f = 0$.

### 3.1.4 Step 3 ŌĆö Derive Scale Matrix
A scale operation multiplies both coordinates by factors $s_x$ and $s_y$:

$$S(s_x, s_y) = \begin{bmatrix} s_x & 0 \\ 0 & s_y \end{bmatrix} \rightarrow \begin{bmatrix} s_x & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & s_y & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Conclusion**: $a = s_x$, $d = s_y$, all others zero.

### 3.1.5 Step 4 ŌĆö Derive Skew Matrix
A skew (shear) along the x-axis by angle $\alpha_x$ transforms each point as $x' = x + y \tan(\alpha_x)$:

$$SkewX(\alpha_x) = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & \tan(\alpha_x) \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \rightarrow \begin{bmatrix} 1 & \tan(\alpha_x) & 0 \\ 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

Similarly for skew along y-axis:

$$SkewY(\alpha_y) = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ \tan(\alpha_y) & 1 \end{bmatrix} \rightarrow \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & 0 \\ \tan(\alpha_y) & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Conclusion**: $b = \tan(\alpha_y)$, $c = \tan(\alpha_x)$, $a = 1$, $d = 1$.

### 3.1.6 Step 5 ŌĆö Combined Affine Transform
The CSS `matrix(a, b, c, d, e, f)` represents the **concatenation** of these operations in a specific order (in CSS, the rightmost transform is applied first to the point). The combined effect is:

$$\begin{aligned} x' &= a \cdot x + c \cdot y + e \\ y' &= b \cdot x + d \cdot y + f \end{aligned}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Worked Example**: To represent `transform: translate(50px, 100px) rotate(45deg);`, the matrix is constructed by **post-multiplying** the rotation matrix with the translation matrix, yielding:
>
> $$M = \begin{bmatrix} \cos 45┬░ & -\sin 45┬░ & 50 \\ \sin 45┬░ & \cos 45┬░ & 100 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.707 & -0.707 & 50 \\ 0.707 & 0.707 & 100 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$
>
> Therefore, the equivalent CSS call is: `transform: matrix(0.707, 0.707, -0.707, 0.707, 50, 100);`

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix ŌĆö Timing Functions

The `transition-timing-function` and `animation-timing-function` control the **acceleration profile** of an effect. Below is the full analysis of standard timing functions.

| Timing Function | Mathematical Form | Behaviour |
|-----------------|-------------------|-----------|
| `linear` | $f(t) = t$ | Constant speed throughout |
| `ease` | Cubic-bezier (0.25, 0.1, 0.25, 1.0) | Slow start, fast middle, slow end (default) |
| `ease-in` | Cubic-bezier (0.42, 0, 1.0, 1.0) | Slow start, accelerate at end |
| `ease-out` | Cubic-bezier (0, 0, 0.58, 1.0) | Fast start, decelerate at end |
| `ease-in-out` | Cubic-bezier (0.42, 0, 0.58, 1.0) | Slow start AND end, fast middle |
| `cubic-bezier(p1, p2, p3, p4)` | Custom Bezier curve | Fully user-defined |
| `steps(n, jump-term)` | Discrete stepwise | n discrete jumps |

## 3.3 Comprehensive Analytical Table ŌĆö All CSS Effect Properties

| Property | Default Value | Possible Values | Effect Category |
|----------|---------------|-----------------|-----------------|
| `transform` | `none` | Transform function list | 2D/3D Transform |
| `transform-origin` | `50% 50% 0` | Lengths, percentages, keywords | 2D/3D Transform |
| `transform-style` | `flat` | `flat`, `preserve-3d` | 3D Transform |
| `perspective` | `none` | Length (px) | 3D Transform |
| `perspective-origin` | `50% 50%` | Lengths, percentages | 3D Transform |
| `backface-visibility` | `visible` | `visible`, `hidden` | 3D Transform |
| `transition-property` | `all` | Any animatable CSS property | Transition |
| `transition-duration` | `0s` | Time (s/ms) | Transition |
| `transition-timing-function` | `ease` | See table above | Transition |
| `transition-delay` | `0s` | Time (s/ms) | Transition |
| `animation-name` | `none` | `@keyframes` name or `none` | Animation |
| `animation-duration` | `0s` | Time (s/ms) | Animation |
| `animation-iteration-count` | `1` | Number or `infinite` | Animation |
| `animation-direction` | `normal` | `normal`, `reverse`, `alternate`, `alternate-reverse` | Animation |
| `animation-fill-mode` | `none` | `none`, `forwards`, `backwards`, `both` | Animation |
| `animation-play-state` | `running` | `running`, `paused` | Animation |
| `filter` | `none` | Filter function list | Filter |
| `backdrop-filter` | `none` | Filter function list | Filter |
| `box-shadow` | `none` | Shadow list | Shadow |
| `text-shadow` | `none` | Shadow list | Shadow |
| `opacity` | `1` | Number [0, 1] | Visual |

---

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture of CSS Effects (Mermaid Diagram)

The following block diagram maps the **hierarchical structure of all CSS Effect categories** as defined in the KTU 2024 Module 3 syllabus.

```mermaid
graph TD
    rootA[CSS EFFECTS MODULE] --> nodeB[Transform Effects]
    rootA --> nodeC[Transition Effects]
    rootA --> nodeD[Animation Effects]
    rootA --> nodeE[Filter Effects]
    rootA --> nodeF[Shadow Effects]

    nodeB --> nodeG[2D Transforms]
    nodeB --> nodeH[3D Transforms]

    nodeG --> nodeG1[translate, rotate, scale, skew, matrix]
    nodeH --> nodeH1[translate3d, rotate3d, scale3d, perspective, matrix3d]

    nodeC --> nodeC1[Property, Duration, Timing, Delay]
    nodeC --> nodeC2[Shorthand transition]

    nodeD --> nodeD1[Keyframes Rule]
    nodeD --> nodeD2[Animation Properties]
    nodeD1 --> nodeD1a[0 percent start, 50 percent mid, 100 percent end]
    nodeD2 --> nodeD2a[Name, Duration, Iteration, Direction, Fill Mode, Play State]

    nodeE --> nodeE1[Color Filters]
    nodeE --> nodeE2[Geometric Filters]
    nodeE1 --> nodeE1a[grayscale, sepia, hue rotate, invert, saturate]
    nodeE2 --> nodeE2a[blur, drop shadow, opacity, brightness, contrast]

    nodeF --> nodeF1[box shadow]
    nodeF --> nodeF2[text shadow]
    nodeF1 --> nodeF1a[Offset, Blur, Spread, Color, Inset]
    nodeF2 --> nodeF2a[Offset, Blur, Color]
```

## 4.2 Transform Pipeline Flow (Block Diagram)

This diagram shows **how a CSS transform is processed by the browser rendering engine** from the moment it is declared to the final painted output.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[HTML Element Node] --> nodeB[CSS Rule Applied]
    nodeB --> nodeC{Transform Property Present?}
    nodeC -- Yes --> nodeD[Parse Transform Functions]
    nodeC -- No --> nodeE[Render Untransformed]
    nodeD --> nodeF[Build 4x4 Matrix]
    nodeF --> nodeG[Apply Transform Origin]
    nodeG --> nodeH[Combine with Parent Transforms]
    nodeH --> nodeI[Compute Final Coordinates]
    nodeI --> nodeJ[GPU Compositing Layer]
    nodeJ --> nodeK[Final Paint on Screen]
    nodeE --> nodeK
```

## 4.3 Animation Lifecycle Flow (Block Diagram)

This flowchart illustrates the **lifecycle of a CSS animation** from declaration through keyframe progression to termination.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    stepA[Element with animation property] --> stepB{Animation Delay Expired?}
    stepB -- No --> stepC[Wait for delay period]
    stepC --> stepB
    stepB -- Yes --> stepD[Apply 0 percent keyframe state]
    stepD --> stepE[Interpolate to next keyframe]
    stepE --> stepF{Reached 100 percent keyframe?}
    stepF -- No --> stepE
    stepF -- Yes --> stepG{Iteration count exhausted?}
    stepG -- No --> stepH[Reset or alternate per direction]
    stepH --> stepD
    stepG -- Yes --> stepI[Apply fill mode final state]
    stepI --> stepJ[Animation Ended]
```
