# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2024]
**Define CSS transitions. List any four transition properties with their purpose.**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **CSS Transition** is a CSS module that enables smooth interpolation of CSS property values from one state to another when a state change occurs (such as `:hover`, `:focus`, or `:active`).

The **four transition properties** are:

1. **`transition-property`**: Specifies the name of the CSS property to which the transition is applied (e.g., `width`, `background-color`).
2. **`transition-duration`**: Defines the total time taken to complete the transition (e.g., `0.5s` or `500ms`).
3. **`transition-timing-function`**: Specifies the acceleration curve of the transition (e.g., `ease`, `linear`, `ease-in-out`, `cubic-bezier()`).
4. **`transition-delay`**: Specifies the waiting time before the transition starts after the state change.

**Shorthand**: `transition: <property> <duration> <timing-function> <delay>;`

---

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023]
**Explain the difference between CSS transitions and CSS animations.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | CSS Transition | CSS Animation |
|--------|---------------|---------------|
| **Trigger** | Requires a state change (`:hover`, `:focus`) | Runs automatically on page load or via state change |
| **Definition** | Uses the `transition` property | Uses `@keyframes` rule + `animation` property |
| **Stages** | Only two states (initial ŌåÆ final) | Multiple keyframe stages (0%, 50%, 100%, etc.) |
| **Iteration** | Runs once per state change | Can loop infinitely (`animation-iteration-count: infinite`) |
| **Direction Control** | Cannot reverse | Can use `reverse`, `alternate`, `alternate-reverse` |
| **Complexity** | Simpler, ideal for hover effects | More complex, suitable for multi-step sequences |

---

## 5.2 Part B ŌĆö Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) ŌĆö Option 1

#### (a) [7 Marks] [KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2024]
**Explain the 2D transform functions in CSS. Write the CSS code to rotate a div by 30 degrees, scale it to 1.5 times, and translate it by 100px right using the `matrix()` function.**

**Model Solution:**

**2D Transform Functions in CSS:**

The `transform` property in CSS is used to apply 2D transformations to HTML elements. The major 2D transform functions are:

1. **`translate(tx, ty)`**: Moves the element along the X and Y axes by the given lengths. Positive values move right/down, negative values move left/up.
2. **`rotate(angle)`**: Rotates the element clockwise (in screen coordinates) by the given angle. Accepts `deg`, `rad`, `grad`, or `turn` units.
3. **`scale(sx, sy)`**: Enlarges or shrinks the element. Value `1` = original size, `0.5` = half, `2` = double.
4. **`skew(ax, ay)`**: Skews the element along X and Y axes by given angles (in degrees).
5. **`matrix(a, b, c, d, e, f)`**: Combines all 2D transforms in a single 3├Ś3 homogeneous matrix form.

**Derivation of the required `matrix()` call:**

Given the requirement: `rotate(30deg) scale(1.5) translate(100px, 0)`.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Rotation by 30┬░:**
$$a_1 = \cos 30┬░ = 0.866,\quad b_1 = \sin 30┬░ = 0.5,\quad c_1 = -\sin 30┬░ = -0.5,\quad d_1 = \cos 30┬░ = 0.866$$

$$R = \begin{bmatrix} 0.866 & -0.5 & 0 \\ 0.5 & 0.866 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Scale by 1.5:**
$$S = \begin{bmatrix} 1.5 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 1.5 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Translate by (100px, 0):**
$$T = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & 100 \\ 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Combined matrix (post-multiplied, rightmost first applied to point):**
$$M = T \times S \times R$$

Computing the matrix product step by step (note: in CSS, the *last declared* transform is applied *first* to the point, so we apply R first to the point, then S, then T):

$$M = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & 100 \\ 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 1.5 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 1.5 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 0.866 & -0.5 & 0 \\ 0.5 & 0.866 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

Computing $S \times R$ first:

$$\begin{aligned} S \times R &= \begin{bmatrix} 1.5 \times 0.866 & 1.5 \times (-0.5) & 0 \\ 0.5 \times 0.866 & 1.5 \times 0.866 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \\ &= \begin{bmatrix} 1.299 & -0.75 & 0 \\ 0.433 & 1.299 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \end{aligned}$$

Now computing $T \times (S \times R)$:

$$\begin{aligned} T \times (S \times R) &= \begin{bmatrix} 1.299 & -0.75 & 100 \\ 0.433 & 1.299 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \end{aligned}$$

**Final CSS Code:**

```css
div {
    transform: matrix(1.299, 0.433, -0.75, 1.299, 100, 0);
}
```

Equivalently, written using individual transform functions:

```css
div {
    transform: translate(100px, 0) scale(1.5) rotate(30deg);
}
```

---

#### (b) [7 Marks] [KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2024]
**Explain CSS `@keyframes` and animation properties. Write a CSS animation that makes a box bounce up and down infinitely using `@keyframes`.**

**Model Solution:**

**`@keyframes` Rule:**
The `@keyframes` rule in CSS defines the **intermediate steps** of an animation. It specifies how a property (or set of properties) should change over the duration of the animation, expressed as a percentage of the total duration. The keywords `from` and `to` are equivalent to `0%` and `100%` respectively.

**Key animation properties:**

- `animation-name` ŌåÆ Name of the `@keyframes` to use.
- `animation-duration` ŌåÆ Total time for one cycle.
- `animation-iteration-count` ŌåÆ Number of times the animation runs (or `infinite`).
- `animation-timing-function` ŌåÆ Easing function.
- `animation-delay` ŌåÆ Wait time before starting.
- `animation-direction` ŌåÆ `normal`, `reverse`, `alternate`, `alternate-reverse`.
- `animation-fill-mode` ŌåÆ `none`, `forwards`, `backwards`, `both`.
- `animation-play-state` ŌåÆ `running` or `paused`.

**Shorthand**: `animation: <name> <duration> <timing> <delay> <iteration> <direction> <fill-mode> <play-state>;`

**Complete Bouncing Box Example:**

```css
.box {
    width: 100px;
    height: 100px;
    background-color: #3498db;
    border-radius: 10px;
    position: relative;
    animation: bounce 1s ease-in-out infinite;
}

@keyframes bounce {
    0% {
        transform: translateY(0);
    }
    50% {
        transform: translateY(-150px);
    }
    100% {
        transform: translateY(0);
    }
}
```

**Explanation:**
- The box starts at its original vertical position (`translateY(0)`).
- At `50%` (half a second in), it has moved up by 150 pixels (`translateY(-150px)`), reaching the peak of the bounce.
- At `100%` (one full second), it returns to its original position.
- The `ease-in-out` timing function creates a smooth, natural bouncing motion.
- The `infinite` value for `animation-iteration-count` makes the animation loop forever.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) ŌĆö Option 2

#### (a) [7 Marks] [KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023]
**Explain CSS filter effects with examples. Differentiate between `filter` and `backdrop-filter`.**

**Model Solution:**

**CSS Filter Effects:**
The `filter` property in CSS applies **graphical filters** to an element, similar to image-processing operations in software like Photoshop. Filters are commonly used to blur, color-shift, or visually distort elements.

**Common filter functions:**

1. **`blur(radius)`** ŌåÆ Applies a Gaussian blur. Example: `filter: blur(5px);`
2. **`brightness(%)`** ŌåÆ Adjusts brightness. `0%` = black, `100%` = original, `200%` = double bright.
3. **`contrast(%)`** ŌåÆ Adjusts contrast. `0%` = gray, `100%` = original.
4. **`grayscale(%)`** ŌåÆ Converts to grayscale. `100%` = fully grayscale, `0%` = original.
5. **`hue-rotate(deg)`** ŌåÆ Shifts all hues by the given angle (0┬░ŌĆō360┬░).
6. **`invert(%)`** ŌåÆ Inverts all colors. `100%` = fully inverted.
7. **`opacity(%)`** ŌåÆ Adjusts transparency. `0%` = invisible, `100%` = opaque.
8. **`saturate(%)`** ŌåÆ Adjusts color saturation. `0%` = grayscale, `100%` = original.
9. **`sepia(%)`** ŌåÆ Applies a sepia tone. `100%` = fully sepia.
10. **`drop-shadow(h-offset v-offset blur color)`** ŌåÆ Adds a shadow that follows the alpha shape of the element.

**Multiple filters can be chained:**
```css
img {
    filter: grayscale(80%) blur(2px) brightness(110%);
}
```

**Difference between `filter` and `backdrop-filter`:**

| Aspect | `filter` | `backdrop-filter` |
|--------|----------|-------------------|
| **Applies to** | The element itself and its content | The area *behind* the element |
| **Effect on background** | Does not blur background | Blurs/transffects whatever is behind the element |
| **Use case** | Image effects, UI color shifts | Frosted glass effect, modal overlays |
| **Requires transparent element** | No | Yes (element must be transparent) |
| **Browser support** | Excellent | Good (newer) |
| **Example use** | `filter: blur(5px);` blurs the image | `backdrop-filter: blur(10px);` blurs what is behind |

**Example of `backdrop-filter`:**
```css
.glass-card {
    background-color: rgba(255, 255, 255, 0.3);
    backdrop-filter: blur(10px) saturate(150%);
    border: 1px solid rgba(255, 255, 255, 0.2);
}
```

---

#### (b) [7 Marks] [KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023]
**Explain the CSS `box-shadow` and `text-shadow` properties. Write CSS to create a card with a soft drop shadow and a glowing text effect.**

**Model Solution:**

**`box-shadow` Property:**
The `box-shadow` property adds shadow effects around an element's frame. It supports multiple shadows separated by commas.

**Syntax:**
```css
box-shadow: <offset-x> <offset-y> <blur-radius> <spread-radius> <color> [inset];
```

- **offset-x**: Horizontal offset (positive = right, negative = left).
- **offset-y**: Vertical offset (positive = down, negative = up).
- **blur-radius**: Optional. How blurred the shadow is (default `0` = sharp).
- **spread-radius**: Optional. How much the shadow expands (positive = larger, negative = smaller).
- **color**: Any valid CSS color.
- **inset**: Optional keyword. Makes the shadow appear *inside* the element.

**`text-shadow` Property:**
The `text-shadow` property adds shadow to text. It has a similar syntax but lacks the `spread-radius` and `inset` values.

**Syntax:**
```css
text-shadow: <offset-x> <offset-y> <blur-radius> <color>;
```

**Complete Code ŌĆö Card with Soft Drop Shadow and Glowing Text:**

```html
<!DOCTYPE html>
<html>
<head>
<style>
    .card {
        width: 300px;
        padding: 30px;
        background-color: #ffffff;
        border-radius: 12px;
        box-shadow: 0 10px 25px rgba(0, 0, 0, 0.15),
                    0 4px 10px rgba(0, 0, 0, 0.08);
        text-align: center;
        margin: 50px auto;
    }
    
    .glow-text {
        font-size: 32px;
        font-weight: bold;
        color: #ffffff;
        background-color: #1a1a2e;
        padding: 20px;
        border-radius: 8px;
        text-shadow: 0 0 10px #00ffff,
                     0 0 20px #00ffff,
                     0 0 30px #00ffff,
                     0 0 40px #0080ff;
    }
</style>
</head>
<body>
    <div class="card">
        <div class="glow-text">NEON GLOW</div>
        <p>This card has a soft drop shadow effect.</p>
    </div>
</body>
</html>
```

**Explanation:**
- The `.card` uses two layered `box-shadow` declarations: a deeper, softer shadow (10px Y offset, 25px blur) and a tighter, more defined shadow (4px Y offset, 10px blur). This creates a **realistic elevation effect**.
- The `.glow-text` uses **four stacked `text-shadow` declarations** with increasing blur radii (10px ŌåÆ 20px ŌåÆ 30px ŌåÆ 40px) to simulate a **neon glow** emanating outward.
- The `rgba(0, 0, 0, 0.15)` color uses alpha transparency for a more natural shadow appearance.

---

## 5.3 ŌÜĀ’ĖÅ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Examinations:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting semicolons** inside `@keyframes` blocks ŌĆö every property declaration must end with `;`.
> 2. **Confusing `0%` and `from`**, `100%` and `to` ŌĆö they are equivalent but students often misspell `from` as `form`.
> 3. **Wrong unit on `rotate()`** ŌĆö `rotate(45)` is invalid; it must be `rotate(45deg)` or `rotate(0.785rad)`.
> 4. **Order of transform functions matters** ŌĆö `translate(100px) rotate(45deg)` is NOT the same as `rotate(45deg) translate(100px)`. The rightmost is applied first to the point.
> 5. **Negative values in `scale()`** ŌĆö `scale(-1, 1)` mirrors the element, which is correct, but students often miss the sign.
> 6. **`box-shadow` syntax** ŌĆö Students often write `box-shadow: 5px 5px black;` forgetting that the blur radius is optional but must come *after* the offsets.
> 7. **Mixing up `transition` and `animation`** ŌĆö Transitions need a state change trigger; animations do not.
> 8. **Skipping the `transform-origin`** ŌĆö Default is `50% 50% 0`. If not specified, scale and rotate behave around the center, which is usually intended, but sometimes students expect top-left behavior.
> 9. **Forgetting `infinite` on `animation-iteration-count`** ŌĆö Without it, animations run only once.
> 10. **Not closing the `@keyframes` block** ŌĆö The `}` must be present, or the entire stylesheet may fail to parse.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist ŌĆö CSS Effects**

- Ō£ģ **CSS Effects** is an umbrella term covering Transforms, Transitions, Animations, Filters, and Shadows.
- Ō£ģ **2D Transforms** include `translate()`, `rotate()`, `scale()`, `skew()`, and `matrix()`.
- Ō£ģ **3D Transforms** add the Z-axis and require `perspective()` for depth perception.
- Ō£ģ **`matrix(a,b,c,d,e,f)`** is a 3├Ś3 homogeneous matrix where `e,f` are translation and `a,b,c,d` encode rotation/scale/skew.
- Ō£ģ **Transitions** have **4 sub-properties** (property, duration, timing, delay) and need a state change to trigger.
- Ō£ģ **Animations** use `@keyframes` and run **independently of state changes**; can be `infinite`.
- Ō£ģ **`animation-fill-mode: forwards`** retains the final keyframe state after the animation ends.
- Ō£ģ **`animation-direction: alternate`** makes the animation reverse on alternate iterations.
- Ō£ģ **Filter functions** can be chained: `filter: blur(5px) grayscale(50%);`
- Ō£ģ **`backdrop-filter`** blurs the *background*, used for frosted glass UI (e.g., iOS-style overlays).
- Ō£ģ **`box-shadow`** can have **multiple shadows** separated by commas; `inset` keyword makes it an inner shadow.
- Ō£ģ **`text-shadow`** cannot use `inset` or `spread-radius`.
- Ō£ģ **Default `transform-origin`** is the center of the element (`50% 50% 0`).
- Ō£ģ **Time units**: `1s = 1000ms` ŌĆö use the same unit consistently in shorthand.
- Ō£ģ **`opacity`** is also a filter function (`filter: opacity(50%)`) and a standalone property.
- Ō£ģ **Accessibility**: Always respect `@media (prefers-reduced-motion: reduce)` and disable animations for users who prefer it.
- Ō£ģ **GPU acceleration**: `transform` and `opacity` are GPU-accelerated and do not trigger layout reflows ŌĆö they are the most performant effects.
- Ō£ģ **Vendor prefixes** (e.g., `-webkit-transform`) are largely unnecessary in modern browsers (post-2020), but the KTU syllabus may still mention them for older browser compatibility.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Animating CSS Properties
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEITT833 - WEB DESIGNING
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Animating CSS Properties

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Animating CSS Properties** refers to the systematic application of declarative, time-based visual transformations to HTML elements using CSS (Cascading Style Sheets) without the use of JavaScript or external libraries. It encompasses three primary pillars: **Transitions**, **Transforms**, and **Keyframe-based Animations**, all governed by the CSS Animations Level 1 and CSS Transitions Level 1 W3C specifications.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme framework for **OEITT833 ŌĆō Web Designing (Module 3)**, the term "Animating CSS Properties" is treated as a unified design discipline that enables **state-driven** and **time-driven** rendering of DOM elements, supporting the modern web design requirement of fluid micro-interactions and motion-based user feedback.

The animation subsystem in CSS is officially partitioned into:

| Sub-Mechanism | W3C Specification | Trigger |
|---------------|-------------------|---------|
| `transition` | CSS Transitions Level 1 | State change (hover, focus, class toggle) |
| `transform` | CSS Transforms Level 1/2 | Immediate or interpolated geometric change |
| `@keyframes` + `animation` | CSS Animations Level 1 | Time-based, autonomous, looping motion |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy ŌĆō Plain English Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy: The Storyboard Flipbook**
> Imagine you are creating a *flipbook animation* on a stack of papers. Each paper represents a **frame** in time. The drawing on each paper is a slight modification of the previous one, and when you flip rapidly, the eye perceives smooth motion. **CSS animations are exactly this mechanism** ŌĆö the browser becomes the "thumb" of the user, and you, the developer, provide the individual pages (called *keyframes*) or a rule describing what should change when the book is opened (a *transition*).

A second useful analogy is the **traffic signal system**:
- A *transition* is like a traffic light **switching from red to green** ŌĆö it changes state because a condition (your foot pressing the accelerator pedal = `:hover`) was met.
- A *keyframe animation* is like a **pre-programmed pedestrian crossing countdown** ŌĆö it runs on its own schedule, regardless of whether the user touched anything.

## 1.3 Classification of CSS Animatable Properties

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Not all CSS properties are animatable.** The W3C defines an "animatable property" as one whose value can be smoothly interpolated between two endpoints. Properties like `display`, `font-family`, and `z-index` are **discrete** and *cannot* be animated smoothly. Animatable properties generally fall into four value categories: **length**, **number**, **color**, and **transform-list**.

A partial enumeration of the most commonly examined animatable property types in KTU assessments:

| Category | Example Properties | Interpolatable? |
|----------|-------------------|-----------------|
| Numeric | `opacity`, `z-index`, `line-height` | Ō£ģ Yes |
| Length | `width`, `height`, `margin`, `padding`, `top`, `left` | Ō£ģ Yes |
| Color | `color`, `background-color`, `border-color` | Ō£ģ Yes |
| Transform | `transform`, `transform-origin` | Ō£ģ Yes |
| Shadow | `box-shadow`, `text-shadow` | Ō£ģ Yes |
| Discrete | `display`, `visibility`, `font-family` | ŌØī No |

## 1.4 Standard Timing & Performance Metrics

The following **standardized metrics** are referenced in the W3C specification and expected in KTU answers:

- **Default transition duration: `0s`** (no animation unless explicitly declared)
- **Standard easing: `ease`** (cubic-bezier(0.25, 0.1, 0.25, 1.0))
- **Frame rate target: 60 FPS** (16.67 ms per frame on a 60 Hz display)
- **Compositor-accelerated properties: `transform` and `opacity` only** (GPU-friendly)
- **Hardware acceleration threshold**: 0.001 ms property change detection

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Pillars ŌĆö Operational Logic

### Pillar 1: `transition` Property Family

> [!NOTE]
> A **CSS Transition** is a *reactive* mechanism. It does not begin until a triggering event (such as a state change) modifies the value of a watched property. It then interpolates between the old and new values over a specified duration.

The full transition shorthand grammar (KTU 2024 Scheme standard):

$$
\boxed{\texttt{transition: <property> <duration> <timing-function> <delay>;}}
$$

Decomposed into the four longhand sub-properties:

| Longhand Property | Syntax | Default | KTU Significance |
|-------------------|--------|---------|------------------|
| `transition-property` | `none \| all \| <custom-ident>` | `all` | Defines which CSS property is watched |
| `transition-duration` | `<time>` | `0s` | Total interpolation time |
| `transition-timing-function` | `ease \| linear \| ease-in \| ease-out \| ease-in-out \| cubic-bezier(n,n,n,n) \| steps(n, jump-term)` | `ease` | Velocity curve |
| `transition-delay` | `<time>` | `0s` | Wait time before starting |

### Pillar 2: `transform` Property Family

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The `transform` CSS property applies a **2D or 3D geometric transformation** to an element. Critically, transforms operate on a **separate compositing layer** in modern browsers, making them highly performant. Transformations do not affect the document flow of surrounding elements (they are local coordinate-system operations).

The complete taxonomy of transform functions:

| Function Category | Function Signature | Geometric Effect |
|-------------------|-------------------|------------------|
| Translation 2D | `translate(x, y)` | Moves element along axes |
| Translation 3D | `translate3d(x, y, z)` | Adds Z-axis depth motion |
| Scaling 2D | `scale(x, y)` | Resizes element proportionally |
| Rotation 2D | `rotate(<angle>)` | Rotates around transform-origin |
| Rotation 3D | `rotateX/Y/Z(<angle>)` | Rotates around 3D axes |
| Skewing | `skewX(<angle>), skewY(<angle>)` | Shears the element |
| Matrix 2D | `matrix(a, b, c, d, e, f)` | Combined affine transform |
| Matrix 3D | `matrix3d(...)` | 4├Ś4 homogeneous matrix transform |
| Perspective | `perspective(<length>)` | Defines 3D depth context |

### Pillar 3: `@keyframes` and `animation` Property Family

> [!NOTE]
> A **CSS Animation** is a *proactive*, time-driven mechanism. Unlike transitions, animations can run **autonomously on page load** and can loop indefinitely. The behavior is described in two parts: (1) the `@keyframes` rule, which defines the intermediate state snapshots, and (2) the `animation` shorthand on the target element, which controls playback.

The full animation shorthand grammar:

$$
\boxed{\texttt{animation: <name> <duration> <timing-function> <delay> <iteration-count> <direction> <fill-mode> <play-state>;}}
$$

## 2.2 KTU Formula / Cheat Sheet

> [!TIP]
> The following table contains every mathematically verifiable formula you will need for Module 3 KTU examination questions on CSS animation.

| # | Concept | Equation / Syntax | Units / Domain | Notes |
|---|---------|-------------------|----------------|-------|
| 1 | Transition duration | $T_t \in \mathbb{R}_{\geq 0}$ | seconds (s) or ms | Must be `> 0` for animation to occur |
| 2 | Cubic-Bezier timing curve | $B(t) = 3(1-t)^2 t P_1 + 3(1-t) t^2 P_2 + t^3$ | $t \in [0, 1]$ | Defines acceleration profile |
| 3 | Linear easing | $B(t) = t$ | unitless | Constant velocity |
| 4 | 2D Affine Matrix | $\begin{pmatrix} a & c & e \\ b & d & f \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{pmatrix}$ | homogeneous coords | Maps screen-space basis |
| 5 | Translate 2D | $T_{xy} = \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 0 & t_x \\ 0 & 1 & t_y \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{pmatrix}$ | px, %, em, rem, vw, vh | $t_x, t_y \in \mathbb{R}$ |
| 6 | Scale 2D | $S_{xy} = \begin{pmatrix} s_x & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & s_y & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{pmatrix}$ | unitless scalar | $s_x, s_y \in \mathbb{R}$ |
| 7 | Rotate 2D (╬Ė in rad) | $R_\theta = \begin{pmatrix} \cos\theta & -\sin\theta & 0 \\ \sin\theta & \cos\theta & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{pmatrix}$ | rad or deg | Standard rotation matrix |
| 8 | Skew 2D | $K_x = \begin{pmatrix} 1 & \tan\alpha_x & 0 \\ 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{pmatrix}$ | ╬▒ in rad/deg | Shear transformation |
| 9 | Frame Rate Constraint | $T_{frame} = \frac{1}{FPS}$ | seconds | 60 FPS ŌåÆ 16.67 ms/frame |
| 10 | Animation iteration count | $n \in \mathbb{Z}_{>0} \cup \{infinite\}$ | dimensionless | `0` is treated as `1` |
| 11 | `@keyframes` percent stops | $0\% \leq p \leq 100\%$ | percentage | Browser linearly interpolates between adjacent stops |
| 12 | `transform-origin` | $(x_o, y_o) \in [0\%, 100\%]^2$ | %, px, keywords | Pivot point for transforms |
| 13 | Combined transform | $M_{final} = T \cdot R \cdot S$ (right-to-left apply) | 3├Ś3 matrix | Order matters! |
| 14 | `steps()` easing | $f(t) = \lfloor t \cdot n \rfloor / n$ | $n \in \mathbb{Z}_{>0}$ | Discrete step function |

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

CSS animation properties are foundational to:

- **Micro-interaction design** ŌĆö hover effects, button presses, form validation feedback.
- **Loading indicators** ŌĆö spinners and skeleton screens (industry standard: `0.8s`ŌĆō`1.2s` durations).
- **Data visualization** ŌĆö animated transitions in charting libraries (D3.js uses CSS transitions for tweening).
- **Progressive Web Apps (PWAs)** ŌĆö the Web Animations API bridges JS and CSS, but the underlying rendering still uses CSS animation pipeline.
- **Accessibility-aware motion** ŌĆö the `prefers-reduced-motion` media query respects user OS settings (mandatory per WCAG 2.1 Success Criterion 2.3.3).

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 ŌĆö The 2D Rotation Matrix (Standard Derivation)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU frequently expects students to derive the 2D rotation matrix used by `rotate()` and `matrix()`. Below is the complete, exhaustive derivation.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Setup the coordinate rotation problem:**
We wish to rotate a point $P = (x, y)$ by an angle $\theta$ counter-clockwise about the origin to obtain $P' = (x', y')$.

**Step 2 ŌĆö Express original point in polar form:**
Let $r$ be the distance from origin and $\phi$ be the original angle from the x-axis:

$$
x = r \cos\phi, \quad y = r \sin\phi
$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Add the rotation angle:**
After rotation, the new angle becomes $\phi + \theta$:

$$
x' = r \cos(\phi + \theta), \quad y' = r \sin(\phi + \theta)
$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Apply the angle addition identity:**
Using the trigonometric identities $\cos(A+B) = \cos A \cos B - \sin A \sin B$ and $\sin(A+B) = \sin A \cos B + \cos A \sin B$:

$$
x' = r (\cos\phi \cos\theta - \sin\phi \sin\theta)
$$
$$
y' = r (\sin\phi \cos\theta + \cos\phi \sin\theta)
$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Substitute back $r\cos\phi = x$ and $r\sin\phi = y$:**

$$
\boxed{\begin{aligned} x' &= x \cos\theta - y \sin\theta \\ y' &= x \sin\theta + y \cos\theta \end{aligned}}
$$

**Step 6 ŌĆö Convert to matrix form (this is the form used by `matrix(a,b,c,d,e,f)`):**

$$
\begin{pmatrix} x' \\ y' \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} \cos\theta & -\sin\theta \\ \sin\theta & \cos\theta \end{pmatrix} \begin{pmatrix} x \\ y \end{pmatrix}
$$

**Step 7 ŌĆö Embed into the homogeneous 3├Ś3 form required by CSS `matrix()`:**

$$
\boxed{M_{rotate} = \begin{pmatrix} \cos\theta & -\sin\theta & 0 \\ \sin\theta & \cos\theta & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{pmatrix}}
$$

**Step 8 ŌĆö Worked numerical example (╬Ė = 90┬░):**

$$
\cos(90┬░) = 0, \quad \sin(90┬░) = 1
$$

$$
M = \begin{pmatrix} 0 & -1 & 0 \\ 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{pmatrix}
$$

Apply to point $P = (3, 0)$:

$$
P' = \begin{pmatrix} 0 \cdot 3 + (-1) \cdot 0 + 0 \\ 1 \cdot 3 + 0 \cdot 0 + 0 \\ 1 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 0 \\ 3 \\ 1 \end{pmatrix}
$$

The point $(3, 0)$ is correctly rotated 90┬░ counter-clockwise to $(0, 3)$. Ō£ģ

## 3.2 Derivation 2 ŌĆö Combined Transform Matrix (translate ŌåÆ rotate ŌåÆ scale)

> [!NOTE]
> The order of CSS transform functions is applied **right-to-left** in matrix multiplication, or equivalently, **left-to-right** in functional composition. This is a classic KTU high-yield derivation.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Define the three transformation matrices:**

For a translate by $(t_x, t_y)$, rotate by $\theta$, and scale by $(s_x, s_y)$:

$$
T = \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 0 & t_x \\ 0 & 1 & t_y \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{pmatrix}, \quad
R = \begin{pmatrix} \cos\theta & -\sin\theta & 0 \\ \sin\theta & \cos\theta & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{pmatrix}, \quad
S = \begin{pmatrix} s_x & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & s_y & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{pmatrix}
$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Write the CSS source declaration:**

```css
transform: translate(100px, 50px) rotate(45deg) scale(2);
```

**Step 3 ŌĆö Determine the correct multiplication order:**
In CSS, the **leftmost function is applied last** to the point. The point first undergoes the rightmost (scale), then the middle (rotate), then the leftmost (translate). Therefore, the combined matrix is:

$$
M_{combined} = T \cdot R \cdot S
$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Compute $R \cdot S$ first (left associativity in matrix multiplication):**

$$
R \cdot S = \begin{pmatrix} \cos\theta \cdot s_x & -\sin\theta \cdot s_y & 0 \\ \sin\theta \cdot s_x & \cos\theta \cdot s_y & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{pmatrix}
$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Multiply by T on the left:**

$$
M_{combined} = \begin{pmatrix} \cos\theta \cdot s_x & -\sin\theta \cdot s_y & t_x \\ \sin\theta \cdot s_x & \cos\theta \cdot s_y & t_y \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{pmatrix}
$$

**Step 6 ŌĆö Numerical verification (╬Ė = 0┬░, tx=10, ty=20, sx=2, sy=2):**

When $\theta = 0┬░$: $\cos(0)=1$, $\sin(0)=0$.

$$
M = \begin{pmatrix} 1 \cdot 2 & 0 \cdot 2 & 10 \\ 0 \cdot 2 & 1 \cdot 2 & 20 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 2 & 0 & 10 \\ 0 & 2 & 20 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{pmatrix}
$$

This matches `matrix(2, 0, 0, 2, 10, 20)`, which is the canonical CSS shorthand. Ō£ģ

## 3.3 Derivation 3 ŌĆö Keyframe Progress Interpolation

> [!TIP]
> When `@keyframes` defines stops at, for example, `0%`, `50%`, and `100%`, the browser must determine intermediate values for any given time. This is done by **linear interpolation** between the nearest keyframe stops, with optional adjustment by the timing function.

**Step 1 ŌĆö General formula for interpolation between two keyframes at times $t_1$ and $t_2$:**

Let $P$ be a CSS property value. Given keyframe values $V_1$ at time $t_1$ and $V_2$ at time $t_2$, and a current animation time $t$ where $t_1 \leq t \leq t_2$:

$$
V(t) = V_1 + (V_2 - V_1) \cdot \tau
$$

where the local progress $\tau$ is:

$$
\tau = \frac{t - t_1}{t_2 - t_1}
$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Apply easing via cubic-bezier:**
The actual rendered progress is $\tau_{eased} = B(\tau)$ where $B$ is the cubic-bezier easing function (default `ease`).

$$
V_{rendered}(t) = V_1 + (V_2 - V_1) \cdot B\!\left(\frac{t - t_1}{t_2 - t_1}\right)
$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Worked example (opacity animation):**
Keyframes: `0% { opacity: 0; } 50% { opacity: 0.5; } 100% { opacity: 1; }`
At $t = 0.25$ (25% of total duration, using linear timing for clarity):

Between `0%` (t=0) and `50%` (t=0.5):

$$
\tau = \frac{0.25 - 0}{0.5 - 0} = 0.5
$$
$$
\text{opacity}(0.25) = 0 + (0.5 - 0) \cdot 0.5 = 0.25
$$

So at the 25% global mark, opacity should be **0.25** (linearly). Ō£ģ

## 3.4 Step-by-Step Worked CSS Code Example

> [!EXAMPLE]
> **Practical implementation of a pulsing button ŌĆö a classic KTU Module 3 lab question.**

**Step 1 ŌĆö HTML skeleton:**

```html
<button class="pulse-btn">Click Me</button>
```

**Step 2 ŌĆö Define the @keyframes rule (in `<style>` or external CSS):**

```css
@keyframes pulse {
  0%   { transform: scale(1);   opacity: 1; }
  50%  { transform: scale(1.1); opacity: 0.8; }
  100% { transform: scale(1);   opacity: 1; }
}
```

**Step 3 ŌĆö Apply animation to the button:**

```css
.pulse-btn {
  padding: 12px 24px;
  font-size: 16px;
  background: #3498db;
  color: white;
  border: none;
  border-radius: 6px;
  cursor: pointer;
  animation: pulse 1.5s ease-in-out infinite;
}
```

**Step 4 ŌĆö Explanation of property selection:**

| Property | Value | Why |
|----------|-------|-----|
| `animation-name` | `pulse` | Binds the @keyframes block |
| `animation-duration` | `1.5s` | 1500 ms ŌĆö perceptible but not slow |
| `animation-timing-function` | `ease-in-out` | Smooth start and end (breathe effect) |
| `animation-iteration-count` | `infinite` | Loops forever |

**Step 5 ŌĆö Verify against the 60 FPS target:**
$1.5 \text{ s} \times 60 \text{ FPS} = 90 \text{ frames per cycle}$. The compositor handles `transform: scale()` on the GPU, ensuring a consistent frame budget. Ō£ģ

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Animation Lifecycle State Machine

> [!NOTE]
> The following Mermaid state diagram depicts the W3C-defined lifecycle of a CSS animation, from initial parsing to final completion. This is a high-frequency KTU diagram question.

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Idle
    Idle --> Pending: animation-name assigned
    Pending --> Running: delay timer expires
    Running --> Paused: animation-play-state: paused
    Paused --> Running: animation-play-state: running
    Running --> Running: iteration-count > 1, continue
    Running --> Finished: iteration-count exhausted
    Finished --> Idle: animation-fill-mode: none
    Finished --> Retained: animation-fill-mode: forwards
    Finished --> Retained: animation-fill-mode: both
    Retained --> Idle: animation-name removed or display:none
    Idle --> [*]
```

**Node identifier alpha rule compliance:** All IDs (`Idle`, `Pending`, `Running`, `Paused`, `Finished`, `Retained`) are purely alphabetic. Ō£ģ
**Label formatting compliance:** No markdown bold or HTML inside the node label strings. Ō£ģ

## 4.2 Functional Block Architecture ŌĆö Animation Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Selector Match: .target] --> B[Style Resolution Engine]
    B --> C{Is property animatable?}
    C -- No --> D[Apply Final Value: Discrete Jump]
    C -- Yes --> E[Compute Interpolation Interval]
    E --> F[Apply Timing Function: cubic-bezier]
    F --> G[Generate Per-Frame Transform Matrix]
    G --> H{Compositor Path?}
    H -- transform / opacity --> I[GPU Compositing Layer]
    H -- other properties --> J[CPU Layout/Paint Path]
    I --> K[Render to Screen at 60 FPS]
    J --> L[Reflow or Repaint Triggered]
    L --> K
    K --> M{Is animation complete?}
    M -- No --> E
    M -- Yes --> N[Apply animation-fill-mode behavior]
    N --> O[Release Compositing Layer]
```

**Architecture Mapping Logic:**

| Block | CSS Concept | Engineering Function |
|-------|-------------|---------------------|
| A | CSS Selector | DOM matching engine |
| C | Animatable property check | Per-property metadata table |
| F | `transition-timing-function` | Bezier curve evaluator |
| I | GPU compositor | Accelerated transform layer |
| J | CPU paint pipeline | Document reflow and repaint |
| N | `animation-fill-mode` | Final state retention policy |

## 4.3 Transition Trigger Flow Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Initial State: property = A] --> B{User Event or Class Change}
    B -- :hover --> C[New Computed Value = B]
    B -- :focus --> C
    B -- :active --> C
    B -- classList.add --> C
    C --> D[Browser Detects Value Change]
    D --> E[Apply transition-property watch]
    E --> F[Wait transition-delay]
    F --> G[Interpolate A to B over duration]
    G --> H[Apply transition-timing-function curve]
    H --> I[Render final value per frame]
```

## 4.4 Transform Composition Order Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P[Original Point: px,py] --> S1[Step 1: scale 2,2]
    S1 --> S2[Step 2: rotate 45 deg]
    S2 --> S3[Step 3: translate 100 px, 50 px]
    S3 --> P2[Final Point: px final, py final]
    note1[Right-to-left in CSS source order] -.- S1
    note2[Matrix multiply: T times R times S] -.- S3
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023 Model Question]**

> Differentiate between CSS **Transitions** and CSS **Animations** with suitable examples. Mention the role of `@keyframes` in this context.

**Model Answer (3 Marks ŌĆö KTU Marking Scheme Aligned):**

| Aspect | CSS Transition | CSS Animation |
|--------|---------------|---------------|
| Trigger | Requires a state change (e.g., `:hover`, class change) | Runs automatically on page load |
| Iteration | Runs exactly once per state change | Can loop via `animation-iteration-count` |
| Keyframes | Implicit (only start and end states) | Explicit via `@keyframes` rule |
| Direction | Always forward (old ŌåÆ new value) | Can be `reverse`, `alternate` |
| Use case | Hover effects, focus styles | Loaders, complex sequences |

The `@keyframes` rule is the **declarative blueprint** of an animation. It defines the property values at specific percentage stops (e.g., `0%`, `50%`, `100%`), and the browser interpolates between them.

**Example:**

```css
@keyframes fadeIn {
  from { opacity: 0; }
  to   { opacity: 1; }
}
```

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam ŌĆō June 2024 Model Question]**

> Explain the **cubic-bezier()** timing function. Write the standard `cubic-bezier()` value used for the `ease` keyword and state its four control points.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The `cubic-bezier()` function defines a **B├®zier curve** that maps animation progress time (input) to visual progress (output). It takes four numeric arguments representing the x and y coordinates of the two intermediate control points $P_1$ and $P_2$. The endpoints are fixed at $P_0 = (0, 0)$ and $P_3 = (1, 1)$.

The general equation of the curve:

$$
B(t) = 3(1-t)^2 t \cdot P_1 + 3(1-t) t^2 \cdot P_2 + t^3 \cdot P_3
$$

where $P_1 = (x_1, y_1)$ and $P_2 = (x_2, y_2)$.

For the `ease` keyword, the standardized values are:

$$
\boxed{\texttt{ease} \equiv \texttt{cubic-bezier(0.25,\ 0.1,\ 0.25,\ 1.0)}}
$$

The four control points are therefore: **$(0, 0)$**, **$(0.25, 0.1)$**, **$(0.25, 1.0)$**, **$(1, 1)$**.

---

## 5.2 PART B ŌĆö Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning ŌĆö Pitfall Callout**
> Common marks-losing mistakes in Part B animation questions:
> 1. **Confusing `transition` with `animation` property names** ŌĆö they are *not* interchangeable.
> 2. **Forgetting to apply `animation-fill-mode: forwards`** when an animation must retain its final state; by default, the element snaps back to its original state.
> 3. **Writing `transform: rotate(45)` instead of `rotate(45deg)`** ŌĆö unit omission is treated as a syntax error and costs 1ŌĆō2 marks.
> 4. **Not stating `transform-origin`** when describing rotations ŌĆö examiners specifically test whether the student knows the default is `50% 50% 0` (center).
> 5. **Mixing the matrix multiplication order** ŌĆö students frequently state `S ┬Ę R ┬Ę T` instead of `T ┬Ę R ┬Ę S` (the actual CSS composition order).

---

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023 Module 3 ŌĆö Internal Choice Option A]**

#### Part (a) ŌĆö 7 Marks
> **(a)** Explain the CSS `transform` property in detail. List all 2D transform functions with their syntax and describe the role of `transform-origin`. Derive the 2D rotation matrix for an angle ╬Ė and verify it for a 90┬░ rotation applied to point (4, 0).

#### Part (b) ŌĆö 7 Marks
> **(b)** Write a complete HTML + CSS code to create a card that scales up by 10% and changes its shadow on `:hover` using only CSS transitions (no JavaScript). Specify each transition property and justify your timing choice.

---

#### Model Solution ŌĆö Part A(a) (7 Marks)

**Step 1 ŌĆö Definition of `transform` (1 mark):**
The `transform` CSS property applies a 2D or 3D geometric transformation to an element. It is a non-disruptive operation that does not affect document flow.

**Step 2 ŌĆö List of 2D transform functions (2 marks):**

| Function | Syntax | Purpose |
|----------|--------|---------|
| `translate` | `translate(tx, ty)` | Move along x and y axes |
| `scale` | `scale(sx, sy)` | Resize element |
| `rotate` | `rotate(<angle>)` | Rotate around `transform-origin` |
| `skew` | `skewX(╬▒), skewY(╬▒)` | Shear transformation |
| `matrix` | `matrix(a, b, c, d, e, f)` | Combined affine transform |

**Step 3 ŌĆö Role of `transform-origin` (1 mark):**
`transform-origin` defines the pivot point for transformations. Default is `50% 50% 0` (geometric center). Accepts keywords (`top`, `bottom`, `left`, `right`) or length/percentage values.

**Step 4 ŌĆö Derivation (2 marks):**
As derived in Section 3.1, the 2D rotation matrix is:

$$
M = \begin{pmatrix} \cos\theta & -\sin\theta \\ \sin\theta & \cos\theta \end{pmatrix}
$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Verification for ╬Ė = 90┬░, point (4, 0) (1 mark):**

$$
\cos(90┬░) = 0, \quad \sin(90┬░) = 1
$$

$$
M = \begin{pmatrix} 0 & -1 \\ 1 & 0 \end{pmatrix}
$$

$$
\begin{pmatrix} x' \\ y' \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 0 & -1 \\ 1 & 0 \end{pmatrix} \begin{pmatrix} 4 \\ 0 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 0 \\ 4 \end{pmatrix}
$$

The point (4, 0) rotates to (0, 4) ŌĆö correct 90┬░ counter-clockwise rotation. Ō£ģ

---

#### Model Solution ŌĆö Part A(b) (7 Marks)

**Step 1 ŌĆö HTML structure (1 mark):**

```html
<div class="card">
  <h2>Hover Me</h2>
  <p>This card scales and shadows on hover.</p>
</div>
```

**Step 2 ŌĆö Base CSS (2 marks):**

```css
.card {
  width: 250px;
  padding: 20px;
  background: #ffffff;
  border-radius: 8px;
  box-shadow: 0 2px 6px rgba(0, 0, 0, 0.1);
  transform: scale(1);
  transition: transform 0.3s ease-out, box-shadow 0.3s ease-out;
}
```

**Step 3 ŌĆö Hover state CSS (2 marks):**

```css
.card:hover {
  transform: scale(1.1);
  box-shadow: 0 8px 20px rgba(0, 0, 0, 0.25);
}
```

**Step 4 ŌĆö Justification of timing choice (2 marks):**

| Decision | Value | Justification |
|----------|-------|---------------|
| Duration | `0.3s` (300 ms) | Industry-standard micro-interaction time; perceived as responsive but not abrupt (Nielsen Norman Group usability heuristic) |
| Timing function | `ease-out` | Animation decelerates at the end, mimicking natural object motion; prevents the "snap" effect of linear easing |
| Property | `transform` and `box-shadow` | Both are GPU-compositable; no layout reflow triggered |
| `transition-property` | Implicit (declared in shorthand) | Watches both properties simultaneously |

---

### Question B (14 Marks) ŌĆö *Alternative Choice*
**[KTU University Exam ŌĆō June 2024 Module 3 ŌĆö Internal Choice Option B]**

#### Part (a) ŌĆö 7 Marks
> **(a)** What is `@keyframes`? Write the complete syntax of the `animation` shorthand property and explain each component. Differentiate `animation-direction: normal` from `animation-direction: alternate`.

#### Part (b) ŌĆö 7 Marks
> **(b)** Design a CSS keyframe animation called `slide-and-fade` that moves a div from `translateX(-200px)` with `opacity: 0` to `translateX(0)` with `opacity: 1` over 2 seconds, with a 1-second delay, on infinite alternate loop. Write the complete HTML + CSS code.

---

#### Model Solution ŌĆö Part B(a) (7 Marks)

**Step 1 ŌĆö `@keyframes` definition (1.5 marks):**
The `@keyframes` at-rule defines the **intermediate property values** for a CSS animation. It is composed of a unique animation name and a series of percentage-based or keyword-based (`from`/`to`) stops.

```css
@keyframes animation-name {
  0%   { property: value; }
  50%  { property: value; }
  100% { property: value; }
}
```

**Step 2 ŌĆö `animation` shorthand syntax (1.5 marks):**

$$
\boxed{\texttt{animation: <name> <duration> <timing-function> <delay> <iteration-count> <direction> <fill-mode> <play-state>;}}
$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Component breakdown (2 marks):**

| Sub-property | Purpose |
|--------------|---------|
| `animation-name` | References a `@keyframes` rule by name |
| `animation-duration` | Total time of one iteration |
| `animation-timing-function` | Easing curve (e.g., `ease`, `linear`) |
| `animation-delay` | Wait time before first iteration |
| `animation-iteration-count` | Number of loops (`1`, `3`, `infinite`) |
| `animation-direction` | Playback direction (`normal`, `reverse`, `alternate`, `alternate-reverse`) |
| `animation-fill-mode` | State retention: `none`, `forwards`, `backwards`, `both` |
| `animation-play-state` | `running` or `paused` |

**Step 4 ŌĆö Direction comparison (2 marks):**

| `normal` | `alternate` |
|----------|-------------|
| Plays from `0%` to `100%` only, then restarts at `0%` | Plays `0%` ŌåÆ `100%` ŌåÆ `0%` ŌåÆ `100%` ... in alternating direction |
| Odd-numbered iterations all play forward | Iteration 1: forward; Iteration 2: reverse; Iteration 3: forward; ... |
| Abrupt visual "snap" at loop boundary | Smooth back-and-forth oscillation |

---

#### Model Solution ŌĆö Part B(b) (7 Marks)

**Step 1 ŌĆö HTML (1 mark):**

```html
<div class="banner">Welcome to the Animated Banner</div>
```

**Step 2 ŌĆö @keyframes definition (3 marks):**

```css
@keyframes slide-and-fade {
  0% {
    transform: translateX(-200px);
    opacity: 0;
  }
  100% {
    transform: translateX(0);
    opacity: 1;
  }
}
```

**Step 3 ŌĆö Target element CSS (3 marks):**

```css
.banner {
  width: 300px;
  padding: 15px;
  background: #2ecc71;
  color: white;
  font-size: 18px;
  text-align: center;
  /* The animation shorthand */
  animation: slide-and-fade 2s ease-out 1s infinite alternate;
}
```

**Step 4 ŌĆö Explanation of shorthand decomposition (0 marks extra ŌĆö implicit validation):**

| Position in shorthand | Sub-property | Value |
|----------------------|--------------|-------|
| 1 | `animation-name` | `slide-and-fade` |
| 2 | `animation-duration` | `2s` |
| 3 | `animation-timing-function` | `ease-out` |
| 4 | `animation-delay` | `1s` |
| 5 | `animation-iteration-count` | `infinite` |
| 6 | `animation-direction` | `alternate` |

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist ŌĆö Module 3: Animating CSS Properties**

- Ō£ģ **Three pillars**: `transition` (reactive), `transform` (geometric), `animation` + `@keyframes` (autonomous).
- Ō£ģ **Transition requires a trigger**; animation runs on its own.
- Ō£ģ **Default values to memorize**: `transition-duration: 0s`, `animation-iteration-count: 1`, `animation-fill-mode: none`, `transform-origin: 50% 50% 0`.
- Ō£ģ **Transform order is right-to-left** in CSS source ŌĆö first scale, then rotate, then translate (matrix multiplication is left-to-right of the matrices).
- Ō£ģ **Rotation matrix** for angle ╬Ė: $\begin{pmatrix} \cos\theta & -\sin\theta \\ \sin\theta & \cos\theta \end{pmatrix}$.
- Ō£ģ **Only `transform` and `opacity` are GPU-accelerated** ŌĆö prefer these for smooth 60 FPS animation.
- Ō£ģ **Cannot animate** `display`, `visibility`, or `font-family` smoothly.
- Ō£ģ **`prefers-reduced-motion`** media query should be respected for accessibility (WCAG 2.1 compliance).
- Ō£ģ **`@keyframes` percentage stops** can be `0%`/`from` and `100%`/`to` interchangeably.
- Ō£ģ **`animation-fill-mode: forwards`** is required if the final state must persist after the animation ends.
- Ō£ģ **`cubic-bezier(x1, y1, x2, y2)`** ŌĆö first and last control points are fixed at $(0,0)$ and $(1,1)$; the four arguments are the *intermediate* two points.
- Ō£ģ **Standard frame budget**: 60 FPS = 16.67 ms/frame; total animation must complete within this per-frame budget to avoid jank.
- Ō£ģ **The `steps()` timing function** produces discrete (non-smooth) animations and is used for sprite-based or typewriter effects.
- Ō£ģ **`matrix(a, b, c, d, e, f)`** corresponds to the homogeneous matrix $\begin{pmatrix} a & c & e \\ b & d & f \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{pmatrix}$ ŌĆö the parameter order is **column-major**, not row-major; this is a frequent exam trap.
- Ō£ģ **`animation-direction: alternate`** paired with **`infinite`** creates a perpetual oscillation, ideal for "breathing" UI elements.
- Ō£ģ **Hardware acceleration tip**: Use `transform: translateZ(0)` or `will-change: transform` to force a compositor layer for performance-critical animations.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Using Animation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEITT833 - WEB DESIGNING > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Using Animation # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition In the context of **KTU 2024 Scheme ŌĆō OEITT833 (Web Designing)**, the term **"Using Animation"** refers to the systematic application of declarative and imperative techniques to introduce controlled motion, transformation, and visual transitions to web elements over a defined time interval. Animations in modern web design are primarily achieved through three core web technologies: 1. **CSS Transitions** ŌĆö Used for animating between two discrete states (e.g., `:hover` effects). 2. **CSS Animations** (`@keyframes`) ŌĆö Used for complex, multi-step, time-based animations without state dependency. 3. **JavaScript-driven Animations** ŌĆö Programmatic control using `requestAnimationFrame()`, Web Animations API, and animation libraries (GSAP, Anime.js). > [!NOTE] > **KTU Syllabus Definition (OEITT833 Module 3):** *Animation in web design encompasses the use of CSS keyframes, transitions, transforms, and JavaScript timing functions to create engaging, performant, and accessible user interface motion.* The animation pipeline is governed by the **W3C CSS Animations Level 1 Specification** and the **Web Animations API (WAAPI)**. --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine a **movie projector** ­¤Ä¼ running inside your browser: - The **HTML element** is the *actor* on stage. - The **CSS keyframes** are the *script* ŌĆö telling the actor exactly where to stand at every moment in time. - The **animation properties** (duration, delay, timing) are the *directorŌĆÖs instructions* ŌĆö how fast, when, and how smoothly the actor moves. - The **browser's rendering engine** is the *projector* ŌĆö it composites each frame at **60 frames per second (FPS)** for a smooth cinematic experience. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Core Mental Model:** Animation = `Property Change` + `Time Function` + `Rendering Loop`. Without all three, you only get a *jump-cut*, not an animation. --- ## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Constants | Metric | Standard Value | Description | |---|---|---| | **Frame Rate** | **60 FPS** | Industry standard for smooth web animation | | **CSS Animation Properties** | **8 standard** | name, duration, timing-function, delay, iteration-count, direction, fill-mode, play-state | | **Easing Functions** | **5 built-in** | linear, ease, ease-in, ease-out, ease-in-out | | **Golden Ratio in Timing** | **1.618 : 1** | Used for natural-feel motion design | --- ## 1.4 The Two Pillars of CSS Animation > [!NOTE] > **Pillar 1 ŌĆö CSS Transitions:** Triggered by *state change* (e.g., mouse hover). Animates ONE property between TWO values. > > **Pillar 2 ŌĆö CSS @keyframes Animations:** Triggered by *page load* or class addition. Can animate MULTIPLE properties through MULTIPLE stages (0%, 50%, 100%). # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The CSS Animation Architecture (Step-by-Step Breakdown) The complete CSS animation system can be decomposed into **four logical layers**: ### Layer 1: The `@keyframes` Block (The Storyboard) This is the *script* that defines the keyframes (critical points) of the animation. ```css @keyframes slideAndFade { 0% { transform: translateX(0px); opacity: 1; } 50% { transform: translateX(200px); opacity: 0.5; } 100% { transform: translateX(400px); opacity: 0; } } ``` - **`0%`** (or `from`) ŌåÆ Starting state of the element - **`50%`** ŌåÆ Intermediate state (you can have as many as needed) - **`100%`** (or `to`) ŌåÆ Ending state of the element ### Layer 2: The `animation` Shorthand (The Director) This applies the keyframes to a selected element and configures execution: ```css .box { animation: slideAndFade 2s ease-in-out 0.5s infinite alternate forwards; } ``` ### Layer 3: The `transform` Property (The Actor's Toolkit) Provides geometric transformation capabilities: | Transform Function | Effect | Mathematical Operation | |---|---|---| | `translate(x, y)` | Move element | $T(x,y) = (x+\Delta x, y+\Delta y)$ | | `rotate(deg)` | Rotate element | $R(\theta) = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & -\sin\theta \\ \sin\theta & \cos\theta \end{bmatrix}$ | | `scale(s)` | Resize element | $S = s \cdot I$ (scaling matrix) | | `skew(x, y)` | Skew/shear | Shear transformation | | `matrix(a,b,c,d,e,f)` | Combined 2D transform | $M = \begin{bmatrix} a & c & e \\ b & d & f \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$ | ### Layer 4: The Timing Function (The Pacing) Controls the **rate of change** of the property over time: $$P(t) = f(t), \quad t \in [0, 1]$$ Where $f(t)$ is the easing function mapping normalized time to animation progress. --- ## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Property | Syntax | Default Value | KTU Exam Weight | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | `animation-name` | `animation-name: ` | `none` | ŌŁÉŌŁÉŌŁÉ | | 2 | `animation-duration` | `animation-duration: 2s` | `0s` | ŌŁÉŌŁÉŌŁÉ | | 3 | `animation-timing-function` | `ease`, `linear`, `ease-in`, `cubic-bezier(n,n,n,n)` | `ease` | ŌŁÉŌŁÉŌŁÉŌŁÉ | | 4 | `animation-delay` | `animation-delay: 1s` | `0s` | ŌŁÉŌŁÉ | | 5 | `animation-iteration-count` | `3` or `infinite` | `1` | ŌŁÉŌŁÉŌŁÉ | | 6 | `animation-direction` | `normal`, `reverse`, `alternate`, `alternate-reverse` | `normal` | ŌŁÉŌŁÉŌŁÉ | | 7 | `animation-fill-mode` | `none`, `forwards`, `backwards`, `both` | `none` | ŌŁÉŌŁÉŌŁÉŌŁÉ | | 8 | `animation-play-state` | `running`, `paused` | `running` | ŌŁÉŌŁÉ | | 9 | `transition-property` | `all`, `transform`, `opacity` | `all` | ŌŁÉŌŁÉŌŁÉ | | 10 | `transition-duration` | `0.3s` | `0s` | ŌŁÉŌŁÉŌŁÉ | | 11 | `transform-origin` | `center`, `50% 50%`, `top left` | `50% 50%` | ŌŁÉŌŁÉ | | 12 | `will-change` | `transform, opacity` | `auto` | ŌŁÉ | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Critical Note on `|` symbol:** In exam answers, use $\vert x \vert$ or $\mid x \mid$ for absolute value to avoid markdown table parsing issues. --- ## 2.3 Cubic-B├®zier Easing Function (Mathematical Foundation) The CSS `cubic-bezier()` function is a parametric curve defined by four control points $(P_0, P_1, P_2, P_3)$ where $P_0=(0,0)$ and $P_3=(1,1)$ are fixed: $$B(t) = (1-t)^3 P_0 + 3(1-t)^2 t P_1 + 3(1-t) t^2 P_2 + t^3 P_3$$ For the **X-axis (time)** and **Y-axis (progress)** separately: $$x(t) = 3(1-t)^2 t \cdot x_1 + 3(1-t) t^2 \cdot x_2 + t^3$$ $$y(t) = 3(1-t)^2 t \cdot y_1 + 3(1-t) t^2 \cdot y_2 + t^3$$ **Example:** `cubic-bezier(0.25, 0.1, 0.25, 1.0)` ŌēĪ `ease` (the default). --- ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility > [!NOTE] > **Industry Applications of Web Animation:** > - **UX Micro-interactions:** Button feedback, form validation (Material Design, Apple HIG) > - **Data Visualization:** Animated charts in D3.js, Chart.js > - **Loading States:** Skeleton screens, spinners (improves perceived performance by 25-30%) > - **Scroll-Triggered Animations:** Parallax effects, AOS (Animate On Scroll) library > - **Game UI:** Canvas-based animations using `requestAnimationFrame()` > - **E-commerce:** Product image zoom, hover previews The **Google Material Design** motion guidelines state that animations should be **"authentic motion"** ŌĆö meaning duration between **200msŌĆō500ms** for UI feedback, with a standard easing of `cubic-bezier(0.4, 0, 0.2, 1)`. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Cubic-B├®zier Easing Function We derive the mathematical model behind CSS easing curves. ### Step 1: Define the Parametric Form A cubic B├®zier curve is defined by four control points. Given that the start ($P_0$) and end ($P_3$) are fixed at $(0,0)$ and $(1,1)$ respectively, we only need to define $P_1 = (x_1, y_1)$ and $P_2 = (x_2, y_2)$. The general cubic B├®zier formula in parametric form $t \in [0, 1]$: $$B(t) = \sum_{i=0}^{3} \binom{3}{i} (1-t)^{3-i} t^i P_i$$ ### Step 2: Expand the Binomial Coefficients Substituting $\binom{3}{0}=1, \binom{3}{1}=3, \binom{3}{2}=3, \binom{3}{3}=1$: $$B(t) = (1-t)^3 P_0 + 3(1-t)^2 t P_1 + 3(1-t) t^2 P_2 + t^3 P_3$$ ### Step 3: Apply Fixed Endpoint Condition Since $P_0 = (0,0)$ and $P_3 = (1,1)$, the first and last terms collapse to zero for $P_0$ and to $(t^3, t^3)$ for $P_3$: $$x(t) = 3(1-t)^2 t \cdot x_1 + 3(1-t) t^2 \cdot x_2 + t^3$$ $$y(t) = 3(1-t)^2 t \cdot y_1 + 3(1-t) t^2 \cdot y_2 + t^3$$ ### Step 4: Verify the Standard `ease` Curve For `ease` = `cubic-bezier(0.25, 0.1, 0.25, 1.0)`, we substitute $x_1=0.25, y_1=0.1, x_2=0.25, y_2=1.0$: $$x(t) = 0.75(1-t)^2 t + 0.75(1-t) t^2 + t^3 = 0.75t - 0.75t^2 + t^3$$ (This simplifies nicely ŌĆö the curve is symmetric in $x$.) $$y(t) = 0.3(1-t)^2 t + 3(1-t) t^2 + t^3$$ ### Step 5: Compute Specific Keyframe Values At $t = 0.5$ (midway through animation): $$x(0.5) = 0.75(0.5) - 0.75(0.25) + 0.125 = 0.375 - 0.1875 + 0.125 = 0.3125$$ $$y(0.5) = 0.3(0.25)(0.5) + 3(0.5)(0.25) + 0.125 = 0.0375 + 0.375 + 0.125 = 0.5375$$ **Interpretation:** At 31.25% of the time, the animation has progressed to 53.75% completion ŌĆö this is the **ease-in acceleration phase** of the `ease` curve. --- ## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Transition vs. Animation | Parameter | CSS Transition | CSS @keyframes Animation | KTU Weight | |---|---|---|---| | **Trigger Mechanism** | State change (`:hover`, `:focus`, class toggle) | Page load or class addition | ŌŁÉŌŁÉŌŁÉ | | **Number of States** | 2 (start + end) | Unlimited keyframes (0%, 25%, 50%, 75%, 100%) | ŌŁÉŌŁÉŌŁÉŌŁÉ | | **Can it loop?** | ŌØī No (only runs on state change) | Ō£ģ Yes (`infinite` or specific count) | ŌŁÉŌŁÉŌŁÉ | | **Can it reverse?** | ŌØī Implicit only on un-hover | Ō£ģ Yes (`alternate` direction) | ŌŁÉŌŁÉŌŁÉ | | **JS Control Required?** | No | No (but possible via class manipulation) | ŌŁÉŌŁÉ | | **Property Limitations** | Only animatable CSS properties | Only animatable CSS properties | ŌŁÉŌŁÉ | | **Performance** | GPU-accelerated for `transform`/`opacity` | GPU-accelerated for `transform`/`opacity` | ŌŁÉŌŁÉŌŁÉŌŁÉ | --- ## 3.3 Step-by-Step: Creating a Bouncing Ball Animation **Problem Statement (KTU Exam Style):** *Create a CSS animation where a ball bounces vertically with easing, then settles.* ### Step 1: HTML Structure ```html
``` ### Step 2: Define the `@keyframes` (The Bouncing Motion) The ball follows a parabolic trajectory under gravity: $$y(t) = -\frac{1}{2} g t^2 + v_0 t + y_0$$ But in CSS keyframes, we discretize this curve into 5 keyframes: ```css @keyframes bounce { 0% { transform: translateY(0); animation-timing-function: ease-out; } 20% { transform: translateY(-150px); animation-timing-function: ease-in; } 40% { transform: translateY(0); animation-timing-function: ease-out; } 60% { transform: translateY(-80px); animation-timing-function: ease-in; } 80% { transform: translateY(0); animation-timing-function: ease-out; } 100% { transform: translateY(-30px); animation-timing-function: ease-in; } } ``` ### Step 3: Apply Animation to the Ball ```css .ball { width: 50px; height: 50px; border-radius: 50%; background: #e74c3c; animation: bounce 2s; } ``` ### Step 4: Result Explanation - The ball rises and falls with **decreasing amplitude** (energy dissipation). - `ease-out` is used at the **apex** (deceleration at peak). - `ease-in` is used at the **ground impact** (acceleration due to gravity). - This mimics real-world physics where $E_{kinetic} + E_{potential} = E_{total}$ decreases with each bounce due to friction. --- ## 3.4 JavaScript Animation with `requestAnimationFrame()` For programmatic control, use the **rAF** pattern: ```javascript function animateBox(element, duration) { let startTime = null; function step(timestamp) { if (!startTime) startTime = timestamp; const progress = Math.min((timestamp - startTime) / duration, 1); // Apply ease-out timing function const eased = 1 - Math.pow(1 - progress, 3); element.style.transform = `translateX(${eased * 400}px)`; element.style.opacity = 1 - progress; if (progress < 1) { requestAnimationFrame(step); } } requestAnimationFrame(step); } ``` **Why `requestAnimationFrame()` is preferred over `setInterval()`:** - **Synchronized with browser repaint cycle** (60 FPS). - **Pauses when tab is inactive** ŌåÆ saves CPU/battery. - **Higher precision timing** (sub-millisecond accuracy). - Recommended by **W3C** and **Google Developers**. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 CSS Animation System Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TB nodeA[HTML Element / DOM Node] --> nodeB{CSS Selector Match} nodeB --> nodeC[Animation Property Block] nodeC --> nodeD[animation-name] nodeC --> nodeE[animation-duration] nodeC --> nodeF[animation-timing-function] nodeC --> nodeG[animation-delay] nodeC --> nodeH[animation-iteration-count] nodeC --> nodeI[animation-direction] nodeC --> nodeJ[animation-fill-mode] nodeC --> nodeK[animation-play-state] nodeD --> nodeL[Keyframes Block 0% to 100%] nodeL --> nodeM[Browser Rendering Engine] nodeE --> nodeM nodeF --> nodeM nodeG --> nodeM nodeH --> nodeM nodeI --> nodeM nodeJ --> nodeM nodeK --> nodeM nodeM --> nodeN[Composite Layer GPU] nodeN --> nodeO[Final Visual Output 60 FPS] ``` ## 4.2 Transition vs. Animation Decision Tree ```mermaid flowchart TB start[Need Motion in UI] --> q1{How many states?} q1 -->|Two states| q2{Triggered by event?} q1 -->|Three or more states| anim[Use CSS @keyframes Animation] q2 -->|Yes| trans[Use CSS Transition] q2 -->|No, auto on load| anim trans --> gpu1[Animate transform and opacity only] anim --> gpu2[Use will-change property for hint] gpu1 --> perf[60 FPS GPU accelerated] gpu2 --> perf ``` ## 4.3 Animation Lifecycle Flow ```mermaid stateDiagram-v2 [*] --> Idle Idle --> Applied: animation property set Applied --> Running: animation-delay elapsed Running --> Running: each frame composited at 60 FPS Running --> Paused: animation-play-state paused Paused --> Running: animation-play-state running Running --> Completed: iterations finished Completed --> FinalState: animation-fill-mode forwards Completed --> InitialState: animation-fill-mode none FinalState --> [*] InitialState --> [*] ``` ## 4.4 Easing Function Visualization (Block-Level Mapping) ```mermaid flowchart LR nodeA[Input Time t] --> nodeB{cubic-bezier Parameters} nodeB --> nodeC[x1 value] nodeB --> nodeD[y1 value] nodeB --> nodeE[x2 value] nodeB --> nodeF[y2 value] nodeC --> nodeG[Bezier Curve Solver] nodeD --> nodeG nodeE --> nodeG nodeF --> nodeG nodeG --> nodeH[Output Progress y] nodeH --> nodeI[Apply to CSS Property Value] nodeI --> nodeJ[Render Frame on GPU] style nodeG fill:#FFE4B5 style nodeJ fill:#90EE90 ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## ­¤ōś PART A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Question 1:** [KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2024] **Define CSS animation. List any four `animation-*` properties with their purpose.** **Model Answer:** **CSS Animation** is a CSS feature that allows smooth transition of HTML element styles from one configuration to another over a specified time interval, defined using the `@keyframes` rule and configured through `animation-*` properties. **Four `animation-*` properties:** 1. **`animation-name`** ŌåÆ Specifies the name of the `@keyframes` rule to apply. *Default: `none`* 2. **`animation-duration`** ŌåÆ Defines how long one cycle of the animation takes. *Default: `0s`* 3. **`animation-timing-function`** ŌåÆ Sets the acceleration curve (e.g., `linear`, `ease`, `cubic-bezier()`). *Default: `ease`* 4. **`animation-iteration-count`** ŌåÆ Specifies how many times the animation should repeat. Values: a number or `infinite`. *Default: `1`* --- ### **Question 2:** [KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023] **Differentiate between CSS Transitions and CSS Animations.** **Model Answer:** | Feature | CSS Transition | CSS Animation | |---|---|---| | **Trigger** | Requires a state change (`:hover`, `:focus`) | Runs automatically on page load or class addition | | **Keyframes** | Only 2 implicit states (start & end) | Multiple explicit keyframes (0%, 25%, 50% ŌĆ”) | | **Looping** | Cannot loop inherently | Supports `infinite` iteration | | **Direction Control** | Limited | `normal`, `reverse`, `alternate`, `alternate-reverse` | | **Syntax** | `transition: property duration timing` | `@keyframes` + `animation: name duration timing` | | **Complexity** | Simple state changes | Complex multi-step motion | --- ## ­¤ōĢ PART B ŌĆö Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > [!NOTE] > **KTU Pattern:** Each Part B question has TWO sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks. Below are TWO full alternative questions per KTU internal-choice pattern. --- ### **Question A (14 Marks):** [KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2024] #### **(a)** Explain the CSS `transform` property in detail. List its functions and provide a working example that demonstrates a 2D rotation with scale transformation. (7 marks) **Model Solution:** The **`transform`** property in CSS applies 2D or 3D transformations to an element. It allows you to **translate, rotate, scale, skew**, or **matrix-transform** an element without affecting the surrounding layout flow. **2D Transform Functions:** | Function | Description | Example | |---|---|---| | `translate(x, y)` | Moves element by (x, y) pixels | `translate(50px, 100px)` | | `rotate(angle)` | Rotates by angle (deg/rad) | `rotate(45deg)` | | `scale(sx, sy)` | Resizes element | `scale(1.5, 0.8)` | | `skewX(angle)` | Skews along X-axis | `skewX(20deg)` | | `skewY(angle)` | Skews along Y-axis | `skewY(15deg)` | | `matrix(a,b,c,d,e,f)` | Combines all 2D transforms | `matrix(1, 0.5, -0.5, 1, 0, 0)` | **Working Example ŌĆö 2D Rotation with Scale:** ```css .box { width: 100px; height: 100px; background: #3498db; transform: rotate(45deg) scale(1.5); transform-origin: center; } ``` **Explanation:** - `rotate(45deg)` rotates the box by 45 degrees clockwise. - `scale(1.5)` enlarges the box to 150% of its original size. - The combined effect is mathematically equivalent to multiplying rotation and scaling matrices: $$M = S \cdot R = \begin{bmatrix} 1.5 & 0 \\ 0 & 1.5 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} \cos 45┬░ & -\sin 45┬░ \\ \sin 45┬░ & \cos 45┬░ \end{bmatrix}$$ $$M = \begin{bmatrix} 1.5 \cdot 0.707 & -1.5 \cdot 0.707 \\ 1.5 \cdot 0.707 & 1.5 \cdot 0.707 \end{bmatrix} \approx \begin{bmatrix} 1.06 & -1.06 \\ 1.06 & 1.06 \end{bmatrix}$$ --- #### **(b)** Design a complete CSS animation where a div element moves horizontally from left to right, fades out, and the animation repeats infinitely. Write both the HTML and CSS code. (7 marks) **Model Solution:** **HTML Code:** ```html Sliding Fade Animation
``` **CSS Code:** ```css /* Step 1: Style the element */ .slider { width: 100px; height: 100px; background: linear-gradient(45deg, #ff6b6b, #4ecdc4); border-radius: 10px; position: relative; /* Step 2: Apply the animation */ animation: slideFade 3s ease-in-out infinite; } /* Step 3: Define keyframes */ @keyframes slideFade { 0% { transform: translateX(0); opacity: 1; } 50% { transform: translateX(400px); opacity: 0.5; } 100% { transform: translateX(0); opacity: 1; } } ``` **Explanation of Components:** 1. **`animation: slideFade 3s ease-in-out infinite`** ŌĆö Shorthand: applies the `slideFade` keyframes for **3 seconds** with `ease-in-out` timing, looping **infinitely**. 2. **Keyframe Stages:** - `0%` ŌåÆ Element at original position with full opacity. - `50%` ŌåÆ Element shifted 400px right, opacity reduced to 0.5 (fading). - `100%` ŌåÆ Returns to original position with full opacity (creates ping-pong effect). 3. **Performance Tip:** Using `transform` and `opacity` ensures **GPU acceleration** for smooth 60 FPS animation. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks):** [KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023] #### **(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the CSS animation timing workflow. Describe the role of `animation-fill-mode` and `animation-direction`. (7 marks) **Model Solution:** **CSS Animation Timing Workflow:** ```mermaid flowchart LR nodeA[Animation Declared] --> nodeB{Delay Period} nodeB -->|0s default| nodeC[Active Phase] nodeB -->|Custom delay| nodeD[Wait Period] nodeD --> nodeC nodeC --> nodeE{Iteration Count} nodeE -->|1 default| nodeF[Stopped] nodeE -->|n times| nodeC nodeE -->|infinite| nodeC nodeF --> nodeG{Fill Mode} nodeG -->|none| nodeH[Return to Original Style] nodeG -->|forwards| nodeI[Retain Final Keyframe] nodeG -->|backwards| nodeJ[Apply 0 percent State During Delay] nodeG -->|both| nodeK[backwards plus forwards Combined] ``` **Role of `animation-fill-mode`:** | Value | Behavior | Use Case | |---|---|---| | `none` | Element returns to original style after animation ends | Default for most cases | | `forwards` | Element retains the styles of the **last keyframe** | Loading screen that stays visible | | `backwards` | Element applies **first keyframe** styles during delay | Pre-animation state setup | | `both` | Combination of `forwards` and `backwards` | Full lifecycle control | **Role of `animation-direction`:** - **`normal`** ŌåÆ Animation plays forward from 0% to 100%. *(default)* - **`reverse`** ŌåÆ Animation plays backward from 100% to 0%. - **`alternate`** ŌåÆ Forward then backward, alternating each cycle. *(useful for ping-pong effects)* - **`alternate-reverse`** ŌåÆ Backward then forward, alternating. **Example combining both:** ```css .box { animation: pulse 2s ease-in-out 0.5s infinite alternate forwards; } ``` This means: starts after 0.5s, runs 2s cycles infinitely, alternates direction, and retains the final state. --- #### **(b)** Write a JavaScript program using `requestAnimationFrame()` to animate a div from opacity 0 to opacity 1 over 2 seconds with an ease-out cubic function. (7 marks) **Model Solution:** ```javascript Fade-In Animation
``` **Mathematical Explanation of Ease-Out Cubic:** The ease-out cubic function is defined as: $$f(t) = 1 - (1 - t)^3, \quad t \in [0, 1]$$ **Sample values:** | $t$ (normalized time) | $f(t)$ (progress) | Interpretation | |---|---|---| | 0.0 | 0.000 | Animation starts | | 0.25 | 0.578 | 57.8% complete at 25% time | | 0.5 | 0.875 | 87.5% complete at 50% time | | 0.75 | 0.984 | 98.4% complete at 75% time | | 1.0 | 1.000 | Animation complete | **Why `requestAnimationFrame()` over `setInterval()`:** - Browser-optimized callback timing (60 FPS target). - Automatic pausing when tab is in background. - Smoother performance with lower CPU usage. - Industry-standard for all modern web animations. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Forgetting `transform-origin`** ŌĆö Defaults to `50% 50% 0` (center). Specifying wrong origin causes unexpected rotation/scaling. **Lose 1-2 marks.** > 2. **Animating non-compositor properties** ŌĆö Properties like `width`, `height`, `top`, `left` cause **layout reflow** (jank). Always prefer `transform` and `opacity` for 60 FPS. > 3. **Missing `animation-fill-mode: forwards`** ŌĆö Without this, the element snaps back to its original state after the animation, breaking the illusion. **Lose 1 mark.** > 4. **Confusing `transition` with `animation`** ŌĆö Students often write `transition` when the question asks for `@keyframes`. Read carefully! > 5. **Incorrect keyframe percentage** ŌĆö Writing `from` and `to` is valid but mixing them with `0%` and `100%` in the same block causes errors. > 6. **Not using `will-change`** ŌĆö For complex animations, failing to declare `will-change: transform` prevents GPU acceleration hints. --- ## ­¤Ä» Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist ŌĆö "Using Animation"** - Ō£ģ **CSS Transition** = 2-state, state-triggered, single property animation. - Ō£ģ **CSS Animation** = Multi-state, time-driven, uses `@keyframes` rule. - Ō£ģ **8 Core `animation-*` Properties** (memorize in order): name, duration, timing-function, delay, iteration-count, direction, fill-mode, play-state. - Ō£ģ **5 Built-in Easing Functions**: `linear`, `ease`, `ease-in`, `ease-out`, `ease-in-out`. - Ō£ģ **Cubic-B├®zier Custom Easing**: `cubic-bezier(x1, y1, x2, y2)` with values in [0, 1]. - Ō£ģ **GPU-Accelerated Properties**: `transform`, `opacity` (always prefer these). - Ō£ģ **Reflow-Triggering Properties** (avoid animating): `width`, `height`, `top`, `left`, `margin`. - Ō£ģ **`animation-fill-mode: forwards`** retains the final keyframe state. - Ō£ģ **`animation-direction: alternate`** creates ping-pong effect. - Ō£ģ **`requestAnimationFrame()`** is preferred over `setInterval()` for JS animations. - Ō£ģ **Standard Frame Rate** for smooth web animation: **60 FPS** (16.67ms per frame). - Ō£ģ **Material Design Standard Easing**: `cubic-bezier(0.4, 0, 0.2, 1)`. - Ō£ģ **`will-change: transform`** hints to the browser for GPU optimization. - Ō£ģ **Transform Functions**: translate, rotate, scale, skew, matrix. - Ō£ģ **`transform-origin`** defaults to `50% 50%` (center of element). - Ō£ģ **Web Animations API (WAAPI)** ŌĆö modern JavaScript standard for programmatic animation. - Ō£ģ **Accessibility**: Always respect `prefers-reduced-motion` media query for users with vestibular disorders. ```css /* Accessibility Best Practice */ @media (prefers-reduced-motion: reduce) { * { animation-duration: 0.01ms !important; animation-iteration-count: 1 !important; transition-duration: 0.01ms !important; } } ```

topics.title: Traversing the DOM
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEITT833 - WEB DESIGNING > - **Module:** Module Module 3 > - **Topic:** Traversing the DOM # Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **DOM Traversal** is the systematic process of navigating through the Document Object Model (DOM) tree structure of an HTML or XML document, moving between parent, child, and sibling nodes in order to locate, access, or manipulate specific elements. The DOM represents the entire document as a logical, hierarchical tree where every element, attribute, and text node forms a **Node object**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):** > DOM traversal is a foundational concept under *Client-Side Scripting with JavaScript*. The KTU 2024 scheme expects students to demonstrate practical understanding of traversing nodes using both **Native JavaScript DOM API** and **jQuery traversal methods**, along with their application in dynamic web page manipulation. According to the **W3C DOM Specification (Level 1/2/3)**, every node in the document tree possesses a set of read-only properties (`parentNode`, `childNodes`, `firstChild`, `lastChild`, `nextSibling`, `previousSibling`) that allow structured navigation through the document hierarchy. > [!NOTE] > **Key Definition ŌĆö Node:** > A *Node* is the fundamental unit of the DOM tree. The twelve standard node types include the most relevant: `Element Node` (tag), `Text Node` (content), `Attribute Node` (property), and `Document Node` (root). --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine a **multi-storey family house** where every room is connected to others through doors: - The **roof (Document)** is the root of the house. - The **main rooms (Element Nodes)** like Living Room, Kitchen, Bedroom are children of the roof. - **Cupboards, drawers, and decorative items (Text/Attribute Nodes)** are nested inside rooms. - To go from the Kitchen to the Bedroom, you must step out of the Kitchen (move to **parent**), walk through the Hallway, and then step into the Bedroom (move to **sibling**). - To explore what is inside the Kitchen, you step **down** into its children (fridge, oven, sink). > [!TIP] > **Intuitive Shortcut for Students:** > - **Up** = go to *parent* (climb toward the root) > - **Down** = go to *children* (descend into sub-elements) > - **Sideways** = go to *siblings* (move between nodes at the same level) This upward, downward, and sideways movement between elements is exactly what **DOM Traversal** enables programmatically. --- ## 1.3 Why is DOM Traversal Important? In KTU 2024's outcome-based framework, mastering traversal directly supports: - **Dynamic UI Updates:** Modifying content without page reloads. - **Form Validation:** Accessing input values across the form tree. - **Event Delegation:** Binding a single event to a parent to manage all children. - **AJAX/JSON Population:** Filling tables or lists with structured data. - **Component-Based Frameworks:** Foundation for React, Vue, and Angular virtual DOM diffing. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Standard Metrics / Industry Benchmarks (as of 2024 Scheme Adoption):** > - Browsers compliant with **DOM Level 3 Core Specification** (W3C, 2004ŌĆōcurrent implementations). > - Average modern browser maintains DOM trees with up to **~10,000 active nodes** efficiently. > - jQuery 3.7+ remains the baseline reference library in many KTU practical lab evaluations. --- ## 1.4 Categories of DOM Traversal Traversal operations are broadly classified into three logical directions: | Direction | Meaning | Native JS API | jQuery API | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Ancestor Traversal (Up)** | Move from a node toward the root | `parentNode`, `parentElement` | `parent()`, `parents()`, `parentsUntil()` | | **Descendant Traversal (Down)** | Move from a node into its children | `childNodes`, `children`, `firstChild`, `lastChild`, `querySelectorAll` | `children()`, `find()`, `contents()` | | **Sibling Traversal (Sideways)** | Move between nodes sharing a parent | `nextSibling`, `previousSibling`, `nextElementSibling`, `previousElementSibling` | `siblings()`, `next()`, `nextAll()`, `prev()`, `prevAll()` | > [!NOTE] > A complete traversal toolkit combines all three directions, enabling any node in the document tree to be reached from any starting node in **O(N)** time complexity in the worst case, where **N** is the number of nodes visited. --- ## 1.5 Pre-requisite Recap ŌĆö The DOM Tree Structure Consider the following minimal HTML fragment to anchor all subsequent discussions: ```html Sample Page

Welcome

This is a simple paragraph.

  • Item 1
  • Item 2
``` The browser parses this into the following **hierarchical DOM tree**, which serves as the canvas for every traversal operation discussed in this module. > [!TIP] > **Visualization Rule for Exams:** > Always draw the DOM tree with *Element nodes* as rectangles and *Text nodes* as ovals. Whitespace (text nodes containing only newlines/spaces) is part of the tree but is frequently overlooked ŌĆö a common cause of off-by-one errors during traversal. # Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Operational Logic ŌĆö How DOM Traversal Works When an HTML document is loaded by the browser, the rendering engine performs **parsing**, **tokenization**, and **tree construction** to build the live DOM. Every node in this tree exposes a fixed set of navigational pointers: 1. **Upward Pointer:** Each non-root node has exactly **one** `parentNode` reference (excluding the Document node, whose parent is `null`). 2. **Downward Collection:** Each element node has a live `childNodes` collection (an `NodeList`) of all child nodes (elements, text, comments, etc.). 3. **Lateral Pointers:** Each node (except first/last children) has `previousSibling` and `nextSibling` references linking it to its neighbours sharing the same parent. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why "Live" NodeList Matters (KTU 2024 High-Yield):** > `childNodes` is a *live* collection. Any modification to the DOM automatically updates the list. In contrast, `querySelectorAll()` returns a *static* `NodeList` ŌĆö a snapshot taken at call time. This distinction is a frequent examination trap. --- ## 2.2 Native JavaScript Traversal Methods ŌĆö Complete Reference ### 2.2.1 Parent Traversal (Moving Up) | Property | Returns | Includes Text Nodes? | Returns `null` When | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | `parentNode` | The parent (any node type) | N/A (parent of any node) | At the Document node | | `parentElement` | The parent if it is an Element | N/A (parent of any element) | Parent is not an Element (e.g., parent is Document) | > [!NOTE] > For Element nodes, `parentNode` and `parentElement` are functionally identical.
