          Username:

          Password:

          ``` **Explanation of key steps (2 marks):** - `e.preventDefault()` stops the browser's default form submission. - `.trim()` removes leading/trailing whitespace to catch passwords containing only spaces. - Returning `false` from the handler cancels the submission, ensuring the page does not navigate. - The `$(".err").text("")` line clears stale messages from prior failed attempts. --- ### **Question B `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 ŌĆö Set B]`** (14 Marks) **(a)** Explain the `$.ajax()` function in jQuery with its commonly used options. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Write a jQuery + Ajax program to fetch user records from a JSON file `students.json` and display them in an HTML table. The table must have columns: *Roll No, Name, and Department*. **(7 Marks)** --- #### Model Solution for Question B **Part (a) ŌĆö The `$.ajax()` Function (7 Marks):** The `$.ajax()` function is the **most powerful and configurable** Ajax method in jQuery. It accepts a single configuration object whose properties define the entire request lifecycle. **Signature (1 mark):** ```javascript $.ajax(settings); ``` **Commonly Used Options (6 marks):** | Option | Type | Description | |---|---|---| | `url` | String | Target server endpoint (relative or absolute). | | `type` | String | HTTP method ŌĆö `GET`, `POST`, `PUT`, `DELETE`. Default: `GET`. | | `data` | Object/String | Data sent to the server; auto-converted to query string for GET. | | `dataType` | String | Expected response ŌĆö `json`, `html`, `xml`, `text`, `script`. | | `contentType` | String | MIME type of request body. Default: `application/x-www-form-urlencoded`. | | `success` | Function | Called on HTTP 2xx; receives parsed response. | | `error` | Function | Called on HTTP 4xx/5xx or timeout. | | `complete` | Function | Always called after success/error, useful for hiding loaders. | | `timeout` | Number | Milliseconds before the request is aborted. | | `async` | Boolean | `true` = asynchronous (default), `false` = synchronous (blocking). | | `beforeSend` | Function | Hook to modify XHR object (e.g., add auth headers). | | `cache` | Boolean | If `false`, appends `_={timestamp}` to URL to bust caching. | **Generic Skeleton (1 mark):** ```javascript $.ajax({ url: "server.php", type: "POST", data: { name: "Anand", age: 21 }, dataType: "json", success: function(res) { console.log(res); }, error: function(xhr, status, err) { console.error(err); } }); ``` **Part (b) ŌĆö Fetch and Display Students (7 Marks):** **`students.json`:** ```json [ { "roll": 1, "name": "Anand Krishnan", "dept": "CSE" }, { "roll": 2, "name": "Meera Pillai", "dept": "ECE" }, { "roll": 3, "name": "Rahul Menon", "dept": "MECH" } ] ``` **HTML + jQuery Solution:** ```html Student Records

          Student Directory

          Roll NoNameDepartment
          ``` **Key Concepts Demonstrated (2 marks):** - `dataType: "json"` triggers jQuery to auto-parse the response using `JSON.parse()`. - `$.each()` is jQuery's native iterator (analogous to `Array.prototype.forEach`). - `.empty()` is used instead of `.html("")` for a **marginal performance** and safety advantage. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls ŌĆö Where Students Lose Marks:** > 1. **Forgetting `e.preventDefault()`** in form handlers ŌåÆ page navigates away ŌåÆ entire Ajax logic never executes. **Penalty: full 7 marks.** > 2. **Writing `$.get(...)` without a `dataType` and then trying to access `.roll` on a string** ŌåÆ `undefined` errors. Always set `dataType: "json"`. > 3. **Using `.innerHTML = ...` on a jQuery object** ŌåÆ silently fails because jQuery objects are not raw DOM nodes. Use `.html()` or `.append()`. > 4. **Loading jQuery twice** (once via CDN and once locally) ŌåÆ the second overwrites `$`, leading to "undefined function" errors. > 5. **Not enclosing code inside `$(function(){...})`** ŌåÆ DOM not ready ŌåÆ selectors return empty sets. > 6. **Confusing `$.ajax` options with `XMLHttpRequest` properties** ŌĆö the `success` callback receives **parsed data**, not the raw `xhr` object. (Raw XHR is passed to the `error` callback as the first argument.) > 7. **Mistaking `type` for "DataType"** ŌĆö `type` is the HTTP verb, `dataType` is the response format. This is the **#1 most common KTU paper error**. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) - [x] **jQuery** is a client-side JavaScript library (`jquery.min.js` ~87 KB) that simplifies DOM, events, effects, and Ajax. - [x] The **alias `$` is interchangeable with `jQuery`** ŌĆö use `$` unless it conflicts with another library. - [x] Always wrap code inside **`$(function(){...})`** (shorthand for `$(document).ready()`). - [x] **Selectors** are CSS3-based: `$("p")`, `$("#id")`, `$(".class")`, `$("a, b")`, `$("parent > child")`. - [x] **DOM methods**: `.html()`, `.text()`, `.val()`, `.attr()`, `.css()`, `.addClass()`. - [x] **Effects**: `.show()`, `.hide()`, `.fadeIn()`, `.fadeOut()`, `.slideUp()`, `.animate()`. - [x] **Ajax** = Asynchronous JavaScript and XML ŌĆö pattern using `XMLHttpRequest` for background server calls. - [x] **`$.ajax(options)`** is the master method; `$.get`, `$.post`, `$.getJSON` are **shorthand wrappers**. - [x] The `success` callback fires only when **`readyState === 4` AND `status === 200`**. - [x] **`dataType: "json"`** auto-parses server response into a JavaScript object/array. - [x] Always pass an **anonymous function as the success callback**, never a string (deprecated and unsafe). - [x] **XHR `readyState` values:** 0=UNSENT, 1=OPENED, 2=HEADERS_RECEIVED, 3=LOADING, 4=DONE. - [x] jQuery can be loaded from a **CDN** (`https://code.jquery.com/jquery-3.7.1.min.js`) or locally. - [x] **jQuery 3.7.1** is the current stable version referenced in the KTU 2024 syllabus. - [x] Use **`$.each(collection, function(index, item){...})`** to iterate over arrays/objects. - [x] In KTU lab exams, the **standard question pattern** is: *fetch JSON and render into a table or list without page reload*. ---

topics.title: Introduction to XML: Syntax
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEITT833 - WEB DESIGNING > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Introduction to XML: Syntax ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition of XML **Extensible Markup Language (XML)** is a **W3C-recommended**, text-based, platform-independent data representation and interchange format that uses **user-defined tags** enclosed in angle brackets to structure, store, and transport data in a strictly hierarchical (tree-based) manner. Unlike HTML, which is presentation-oriented, XML is purely **semantic** ŌĆö its tags describe *what the data is*, not *how it should look*. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** XML is introduced as the foundational data-formatting language for the web, sitting alongside HTML in the Markup Languages family. Students must master the rules of **well-formedness** because the KTU board examiner awards marks specifically for the strict syntactic rules of XML. Mathematically, an XML document can be modeled as a **rooted, ordered, labeled tree**: $$ D = (V, E, r, \Sigma, L) $$ Where: - $V$ = set of **element nodes** (vertices) - $E$ = set of **parentŌĆōchild edges** - $r \in V$ = the **root element** (unique, exactly one) - $\Sigma$ = the **alphabet** of legal Unicode characters - $L : V \rightarrow \Sigma^{*}$ = a labeling function mapping each node to a tag name ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy ŌĆö The "Smart Notebook" Intuition Imagine you maintain a **lab record book** for your B.Tech experiments. Instead of writing plain sentences, you invent your own neat labels: ```xml Half-Wave Rectifier 15-March-2024 ``` Each opening tag `` is like a **box with a label**, and its closing tag `` is the **lid of that box**. Inside the box, you may place *smaller labeled boxes* (``, `<Date>`, `<Readings>`). Nested boxes must always close from the **inside out**, just like Russian dolls. > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy ŌĆö The Family Tree:** An XML document is identical in structure to a family tree. There is exactly **one ancestor (root)**, every person (element) has **one parent (except the root)**, and children appear in a **strict left-to-right order**. Just as a person cannot have two biological fathers, an element cannot have two parents in valid XML. ### 1.3 Why XML Was Invented ŌĆö The Engineering Need Before XML (1998), data exchange between heterogeneous systems relied on **proprietary binary formats** or loose, tag-deficient SGML constructs. The W3C designed XML to satisfy **10 core design goals**, the most important of which are: | Goal | Engineering Significance | |---|---| | **Straightforwardly usable over the Internet** | Compatible with HTTP, FTP, and modern transport protocols | | **Support a wide variety of applications** | Used in RSS, SOAP, Android UI, SVG, Microsoft Office (.docx) | | **Compatible with SGML** | A strict, simplified subset of the older SGML standard | | **Easily authored and processed** | Human-readable + parser-friendly | | **Human-legible and reasonably clear** | Improves debugging and team collaboration | > [!TIP] > **Examiner's Pattern Tip:** When the KTU question reads *"Differentiate HTML and XML"* or *"Why is XML called extensible?"*, the answer should explicitly mention **user-defined tags** and **data-vs-presentation separation**. --- <!-- SECTION_1_END --> <!-- SECTION_2_START --> ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Syntax Rule Sheet ### 2.1 Anatomy of an XML Document ŌĆö The 6 Logical Parts A complete, well-formed XML document is composed of **six optional-but-standardized logical parts**, which we will dissect from top to bottom: 1. **XML Declaration** (optional, but strongly recommended) 2. **Processing Instructions** (e.g., stylesheet link) 3. **Comments** 4. **Document Type Declaration (DTD)** ŌĆö optional 5. **Root Element** ŌĆö mandatory, exactly one 6. **Content** ŌĆö elements, text, attributes, entities, CDATA The high-level structure is: $$ \boxed{\text{XML Doc} = \text{Decl} \cup \text{PI}^{*} \cup \text{Comment}^{*} \cup \text{DTD}_{?} \cup \text{RootElement} \cup \text{Misc}^{*}} $$ ### 2.2 The 10 Cardinal Rules of XML Syntax (Board-Exam Gold) The KTU 2024 syllabus specifies that students must internalize the **well-formedness rules**. Violating any of these produces a document that browsers and parsers will **reject outright**. | # | Rule Name | Formal Statement | Common Mistake | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Single Root** | Exactly **one** root element must enclose all content | Forgetting to close `<html>` style | | 2 | **Case Sensitivity** | Tag names are **case-sensitive**: `<Name>` ŌēĀ `<name>` | Mixing `<Book>` and `<book>` | | 3 | **Proper Nesting** | Elements must be properly nested; no overlap allowed | `<b><i>text</b></i>` is illegal | | 4 | **Mandatory Closing Tags** | Every opening tag **must** have a matching closing tag | `<br>` without `</br>` | | 5 | **Quoted Attributes** | All attribute values **must** be enclosed in single OR double quotes | `<img src=pic.jpg/>` is illegal | | 6 | **Unique Attributes** | An element cannot have **two attributes with the same name** | `<img src="a" src="b"/>` | | 7 | **Reserved Character Escaping** | `<`, `>`, `&`, `'`, `"` in text must be escaped as **entities** | Writing `if a < b` in plain text | | 8 | **Whitespace Preservation** | Whitespace is **significant** in XML; HTML-style collapsing does **not** occur | Expecting multiple spaces to collapse | | 9 | **No Empty Bare Tags** | Self-closing tags must end with `/>` | Writing `<br>` alone | | 10 | **Properly Formed Declaration** | The XML declaration `<?xml version="1.0"?>` must be the very first line | A blank line before the declaration | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 High-Yield Distinction:** The board examiner frequently tests the difference between **HTML's lenient syntax** and **XML's strict syntax**. Remember: HTML allows unquoted attributes, unclosed tags, and case-insensitivity. XML forbids all three. ### 2.3 The XML Declaration ŌĆö The First Line The XML declaration is **not a processing instruction** (despite its `<?...?>` appearance). It has a fixed structure: ```xml <?xml version="1.0" encoding="UTF-8" standalone="yes"?> ``` The three attributes are: - **version** ŌĆö currently must be `"1.0"` (1.1 exists but is rarely used) - **encoding** ŌĆö declares the character encoding; default is **UTF-8** if omitted - **standalone** ŌĆö `yes` means no external DTD is required; `no` means external markup declarations may affect content ### 2.4 XML Elements ŌĆö The Building Blocks An element consists of: - An **opening tag**: `<tagname>` - **Content**: text, child elements, or a mix - A **closing tag**: `</tagname>` There are **four legal content models** an element can follow: $$ \text{Content} \in \{\text{Empty}, \text{Element-only}, \text{Text-only}, \text{Mixed}\} $$ 1. **Empty element** ŌĆö no content: `<line-break/>` or `<line-break></line-break>` 2. **Element-only** ŌĆö contains only child elements: `<ul><li/><li/></ul>` 3. **Text-only** ŌĆö contains only character data: `<name>Arya</name>` 4. **Mixed** ŌĆö contains both text and elements: `<p>This is <b>bold</b> text</p>` ### 2.5 XML Attributes ŌĆö The Metadata Sidecar Attributes appear **only inside opening tags** (or self-closing tags) and provide additional information *about* an element. They follow the format: $$ \text{Attribute} = \texttt{name} = \texttt{"value"} $$ **Example**: ```xml <book ISBN="978-0-13-468599-1" edition="2nd" available="true"> <title>The Pragmatic Programmer ``` ### 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet ŌĆö XML Syntax Quick Reference | Construct | Syntax Template | KTU Board Significance | |---|---|---| | XML Declaration | `` | First line, mandatory before content | | Comment | `` | Cannot contain `--` inside | | Opening Tag | `` | Lowercase recommended (convention) | | Closing Tag | `` | Must match opening case exactly | | Self-Closing | `` | Used for empty elements | | Attribute | `name="value"` | Always quoted | | Entity Ref | `&entity;` | Built-in: `<`, `>`, `&`, `'`, `"` | | Numeric Char Ref | `A` or `A` | Decimal `65` = `A`; hex `41` = `A` | | CDATA Section | `` | Raw text, no parsing inside | | PI | `` | e.g., `` | | Namespace Decl | `xmlns:prefix="URI"` | Disambiguates tag names | ### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility of XML Syntax | Domain | XML Application | |---|---| | **Web Services** | SOAP envelopes, WSDL descriptions | | **Mobile Development** | Android layout files (`.xml`), drawables, manifests | | **Document Formats** | Microsoft Office Open XML (`.docx`, `.xlsx`, `.pptx`) | | **Graphics** | SVG (Scalable Vector Graphics) | | **Data Feeds** | RSS, Atom, podcast feeds | | **Configuration** | Maven `pom.xml`, Spring `beans.xml`, Android `AndroidManifest.xml` | | **Scientific Data** | CML (Chemical Markup Language), MathML | --- ## 3. Step-by-Step XML Document Construction & Analytical Walkthrough ### 3.1 Worked Example ŌĆö Building a Complete Well-Formed XML Document **Problem:** Construct a well-formed XML document that stores information about a college library's book inventory, containing **at least one book** with title, author(s), ISBN, price, and publication year. **Step 1 ŌĆö Identify the root element.** A library contains multiple books, so the logical root is ``. This will be the **single, outermost container**. **Step 2 ŌĆö Identify the child element pattern.** Each book is represented as a `` element, and each `` contains metadata as either child elements or attributes. **Step 3 ŌĆö Decide between attributes vs. child elements.** The KTU examiner loves this question. The rule of thumb is: - Use **attributes** for *metadata about the element* (e.g., ISBN, language) - Use **child elements** for *data that may repeat or have sub-structure* (e.g., multiple authors) **Step 4 ŌĆö Compose the XML declaration.** ```xml ``` **Step 5 ŌĆö Add an optional comment and stylesheet processing instruction.** ```xml ``` **Step 6 ŌĆö Open the root element and add child `` elements.** ```xml The Pragmatic Programmer David Thomas Andrew Hunt 3500.00 Data Structures Using C Reema Thareja 425.00 ``` ### 3.2 Exhaustive Step-by-Step Validation Against the 10 Rules Let us now **formally validate** the document against the rule set: **Rule 1 ŌĆö Single Root Check:** The outermost element is ``. All other elements are nested inside it. Ō£ģ **PASS** **Rule 2 ŌĆö Case Sensitivity Check:** ``, ``, ``, `<author>`, `<name>`, `<price>` ŌĆö all consistently lowercase. Ō£ģ **PASS** **Rule 3 ŌĆö Proper Nesting Check:** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Open Order:} \quad & \texttt{<library>} \rightarrow \texttt{<book>} \rightarrow \texttt{<title>} \\ \text{Close Order:} \quad & \texttt{} \rightarrow \texttt{} \rightarrow \texttt{} \end{aligned} $$ This is a valid **LIFO (Last-In-First-Out)** stack discipline. Ō£ģ **PASS** **Rule 4 ŌĆö Mandatory Closing Tags Check:** Every opening tag has a matching closing tag. The `` element uses full closing tags, while the self-closing is not used here. Ō£ģ **PASS** **Rule 5 ŌĆö Quoted Attributes Check:** `ISBN="978-0-13-468599-1"`, `year="2019"`, `available="yes"` ŌĆö all values are double-quoted. Ō£ģ **PASS** **Rule 6 ŌĆö Unique Attributes Check:** Within ``, the attributes are `ISBN`, `year`, `available` ŌĆö all distinct. Ō£ģ **PASS** **Rule 7 ŌĆö Reserved Character Escaping Check:** Search the text for `<`, `>`, `&`, `'`, `"`. None appear in the text content. Ō£ģ **PASS** **Rule 8 ŌĆö Whitespace Preservation Check:** The indentation is consistent and the parser will preserve internal whitespace (unlike HTML). Ō£ģ **PASS** **Rule 9 ŌĆö Self-Closing Tags Check:** No improperly used self-closing tags. Ō£ģ **PASS** **Rule 10 ŌĆö Declaration Position Check:** The `` line is the absolute first line, with no preceding whitespace. Ō£ģ **PASS** ### 3.3 Analytical Derivation ŌĆö When to Use Entity References The five **predefined XML entities** are derived from the requirement that the **parser must be able to unambiguously distinguish markup from content**. The mathematical mapping is: $$ \begin{aligned} \texttt{<} &\;\longmapsto\; \texttt{\<} \quad \text{(less-than, ASCII 60)} \\ \texttt{>} &\;\longmapsto\; \texttt{\>} \quad \text{(greater-than, ASCII 62)} \\ \texttt{\&} &\;\longmapsto\; \texttt{\&} \quad \text{(ampersand, ASCII 38)} \\ \texttt{'} &\;\longmapsto\; \texttt{\'} \quad \text{(apostrophe, ASCII 39)} \\ \texttt{"} &\;\longmapsto\; \texttt{\"} \quad \text{(double-quote, ASCII 34)} \end{aligned} $$ **Worked Walkthrough ŌĆö Why this matters:** Suppose we want to write the equation $a < b$ inside an XML element: ```xml a < b ``` The parser reads `< b` and thinks a new tag is starting. Corrected: ```xml a < b ``` Numeric character references provide an **escape hatch** for any Unicode character. The decimal `65` is the ASCII code for `A`: $$ \texttt{\A} \;\equiv\; \texttt{"A"} $$ The hexadecimal form is also legal: $$ \texttt{\A} \;\equiv\; \texttt{"A"} $$ ### 3.4 CDATA Sections ŌĆö The "Verbatim" Block When an element must contain a large block containing many reserved characters (e.g., embedded HTML, source code), the **CDATA section** is the cleanest solution: ```xml d) { print("Hello "); } ]]> ``` The parser treats everything between `` as **pure character data** ŌĆö no markup recognition, no entity expansion. > [!NOTE] > **CDATA Rule:** A CDATA section **cannot** contain the literal string `]]>`, since that is its terminator. If you need to embed that sequence, you must split the CDATA block. --- ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 XML Document Tree ŌĆö Visual Block Architecture The following Mermaid block-level diagram maps the **hierarchical parse tree** of the library inventory XML document constructed in Section 3.1. It shows the parentŌĆōchild relationships between nodes, mirroring the exact structural rules defined earlier. ```mermaid graph TD decl["XML Declaration version 1.0 encoding UTF-8"] pi["Processing Instruction xml-stylesheet href library xsl"] comment["Comment Library Inventory Updated March 2024"] root["Root Element library name KTU Central Library location Trivandrum"] book1["Element book ISBN 978-0-13-468599-1 year 2019 available yes"] book2["Element book ISBN 978-81-203-5127-2 year 2017 available no"] title1["Element title Text The Pragmatic Programmer"] title2["Element title Text Data Structures Using C"] author1["Element author"] author2["Element author"] name1a["Element name Text David Thomas"] name1b["Element name Text Andrew Hunt"] name2a["Element name Text Reema Thareja"] price1["Element price currency INR Text 3500.00"] price2["Element price currency INR Text 425.00"] decl --> root pi --> root comment --> root root --> book1 root --> book2 book1 --> title1 book1 --> author1 book1 --> price1 book2 --> title2 book2 --> author2 book2 --> price2 author1 --> name1a author1 --> name1b author2 --> name2a ``` ### 4.2 XML Parsing Pipeline ŌĆö Block Functional Architecture The following diagram illustrates how an XML parser (SAX, DOM, or StAX) processes an input document, enforcing the 10 well-formedness rules at each phase. ```mermaid graph LR A["Input XML Document"] --> B["Tokenizer Scanner"] B --> C{"Lexical Analyzer"} C -->|"Tokens: Tags, Text, Entities"| D["Syntax Validator"] D -->|"Rule Check 1 to 10"| E{"Well-Formed?"} E -->|"No"| F["Error Report Line and Column"] E -->|"Yes"| G["Tree Builder DOM or Event Stream SAX"] G --> H["Application Logic Accesses Data"] H --> I["Output Render or Transform"] ``` ### 4.3 XML vs HTML Tag Behavior ŌĆö Comparison Flow ```mermaid graph TD Q["Input Tag Encountered"] --> R{"Is it HTML or XML?"} R -->|"HTML"| S["Lenient Parser Unquoted OK, Unclosed OK, Case Insensitive"] R -->|"XML"| T["Strict Parser Quotes Mandatory, Closing Mandatory, Case Sensitive"] S --> U["Browser Renders Page"] T --> V{"Validates Well-Formedness"} V -->|"Pass"| W["Application Reads Data"] V -->|"Fail"| X["Parser Reports Error and Stops"] ``` ### 4.4 XML Document Structure ŌĆö Hierarchical Block View ```mermaid graph TD L1["Prolog Optional: XML Decl plus PI plus Comments plus DTD"] L2["Root Element Mandatory and Unique"] L3["Content: Child Elements plus Text plus Attributes"] L4["Epilog Optional: Comments plus Whitespace after Root"] L1 --> L2 L2 --> L3 L3 --> L4 ``` --- ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2024 Model]** *Define XML. List any **four** features of XML that distinguish it from HTML.* **Model Answer (3 Marks):** **Definition (1 Mark):** XML (Extensible Markup Language) is a **W3C-recommended, text-based markup language** used to structure, store, and transport data using **user-defined tags** in a strictly hierarchical, self-describing format. **Four Distinguishing Features (2 Marks ŌĆö 0.5 each):** 1. **Extensible tags** ŌĆö Users can define their own tags, whereas HTML has a fixed tag set. 2. **Case-sensitive** ŌĆö `` and `` are different; HTML is case-insensitive. 3. **Strict syntax** ŌĆö XML requires closing tags and quoted attributes; HTML is lenient. 4. **Data-centric** ŌĆö XML is designed for data interchange; HTML is designed for display/presentation. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023 Model]** *Explain the purpose of the XML declaration and the XML prolog with an example.* **Model Answer (3 Marks):** The **XML declaration** is the first line of an XML document and identifies the document as an XML document. It specifies the **version**, **character encoding**, and **standalone** status. The **prolog** is everything that appears *before* the root element and may include the XML declaration, processing instructions, comments, and an optional Document Type Declaration (DTD). **Example (2 Marks):** ```xml ``` The `` line is the **declaration**, while the comment and stylesheet PI are also part of the **prolog**. --- ### 5.2 Part B ŌĆö Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each ŌĆö Internal Choice) --- **Q.A. [KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2024 Model ŌĆö Choice A]** **(a)** Explain the **basic structure of an XML document** with a suitable example. Discuss the role of the **root element** and **child elements**. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Write the **syntax rules** that must be followed while writing a well-formed XML document. Illustrate each rule with a short example. **(7 Marks)** #### Model Solution to Q.A: **(a) Structure of an XML Document (7 Marks):** An XML document consists of the following logical parts: - **XML Declaration** ŌĆö the optional `` line. - **Prolog** ŌĆö declarations, processing instructions, comments, DTD. - **Root Element** ŌĆö the single outermost element. - **Content** ŌĆö child elements, text, attributes. - **Epilog** ŌĆö optional comments/whitespace after the root. **Example (4 Marks):** ```xml Dr. Sharma Anjali 9.2 ``` **Role of the Root Element (1.5 Marks):** The root element `` is the **single, top-level container** that encloses all other content. It must appear exactly once and serves as the **starting point of the document tree**. Without it, the document is not well-formed. **Role of Child Elements (1.5 Marks):** Child elements such as ``, ``, `` provide **structured sub-divisions** of the data. They appear nested within the root or other parents, inheriting a strict parent-child relationship. Each child element can itself contain text or further nested children, enabling arbitrarily complex tree structures. --- **(b) XML Syntax Rules (7 Marks ŌĆö 1 Mark per major rule with example):** | # | Rule | Example | |---|---|---| | 1 | Document must have exactly **one root element** | `...` is the only outermost tag | | 2 | **Tags are case-sensitive** | `...` is **illegal** | | 3 | Elements must be **properly nested** | `text` legal; `text` illegal | | 4 | Every start tag needs an **end tag** | `

Hello

` not `

Hello` | | 5 | Attribute values must be **quoted** | `` | | 6 | **No duplicate** attribute names | `` is illegal | | 7 | **Special characters** must be escaped | Use `<` for `<`, `&` for `&` | | 8 | **Whitespace is preserved** | Multiple spaces in text are kept intact | | 9 | **Self-closing** syntax for empty elements | `
` is correct | | 10 | XML declaration must be **first** | No blank line before `` | --- **Q.B. [KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023 Model ŌĆö Choice B ŌĆö Alternative to Q.A]** **(a)** What is XML? Explain any **five syntax rules** of XML with examples. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Differentiate between **HTML and XML** with at least **six points** of comparison. Write a small XML document representing a student database. **(7 Marks)** #### Model Solution to Q.B: **(a) XML Definition + Five Syntax Rules (7 Marks):** **Definition (2 Marks):** XML (Extensible Markup Language) is a **structured, text-based data format** standardized by the W3C that allows users to define custom tags to describe data in a hierarchical tree structure. It is primarily used for **data storage and transport** between heterogeneous systems, and is both human-readable and machine-parseable. **Five Syntax Rules (5 Marks ŌĆö 1 each):** **Rule 1 ŌĆö Single Root Element:** Every XML document must have **exactly one** root element that wraps all other content. ```xml ... ... ``` **Rule 2 ŌĆö Closing Tags Mandatory:** Every opening tag must have a matching closing tag. ```xml John John ``` **Rule 3 ŌĆö Case Sensitivity:** XML tag names are case-sensitive. ```xml ... ... ... ``` **Rule 4 ŌĆö Quoted Attributes:** Attribute values must be enclosed in **single or double quotes**. ```xml ``` **Rule 5 ŌĆö Proper Nesting:** Tags must close in the reverse order of their opening (LIFO discipline). ```xml Bold Italic Bold Italic ``` --- **(b) HTML vs XML ŌĆö Six Points + XML Document (7 Marks):** **Comparison Table (3 Marks):** | # | HTML | XML | |---|---|---| | 1 | HyperText Markup Language | Extensible Markup Language | | 2 | **Presentation-focused** (how data looks) | **Data-focused** (what data means) | | 3 | Has a **fixed set of tags** | Users **define their own tags** | | 4 | **Case-insensitive** | **Case-sensitive** | | 5 | **Lenient parsing** ŌĆö unclosed tags allowed | **Strict parsing** ŌĆö well-formedness mandatory | | 6 | Tags need **not be quoted** in attributes | All attribute values **must be quoted** | **Sample XML Document (4 Marks):** ```xml Anjali Menon 6 9.12 anjali@ktu.in Rohit Krishnan 6 8.75 rohit@ktu.in ``` --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning & Common Pitfalls > [!WARNING] > **Where KTU Students Typically Lose Marks on XML Syntax Questions:** > > 1. **Mismatched closing tags** (Case-sensitivity trap): Students write `...`. The parser reports an *unclosed tag error*, and the examiner deducts 1ŌĆō2 marks. > 2. **Forgetting to quote attribute values**: Writing `` instead of ``. Costs 1 mark. > 3. **Improper nesting**: Writing `text` instead of `text`. Costs 2 marks. > 4. **Using ` ` or other HTML entities**: Only the **five predefined XML entities** are always available; HTML-only entities must be declared manually. > 5. **Forgetting the `?>` in the XML declaration**: The declaration `` is a fatal syntax error. > 6. **Adding a blank line before the XML declaration**: Even a single space at column 1 makes the document technically non-well-formed in strict parsers. > 7. **Confusing attributes with child elements**: Examiners often ask *"Why use an attribute here instead of a child element?"* ŌĆö students lose marks by not articulating the *metadata vs. data* rule. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) > [!TIP] > **Last-Minute KTU Board Revision ŌĆö 60-Second XML Syntax Cheat Sheet** - Ō£ģ **XML = eXtensible Markup Language**, W3C standard, used for data exchange. - Ō£ģ **HTML displays data; XML describes data.** - Ō£ģ XML is **case-sensitive**, **strictly nested**, and **self-describing**. - Ō£ģ Every well-formed XML document has **one and only one root element**. - Ō£ģ Every opening tag **must** have a closing tag; empty elements use **``**. - Ō£ģ All attribute values **must be quoted** using `"..."` or `'...'`. - Ō£ģ The **five predefined entities** are `<`, `>`, `&`, `'`, `"`. - Ō£ģ Use **``** to embed raw text containing markup characters. - Ō£ģ The **XML declaration** `` must be the **first line**. - Ō£ģ Comments use `` and **cannot contain `--`**. - Ō£ģ XML is **whitespace-sensitive** ŌĆö multiple spaces are preserved as written. - Ō£ģ Real-world XML usage: **Android layouts, SOAP, RSS, SVG, .docx, Spring config**. - Ō£ģ KTU favorite question: *"Write syntax rules of XML"* or *"Differentiate HTML and XML."* - Ō£ģ Always **validate** your XML mentally against the 10 rules before writing the answer. - Ō£ģ Use **lowercase tag names** as a KTU board-friendly convention. ---

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEITT833 - WEB DESIGNING > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Element # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition In the context of **HTML (HyperText Markup Language)** and the broader KTU 2024 Web Designing syllabus, an **Element** is the fundamental structural building block of a web document. According to the W3C (World Wide Web Consortium) and WHATWG (Web Hypertext Application Technology Working Group) standards, an HTML element is a discrete component of an HTML document used to structure, present, or interact with the content delivered to a web browser. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Definition (Board-Examiner Standard)** > An HTML **Element** is defined as everything from the *start tag* to the *end tag*, including the tag names, the attributes enclosed within the opening tag, and the textual or nested content residing between them. Mathematically, an HTML element can be expressed as a 3-tuple structure: $$E = \{T_{start}, \ C, \ T_{end}\}$$ Where: - $E$ represents the complete HTML Element - $T_{start}$ denotes the opening tag (e.g., `

`) - $C$ represents the content (text or nested elements) - $T_{end}$ denotes the closing tag (e.g., `

`) In the **Module 4** context of OEITT833, the term "Element" specifically emphasizes **Form Elements and Input Controls**, which are the interactive components that allow a web page to collect, validate, and submit user data to a server. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Think of a web page as a **printed government application form**. A blank sheet of paper is just a surface ŌĆö it has no power to gather information. The moment you print labeled boxes like "Name:", "Date of Birth:", "Gender (M/F):", "Signature:", you have created **form elements**. Each labeled box is an *element* with a specific purpose. Similarly, in HTML: - The **web page** is the blank document. - **HTML Elements** (like `
`, ``, ` ``` **Conversion Logic for `

``` **Three HTML5 validation attributes used:** 1. `required` ŌĆö for name, roll, department, rating. 2. `minlength="3"` and `maxlength="50"` ŌĆö for name length bounds. 3. `pattern="[A-Z]{2}[0-9]{6}"` ŌĆö enforces roll number format using RegEx. --- ### Question B `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]` **(a)** Differentiate between ``. When would you prefer one over the other? Provide a code example for each. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Explain the `pattern`, `min`, `max`, `step`, and `maxlength` attributes used in `` elements. Write an HTML snippet that uses all these attributes in a single form. **(7 Marks)** --- **Model Solution (a):** | Aspect | `` | |---|---|---| | Rendering | Dropdown (compact) | Visible list of buttons | | Best for | 5+ options | 2ŌĆō4 options | | Space usage | Saves screen space | Takes more vertical space | | Custom labels | Plain text only | Can include images/HTML | | Keyboard nav | Arrow keys to navigate | Tab + arrow keys | | Mobile UX | Native picker on most devices | Tap targets | **Code Example ŌĆö ` ``` **Code Example ŌĆö ``:** ```html

Choose your role:

``` **Preference Rule:** Use radio when options are *few, equally visible, and frequently scanned*. Use select when options are *many, space-constrained, or hierarchically grouped*. --- **Model Solution (b):** - **`pattern`**: Specifies a Regular Expression (RegEx) the input value must match. Common examples: `pattern="[0-9]{10}"` for 10-digit phone, `pattern="[A-Za-z0-9]+"` for alphanumeric. - **`min`**: Lower numerical or date boundary. Example: `min="1"`, `min="2024-01-01"`. - **`max`**: Upper numerical or date boundary. Example: `max="100"`, `max="2025-12-31"`. - **`step`**: Granularity of allowed values. Example: `step="0.01"` for currency, `step="5"` for multiples of 5. - **`maxlength`**: Maximum number of characters allowed in a text-type input. Example: `maxlength="100"`. **Combined HTML Snippet:** ```html




``` --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Forgetting to set `name` attribute:** Many students create inputs without `name`. Without `name`, the input's data is **not sent to the server** upon submission. This silently breaks form functionality. Always verify that every data-collecting input has a `name`. > 2. **Wrong radio group behavior:** Creating radio buttons with *different* `name` values makes them act as independent toggles, not a group. The grouping `name` must be identical across all radio buttons in the same logical group. > 3. **Forgetting to use `for` in `

topics.title: Attribute
topics.content: > **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEITT833 - WEB DESIGNING > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Attribute ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition In the context of **HTML (HyperText Markup Language)** and the **Document Object Model (DOM)**, an **Attribute** is a named property or modifier placed within the opening tag of an HTML element that provides **additional metadata, configuration, or behavioral instructions** about that element. Attributes consist of a **nameŌĆōvalue pair** structured as `name="value"` and act as the primary mechanism through which elements are customized, styled, linked, and made interactive. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Attributes are **NOT** displayed in the browser viewport. They exist solely to instruct the browser *how* to render, behave, or relate an element to other resources. Misplaced attributes cause silent rendering failures ŌĆö a common KTU exam pitfall. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy ŌĆö The "Wardrobe Label" Intuition Imagine an HTML element is a **plain white T-shirt** hanging in your wardrobe (the webpage). - The **T-shirt itself** = the HTML element (e.g., ``, ``, ``). - The **labels stitched inside the collar** = Attributes. These labels tell you: - What **size** it is (the `width` / `height` attributes) - What **color** it is (the `style="color:red"` attribute) - Which **drawer** it belongs to (the `class` or `id` attribute) - When it was **manufactured** (the `datetime` attribute) Just like a T-shirt without labels is functional but generic, an HTML element without attributes works but lacks specificity, behavior, and styling. Attributes are the **soul of customization** in web design. ### 1.3 KTU Board Taxonomy of Attributes Attributes are officially classified under KTU's web-engineering framework into the following families: | Attribute Family | Purpose | Example | |------------------|---------|---------| | **Core / Required Attributes** | Mandatory for element functionality | `src` in ``, `href` in `` | | **Global Attributes** | Applicable to *all* HTML5 elements | `id`, `class`, `style`, `title` | | **Event Attributes** | Trigger JavaScript on user/browser action | `onclick`, `onmouseover`, `onload` | | **Boolean Attributes** | Presence implies `true`; absence implies `false` | `disabled`, `readonly`, `checked` | | **Custom Data Attributes** | Store private custom data for scripts | `data-user-id`, `data-role` | | **Form Attributes** | Specific to form controls | `name`, `value`, `placeholder`, `required` | > [!NOTE] > **Standard Specification:** All HTML attributes are formally defined by the **W3C (World Wide Web Consortium)** and **WHATWG (Web Hypertext Application Technology Working Group)** under the HTML Living Standard. The number of officially recognized attributes in HTML5 exceeds **140**, spanning every element. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Anatomical Structure of an Attribute An attribute declaration follows a strict syntactic grammar. Parsing this grammar is a guaranteed **2-mark KTU short-answer** territory. ``` ``` **Parsing Rule Steps (KTU High-Yield):** 1. **Whitespace separation** ŌĆö Attributes must be separated by one or more space characters, tabs, or newlines. 2. **Name token** ŌĆö Attribute names are case-**insensitive** in HTML5 (unlike XHTML where they were case-sensitive). 3. **Assignment operator** ŌĆö Optional in HTML5. If omitted, the attribute behaves as a **Boolean** (e.g., ``). 4. **Quoted value** ŌĆö Values *should* be wrapped in **double quotes** `" "` or **single quotes** `' '`. Unquoted values are valid only if they contain no special characters. 5. **No attribute duplication** ŌĆö Repeating the same attribute name is a parsing error; the **first occurrence wins**. ### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet ŌĆö Attribute Syntax Cheat Table | # | Syntax Rule | Mathematical/Logical Notation | Validity Status | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Quoted value (double) | `attr="value"` | Ō£ģ Valid (Recommended) | | 2 | Quoted value (single) | `attr='value'` | Ō£ģ Valid | | 3 | Unquoted value | `attr=value` | ŌÜĀ’ĖÅ Valid *only if* no spaces/special chars | | 4 | Boolean presence | `` | Ō£ģ Equivalent to `attr=""` or `attr="attr"` | | 5 | Empty value | `attr=""` | Ō£ģ Valid empty string | | 6 | Attribute minimization | `attr=attr` | Ō£ģ Valid HTML5 boolean shorthand | | 7 | Case sensitivity (names) | `CLASS` ŌēĪ `class` | Ō£ģ Same (case-insensitive) | | 8 | Case sensitivity (values) | `en="US"` ŌēĀ `en="us"` | ŌØī Values *are* case-sensitive for some enums | ### 2.3 Why Attributes Matter ŌĆö Engineering Utility In real-world KTU-aligned web projects, attributes serve four mission-critical engineering purposes: 1. **Semantic Web Compliance** ŌĆö Attributes like `lang`, `alt`, and `role` make webpages accessible to screen readers and search engine crawlers (SEO). 2. **Form Data Transmission** ŌĆö `name`, `value`, `action`, and `method` attributes are the backbone of HTTP `GET` and `POST` request payload construction. 3. **DOM Manipulation Bridge** ŌĆö JavaScript accesses attributes via `element.getAttribute()` and `element.setAttribute()`, forming the foundation of dynamic Single Page Applications (SPA). 4. **Responsive Design Anchors** ŌĆö Attributes such as `srcset`, `sizes`, and `loading="lazy"` are critical for performance engineering and Core Web Vitals optimization. > [!TIP] > **Real-world industry callout:** In production frameworks like **React**, Angular, and Vue, attribute management evolved into **props** and **directives** ŌĆö but they are all syntactic descendants of plain HTML attributes. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: Parsing an Attribute NameŌĆōValue Pair into the DOM Consider a raw HTML token entering the browser's HTML parser. The parser must tokenize, validate, and store the attribute. Below is the step-by-step logical derivation that mirrors how **WebKit / Blink / Gecko** engines process an attribute. $$ \text{Raw Token} = \texttt{} $$ **Step 1 ŌĆö Tag Detection** The parser detects the `<` character, initiating *tag-open mode*. It reads characters until whitespace or `>`. **Step 2 ŌĆö Element Name Extraction** The first contiguous alphabetic run becomes the element name. $$ \text{ElementName} = \texttt{"img"} $$ **Step 3 ŌĆö Attribute Name Tokenization** For each subsequent whitespace-delimited segment, the parser scans for `=`. If `=` is absent, the token is a **Boolean attribute**. $$ \text{AttrName}_1 = \texttt{"src"}, \quad \text{AttrName}_2 = \texttt{"alt"}, \quad \text{AttrName}_3 = \texttt{"width"} $$ **Step 4 ŌĆö Value Extraction & Quote Matching** After `=`, the parser matches either `"..."`, `'...'`, or an unquoted run terminated by whitespace/`>`. $$ \text{AttrValue}_1 = \texttt{"logo.png"}, \quad \text{AttrValue}_2 = \texttt{"Company Logo"}, \quad \text{AttrValue}_3 = \texttt{"200"} $$ **Step 5 ŌĆö DOM Property Injection** Each nameŌĆōvalue pair is stored on the element's interface. $$ \text{element.getAttribute("src")} \rightarrow \texttt{"logo.png"} $$ **Step 6 ŌĆö IDL Reflection (Property Mapping)** Certain attributes are *reflected* as **IDL (Interface Definition Language) properties** on the DOM object. $$ \text{element.src} \equiv \text{element.getAttribute("src")} $$ ### 3.2 Derivation: Boolean Attribute Truth Logic Boolean attributes follow a unique logical model. The derivation of their effective state is: $$ \text{BoolState}(attr) = \begin{cases} \text{true} & \text{if } attr \text{ is present (any value, including empty)} \\ \text{false} & \text{if } attr \text{ is omitted entirely} \end{cases} $$ **Worked Example ŌĆö `disabled` attribute on a button:** ```html ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Trap:** Writing `disabled="false"` does **NOT** enable the element. Boolean attributes are **presence-based**, not value-based. This is one of the most frequent 1-mark silly mistakes in KTU semester exams. ### 3.3 Analytical Matrix ŌĆö Top 12 Most Tested Attributes (KTU Frequency Index) | Rank | Attribute | Element Family | KTU Exam Frequency | Exam Weightage | |------|-----------|----------------|--------------------|----------------| | 1 | `href` | ``, `` | ŌśģŌśģŌśģŌśģŌśģ | 3ŌĆō7 marks | | 2 | `src` | ``, `

topics.title: HttpRequest, SVG: Drawing with XML
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEITT833 - WEB DESIGNING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** HttpRequest, SVG: Drawing with XML

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 HttpRequest (XMLHttpRequest)

### Formal Academic Definition
**HttpRequest** in web design refers to the `XMLHttpRequest` (XHR) object in JavaScript, which is the foundational API used to implement **AJAX (Asynchronous JavaScript and XML)**. It allows web pages to send and receive data from a server asynchronously without requiring a full page reload, thereby enabling dynamic, responsive, and interactive user experiences.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Under the NEP 2020 outcome-based framework, the `XMLHttpRequest` object is classified as a core *client-side scripting construct* bridging the Document Object Model (DOM) and the server. The modern alternative is the `fetch()` API, but XMLHttpRequest remains the textbook/board-expectation focus.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Imagine you are sitting in a restaurant. The traditional (non-AJAX) way of ordering food is: you give the entire order to the waiter, the chef cooks **everything** in the kitchen, and the waiter brings back a **brand new table** with the food. The whole "page" gets refreshed.

With **HttpRequest (AJAX)**, it's like having a smart waiter with a notepad. He runs to the kitchen, asks the chef for **just one extra dish**, and brings it back to your existing table. The other dishes on your table are untouched ŌĆö only a small portion of the page is updated. This is the essence of an *asynchronous partial update*.

### Key Properties of XMLHttpRequest (Highlighted Standards)

| Property | Type | Description |
|---|---|---|
| `readyState` | Integer | Holds the status of the XMLHttpRequest. Values: **0 (UNSENT)**, **1 (OPENED)**, **2 (HEADERS_RECEIVED)**, **3 (LOADING)**, **4 (DONE)**. |
| `status` | Integer | Returns the HTTP status code (e.g., **200 = OK**, **404 = Not Found**). |
| `responseText` | String | Returns the response as a text string. |
| `responseXML` | Document | Returns the response as an XML DOM object (parseable when content-type is XML). |
| `onreadystatechange` | Event Handler | A function that fires every time `readyState` changes. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Standard:** The HTTP protocol itself is governed by **IETF RFC 7231** (HTTP/1.1) and **RFC 9110** (HTTP Semantics). Status codes follow the **3-digit classification**:
> - **1xx** = Informational
> - **2xx** = Success (most common: **200 OK**)
> - **3xx** = Redirection
> - **4xx** = Client Error (e.g., **404 Not Found**, **403 Forbidden**)
> - **5xx** = Server Error (e.g., **500 Internal Server Error**)

---

## 1.2 SVG: Drawing with XML

### Formal Academic Definition
**SVG (Scalable Vector Graphics)** is an **XML-based**, W3C-recommended markup language used to describe **two-dimensional vector graphics**. Unlike raster formats (PNG, JPEG) that store pixel information, SVG describes shapes using geometric primitives (lines, curves, paths) defined mathematically ŌĆö making it **infinitely scalable** without loss of quality.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** SVG is part of HTML5's native graphics stack. Since SVG is pure XML, it can be authored in any text editor, styled with CSS, scripted with JavaScript, and indexed by search engines. This is its unique advantage over the canvas-based bitmap API.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Think of two ways to draw a circle on a computer:
1. **Raster (PNG/JPEG):** You take a photograph of a drawn circle ŌĆö you store the color of every single dot. If you zoom in, it gets blurry and pixelated.
2. **Vector (SVG):** You write a **mathematical recipe**: "Draw a circle whose center is at point (100, 100) with radius 50." The computer then "re-draws" the picture from the recipe every time. Zoom in infinitely ŌĆö it stays perfectly sharp.

**SVG is the recipe book. PNG is the photograph.**

> [!NOTE]
> **W3C Standard:** SVG is standardized by the **World Wide Web Consortium (W3C)**. The current stable recommendation is **SVG 1.1 (Second Edition)** and **SVG 2** integrated with HTML5. SVG documents use the MIME type **`image/svg+xml`**.

### Why SVG Matters in Modern Web Design
- **Resolution Independent:** Perfect for retina displays and responsive design.
- **Small File Size:** For simple icons/logos, SVG is dramatically smaller than PNGs.
- **CSS Styleable:** Every SVG element can be styled with CSS, animated, and transformed.
- **Accessible:** SVG content is text-based and screen-readable.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The XMLHttpRequest Lifecycle (Operational Theory)

The XMLHttpRequest object follows a deterministic **state machine**. Understanding each transition is critical for KTU exam questions on "explain AJAX request flow."

### Step-by-Step Logic of an HTTP Request

**Step 1 ŌĆö Object Instantiation**
A new `XMLHttpRequest` object is created. At this moment, `readyState = 0` (**UNSENT**). No connection exists yet.

**Step 2 ŌĆö Initialization: `open(method, url, async)`**
The `open()` method configures the request but does **not** send it.
- `method`: HTTP verb ŌĆö typically **"GET"** (retrieve data) or **"POST"** (send data to server).
- `url`: The endpoint on the server.
- `async`: Boolean ŌĆö `true` for asynchronous (default in modern apps), `false` for synchronous (legacy).
After this call, `readyState = 1` (**OPENED**).

**Step 3 ŌĆö Event Handler Binding**
The `onreadystatechange` property is assigned a callback function. This function will fire **every time** `readyState` changes. Inside the callback, we typically check:
- `readyState == 4` (DONE)
- `status == 200` (OK)

**Step 4 ŌĆö Sending the Request: `send(data)`**
The `send()` method actually transmits the request to the server. For **GET** requests, the parameter is `null`. For **POST** requests, the parameter contains the data payload (often a URL-encoded string or JSON). After sending, `readyState = 2` ŌåÆ `3` ŌåÆ `4` as the response arrives.

**Step 5 ŌĆö Response Handling**
Inside the `onreadystatechange` callback, the response data is extracted using `responseText` (as a string) or `responseXML` (as a parsed XML DOM tree), and the DOM of the current page is updated ŌĆö completing the partial page refresh.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why 'Why' matters:** The "Why" behind this state machine is to allow the browser to give the JavaScript engine a structured way to handle network latency. Without `readyState`, developers would have no deterministic place to react to "data is partially here" vs "data is fully here."

### KTU Formula / Cheat Sheet ŌĆö XMLHttpRequest

| Component | Syntax / Value | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| Create object | `xhr = new XMLHttpRequest()` | Allocates a new XHR instance |
| `open()` | `xhr.open("GET", "data.txt", true)` | Configures HTTP method, URL, async flag |
| `send()` | `xhr.send()` or `xhr.send(payload)` | Dispatches the HTTP request |
| `readyState` | 0, 1, 2, 3, 4 | Lifecycle stage |
| `status` | 200, 404, 500, etc. | HTTP response status code |
| `responseText` | String | Server response as plain text |
| `responseXML` | Document object | Server response as XML DOM |
| `onreadystatechange` | Function | Callback invoked on every state change |
| `setRequestHeader()` | `xhr.setRequestHeader("Content-Type", "application/x-www-form-urlencoded")` | Sets HTTP request headers (used in POST) |

---

## 2.2 SVG: Drawing with XML ŌĆö Theoretical Foundations

SVG documents are pure XML, meaning they follow a strict tree structure rooted at the `<svg>` element. Every visual element is a child node with attributes that define geometry.

### Step-by-Step Logic of SVG Drawing

**Step 1 ŌĆö Establish the Coordinate System (Viewport)**
The `<svg>` element defines the drawing canvas. Its `width` and `height` attributes set the **viewport** in user units. Internally, SVG uses a **left-handed coordinate system** where the origin `(0, 0)` is the **top-left corner** of the viewport, the **X-axis** runs to the right, and the **Y-axis** runs **downward**. This is the opposite of standard Cartesian math.

**Step 2 ŌĆö Select and Configure a Shape**
You embed shape elements (e.g., `<rect>`, `<circle>`, `<line>`, `<ellipse>`, `<polyline>`, `<polygon>`, `<path>`) as children. Each shape has presentation attributes:
- **Geometric:** `x`, `y`, `cx`, `cy`, `r`, `rx`, `ry`, `width`, `height`, `x1`, `y1`, `x2`, `y2`
- **Styling:** `fill`, `stroke`, `stroke-width`, `opacity`, `fill-opacity`, `stroke-opacity`
- **Transformations:** `transform="translate(x,y) rotate(angle) scale(s)"`

**Step 3 ŌĆö Define the Path (for complex shapes)**
The `<path>` element uses the `d` (data) attribute ŌĆö a mini-language of commands:
- `M x y` ŌĆö Move to absolute coordinate
- `L x y` ŌĆö Line to absolute coordinate
- `H x` / `V y` ŌĆö Horizontal/Vertical line
- `C x1 y1, x2 y2, x y` ŌĆö Cubic Bezier curve
- `Z` ŌĆö Close current path

**Step 4 ŌĆö Apply Styling and Grouping**
Shapes can be wrapped in `<g>` (group) elements to share attributes and inherit styles. CSS can be applied via `class` or inline `style`. Gradients (`<linearGradient>`, `<radialGradient>`) and filters (`<filter>`) add advanced visual effects.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world Engineering Utility:** SVG is used in **CAD systems**, **GIS mapping (OpenStreetMap)**, **data visualization libraries (D3.js)**, **UI icon systems (Material Icons, Font Awesome)**, **logo design**, and **print-ready web graphics**. Its mathematical precision makes it the de-facto standard for any graphics that must scale across devices.

### KTU Formula / Cheat Sheet ŌĆö SVG Elements & Geometry

| SVG Element | Core Attributes | Mathematical Description |
|---|---|---|
| `<svg>` | `width`, `height`, `viewBox` | Defines the viewport: a rectangle of width W and height H in user units |
| `<rect>` | `x`, `y`, `width`, `height`, `rx`, `ry` | Axis-aligned rectangle, with optional rounded corners of radius `rx`, `ry` |
| `<circle>` | `cx`, `cy`, `r` | Circle centered at `(cx, cy)` with radius `r`. Equation: $(x - c_x)^2 + (y - c_y)^2 = r^2$ |
| `<ellipse>` | `cx`, `cy`, `rx`, `ry` | Ellipse with semi-major axis `rx` and semi-minor axis `ry` |
| `<line>` | `x1`, `y1`, `x2`, `y2` | Straight line from $(x_1, y_1)$ to $(x_2, y_2)$ |
| `<polygon>` | `points="x1,y1 x2,y2 ..."` | Closed shape with $n$ vertices, automatically closes back to first vertex |
| `<polyline>` | `points="x1,y1 x2,y2 ..."` | Connected lines with $n$ vertices, **does not** close automatically |
| `<path>` | `d="M x y L x y ..."` | Arbitrary path; supports lines, curves, arcs, and closures |
| `<text>` | `x`, `y`, `font-size`, `fill` | Renders text at the given anchor point |

### KTU Formula / Cheat Sheet ŌĆö SVG Geometric Equations

| Shape | Area Formula | Perimeter / Circumference |
|---|---|---|
| Circle | $A = \pi r^2$ | $C = 2\pi r$ |
| Ellipse | $A = \pi r_x r_y$ | Approx: $C \approx \pi [3(r_x + r_y) - \sqrt{(3r_x + r_y)(r_x + 3r_y)}]$ (Ramanujan) |
| Rectangle | $A = w \cdot h$ | $P = 2(w + h)$ |
| Distance (Line) | $d = \sqrt{(x_2 - x_1)^2 + (y_2 - y_1)^2}$ | Used implicitly when defining `<line>` |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Pitfall:** The SVG Y-axis is **inverted** compared to standard math coordinates. Positive `y` moves **downward**, not upward. This is a common KTU answer-script error.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Analytical Example: SVG Circle and Ellipse Geometry

To meet the "exhaustive derivation" mandate, let us derive the bounding-box and area equations for SVG shapes that are heavily tested.

### Derivation 1: Bounding Box of a Circle in SVG

> A circle is defined with center $(c_x, c_y)$ and radius $r$. Find the bounding box (i.e., the smallest axis-aligned rectangle that encloses the circle).

**Step 1: Identify extreme points on the circle.**

The general equation of a circle is:
$$(x - c_x)^2 + (y - c_y)^2 = r^2$$

**Step 2: Solve for x when y is at the vertical extremes (top and bottom of circle).**

The topmost and bottommost points occur where the tangent line is horizontal, which happens at the vertical extremities:
$$y_{\min} = c_y - r$$
$$y_{\max} = c_y + r$$

**Step 3: Solve for x when x is at the horizontal extremes.**

Similarly, the leftmost and rightmost points are:
$$x_{\min} = c_x - r$$
$$x_{\max} = c_x + r$$

**Step 4: Assemble the bounding box.**

The enclosing rectangle is the `<rect>` that SVG would need to fully contain the circle:
$$\text{Top-left corner: } (c_x - r, \; c_y - r)$$
$$\text{Bottom-right corner: } (c_x + r, \; c_y + r)$$
$$\text{Rectangle width} = w = 2r$$
$$\text{Rectangle height} = h = 2r$$

**Step 5: Write the equivalent SVG code.**

If the original circle is `<circle cx="100" cy="80" r="40"/>`, then the bounding rect would be:
`<rect x="60" y="40" width="80" height="80"/>`.

> **Conversion Logic:** $x = c_x - r = 100 - 40 = 60$, $y = c_y - r = 80 - 40 = 40$, $w = h = 2r = 80$.

---

### Derivation 2: Area and Perimeter of an SVG Ellipse

> An ellipse is given with center $(c_x, c_y)$, horizontal semi-axis $r_x$, and vertical semi-axis $r_y$. Derive its area and the Ramanujan approximation for circumference.

**Step 1: Write the canonical ellipse equation.**

$$\frac{(x - c_x)^2}{r_x^2} + \frac{(y - c_y)^2}{r_y^2} = 1$$

**Step 2: Integrate to find area.**

For a standard ellipse centered at the origin, the area is given by the integral:
$$A = 4 \int_{0}^{r_x} y \, dx$$

Substituting $y = r_y \sqrt{1 - \frac{x^2}{r_x^2}}$:
$$A = 4 \int_{0}^{r_x} r_y \sqrt{1 - \frac{x^2}{r_x^2}} \, dx$$

Using the substitution $x = r_x \sin(\theta)$, $dx = r_x \cos(\theta) d\theta$:
$$A = 4 r_x r_y \int_{0}^{\pi/2} \cos^2(\theta) \, d\theta = 4 r_x r_y \cdot \frac{\pi}{4}$$

Thus:
$$A = \pi \, r_x \, r_y$$

**Step 3: Apply Ramanujan's first approximation for circumference.**

Ramanujan's 1914 approximation is the most cited closed-form estimate for ellipse perimeter:
$$C \approx \pi \left[ 3(r_x + r_y) - \sqrt{(3r_x + r_y)(r_x + 3r_y)} \right]$$

**Step 4: Numerical validation.**

Take $r_x = 5$, $r_y = 3$ (a standard SVG ellipse). Then:
$$h = \frac{(r_x - r_y)^2}{(r_x + r_y)^2} = \frac{4}{64} = 0.0625$$
$$C \approx \pi (a + b) \left[ 1 + \frac{3h}{10 + \sqrt{4 - 3h}} \right] \approx 25.527$$

This matches the analytical reality for the ellipse $\frac{x^2}{25} + \frac{y^2}{9} = 1$.

> **Conversion Logic:** The Ramanujan formula provides a closed-form approximation to the elliptic integral, which has no elementary antiderivative. This is why circumference of an ellipse is **not** simply $2\pi \sqrt{\frac{r_x^2 + r_y^2}{2}}$ (that is only an estimate).

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: XMLHttpRequest State Transitions

The KTU board frequently tests the lifecycle. Below is the complete state transition matrix:

| `readyState` Value | Constant Name | Meaning | Trigger Event | Network Status |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **0** | `UNSENT` | Client created; `open()` not yet called | Object instantiation | No connection |
| **1** | `OPENED` | `open()` has been called | `open()` invocation | Connection established but not sent |
| **2** | `HEADERS_RECEIVED` | `send()` called; response headers received | Server returns HTTP headers | Headers downloaded |
| **3** | `LOADING` | Response body is downloading | Data streaming | Body partially received |
| **4** | `DONE` | Operation complete | Final byte received | Full response available |

> **Conversion Logic:** The student must memorize that `onreadystatechange` fires **4 times** during a complete request (for transitions 1ŌåÆ2, 2ŌåÆ3, 3ŌåÆ4, plus the initial assignment if explicitly invoked). The safe pattern is to check `if (xhr.readyState == 4 && xhr.status == 200)`.

### Reference Code Structure (XMLHttpRequest GET Example)

```javascript
function loadData() {
    // Step 1: Create the XHR object
    var xhr = new XMLHttpRequest();

    // Step 2: Bind the callback
    xhr.onreadystatechange = function() {
        if (xhr.readyState == 4 && xhr.status == 200) {
            document.getElementById("result").innerHTML = xhr.responseText;
        }
    };

    // Step 3: Configure (GET is default; URL is the endpoint)
    xhr.open("GET", "data.txt", true);

    // Step 4: Dispatch
    xhr.send();
}
```

> **Conversion Logic:** This is a textbook KTU Part B answer. The "Why" of `true` in `open()` is to allow the browser to continue executing other JavaScript while waiting for the server response ŌĆö non-blocking I/O.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 XMLHttpRequest Lifecycle Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Start: Create xhr = new XMLHttpRequest"] --> B["readyState = 0 UNSENT"]
    B --> C["Call xhr.open method, url, async"]
    C --> D["readyState = 1 OPENED"]
    D --> E["Assign onreadystatechange callback"]
    E --> F["Call xhr.send data or null"]
    F --> G["readyState = 2 HEADERS_RECEIVED"]
    G --> H["readyState = 3 LOADING response body"]
    H --> I["readyState = 4 DONE"]
    I --> J{"Check status == 200"}
    J -->|"Yes"| K["Read responseText or responseXML"]
    K --> L["Update DOM innerHTML or appendChild"]
    J -->|"No"| M["Handle error: display status code"]
    L --> N["End: Partial page refresh complete"]
    M --> N
```

> **Interpretation:** The diagram above shows the full asynchronous flow. The "callback" arrow is implicit ŌĆö every time the `readyState` changes, control returns to the `onreadystatechange` function. The `if (readyState == 4 && status == 200)` guard is the **only safe place** to act on the response.

## 4.2 SVG Document Tree & Rendering Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["SVG Root: svg width, height, viewBox"] --> B["Group: g transform, fill, stroke"]
    B --> C1["Shape: circle cx, cy, r"]
    B --> C2["Shape: rect x, y, w, h"]
    B --> C3["Shape: ellipse cx, cy, rx, ry"]
    B --> C4["Path: path d = M, L, C, Z"]
    B --> D1["Text: text x, y, content"]
    B --> E1["Defs: defs id = gradient1"]
    E1 --> E2["linearGradient stops"]
    B --> F1["Style: style CSS selectors"]
    F1 --> G1["Render to bitmap for display"]
    C1 --> G1
    C2 --> G1
    C3 --> G1
    C4 --> G1
    D1 --> G1
    E2 --> G1
```

> **Interpretation:** The browser parses the SVG XML tree, resolves the inheritance of `fill`, `stroke`, and `transform` attributes through the `<g>` hierarchy, evaluates any `<defs>` like gradients/filters, and finally rasterizes the mathematical shapes into pixels for the current viewport. This rasterization happens **on every frame** if animations are present, which is why SVG remains crisp at all zoom levels.

## 4.3 AJAX vs Traditional Page Request ŌĆö Comparative Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph T["Traditional Synchronous Request"]
        T1["Browser submits form"] --> T2["Server processes"]
        T2 --> T3["Server sends ENTIRE new HTML page"]
        T3 --> T4["Browser reloads and re-renders everything"]
    end
    subgraph A["AJAX Asynchronous Request"]
        A1["JS triggers XHR.send"] --> A2["Server processes ONLY the requested data"]
        A2 --> A3["Server sends small data payload JSON or XML or text"]
        A3 --> A4["JS callback updates a DOM NODE only"]
    end
    T4 -.->|"Full page reload\nSlow, wasteful bandwidth"| T_OUT["User sees flash of blank page"]
    A4 -.->|"Partial update\nFast, smooth UX"| A_OUT["User sees no reload seamless"]
```

> **Interpretation:** This comparative flow visualizes the **fundamental engineering advantage** of AJAX: reducing bandwidth, eliminating flicker, and providing desktop-application-like responsiveness in a web context.

## 4.4 SVG Coordinate System Mapping

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph V["SVG Viewport: width=400, height=300"]
        O["Origin (0,0) Top Left"] -->|"+X axis rightward"| X["(400, 0) Top Right"]
        O -->|"+Y axis downward"| Y["(0, 300) Bottom Left"]
        X --> D["(400, 300) Bottom Right"]
        Y --> D
    end
    V -.->|"Internally Y is inverted\nvs math convention"| M["Standard Math: +Y is upward"]
```

> **Interpretation:** This is a high-yield diagram for KTU: the inversion of the Y-axis is the **#1 most-tested concept** in SVG geometry. Students frequently draw SVG coordinates as if they were math coordinates and lose marks.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define XMLHttpRequest. List its `readyState` values.
**`[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Model Question Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme]`**

**Model Answer:**

The `XMLHttpRequest` (XHR) is a JavaScript object that enables asynchronous communication between the client (browser) and the server without requiring a full page reload. It is the backbone of AJAX (Asynchronous JavaScript and XML).

The five `readyState` values are:
1. **0 (UNSENT):** Object created, but `open()` not yet called.
2. **1 (OPENED):** `open()` method has been called.
3. **2 (HEADERS_RECEIVED):** `send()` called, HTTP headers received.
4. **3 (LOADING):** Response body is being downloaded.
5. **4 (DONE):** Operation complete; full response available.

> [!NOTE]
> **Valuation Key:** Listing all five values with their correct constant names fetches full marks. Skipping the constants (UNSENT, OPENED, etc.) and only writing numbers loses **1 mark**.

---

### Question 2: What is SVG? Mention any four SVG shape elements.
**`[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Model Question Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme]`**

**Model Answer:**

**SVG (Scalable Vector Graphics)** is an XML-based, W3C-recommended markup language for describing two-dimensional vector graphics. SVG images are defined using geometric primitives and are infinitely scalable without loss of quality.

Four SVG shape elements are:
1. **`<circle>`** ŌĆö defined by `cx`, `cy`, `r`.
2. **`<rect>`** ŌĆö defined by `x`, `y`, `width`, `height`.
3. **`<line>`** ŌĆö defined by `x1`, `y1`, `x2`, `y2`.
4. **`<ellipse>`** ŌĆö defined by `cx`, `cy`, `rx`, `ry`.
*(Additional valid: `<polygon>`, `<polyline>`, `<path>`, `<text>`)*

---

## Part B ŌĆö Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** KTU mandates an internal choice within each module. Students answer either **Question A** OR **Question B**.

---

### Question A (14 Marks)
**`[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Module 4 Internal Choice, KTU 2024 Scheme]`**

**(a)** Explain the `XMLHttpRequest` object in detail. Discuss its methods and properties. (7 Marks)

**(b)** Write the steps to create an AJAX-based application that loads text from a file `data.txt` and displays it inside a `<div id="result">` without reloading the page. (7 Marks)

---

### Model Solution for Question A(a) ŌĆö 7 Marks

The `XMLHttpRequest` (XHR) object is a built-in browser API used to send HTTP/HTTPS requests to a server and process the response, all **asynchronously**, without reloading the current web page. It is the foundational technology behind **AJAX**.

**Methods of XMLHttpRequest (3 marks):**

1. **`open(method, url, async)`** ŌĆö Initializes a request. Parameters:
   - `method`: HTTP verb such as `"GET"` or `"POST"`.
   - `url`: target endpoint (string).
   - `async`: `true` (default, asynchronous) or `false` (synchronous, deprecated).
   This method does **not** send the request; it only configures it.

2. **`send(data)`** ŌĆö Dispatches the request to the server. For `GET` requests, the argument is `null`. For `POST`, the argument is the payload (string or `FormData`).

3. **`setRequestHeader(header, value)`** ŌĆö Sets a custom HTTP header (e.g., `Content-Type` for POST). Must be called **after** `open()` and **before** `send()`.

4. **`abort()`** ŌĆö Cancels the current request, resetting `readyState` to 0.

**Properties of XMLHttpRequest (3 marks):**

- **`onreadystatechange`** ŌĆö A function reference that fires on every `readyState` change.
- **`readyState`** ŌĆö Integer from 0 to 4 indicating the request lifecycle.
- **`status`** ŌĆö HTTP response code (e.g., 200, 404, 500).
- **`statusText`** ŌĆö HTTP status message (e.g., `"OK"`, `"Not Found"`).
- **`responseText`** ŌĆö Server response as a plain string.
- **`responseXML`** ŌĆö Server response as a parsed XML DOM document (when content-type is XML).
- **`withCredentials`** ŌĆö Boolean to send cookies/auth headers with cross-origin requests.

**Conclusion (1 mark):**
XMLHttpRequest is the cornerstone of asynchronous web programming, enabling modern single-page applications (SPAs) and partial DOM updates.

---

### Model Solution for Question A(b) ŌĆö 7 Marks

**Step 1: HTML Structure** (1 mark)
Create a target `<div>` and a button trigger:
```html
<!DOCTYPE html>
<html>
<head><title>AJAX Demo</title></head>
<body>
    <h2>AJAX Demo</h2>
    <div id="result">Initial content...</div>
    <button onclick="loadData()">Load Data</button>
</body>
</html>
```

**Step 2: JavaScript Function with XHR** (5 marks)
```javascript
function loadData() {
    // (i) Create the XHR object
    var xhr = new XMLHttpRequest();

    // (ii) Define what happens when state changes
    xhr.onreadystatechange = function() {
        // (iii) Wait until request is fully done AND status is 200 OK
        if (xhr.readyState == 4 && xhr.status == 200) {
            // (iv) Inject response text into the DOM node
            document.getElementById("result").innerHTML = xhr.responseText;
        } else if (xhr.readyState == 4 && xhr.status != 200) {
            document.getElementById("result").innerHTML = "Error: " + xhr.status;
        }
    };

    // (v) Configure: GET request to data.txt, asynchronous
    xhr.open("GET", "data.txt", true);

    // (vi) Dispatch the request
    xhr.send();
}
```

**Step 3: Server-side `data.txt` File** (1 mark)
The file `data.txt` should contain plain text (e.g., `"Hello from the server! This was loaded using XMLHttpRequest."`). It must reside in the same directory as the HTML file or at the URL provided to `open()`.

**Workflow Summary:**
The button click invokes `loadData()`. A new `XMLHttpRequest` object is created, the `GET` method is configured for `data.txt`, and the request is sent. The browser fires `onreadystatechange` repeatedly; when `readyState` reaches **4** and the HTTP status is **200**, the response text is written into the `<div>` ŌĆö **without** a full page reload.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) ŌĆö Alternative Choice
**`[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Module 4 Internal Choice, KTU 2024 Scheme]`**

**(a)** What is SVG? Explain the `<circle>`, `<rect>`, `<line>`, and `<ellipse>` elements with attributes and examples. (7 Marks)

**(b)** Design an SVG graphic depicting the Indian flag (rectangle with three horizontal bands). Provide the complete code and explain the role of the `viewBox` attribute. (7 Marks)

---

### Model Solution for Question B(a) ŌĆö 7 Marks

**Introduction to SVG (1 mark):**
**SVG (Scalable Vector Graphics)** is an XML-based W3C standard for describing 2D vector graphics. It is rendered by browsers natively and is resolution-independent.

**Shape Elements (6 marks ŌĆö 1.5 marks each):**

**1. `<circle>`** ŌĆö Draws a circle.
Attributes: `cx` (center X), `cy` (center Y), `r` (radius), plus style attributes.
```xml
<svg width="200" height="200">
    <circle cx="100" cy="100" r="50" fill="blue" stroke="black" stroke-width="2"/>
</svg>
```

**2. `<rect>`** ŌĆö Draws a rectangle.
Attributes: `x`, `y` (top-left corner), `width`, `height`, optional `rx`/`ry` for rounded corners.
```xml
<svg width="200" height="200">
    <rect x="20" y="20" width="150" height="100" fill="lightgreen" stroke="darkgreen" stroke-width="3"/>
</svg>
```

**3. `<line>`** ŌĆö Draws a straight line segment.
Attributes: `x1`, `y1` (start), `x2`, `y2` (end).
```xml
<svg width="200" height="200">
    <line x1="10" y1="10" x2="180" y2="180" stroke="red" stroke-width="4"/>
</svg>
```

**4. `<ellipse>`** ŌĆö Draws an ellipse.
Attributes: `cx`, `cy` (center), `rx` (horizontal semi-axis), `ry` (vertical semi-axis).
```xml
<svg width="200" height="200">
    <ellipse cx="100" cy="100" rx="80" ry="40" fill="orange"/>
</svg>
```

**Conclusion:** All four primitives inherit the SVG coordinate system and can be styled with `fill`, `stroke`, and `stroke-width`.

---

### Model Solution for Question B(b) ŌĆö 7 Marks

**Step 1: The `viewBox` Concept (2 marks):**
The `viewBox` attribute defines the **internal coordinate system** of the SVG. It takes four values: `viewBox="min-x min-y width height"`. This decouples the drawing's internal units from the actual rendered size, allowing the same graphic to scale responsively.

For example: `viewBox="0 0 900 600"` means internally the canvas is 900 units wide and 600 units tall, but the actual rendered size is determined by `width` and `height`.

**Step 2: Flag Code (4 marks):**
```xml
<svg width="600" height="400" viewBox="0 0 900 600" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg">
    <!-- Saffron (top) band -->
    <rect x="0" y="0" width="900" height="200" fill="#FF9933"/>
    <!-- White (middle) band -->
    <rect x="0" y="200" width="900" height="200" fill="#FFFFFF"/>
    <!-- Green (bottom) band -->
    <rect x="0" y="400" width="900" height="200" fill="#138808"/>
    <!-- Ashoka Chakra (blue wheel) -->
    <circle cx="450" cy="300" r="80" fill="none" stroke="#000080" stroke-width="6"/>
    <!-- 24 spokes (simplified) -->
    <g stroke="#000080" stroke-width="2">
        <line x1="450" y1="220" x2="450" y2="380"/>
        <line x1="370" y1="300" x2="530" y2="300"/>
        <line x1="394" y1="244" x2="506" y2="356"/>
        <line x1="506" y1="244" x2="394" y2="356"/>
    </g>
</svg>
```

**Step 3: Explanation (1 mark):**
- The outer `<svg>` tag declares the drawing canvas (`600x400` display size).
- `viewBox="0 0 900 600"` lets us draw in a 900├Ś600 logical grid for cleaner numbers.
- Each `<rect>` represents a band; their `y` values (0, 200, 400) are evenly spaced.
- The `<circle>` is the Ashoka Chakra, with `<line>` elements simulating its spokes.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls ŌĆö Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the `&& status == 200` check:** Many students write only `if (xhr.readyState == 4)`. This means they act on **any** completed request, including 404s and 500s. Valuer docks **2 marks**.
> 2. **Confusing `responseText` with `responseXML`:** `responseText` always returns a string; `responseXML` returns a DOM object **only if** the server's `Content-Type` is `application/xml` or `text/xml`. Mixing them up loses **1 mark**.
> 3. **Omitting `xmlns` in SVG:** Modern browsers (especially in standalone `.svg` files) require `xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"` in the root `<svg>` tag. Skipping it in inline embedded code often works in Chrome, but fails in strict validators. Examiner may deduct **0.5 to 1 mark**.
> 4. **Y-axis inversion mistakes:** Drawing a circle with `cy=400` and expecting it to appear near the top of a `height=500` SVG loses **1 mark** ŌĆö the circle appears near the bottom.
> 5. **Not closing the `onreadystatechange` function properly:** Missing `;` or `}` after `xhr.send()` is a common syntactic error. Examiners count this as "code does not run" ŌåÆ 0 marks for that sub-question.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid Revision Checklist ŌĆö High-Yield for KTU 2024:**

- **XMLHttpRequest** is a JavaScript API for asynchronous HTTP requests, the core of **AJAX**.
- The five `readyState` values are: **0 UNSENT ŌåÆ 1 OPENED ŌåÆ 2 HEADERS_RECEIVED ŌåÆ 3 LOADING ŌåÆ 4 DONE**.
- The standard safe handler is: `if (xhr.readyState == 4 && xhr.status == 200) { ... }`.
- `xhr.open(method, url, async)` *configures*; `xhr.send(data)` *dispatches*.
- `responseText` ŌåÆ string; `responseXML` ŌåÆ XML DOM (only when content-type is XML).
- **SVG** is XML-based, W3C-standardized, and **resolution-independent**.
- SVG coordinate origin is **top-left**; **Y-axis is inverted** (+y is down).
- Core shape elements: `<rect>`, `<circle>`, `<ellipse>`, `<line>`, `<polygon>`, `<polyline>`, `<path>`, `<text>`.
- Always include `xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"` in the root `<svg>` for cross-browser safety.
- The `viewBox` attribute decouples **internal units** from **display size**, enabling responsive scaling.
- Group shapes with `<g>` to share `transform`, `fill`, and `stroke` attributes.
- **Circle Area** = $\pi r^2$; **Ellipse Area** = $\pi r_x r_y$; **Distance** = $\sqrt{(x_2-x_1)^2 + (y_2-y_1)^2}$.
- **Modern alternative to XHR:** `fetch()` API, but `XMLHttpRequest` remains the **KTU board expectation**.
- **Modern alternative to SVG-rendered charts:** `<canvas>` (bitmap-based, JS-controlled), but SVG remains preferred for icons, logos, and printable graphics due to scalability and accessibility.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Features of SVG as XML
topics.content: > **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEITT833 - WEB DESIGNING > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Features of SVG as XML # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## Formal Definition **SVG (Scalable Vector Graphics)** is an XML-based markup language recommended by the **W3C (World Wide Web Consortium)** for describing two-dimensional vector graphics, both static and animated, on the web. As an XML document, every SVG file is a well-formed XML 1.0 document that uses standard XML syntax, parsing rules, and namespace conventions. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus):** > SVG is not a binary image format (like PNG or JPEG). It is a **text-based, declarative, structured XML document** where graphical elements are represented as XML elements with attributes that define geometry, styling, and behavior. ## Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Think of SVG as a **"blueprint written in plain English sentences"**. - Imagine you are an architect describing a house to a builder. Instead of handing them a *photograph* of the house (a raster image like JPG), you hand them a *written plan*: "Draw a rectangle 100 meters wide and 50 meters tall, then place a triangle on top as a roof." - That written plan is exactly what SVG is for graphics. The browser reads the **text instructions (XML tags)** and **draws** the picture on the screen. - Because the document is plain text, you can open it in Notepad, search inside it, change a number, and the picture updates ŌĆö something impossible with a compressed JPG. > [!NOTE] > **Standard Metric (W3C):** SVG 1.1 was published on **14 January 2003** and SVG 2 reached full Recommendation status in **September 2018**. The current namespace URI is: > $$\text{http://www.w3.org/2000/svg}$$ ## What "SVG as XML" Specifically Means | Aspect | Meaning for SVG | |---|---| | **Syntax** | Follows strict XML 1.0 well-formedness rules | | **Root Element** | `

topics.title: SVG Tools
topics.content: > **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEITT833 - WEB DESIGNING > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** SVG Tools # Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition of SVG Tools **SVG (Scalable Vector Graphics)** is an **XML-based**, W3C-recommended markup language used for describing two-dimensional vector graphics. It is a text-based, open standard file format that supports interactivity, animation, and full styling via CSS. > [!IMPORTANT] > **SVG Tools** refer to the collection of software applications, online platforms, command-line utilities, and libraries used to **create, edit, optimize, animate, convert, and integrate** SVG graphics into modern web development workflows.
