## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Analytical Matrix: HTTP Request/Response Cycle ŌĆö Exhaustive Walkthrough

The KTU 2024 syllabus expects students to be able to trace a complete HTTP transaction. Let us derive the entire request-response cycle step by step.

**Scenario**: A user types `http://www.examplewebsite.com/index.html` into Chrome.

---

**Step 1 ŌĆö URL Parsing by the Browser**

The browser decomposes the URL into its structural components:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{URL} &= \text{Protocol} + \text{"://"} + \text{Host} + \text{":" + Port} + \text{Path} + \text{"?" + Query} + \text{"#" + Fragment} \\
\text{Example} &= \underbrace{\text{http}}_{\text{Protocol}} \text{"://"} \underbrace{\text{www.examplewebsite.com}}_{\text{Host}} \text{":"}\underbrace{80}_{\text{Port (default)}} \underbrace{\text{/index.html}}_{\text{Path}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conversion Logic**: The browser identifies `http` as the application-layer protocol, `www.examplewebsite.com` as the destination hostname, port `80` as implicit (because HTTP defaults to 80), and `/index.html` as the resource path on the server.

---

**Step 2 ŌĆö DNS Resolution (Hierarchical Lookup)**

The browser needs the IP address corresponding to `www.examplewebsite.com`. It queries the DNS system:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Browser Cache Check} &\rightarrow \text{No record} \\
\text{OS Cache Check} &\rightarrow \text{No record} \\
\text{ISP DNS Resolver} &\rightarrow \text{No record} \rightarrow \text{Query Root Server} \\
\text{Root Server} &\rightarrow \text{Refer to .com TLD Server} \\
\text{.com TLD Server} &\rightarrow \text{Refer to Authoritative NS for examplewebsite.com} \\
\text{Authoritative NS} &\rightarrow \text{Return A Record: 93.184.216.34}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conversion Logic**: The IP address `93.184.216.34` is the actual network-layer address where the web server is reachable. The browser now has all the information needed to establish a TCP connection.

---

**Step 3 ŌĆö TCP Three-Way Handshake**

The browser's TCP layer initiates a connection:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 3.1: SYN} &\rightarrow \text{Client sends SYN packet (seq=x) to Server} \\
\text{Step 3.2: SYN-ACK} &\rightarrow \text{Server responds with SYN-ACK (seq=y, ack=x+1)} \\
\text{Step 3.3: ACK} &\rightarrow \text{Client sends ACK (seq=x+1, ack=y+1)} \\
\text{Result} &\rightarrow \text{TCP connection established on port 80}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conversion Logic**: This handshake ensures both client and server are ready for reliable, ordered data transmission. After Step 3.3, the connection is in the `ESTABLISHED` state.

---

**Step 4 ŌĆö HTTP Request Message Construction**

The browser constructs an HTTP request:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{HTTP Request} &= \text{Request Line} + \text{Headers} + \text{Blank Line} + \text{Body (optional)} \\
\text{Request Line} &= \text{"GET /index.html HTTP/1.1"} \\
\text{Headers} &= \text{Host: www.examplewebsite.com} \\
&\quad \text{User-Agent: Mozilla/5.0 (Windows NT 10.0)} \\
&\quad \text{Accept: text/html, application/xhtml+xml} \\
&\quad \text{Accept-Language: en-US,en;q=0.9} \\
&\quad \text{Connection: keep-alive}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conversion Logic**: The `GET` method requests a representation of the resource `/index.html`. The `Host` header is mandatory in HTTP/1.1 to support virtual hosting (multiple domains on one IP).

---

**Step 5 ŌĆö Server-Side Processing**

The web server (e.g., Apache) receives the request:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Apache Processing Pipeline} &= \text{Receive Request} \rightarrow \text{Parse Headers} \rightarrow \text{URL Rewrite} \\
&\rightarrow \text{Authentication Check} \rightarrow \text{Authorization Check} \\
&\rightarrow \text{Locate /var/www/html/index.html} \\
&\rightarrow \text{Read File from Disk} \\
&\rightarrow \text{Apply Compression (gzip if Accept-Encoding allows)} \\
&\rightarrow \text{Generate Response}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conversion Logic**: If the file is static HTML, Apache simply reads it. If it's a `.php` file, the PHP interpreter executes the script and produces HTML output. The result is wrapped in an HTTP response.

---

**Step 6 ŌĆö HTTP Response Message Transmission**

The server sends back:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Status Line} &= \text{"HTTP/1.1 200 OK"} \\
\text{Response Headers} &= \text{Content-Type: text/html; charset=UTF-8} \\
&\quad \text{Content-Length: 1256} \\
&\quad \text{Date: Mon, 15 Jan 2024 10:30:00 GMT} \\
&\quad \text{Server: Apache/2.4.52} \\
\text{Blank Line} &= \text{CRLF} \\
\text{Body} &= \text{"<!DOCTYPE html><html>...</html>"} \quad (1256 \text{ bytes})
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conversion Logic**: Status `200 OK` indicates success. The `Content-Type` header tells the browser to interpret the body as HTML. `Content-Length` allows the browser to know when the response body ends.

---

**Step 7 ŌĆö Browser Rendering Pipeline**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Bytes Received} &\rightarrow \text{Decode (UTF-8)} \rightarrow \text{Parse HTML} \rightarrow \text{Build DOM Tree} \\
\text{Find Linked Resources} &\rightarrow \text{Link CSS} \rightarrow \text{Build CSSOM} \\
\text{Combine DOM + CSSOM} &\rightarrow \text{Build Render Tree} \\
\text{Layout (Reflow)} &\rightarrow \text{Paint Pixels} \rightarrow \text{Composite Layers} \\
\text{Display on Screen} &\rightarrow \text{Page Fully Loaded}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conversion Logic**: This is the critical phase where raw network bytes become a visual webpage. Modern browsers use hardware acceleration for the final composite step.

---

**Step 8 ŌĆö Connection Lifecycle**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{If } \text{Connection: keep-alive} &\rightarrow \text{TCP connection reused for further requests (images, CSS, JS)} \\
\text{If } \text{Connection: close} &\rightarrow \text{TCP FIN handshake: FIN ŌåÆ ACK ŌåÆ FIN ŌåÆ ACK ŌåÆ CLOSED} \\
\text{HTTP/2 Multiplexing} &\rightarrow \text{Multiple requests/responses over a single TCP connection simultaneously}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Web Hosting Cost-Performance Tradeoff

For KTU 2024 numerical/analytical questions, students may be asked to compare hosting types. The following derivation establishes the relationship between cost, performance, and resource allocation.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total Cost of Ownership (TCO)} &= C_{\text{server}} + C_{\text{bandwidth}} + C_{\text{storage}} + C_{\text{maintenance}} + C_{\text{security}} \\
\text{Performance Score (P)} &= \alpha \cdot \text{CPU}_{cores} + \beta \cdot \text{RAM}_{GB} + \gamma \cdot \text{IOPS} \\
\text{Where} \quad \alpha &= 0.4, \quad \beta = 0.3, \quad \gamma = 0.3 \quad \text{(weighting coefficients)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Example Calculation (KTU-style)**:

A startup has two hosting options:
- **Option A ŌĆö Shared Hosting**: Ōé╣200/month, shared CPU (1 core), 512 MB RAM, 1000 IOPS.
- **Option B ŌĆö VPS Hosting**: Ōé╣1500/month, dedicated 2 CPU cores, 4 GB RAM, 5000 IOPS.

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_A &= 0.4 \times 1 + 0.3 \times 0.5 + 0.3 \times 1.0 = 0.4 + 0.15 + 0.3 = 0.85 \\
P_B &= 0.4 \times 2 + 0.3 \times 4 + 0.3 \times 5.0 = 0.8 + 1.2 + 1.5 = 3.5 \\
\text{Cost per Performance Unit (A)} &= \frac{200}{0.85} \approx 235.29 \\
\text{Cost per Performance Unit (B)} &= \frac{1500}{3.5} \approx 428.57
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conversion Logic**: Option A is cheaper per performance unit, but Option B delivers 4.1├Ś more raw performance. For a low-traffic site, Option A is better value. For a growing application expecting >10,000 daily visitors, Option B's higher performance ceiling justifies the cost.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip ŌĆö Numerical Questions on Web Hosting:**
> When comparing hosting options, always compute:
> 1. **Performance Score (P)** using CPU, RAM, and IOPS weighting.
> 2. **Cost per Performance Unit** = Monthly Cost ├Ę P.
> 3. **Scalability Factor** = Max concurrent users supported.
> 4. **Recommendation** based on traffic projection, budget, and technical expertise.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: DNS Resolution Time Calculation

DNS resolution adds latency to every web request. The total DNS resolution time can be modeled as:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{DNS} &= T_{cache} + T_{query} + T_{response} + T_{processing} \\
T_{cache} &= \begin{cases} 0 \text{ ms} & \text{if cached locally} \\ 20\text{ŌĆō}50 \text{ ms} & \text{if ISP cache hit} \\ 100\text{ŌĆō}300 \text{ ms} & \text{if full recursive lookup} \end{cases} \\
T_{query} &= \frac{\text{Packet Size}}{\text{Bandwidth}} = \frac{64 \text{ bytes}}{10^8 \text{ bps}} \approx 0.005 \text{ ms} \\
T_{response} &= RTT \times N_{hops} \quad \text{(RTT = Round-Trip Time, } N_{hops} \text{ = number of network hops)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conversion Logic**: On a typical broadband connection, a cached DNS lookup takes ~20 ms, while a full cold lookup can take 100ŌĆō300 ms. CDNs and DNS prefetching (`<link rel="dns-prefetch" href="//cdn.example.com">`) are used to minimize this latency.

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Diagram: End-to-End Web Server Access Flow

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the complete flow of how a client accesses a web server, from URL entry to page rendering.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[User Types URL in Browser] --> B[Browser Parses URL]
    B --> C{DNS Cache Hit?}
    C -->|Yes| E[Use Cached IP Address]
    C -->|No| D[Send DNS Query to ISP Resolver]
    D --> D1[Root Server Lookup]
    D1 --> D2[TLD Server Lookup]
    D2 --> D3[Authoritative NS Lookup]
    D3 --> E
    E --> F[Establish TCP Connection via 3-Way Handshake]
    F --> G[Send HTTP GET Request to Server Port 80 or 443]
    G --> H[Web Server Receives Request]
    H --> I{Static or Dynamic Resource?}
    I -->|Static| J1[Read File from Disk]
    I -->|Dynamic| J2[Execute Server-Side Script PHP Python Nodejs]
    J1 --> K[Generate HTTP Response with Status Code]
    J2 --> K
    K --> L[Send Response to Browser]
    L --> M[Browser Parses HTML CSS JS]
    M --> N[Construct DOM and Render Tree]
    N --> O[Display Final Web Page to User]
    O --> P[Close TCP Connection or Keep Alive]
```

**Interpretation of the Flow**:
- The diagram traces every critical step from user input to visual output.
- The **DNS Cache** decision point (`C`) is a key performance optimization.
- The **Static vs Dynamic** branch (`I`) distinguishes simple file serving from application logic.
- The final state (`P`) represents connection cleanup, essential for resource management.

---

### 4.2 Diagram: Web Hosting Architecture Comparison (Block-Level)

Since physical server rack drawings are not feasible in Mermaid, the following block diagram compares the architectural differences between **Shared**, **VPS**, and **Dedicated** hosting.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SHARED[Shared Hosting - 1 Physical Server = Many Websites]
        SH1[Website A]
        SH2[Website B]
        SH3[Website C]
        SH4[Website D]
        SH_OS[Single OS - Single Apache Instance]
        SH_CPU[Shared CPU Pool]
        SH_RAM[Shared RAM Pool]
        SH1 --> SH_OS
        SH2 --> SH_OS
        SH3 --> SH_OS
        SH4 --> SH_OS
        SH_OS --> SH_CPU
        SH_OS --> SH_RAM
    end

    subgraph VPS[VPS Hosting - 1 Physical Server = Multiple Virtual Servers]
        VP_HV[Hypervisor Layer - KVM or Xen]
        VP1[VM 1 - Website A - Dedicated OS]
        VP2[VM 2 - Website B - Dedicated OS]
        VP3[VM 3 - Website C - Dedicated OS]
        VP_CPU[Physical CPU]
        VP_RAM[Physical RAM]
        VP1 --> VP_HV
        VP2 --> VP_HV
        VP3 --> VP_HV
        VP_HV --> VP_CPU
        VP_HV --> VP_RAM
    end

    subgraph DED[Dedicated Hosting - 1 Physical Server = 1 Client]
        D1[Single Client Website]
        D_OS[Full OS - Full Root Access]
        D_CPU[Entire Physical CPU]
        D_RAM[Entire Physical RAM]
        D1 --> D_OS
        D_OS --> D_CPU
        D_OS --> D_RAM
    end
```

**Interpretation of the Architecture**:
- In **Shared Hosting**, all sites compete for the same CPU/RAM pool ŌĆö performance is unpredictable during traffic spikes.
- In **VPS Hosting**, the hypervisor enforces resource isolation ŌĆö each VM gets guaranteed CPU/RAM slices.
- In **Dedicated Hosting**, there is no sharing ŌĆö the client has full control over the entire machine.

---

### 4.3 Diagram: DNS Resolution Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Browser Asks: What is IP of www.ktu.ac.in?] --> B[Check Local Cache]
    B --> C{Hit?}
    C -->|Yes| Z[Return Cached IP]
    C -->|No| D[Query ISP Recursive Resolver]
    D --> E[Query Root Server - 13 worldwide]
    E --> F[Root Refers to .in TLD Server]
    F --> G[Query .in TLD Server]
    G --> H[TLD Refers to ac.in NS]
    H --> I[Query ac.in Authoritative Name Server]
    I --> J[Authoritative NS Returns A Record: 103.42.156.10]
    J --> K[Browser Connects to 103.42.156.10 Port 443]
    K --> Z
```

**Interpretation of the Hierarchy**:
- DNS resolution is **hierarchical and distributed** ŌĆö no single server holds the entire mapping.
- The **Root Server** only knows where to find TLD servers.
- The **TLD Server** (e.g., for `.in`) only knows where to find authoritative servers for second-level domains.
- The **Authoritative Name Server** holds the definitive A/CNAME/MX records for the specific domain.

---

### 4.4 Diagram: Web Publishing Workflow Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Step 1: Design and Develop Website Locally] --> P2[Step 2: Test on Local Server XAMPP WAMP]
    P2 --> P3[Step 3: Register Domain Name at Registrar]
    P3 --> P4[Step 4: Purchase Hosting Plan from Provider]
    P4 --> P5[Step 5: Configure DNS - Point Domain to Hosting Server]
    P5 --> P6[Step 6: Upload Files via FTP or SFTP]
    P6 --> P7[Step 7: Install SSL Certificate for HTTPS]
    P7 --> P8[Step 8: Test and Launch Website]
    P8 --> P9[Step 9: Monitor Performance and Analytics]
    P9 --> P10[Step 10: Maintain and Update Content Regularly]
```

**Interpretation of the Pipeline**:
- This is the **standard 10-step publishing workflow** used by professional web developers.
- Steps 1ŌĆō2 happen *before* going live (local development).
- Steps 3ŌĆō7 are the *deployment phase* where the site becomes publicly accessible.
- Steps 8ŌĆō10 are the *post-launch phase* (monitoring, analytics, maintenance).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Practical / Lab Tip:** Most B.Tech project evaluations at KTU require students to demonstrate hosting a website on **GitHub Pages**, **Netlify**, or **000webhost** (free tier). The steps in this pipeline map directly to the deployment viva questions.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam ŌĆō Model Question, Module 4]**
*Define a web server. List any four popular web server software with their developers.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A **web server** is a software application (running on hardware) that processes incoming HTTP/HTTPS requests from clients (web browsers) and serves the requested web resources (HTML pages, images, CSS, JavaScript files) as responses.

| # | Web Server | Developer |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Apache HTTP Server** | Apache Software Foundation |
| 2 | **Nginx** | Igor Sysoev (originally), now F5 Networks |
| 3 | **Microsoft IIS** | Microsoft Corporation |
| 4 | **LiteSpeed** | LiteSpeed Technologies |

*(1 mark for definition + 2 marks for the table)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam ŌĆō Model Question, Module 4]**
*What is the difference between a domain name and an IP address? Why is DNS necessary?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

- **IP Address**: A numerical label (e.g., `103.42.156.10`) assigned to every device on a network, used by machines to locate each other. *(1 mark)*
- **Domain Name**: A human-readable name (e.g., `www.ktu.ac.in`) that maps to an IP address. *(1 mark)*
- **Why DNS is Necessary**: Humans cannot easily remember numerical IP addresses (especially IPv6 which is 128-bit). DNS acts as a *phonebook* of the internet, translating domain names into IP addresses automatically. Without DNS, users would need to memorize raw IP addresses for every website they want to visit. *(1 mark)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each ŌĆö Internal Choice)

---

**Q3A. [KTU University Exam ŌĆō Model Question, Module 4] (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Explain in detail the different types of web hosting services. Compare shared hosting, VPS hosting, and dedicated hosting in terms of cost, performance, and resource isolation. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Describe the complete HTTP request-response cycle with a neat diagram. Explain the role of DNS, TCP, and HTTP in this cycle. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Types of Web Hosting Services (7 Marks):**

Web hosting services can be categorized as follows:

1. **Shared Hosting** (1 mark): Multiple websites share a single physical server and its resources (CPU, RAM, bandwidth). It is the **cheapest** option (Ōé╣100ŌĆōŌé╣500/month) and ideal for beginners, personal blogs, and small static sites. The downside is that a traffic surge on one site can slow down all sites on the same server ("noisy neighbor" effect).

2. **VPS (Virtual Private Server) Hosting** (1.5 marks): A physical server is partitioned into multiple virtual machines using a **hypervisor** (KVM, Xen, VMware). Each VPS has dedicated CPU cores, RAM, and storage allocations. It offers **better resource isolation** than shared hosting. Cost: Ōé╣500ŌĆōŌé╣3000/month. Suitable for medium-traffic business sites and developers needing root access.

3. **Dedicated Hosting** (1.5 marks): The entire physical server is leased to a single client. The client has **full root access** and total control over the hardware and software. It provides the **highest performance and security** but is expensive (Ōé╣5,000ŌĆōŌé╣50,000/month). Used by large enterprises with high traffic (e.g., e-commerce platforms with millions of users).

4. **Cloud Hosting** (1 mark): Resources are distributed across a network of virtual servers (AWS, Azure, Google Cloud). Offers **elastic scalability** ŌĆö resources can be scaled up or down based on demand. Pricing is pay-as-you-go. Examples: AWS EC2, Azure VM, Google Compute Engine.

5. **Managed Hosting** (1 mark): The hosting provider handles all technical management ŌĆö server updates, security patches, backups, monitoring. Suitable for clients without dedicated IT staff.

**Comparison Table (1 mark):**

| Parameter | Shared | VPS | Dedicated |
|---|---|---|---|
| Cost | Lowest | Moderate | Highest |
| Performance | Low (shared) | Medium (isolated) | High (exclusive) |
| Resource Isolation | None | Partial (via hypervisor) | Complete |
| Control | Limited (cPanel) | Root access | Full root access |
| Scalability | Very Limited | Moderate | Limited (hardware-bound) |
| Best For | Beginners | Medium sites | Enterprises |

---

**(b) HTTP Request-Response Cycle (7 Marks):**

The HTTP request-response cycle consists of the following stages:

1. **URL Entry and Parsing** (1 mark): The user enters a URL (e.g., `http://www.example.com/page.html`) in the browser. The browser parses it into protocol, host, port, and path.

2. **DNS Resolution** (1 mark): The browser queries the DNS system to translate the domain name `www.example.com` into an IP address (e.g., `93.184.216.34`). If cached locally, this takes <20 ms; otherwise, a full recursive lookup takes 100ŌĆō300 ms.

3. **TCP Connection** (1 mark): A TCP three-way handshake (SYN ŌåÆ SYN-ACK ŌåÆ ACK) is performed to establish a reliable connection to the server on port 80 (HTTP) or 443 (HTTPS).

4. **HTTP Request** (1 mark): The browser sends an HTTP request:
   ```
   GET /page.html HTTP/1.1
   Host: www.example.com
   User-Agent: Mozilla/5.0
   Accept: text/html
   ```

5. **Server Processing** (1 mark): The web server (Apache/Nginx) locates the file, executes any server-side logic, and prepares the response.

6. **HTTP Response** (1 mark): The server sends back:
   ```
   HTTP/1.1 200 OK
   Content-Type: text/html
   Content-Length: 1256
   <!DOCTYPE html>...
   ```

7. **Browser Rendering & Connection Closure** (1 mark): The browser parses the HTML, fetches linked resources, builds the DOM, and renders the page. The TCP connection is then closed (or kept alive for further requests).

**Role of Key Components**:
- **DNS**: Translates domain name to IP address.
- **TCP**: Provides reliable, ordered byte-stream delivery.
- **HTTP**: Defines the message format and semantics for web communication.

**Q3B. [KTU University Exam ŌĆō Model Question, Module 4] (14 Marks) ŌĆö Alternative Choice**

**(a)** What is Web Publishing? Explain the step-by-step process of publishing a website on the internet, including domain registration, hosting selection, and file upload. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain the Domain Name System (DNS) in detail. With a suitable diagram, describe the hierarchical DNS resolution process. List at least four DNS record types with examples. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Web Publishing (7 Marks):**

**Web Publishing** is the process of making web content (HTML, CSS, JavaScript, multimedia files) publicly accessible on the World Wide Web by uploading it to a web server that is connected to the internet.

**Step-by-Step Process (7 Marks, ~1 mark each):**

1. **Design and Develop the Website**: Create HTML pages, style with CSS, add interactivity with JavaScript. Use tools like VS Code, Adobe Dreamweaver.

2. **Test Locally**: Before publishing, test the website on a local server (XAMPP, WAMP, MAMP) to ensure all links, scripts, and layouts work correctly.

3. **Register a Domain Name**: Choose a unique domain name (e.g., `mycollegeproject.com`) and register it through a domain registrar (GoDaddy, Namecheap, BigRock). Registration is typically annual (Ōé╣500ŌĆōŌé╣2000/year for `.com`).

4. **Choose a Web Hosting Plan**: Select a hosting provider (Bluehost, Hostinger, SiteGround for paid; 000webhost, GitHub Pages for free). Pick a plan based on traffic, storage, and budget.

5. **Configure DNS**: Update the domain's nameservers (NS records) to point to the hosting provider's DNS. Alternatively, set an **A Record** pointing the domain to the hosting server's IP address.

6. **Upload Website Files**: Use an FTP client (FileZilla) or SFTP to upload the website files to the hosting server's `public_html` or `www` directory. The file `index.html` is the default homepage.

7. **Launch and Promote**: Once DNS propagation completes (takes 2ŌĆō48 hours), the website becomes accessible globally at the registered domain name. Submit the URL to search engines (Google Search Console) for indexing.

---

**(b) Domain Name System (DNS) (7 Marks):**

The **Domain Name System (DNS)** is a distributed, hierarchical naming system that translates human-readable domain names (e.g., `www.ktu.ac.in`) into machine-readable IP addresses (e.g., `103.42.156.10`). It is often called the *"phonebook of the internet."*

**DNS Hierarchy (2 marks):**

```
Root (.)
   Ōö£ŌöĆŌöĆ TLD (.com, .org, .net, .in, .edu)
   Ōöé     Ōö£ŌöĆŌöĆ Second-Level Domain (google, ktu)
   Ōöé     Ōöé     Ōö£ŌöĆŌöĆ Subdomain (www, mail, blog)
   Ōöé     Ōöé     Ōöé     ŌööŌöĆŌöĆ Host / FQDN (www.google.com)
```

**DNS Resolution Process (2 marks):**

When a browser requests `www.example.com`:
1. The browser checks its **local cache**.
2. If not found, it queries the **ISP's recursive resolver**.
3. The resolver queries the **Root Server** (`.`) ŌåÆ gets referral to `.com` TLD server.
4. The resolver queries the **`.com` TLD Server** ŌåÆ gets referral to the authoritative name server for `example.com`.
5. The resolver queries the **Authoritative NS** ŌåÆ gets the **A Record** with the IP address.
6. The IP is returned to the browser, which then establishes a TCP connection to the web server.

**DNS Record Types (3 marks ŌĆö 0.75 each):**

| Record Type | Purpose | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **A Record** | Maps domain to IPv4 address | `example.com ŌåÆ 93.184.216.34` |
| **AAAA Record** | Maps domain to IPv6 address | `example.com ŌåÆ 2606:2800:220:1:248:1893:25c8:1946` |
| **CNAME Record** | Creates an alias (domain ŌåÆ domain) | `www.example.com ŌåÆ example.com` |
| **MX Record** | Specifies mail server for the domain | `example.com ŌåÆ mail.example.com (priority 10)` |

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning ŌĆö Common Pitfalls:**
>
> 1. **Confusing Publishing with Hosting**: Students often write "web hosting" when asked about "web publishing" or vice versa. Remember: *Publishing* = the act of uploading; *Hosting* = the infrastructure service. (Lose 1ŌĆō2 marks)
>
> 2. **Forgetting Default Ports**: HTTP = 80, HTTPS = 443. This is a **favorite 1-mark sub-question** in KTU exams. Missing it costs easy marks.
>
> 3. **Wrong DNS Hierarchy**: Writing ".com" as the root server instead of the actual root (`.`). Root servers are represented by the dot `.` symbol and are 13 logical server clusters worldwide.
>
> 4. **Confusing A Record with CNAME**: A Record = domain ŌåÆ IP; CNAME = domain ŌåÆ another domain. Many students mix these up.
>
> 5. **Missing the "Why" in Hosting Comparisons**: KTU 2024 scheme emphasizes *justification*. Don't just list hosting types ŌĆö explain *why* one is preferred over another for a given use case.
>
> 6. **Not Drawing Diagrams for HTTP Cycle**: A neat flow diagram of the request-response cycle is worth **at least 2ŌĆō3 marks** in a 7-mark sub-question. Skipping it is a major loss.
>
> 7. **Spelling "FTP" as "FTTP" or "FTPS"**: FTP (File Transfer Protocol, port 21) ŌēĀ FTPS (FTP over SSL) ŌēĀ SFTP (SSH File Transfer Protocol, port 22). Examiners catch this.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

Below is a high-density, rapid-revision checklist for **Module 4 ŌĆö Accessing Web Servers, Introduction to Web Publishing or Hosting**:

- Ō£ģ **Web Server** = software + hardware that serves HTTP/HTTPS responses. Examples: Apache, Nginx, IIS, LiteSpeed.
- Ō£ģ **Default Ports** = HTTP ŌåÆ **80**, HTTPS ŌåÆ **443**, FTP ŌåÆ **21**, SFTP ŌåÆ **22**.
- Ō£ģ **HTTP Methods** = GET (read), POST (create), PUT (update), DELETE (remove). The most common in web browsing is **GET**.
- Ō£ģ **HTTP Status Codes** = 200 (OK), 301 (Moved Permanently), 404 (Not Found), 500 (Internal Server Error), 403 (Forbidden).
- Ō£ģ **DNS** = Domain Name System; translates domain names ŌåÆ IP addresses. Hierarchical, distributed.
- Ō£ģ **DNS Root Servers** = 13 logical server clusters globally, managed by ICANN.
- Ō£ģ **DNS Records** = A (IPv4), AAAA (IPv6), CNAME (alias), MX (mail), NS (name server), TXT (verification).
- Ō£ģ **Hosting Types** = Shared (cheapest, lowest performance), VPS (virtualized, moderate), Dedicated (full server, high cost), Cloud (elastic, scalable), Managed (provider-managed), Free (GitHub Pages, Netlify).
- Ō£ģ **Web Publishing Workflow** = Design ŌåÆ Develop ŌåÆ Test Locally ŌåÆ Register Domain ŌåÆ Buy Hosting ŌåÆ Configure DNS ŌåÆ Upload Files (FTP/SFTP) ŌåÆ Install SSL ŌåÆ Launch ŌåÆ Monitor.
- Ō£ģ **FTP Client Tools** = FileZilla, WinSCP, Cyberduck (used to upload website files to server).
- Ō£ģ **Local Server Tools** = XAMPP (Cross-platform Apache MySQL PHP Perl), WAMP (Windows), MAMP (Mac), LAMP (Linux).
- Ō£ģ **Domain Registrars** = GoDaddy, Namecheap, BigRock, Google Domains.
- Ō£ģ **Cloud Providers** = AWS (Amazon), Azure (Microsoft), GCP (Google), DigitalOcean, Linode.
- Ō£ģ **SSL/TLS** = Encrypts HTTP traffic ŌåÆ HTTPS. Modern standard: TLS 1.3. Free certificate authority: **Let's Encrypt**.
- Ō£ģ **HTTP Evolution** = HTTP/0.9 (1991) ŌåÆ HTTP/1.1 (1997) ŌåÆ HTTP/2 (2015, binary multiplexing) ŌåÆ HTTP/3 (2022, QUIC over UDP).
- Ō£ģ **Key Distinction** = *Web Publishing* = process of making content live. *Web Hosting* = service providing server infrastructure.
- Ō£ģ **Three-Way Handshake** = SYN ŌåÆ SYN-ACK ŌåÆ ACK (TCP connection establishment before any HTTP data).
- Ō£ģ **Virtual Hosting** = One physical server hosts multiple domains; HTTP/1.1 `Host` header is mandatory to distinguish them.
- Ō£ģ **CDN (Content Delivery Network)** = Distributed servers that cache content closer to users (e.g., Cloudflare, Akamai) to reduce latency.
- Ō£ģ **For KTU Viva/Practical**: Be ready to explain how to deploy a static site on **GitHub Pages** or **Netlify** ŌĆö these are free, and most B.Tech project evaluations involve live deployment demos.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute KTU Exam Strategy (Module 4):**
> - Memorize the **default ports** and **HTTP status codes** ŌĆö these are 1ŌĆō2 mark guaranteed questions.
> - Be able to **draw the DNS hierarchy** and the **HTTP request-response cycle** from memory.
> - For 14-mark questions, structure your answer as: *Definition ŌåÆ List/Types ŌåÆ Comparison Table ŌåÆ Diagram ŌåÆ Real-world example*. This is the exact pattern KTU board examiners look for.
> - Always conclude hosting-comparison answers with a **recommendation** based on use case (e.g., "For a student portfolio, shared or free hosting is optimal").
